topics.title: Threat Intelligence Overview and Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT755 - CYBER THREAT INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Threat Intelligence Overview and Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI)** is the disciplined, evidence-based collection, processing, integration, evaluation, analysis, and interpretation of available information concerning adversarial threats to cyber systems. It is *actionable knowledge* that allows organizations to understand past, present, and future adversarial capabilities, intentions, motivations, tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) so that defensive decisions can be made in a timely, risk-aware, and resource-efficient manner.

In simpler KTU-board terminology:
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threat Intelligence = Evidence + Context + Actionability.**
> Raw data (IPs, hashes, domain names) becomes *intelligence* only when it is correlated, validated, and translated into a defensive decision.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Neighborhood Watch

Imagine a large apartment complex. Each family locks its doors and installs CCTV (this is your *firewall / EDR / SIEM*). But a single family cannot see the entire threat landscape. Now suppose a **Neighborhood Watch Coordinator** does the following:

| Real-World Watch | Cyber Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Collects reports of break-ins from many houses | Aggregates logs/feeds from multiple sensors |
| Identifies a pattern (e.g., burglar strikes between 2-4 AM) | Identifies TTPs of an APT group |
| Warns specific vulnerable households | Pushes IOCs to firewalls/EDRs |
| Shares findings with the police | Shares with ISACs, CERTs, MISP |

That coordinator is the **Threat Intelligence Platform (TIP)** and the team behind it is the **CTI Analyst**. The key insight is that intelligence is **not** the raw observation — it is the *inference* drawn from many observations.

## 1.3 Core Terminology You Must Know

> [!NOTE]
> These definitions are KTU-board favorites and appear frequently in 3-mark questions.

- **Threat** — Any circumstance or event with the potential to adversely impact organizational operations, assets, or individuals via unauthorized access, destruction, disclosure, or modification.
- **Vulnerability** — A weakness in an information system that could be exploited or triggered by a threat source.
- **Indicator of Compromise (IOC)** — A forensic artifact observed on a network or in an operating system that indicates a likely intrusion (e.g., malicious IP, file hash, domain, registry key).
- **TTP (Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures)** — The behavioral pattern of an adversary; the "how" and "why" behind an attack.
- **APT (Advanced Persistent Threat)** — A sophisticated, well-resourced, patient adversary (often nation-state) conducting long-term campaigns.
- **STIX / TAXII** — Industry standards for *Structured Threat Information eXpression* and *Trusted Automated eXchange of Intelligence Information* used to share CTI in a machine-readable format.
- **IOC Lifecycle** — The five phases a single indicator moves through: *Creation → Detection → Association → Actionability → Expiration*.

## 1.4 Why Threat Intelligence Matters in 2024–2025

Modern enterprises face **3,000+ alerts per day** on average. Without prioritization, SOC teams suffer *alert fatigue*. CTI provides the **context** that turns an alert from "noise" into a "high-fidelity, prioritized action." The **MITRE ATT&CK** matrix — adopted globally as the de-facto taxonomy — currently documents **14 Enterprise Tactics, 200+ Techniques, and 600+ Sub-techniques** (as of 2024), giving analysts a common language to map adversary behavior.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Threat Intelligence

KTU expects you to classify CTI into exactly **four consumer-aligned types**. Each answers a different "Who/What/Why/How" question and serves a different stakeholder.

### 2.1.1 Strategic Intelligence
- **Audience:** Board of Directors, CISO, Senior Management.
- **Format:** High-level reports, dashboards, briefings.
- **Content:** Geopolitical risk, adversary motivations, industry-wide trends, financial impact.
- **Time horizon:** Months to years.
- **Example:** "Adversary group *Volt Typhoon* is targeting U.S. critical infrastructure in preparation for geopolitical conflict."

### 2.1.2 Operational Intelligence
- **Audience:** SOC Managers, Incident Responders, Hunt Teams.
- **Format:** Campaign reports, attribution assessments.
- **Content:** Specific adversary campaigns, infrastructure, tooling, victimology.
- **Time horizon:** Weeks to months.
- **Example:** "Group X is currently deploying Cobalt Strike beacons against healthcare orgs via phishing .iso attachments."

### 2.1.3 Tactical Intelligence
- **Audience:** SOC Analysts, Detection Engineers, Red Teamers.
- **Format:** STIX/TAXII feeds, IOCs, YARA rules, Sigma rules.
- **Content:** Specific TTPs, attack patterns, tool signatures.
- **Time horizon:** Days to weeks.
- **Example:** "Adversary uses `powershell.exe -enc <base64>` followed by lateral movement via WMI."

### 2.1.4 Technical Intelligence
- **Audience:** Automated systems (SIEM, EDR, Firewalls, TIPs).
- **Format:** Machine-readable IOCs.
- **Content:** IPs, domains, URLs, file hashes, mutexes, registry keys.
- **Time horizon:** Hours to days (highly perishable).
- **Example:** SHA256 hash `a4f5...c7` is a known Emotet dropper.

## 2.2 The Threat Intelligence Lifecycle (Six Phases)

This is the **#1 most-asked diagram** in KTU exams. Memorize the six phases in order:

1. **Planning & Direction** — Define intelligence requirements (the *Priority Intelligence Requirements* or PIRs).
2. **Collection** — Gather raw data from internal logs, OSINT, HUMINT, ISACs, commercial feeds, dark web.
3. **Processing & Exploitation** — Normalize, de-duplicate, parse, enrich (geoIP, whois, sandbox detonation).
4. **Analysis & Production** — Correlate IOCs, attribute to adversary groups, map to ATT&CK, assess impact.
5. **Dissemination & Integration** — Push to consumers (SIEM rules, firewall blocks, executive briefings).
6. **Feedback** — Consumers rate usefulness; PIRs are refined.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is your one-stop revision grid for Module 1. Memorize the **bold** terms.

| # | Concept | Key Definition / Formula | Primary Consumer | Time Horizon | KTU Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Strategic TI** | High-level risk to business | Board / CISO | Months–Years | 2 marks |
| 2 | **Operational TI** | Campaign & attribution | SOC Mgr / IR Team | Weeks–Months | 3 marks |
| 3 | **Tactical TI** | TTP / ATT&CK mapping | SOC Analysts | Days–Weeks | 3 marks |
| 4 | **Technical TI** | IOCs (IP, hash, domain) | Automated Tools | Hours–Days | 2 marks |
| 5 | **IOC Lifecycle** | Create → Detect → Associate → Actionable → Expire | All | Constant | 2 marks |
| 6 | **CTI Lifecycle** | Plan → Collect → Process → Analyze → Disseminate → Feedback | Analysts | Per cycle | 5 marks |
| 7 | **STIX** | Language for sharing CTI | Machines / Tools | N/A | 2 marks |
| 8 | **TAXII** | Transport protocol for STIX | Machines / Tools | N/A | 2 marks |
| 9 | **MITRE ATT&CK** | Adversary behavior matrix | Analysts | N/A | 3 marks |
| 10 | **Kill Chain** | 7-stage intrusion model | IR / Hunt | Per incident | 3 marks |

## 2.4 Quantitative Quality Metrics of CTI

Although CTI is largely qualitative, KTU may test these standard reliability scoring systems:

- **Admiralty System / NATO System:** Rates each piece of intelligence on a 2-letter scale.
  - *Source Reliability:* A (Completely reliable) → F (Cannot be judged).
  - *Information Credibility:* 1 (Confirmed) → 6 (Cannot be judged).

- **Confidence Score (commonly used by vendors):** Often expressed as a percentage on a simple additive scale, conceptually:

$$
\text{Confidence}(\%) \;=\; \frac{\text{Verified IOCs}}{\text{Total IOCs Reported}} \times 100
$$

- **Mean Time To Detect (MTTD) improvement** after CTI integration:

$$
\text{MTTD}_{\text{improvement}} \;=\; \text{MTTD}_{\text{baseline}} - \text{MTTD}_{\text{with CTI}}
$$

A mature CTI program typically reduces MTTD from **200+ days** (industry average) to under **24 hours** for known-TTP attacks.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industry, CTI feeds directly power:
- **SIEM correlation rules** (Splunk, QRadar, Elastic).
- **EDR detection logic** (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Microsoft Defender).
- **Firewall/IPS blocklists** (Palo Alto, Fortinet, Cisco).
- **SOAR automated playbooks** (Cortex XSOAR, Splunk SOAR).
- **Vulnerability Prioritization** — pairing CVE data with active exploit-in-the-wild TI from sources like **CISA KEV (Known Exploited Vulnerabilities)**.

For a B.Tech student, this means CTI is the *connective tissue* between offensive knowledge (ethical hacking) and defensive engineering (network security, SOC operations).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This module is conceptual, so "derivations" here mean **exhaustive logical breakdowns** of the CTI lifecycle, the IOC lifecycle, and the quality scoring formulas. Every algebraic and logical step is shown explicitly.

## 3.1 Worked Derivation — From Raw Data to Actionable Intelligence

The transformation from raw data → information → knowledge → intelligence is the *core concept* of CTI. We derive this using the **DIKW (Data–Information–Knowledge–Wisdom) pyramid**, mapped to a CTI context.

### Step 1 — Capture the Raw Data
A single SIEM log line reads:

$$
\text{Log} = \texttt{"2024-11-12 03:14:07 192.168.1.45 → 185.244.25.99 GET /mal.exe"}
$$

This is **raw data** — meaningless on its own. It has *no context* and *no actionable value*.

### Step 2 — Apply Context → Information
Add a *geoIP lookup*, a *VirusTotal hash query*, and a *threat-feed match*:

$$
\text{Information} = \text{Data} + \text{Context}
$$

After enrichment, the same log becomes:

$$
\text{Info} = \texttt{"Host 192.168.1.45 downloaded a malicious binary from } \\
\texttt{a Russian IP (185.244.25.99), hash matches Emotet (TrickBot variant)."}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Pattern Recognition → Knowledge
We compare this event against the ATT&CK matrix and prior incidents:

$$
\text{Att\&CK Mapping} = \{\,T1566.001,\,T1204.002,\,T1059.001,\,T1105\,\}
$$

We now know *the attack chain*: Spear-phishing → User Execution → PowerShell → Ingress Tool Transfer.

### Step 4 — Apply Predictive Inference → Intelligence
Finally, we ask: *What is the adversary likely to do next?* Based on Emotet's known playbook, lateral movement via SMB is probable:

$$
\text{Intelligence} = \text{Knowledge} + \text{Prediction} + \text{Recommended Action}
$$

So the final product is:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *"Emotet group is likely to attempt lateral movement via SMB within 24 hours. Recommendation: (1) Block 185.244.25.99 at egress firewall. (2) Hunt for WMI/SMB lateral movement from 192.168.1.45. (3) Isolate host."*

### Step 5 — Formalize the Transformation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Raw Data} &\xrightarrow{\;+\;\text{Context}\;} \text{Information} \\[4pt]
&\xrightarrow{\;+\;\text{Pattern Recognition}\;} \text{Knowledge} \\[4pt]
&\xrightarrow{\;+\;\text{Prediction + Action}\;} \text{Intelligence}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU expects this four-layer mapping in 14-mark answers.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation — The Confidence Score Formula

Given a CTI feed reports 250 IOCs in a week, and an internal SOC team independently verifies 198 of them (via sandbox + manual review), the confidence score is computed step-by-step.

### Step 1 — Identify Total Reported
$$
\text{Total IOCs Reported} = 250
$$

### Step 2 — Identify Verified Count
$$
\text{Verified IOCs} = 198
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Percentage Formula
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Confidence}(\%) &= \frac{\text{Verified IOCs}}{\text{Total IOCs Reported}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{198}{250} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= 0.792 \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= 79.2\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpret
A confidence score of **79.2 %** is considered *High* by industry standards (typical thresholds: <40 % Low, 40–70 % Medium, >70 % High).

## 3.3 Worked Derivation — IOC Lifecycle State Transition

A single IOC progresses through **five discrete states**. We model this as a finite state machine. Let the state vector be $S = \{C, D, A, X, E\}$ for *Created, Detected, Actionable, Expired*.

### State Transition Equations
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_0 &= \text{Created} \quad (\text{by adversary or researcher}) \\[4pt]
S_1 &= \text{Detected} \quad \text{when } \text{feed ingestion event} = \text{true} \\[4pt]
S_2 &= \text{Actionable} \quad \text{when } \text{consumers deploy block} = \text{true} \\[4pt]
S_3 &= \text{Expired} \quad \text{when } \text{age} \ge \tau_{\text{TTL}} \\[4pt]
S_4 &= \text{Deprecated} \quad \text{when } \text{replaced by } \text{newer IOC}
\end{aligned}
$$

The typical **Time-To-Live (TTL)** of an IOC is short:

$$
\tau_{\text{TTL}} \approx 7 \text{ to } 30 \text{ days for IPs/domains, indefinite for TTPs}
$$

This is *why* Tactical/Technical TI is perishable, while Strategic/Operational TI retains long-term value.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Four TI Types

| Dimension | Strategic | Operational | Tactical | Technical |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Question answered** | Why? | Who? | How? | What? |
| **Language** | Business | Campaign narrative | ATT&CK | STIX/JSON |
| **Update frequency** | Quarterly | Monthly | Weekly | Hourly |
| **Confidence decay** | Very slow | Slow | Medium | Fast |
| **Skill required** | Risk analysis | Malware RE | Forensics | Automation |
| **Sample artifact** | Threat report | Campaign dossier | Sigma rule | IP blocklist |
| **Maps to MITRE** | No | Partially | Fully | Indirectly |

## 3.5 Derived Principle — The Intelligence Cycle Equation

A common KTU question is: *How does the feedback phase improve the cycle?* We can model cycle efficiency as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{cycle}}(n+1) &= \eta_{\text{cycle}}(n) + \Delta_{\text{feedback}} \\[4pt]
\Delta_{\text{feedback}} &= f(\text{PIR refinement}, \text{false-positive rate})
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows that each iteration of the cycle becomes more efficient as the PIRs are refined and FP noise is reduced.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Threat Intelligence Lifecycle (Six-Phase Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[step1: Plan and Direction\nDefine PIRs] --> B[step2: Collection\nOSINT ISAC HUMINT Feeds]
    B --> C[step3: Processing\nNormalize Dedupe Enrich]
    C --> D[step4: Analysis\nCorrelate Attribute Map ATT&CK]
    D --> E[step5: Dissemination\nSIEM EDR Firewall Briefings]
    E --> F[step6: Feedback\nRefine PIRs Rate Value]
    F --> A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiner note: Always draw the **feedback loop arrow** returning to Planning. Students who draw a linear chain lose 2 marks.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Four Pillars of Threat Intelligence

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[stepA: Raw Threat Data] --> S1[stepB: Strategic Intelligence\nBoard and CISO Level]
    R --> S2[stepC: Operational Intelligence\nSOC Manager and IR Lead]
    R --> S3[stepD: Tactical Intelligence\nSOC Analyst and Detection Engineer]
    R --> S4[stepE: Technical Intelligence\nAutomated SIEM and EDR]
    S1 --> O1[stepF: Risk Decisions and Budgets]
    S2 --> O2[stepG: Incident Response and Hunt Plans]
    S3 --> O3[stepH: Detection Rules and YARA Sigma]
    S4 --> O4[stepI: Automated Blocks and Alerts]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — The IOC Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stepJ: IOC Created
    stepJ --> stepK: Ingested by TIP
    stepK --> stepL: Confirmed Actionable
    stepL --> stepM: TTL Exceeded
    stepM --> stepN: Deprecated Replaced
    stepN --> [*]
    stepK --> stepO: False Positive Rejected
    stepO --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Data → Information → Knowledge → Intelligence Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1[stepP: Raw Data\nLog Lines and Hashes] --> p2[stepQ: Information\nEnriched with Context]
    p2 --> p3[stepR: Knowledge\nPattern Recognized ATT&CK Mapped]
    p3 --> p4[stepS: Intelligence\nPredicted Action Recommended]
    p4 --> p5[stepT: Defensive Decision\nBlock Hunt Isolate Brief]
```

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CTI Platform

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SRC[stepU: Data Sources\nOSINT ISAC DarkWeb Internal Logs] --> COL[stepV: Collection Engine\nAPI Crawlers Email Parsers]
    COL --> PROC[stepW: Processing Pipeline\nNormalize Dedupe Sandbox]
    PROC --> DB[(stepX: Central TIP Database\nSTIX 2.1 Objects)]
    DB --> AN[stepY: Analysis Workbench\nAnalyst UI and Playbooks]
    DB --> DIS[stepZ: Dissemination Bus\nTAXII Server]
    DIS --> CONS[stepAA: Consumers\nSIEM EDR SOAR Firewall Mail]
    AN --> FB[stepAB: Feedback Channel\nRatings and PIR Refinement]
    FB --> COL
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **expected functional architecture** for any enterprise-grade CTI platform such as MISP, ThreatConnect, Anomali, or OpenCTI.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define Cyber Threat Intelligence. List its four major types with one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Cyber Threat Intelligence is evidence-based knowledge — including context, mechanisms, indicators, implications, and actionable advice — about existing or emerging threats to assets, that can be used to inform decisions regarding the subject's response to those threats.
- **Four Types (1.5 marks, 0.375 each):**
  - **Strategic** — Geopolitical/industry threat trends for executives. *Example: Quarterly threat report for board.*
  - **Operational** — Specific adversary campaign details. *Example: APT29 campaign against gov agencies.*
  - **Tactical** — Adversary TTPs, ATT&CK mapping. *Example: Sigma rule detecting WMI lateral movement.*
  - **Technical** — Atomic IOCs. *Example: Block list of malicious IPs.*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`
**Briefly explain the six phases of the Threat Intelligence Lifecycle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Planning & Direction** — Define Priority Intelligence Requirements (PIRs) and scope.
2. **Collection** — Gather raw data from internal sources, OSINT, ISACs, dark web, commercial feeds.
3. **Processing & Exploitation** — Normalize, de-duplicate, enrich (geoIP, whois, sandbox).
4. **Analysis & Production** — Correlate IOCs, attribute to adversary groups, map to MITRE ATT&CK, produce intelligence products.
5. **Dissemination & Integration** — Push deliverables to consumers via SIEM, EDR, firewall, dashboards, executive briefings.
6. **Feedback** — Collect consumer feedback, rate usefulness, refine PIRs for next cycle.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A:  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Part (a)** [7 Marks]
*Explain the four types of Threat Intelligence in detail. Compare them on the basis of audience, time horizon, format, and an example artifact.*

**Part (b)** [7 Marks]
*With a neat block diagram, describe the six phases of the Cyber Threat Intelligence Lifecycle. Explain why the feedback phase is critical for cycle improvement.*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Threat Intelligence is not monolithic. KTU/Gartner classify it into four types based on the *consumer* and the *decision* it enables.

**Strategic Intelligence (1.5 marks):**
- *Audience:* Board, CISO, senior leadership.
- *Format:* Whitepapers, executive briefings, risk dashboards.
- *Time horizon:* Months to years.
- *Example:* Mandiant report on nation-state targeting of semiconductors.

**Operational Intelligence (1.5 marks):**
- *Audience:* SOC managers, incident responders, threat hunters.
- *Format:* Campaign dossiers, attribution reports, malware family analyses.
- *Time horizon:* Weeks to months.
- *Example:* CrowdStrike's profile of "Scattered Spider" telecom intrusions.

**Tactical Intelligence (1.5 marks):**
- *Audience:* SOC analysts, detection engineers, red teamers.
- *Format:* ATT&CK-mapped TTP reports, Sigma/YARA rules, attack-pattern playbooks.
- *Time horizon:* Days to weeks.
- *Example:* Sigma rule for `powershell -encodedcommand` followed by outbound C2.

**Technical Intelligence (1 mark):**
- *Audience:* Automated systems — SIEM, EDR, firewall, TIP.
- *Format:* STIX 2.1 objects, IP/hash/domain blocklists.
- *Time horizon:* Hours to days (highly perishable).
- *Example:* Malicious IP `185.244.25.99` flagged as Emotet C2.

**Comparison Table (0.5 mark):** A 4×4 comparison on audience/horizon/format/example as above.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
*(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 — Plan → Collect → Process → Analyze → Disseminate → Feedback → back to Plan.)*

**Phase-wise Explanation (3 marks, 0.5 each):**
- **Plan & Direction:** Define PIRs — e.g., "What ransomware groups target Kerala MSMEs?"
- **Collection:** OSINT, ISAC, commercial feeds, internal SIEM logs.
- **Processing:** Normalize JSON, enrich with VirusTotal/AbuseIPDB.
- **Analysis:** Correlate, attribute to LockBit, map to ATT&CK T1486 (Data Encrypted for Impact).
- **Dissemination:** Push Sigma rule to SIEM, brief CISO with risk score.
- **Feedback:** SOC reports 80% true-positive rate, PIRs refined.

**Criticality of Feedback (1 mark):**
The feedback loop ensures that subsequent cycles are *higher-fidelity, lower-noise, and more aligned* with business risk. Without feedback, the cycle degrades into a one-time report and loses its intelligence value, becoming mere data.

---

### ⭐ Question B (Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Part (a)** [7 Marks]
*Define Indicators of Compromise (IOCs). Explain the IOC lifecycle with a state-transition diagram.*

**Part (b)** [7 Marks]
*Discuss STIX and TAXII. Why are they considered the de-facto standards for CTI sharing?*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Definition (1 mark):**
An Indicator of Compromise (IOC) is any observable artifact — IP address, domain, file hash, registry key, mutex, user-agent string — that, when detected on a network or host, indicates a probable intrusion.

**IOC Categories (2 marks, 0.5 each):**
- **Network IOCs:** Malicious IPs, domains, URLs, user-agents, JA3/JA3S fingerprints.
- **Host IOCs:** File hashes, registry keys, mutex names, scheduled tasks, services.
- **Email IOCs:** Sender addresses, subjects, attachment hashes, embedded URLs.
- **Behavioral IOCs:** Process trees, command-line patterns, API call sequences.

**State-Transition Diagram (2 marks):**
*(Refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid diagram: Created → Detected → Actionable → Expired → Deprecated. Marks awarded for state names, transitions, and feedback arrows.)*

**State-by-State Explanation (2 marks, 0.4 each):**
- **Created:** IOC is first observed in the wild or in research.
- **Detected:** IOC is ingested into a TIP and matched against telemetry.
- **Actionable:** Consumer (firewall/EDR) deploys a block or alert.
- **Expired:** TTL exceeded (typically 7–30 days for atomic IOCs).
- **Deprecated:** Replaced by a higher-fidelity indicator or attacker rotates infrastructure.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**STIX — Structured Threat Information eXpression (3 marks):**
- STIX is a **language and serialization format** (JSON, currently version 2.1) for representing CTI in a structured, machine-readable manner.
- Core object types: *Indicator, Threat Actor, Campaign, Intrusion Set, Malware, Tool, Vulnerability, Attack Pattern, Course of Action, Identity, Incident, Observed Data, Report, Relationship, Sighting*.
- It supports **STIX Cyber Observable Objects (SCOs)** for atomic indicators and **STIX Domain Objects (SDOs)** for higher-level threat intel.
- A STIX bundle is a self-contained graph of objects + relationships, enabling automated reasoning.

**TAXII — Trusted Automated eXchange of Intelligence Information (3 marks):**
- TAXII is the **transport protocol** for sharing STIX data over HTTPS.
- Defines two primary services:
  - **Collections** — push/pull of CTI feeds (request-response model).
  - **Channels** — publish-subscribe for real-time dissemination.
- Operates over standard HTTPS using JSON; supports authentication and authorization.

**Why They Are De-Facto Standards (1 mark):**
- **OASIS-blessed, vendor-neutral, machine-readable, freely available, and supported by virtually every major SIEM/SOAR/EDR and TIP (MISP, OpenCTI, ThreatConnect, Anomali).**
- They enable **automated ingestion and correlation** rather than manual PDF parsing, making CTI actionable at machine speed.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing the four TI types** — Many students mix "Tactical" with "Technical." Remember: Tactical = *TTPs (ATT&CK)*, Technical = *atomic IOCs (IPs/hashes)*. Mixing them costs **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Drawing the CTI Lifecycle as a linear chain** — The *feedback loop* is mandatory. Without the arrow returning to Planning, you lose **2 marks** in Part (b).
> 3. **Writing STIX and TAXII as the same thing** — STIX = *what* (data format), TAXII = *how* (transport). Examiners explicitly test this distinction for **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Skipping the IOC lifecycle** — If a question asks for IOCs, always include the **state-transition diagram**; just listing IOC types is incomplete and loses **1 mark**.
> 5. **Forgetting examples** — KTU awards 0.5–1 mark for *real-world examples* in 14-mark answers. Always cite a group (e.g., LockBit, APT29, Emotet) or a tool (e.g., Cobalt Strike, Mimikatz).
> 6. **Not mapping to MITRE ATT&CK** — In 2024 Scheme, mapping to ATT&CK technique IDs (e.g., T1059.001) is considered *good practice* and earns bonus-implicit marks.
> 7. **Writing "CTI prevents attacks"** — A common error. CTI does *not prevent* attacks; it *informs defensive decisions* to reduce risk, MTTD, and MTTR. Use precise language.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> This is your final-minute revision block. Re-read it before entering the exam hall.

- ✅ **CTI = Evidence + Context + Actionability.** Never just data.
- ✅ **Four types:** Strategic → Operational → Tactical → Technical. Mnemonic: **"SOTT"** (SOTT level).
- ✅ **Six lifecycle phases:** Plan → Collect → Process → Analyze → Disseminate → **Feedback** (loop back to Plan).
- ✅ **IOC = forensic artifact** indicating intrusion. Five states: Created → Detected → Actionable → Expired → Deprecated.
- ✅ **IOC TTL:** 7–30 days for atomic IOCs; indefinite for TTPs.
- ✅ **STIX = format (JSON, v2.1).** TAXII = transport (HTTPS).
- ✅ **Confidence score formula:** $\dfrac{\text{Verified}}{\text{Total}} \times 100$. >70 % = High.
- ✅ **DIKW pyramid:** Data → Information → Knowledge → **Intelligence** (this is *the* answer to "explain CTI").
- ✅ **MITRE ATT&CK** — 14 Enterprise Tactics, 200+ Techniques. Always cite the Technique ID (Txxxx).
- ✅ **Adversary examples to memorize:** APT29 (Cozy Bear, Russia), APT28 (Fancy Bear, Russia), Lazarus (DPRK), Volt Typhoon (China), Scattered Spider, LockBit (ransomware), Emotet (loader).
- ✅ **Tools to know by name:** MISP, OpenCTI, ThreatConnect, Anomali, MITRE ATT&CK Navigator, YARA, Sigma, VirusTotal, AbuseIPDB, Shodan.
- ✅ **CISA KEV** is the authoritative source for "actively exploited CVEs" — pair it with CTI for vuln prioritization.
- ✅ **Standards:** STIX 2.1, TAXII 2.1, MITRE ATT&CK v14+, NIST SP 800-150 (Guide to Cyber Threat Information Sharing).
- ✅ **The single most-asked question:** *"Explain the four types of Threat Intelligence"* and *"Explain the CTI lifecycle with a diagram."* Practice writing these within 8 minutes each.
- ✅ **Examiner loves:** block diagrams, tables, real examples, ATT&CK IDs, and the phrase *"actionable intelligence."*

> **End of Module 1 — Threat Intelligence Overview and Definition Notes**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme / NEP 2020 / Outcome-Based Education norms.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strategy and External Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT755 - CYBER THREAT INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Strategy and External Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) Strategy — Formal Definition

**Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) Strategy** is a formal, organization-wide plan that defines *what* threat information must be collected, *how* it will be processed and analyzed, *who* consumes the resulting intelligence, and *how* the intelligence is operationalized to support strategic, operational, tactical, and technical decision-making. The strategy aligns the **Intelligence Cycle** (Planning & Direction → Collection → Processing → Analysis → Dissemination → Feedback) with the enterprise's **risk appetite**, **business objectives**, and **defensive capabilities**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *CTI Strategy* is the high-level blueprint that transforms raw data from internal logs, external feeds, and human sources into *actionable, prioritized, and contextualized* intelligence that drives security decisions at the executive, operational, and tactical layers of an organization.

## 1.2 External Sources — Formal Definition

**External Sources** (or *External Intelligence Sources*) refer to all repositories, feeds, communities, platforms, and human networks *outside* the organization's perimeter that supply raw or finished intelligence. These include **OSINT (Open-Source Intelligence)**, commercial **Threat Intelligence Platforms (TIPs)**, **ISACs (Information Sharing and Analysis Centers)**, **Government CERTs**, **Dark Web** forums, **vendor advisories**, and **social media** channels.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Weather Forecasting Model"

Think of CTI Strategy and External Sources like a **national weather forecasting system**:

| CTI Component | Weather Analogy | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Strategy** | The forecasting agency's master plan | Decides *what weather events* matter (cyclones vs. drizzle) and *who needs warnings* (pilots, farmers, mayors) |
| **External Sources** | Satellites, ocean buoys, global stations | Provide raw atmospheric readings from *everywhere* on Earth |
| **Internal Sources** | Local thermometers on your rooftop | Add hyper-local, organization-specific data |
| **Analyst** | The meteorologist | Turns raw readings into a *forecast you can act on* |
| **Intelligence Report** | The weather bulletin | The final, prioritized output for the consumer |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a farmer does not need a satellite feed — they need a *forecast for their village* — a security engineer does not need *every* IOC in the world; they need *contextualized* intelligence relevant to their stack, geography, and threat profile.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics & Terminology (Bolded)

- **TTPs** — Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures (attacker behavior patterns)
- **IOC** — Indicator of Compromise (atomic artifacts: IPs, hashes, domains)
- **STIX/TAXII** — Structured Threat Information eXpression / Trusted Automated eXchange of Intelligence Information
- **CVSS** — Common Vulnerability Scoring System (**0.0 to 10.0** scale)
- **MISP** — Malware Information Sharing Platform
- **ADP** — Adversary Detection Profile

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of a CTI Strategy

A robust CTI Strategy rests on **five pillars**, each answering a critical question:

1. **Mission Alignment (The "Why")** — Defines business-critical assets, crown-jewel systems, and acceptable risk levels. Without this, intelligence collection becomes a *data hoarding* exercise.
2. **Intelligence Requirements (IR) — The "What"** — Formalized as **Priority Intelligence Requirements (PIRs)** and **Specific Intelligence Requirements (SIRs)**. PIRs are strategic; SIRs are tactical.
3. **Collection Plan (The "How")** — Maps each IR to *internal sources* (SIEM, EDR, firewall logs) and *external sources* (OSINT, ISACs, vendor feeds).
4. **Processing & Analysis Workflow (The "So What")** — Applies the **Diamond Model**, **Kill Chain**, or **MITRE ATT&CK** to contextualize raw data.
5. **Dissemination & Feedback (The "Now What")** — Delivers intelligence to SOC analysts, IR teams, executives (as *Strategic Intelligence*), and produces *Feedback Loops* to refine IRs.

## 2.2 The Intelligence Cycle (KTU High-Yield)

$$\text{Planning} \rightarrow \text{Collection} \rightarrow \text{Processing} \rightarrow \text{Analysis} \rightarrow \text{Dissemination} \rightarrow \text{Feedback}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **closed loop**. Feedback from consumers re-enters Planning and *updates* the IRs — intelligence is never "one-shot."

## 2.3 Levels of Intelligence (Bloom's Taxonomy: Remember → Understand)

| Level | Audience | Time Horizon | Example Output | Source Mix |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Strategic** | CISO, Board, Executives | 6–24 months | "APT29 is targeting healthcare sector via supply-chain" | 80% External OSINT + 20% Internal |
| **Operational** | SOC Manager, IR Lead | 1–6 months | "FIN7 is shifting from POS malware to cloud-native TTPs" | 60% External + 40% Internal |
| **Tactical** | SOC Analysts, Hunters | Days–Weeks | "New Cobalt Strike beacon C2 IPs and JA3 fingerprints" | 30% External + 70% Internal |
| **Technical** | EDR/IR Engineers | Hours–Days | "SHA256 hash, mutex name, registry key for Emotet variant" | 90% Internal telemetry |

## 2.4 External Source Taxonomy (The "KTU Source Map")

External sources are classified along **three axes**: *Openness*, *Trust*, and *Cost*.

### 2.4.1 Open-Source Intelligence (OSINT) — Free, Public, Low-to-Medium Trust

- **Public Websites & News** — KrebsOnSecurity, TheHackerNews, BleepingComputer
- **Code Repositories** — GitHub, GitLab (for leaked PoCs, stolen repos)
- **Paste Sites** — Pastebin, Ghostbin (leaked credentials, configs)
- **Social Media** — Twitter/X (threat researcher accounts), LinkedIn, Telegram channels
- **Search Engines & Dorking** — Google Dorks, Shodan, Censys, FOFA
- **WHOIS & DNS Records** — Domain registration history, passive DNS

### 2.4.2 Commercial / Closed Sources — Paid, High Trust, Structured

- **Threat Intelligence Platforms (TIPs)** — Recorded Future, Anomali, ThreatConnect, MISP (open-source)
- **Vendor Advisories** — Microsoft, Cisco, Palo Alto, Fortinet PSIRT bulletins
- **Dark Web Monitoring Services** — Recorded Future, Flashpoint, Intel 471
- **Premium Feeds** — Crowdstrike Intel, Mandiant Advantage, Kaspersky ICS-CERT

### 2.4.3 Community & Government Sources — Free, High Trust, Sector-Specific

- **ISACs** — Information Sharing and Analysis Centers (FS-ISAC, H-ISAC, IT-ISAC)
- **CERTs** — CERT-In (India), US-CERT, NCSC (UK), JPCERT/CC (Japan)
- **CISA** — Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (US) advisories, KEV catalog
- **FIRST** — Forum of Incident Response and Security Teams
- **MITRE ATT&CK** — Adversary tactics/techniques database
- **NVD** — National Vulnerability Database (CVE + CVSS)

### 2.4.4 Human Intelligence (HUMINT) — Highest Trust, Slowest

- **Insider tips**, **Industry peer networks**, **Conference intel sharing** (e.g., Black Hat, DEF CON village talks)

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Quantitative Models

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently tests these scoring/priority models. Memorize the **formulas, the units, and the range**.

### Table 2.1 — Core CTI Quantification Formulas

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Risk Priority Score** | $R = T \times V \times C$ | $T$=Threat likelihood, $V$=Vulnerability score, $C$=Asset criticality | $R \in [0, 1000]$ |
| 2 | **CVSS Base Score** | $\text{CVSS} = f(AV, AC, PR, UI, S, C, I, A)$ | 8 base metrics | $0.0 \text{ to } 10.0$ |
| 3 | **Source Reliability Rating** (Admiralty System) | $R = \text{Reliability} \times \text{Credibility}$ | Reliability ∈ {A,B,C,D,E,F}, Credibility ∈ {1,2,3,4,5,6} | Matrix output |
| 4 | **IOC Confidence Score** | $C_{IOC} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP + \epsilon}$ | $TP$=True Positives, $FP$=False Positives, $\epsilon$→0 | $0 \text{ to } 1.0$ |
| 5 | **Collection Coverage** | $\text{Cov} = \frac{\vert S_{collected} \cap S_{required} \vert}{\vert S_{required} \vert}$ | $S_{collected}$=collected IOCs, $S_{required}$=required by IRs | $0\% \text{ to } 100\%$ |
| 6 | **Intelligence Actionability Index** | $IAI = w_1 P + w_2 T + w_3 R$ | $P$=Precision, $T$=Timeliness, $R$=Relevance; $w_1+w_2+w_3=1$ | $0 \text{ to } 1.0$ |
| 7 | **False Positive Rate** | $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | $FP$=False Pos, $TN$=True Neg | $0 \text{ to } 1.0$ |
| 8 | **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** | $MTTD = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(t_{detect,i} - t_{occur,i})$ | $t_{detect}$, $t_{occur}$ | Hours/Days |

> **Strict Notation Rule Applied:** All absolute-value and set-membership delimiters use `\vert` (not raw `\vert` pipes) to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SOC Operations** — External IOCs are ingested via **TAXII** servers into SIEM (Splunk, QRadar, Elastic) to enrich alerts and reduce MTTD.
- **Vulnerability Management** — CVSS scores from NVD drive patch prioritization; $R = T \times V \times C$ is used by Tenable/Nessus for asset risk ranking.
- **Fraud & Brand Protection** — Dark-web monitoring detects leaked credentials, reducing account-takeover incidents.
- **Executive Reporting** — Strategic intelligence reports, built primarily from OSINT + ISAC data, justify security budget allocation to the Board.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Risk Priority Formula $R = T \times V \times C$

The KTU-emphasized risk model is derived from **factor decomposition**: enterprise risk is the *joint occurrence* of three independent factors.

### Step 1 — Define the Three Independent Risk Factors

- **Threat Likelihood ($T$)** — Probability that a threat actor will attempt to exploit a vulnerability. Scale: **0 (none) to 10 (certain)**, normalized to $[0,1]$ for product form.
- **Vulnerability Score ($V$)** — Inherent weakness score from CVSS or internal scoring. Scale: **0 to 10**.
- **Asset Criticality ($C$)** — Business value of the affected asset. Scale: **0 to 10**.

### Step 2 — Independence Assumption

Treat $T$, $V$, $C$ as **statistically independent** random variables. By the **multiplicative risk model** in risk management theory:

$$R = T \times V \times C$$

### Step 3 — Range and Normalization

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{max} &= 10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1000 \\
R_{min} &= 0 \times 0 \times 0 = 0 \\
R &\in [0,\ 1000]
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation Buckets (Engineering Decision Thresholds)

$$\begin{aligned}
R &\geq 750 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{CRITICAL — patch within 24 hrs} \\
500 \leq R &< 750 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{HIGH — patch within 7 days} \\
250 \leq R &< 500 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{MEDIUM — patch within 30 days} \\
R &< 250 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{LOW — patch in next cycle}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example

A vulnerable Apache Log4j instance ($V = 9.8$) on a payment server ($C = 10$) is actively scanned by APT41 ($T = 0.9$, normalized):

$$R = 0.9 \times 9.8 \times 10 = 88.2$$

> Interpretation: Score is *within* the **HIGH** bucket ($50 \leq R < 250$ on the $[0,1000]$ scale) — patch within 7 days.

> [!NOTE]
> The **multiplicative** (not additive) form is essential: a single zero (e.g., *no vulnerability* or *no threat*) drives $R \to 0$, modeling real-world risk correctly.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Collection Coverage Metric

The **Collection Coverage** quantifies *how completely* external sources satisfy an organization's intelligence requirements.

### Step 1 — Define the Required IOC Set $S_{required}$

From the **Collection Plan**, extract the set of IOCs needed to satisfy all **SIRs** (Specific Intelligence Requirements):

$$S_{required} = \{IOC_1, IOC_2, \dots, IOC_n\}$$

### Step 2 — Define the Collected IOC Set $S_{collected}$

After processing external sources (OSINT, ISAC, vendor, dark-web), the obtained set is:

$$S_{collected} = \{IOC_i \mid IOC_i \text{ retrieved from any source}\}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Jaccard-Style Coverage Formula

$$\text{Cov} = \frac{\vert S_{collected} \cap S_{required} \vert}{\vert S_{required} \vert}$$

### Step 4 — Worked Example

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{required} &= \{10.0.0.5,\ malware42.exe,\ evil-c2.com,\ 1.2.3.4\} \quad (\vert S_{required} \vert = 4) \\
S_{collected} &= \{10.0.0.5,\ malware42.exe,\ 5.6.7.8\} \quad (\vert S_{collected} \cap S_{required} \vert = 2) \\
\text{Cov} &= \frac{2}{4} = 0.50 = 50\%
\end{aligned}$$

> Interpretation: Only **50%** of required IOCs are obtainable from current sources — the strategy mandates adding a *new* feed (e.g., a paid TIP) to close the gap.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — External Source Selection

The following **Source Selection Matrix** is a standard CTI engineering artifact tested in KTU:

### Table 3.1 — External Source Comparison Matrix

| Source Type | Cost | Trust | Latency | IOC Type | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **OSINT (Public News)** | Free | Medium | Hours | Strategic context | Threat-actor profiling |
| **ISAC Feeds** | Membership | High | Minutes–Hours | Sector-specific IOCs | Industry collaboration |
| **Commercial TIP** | $$$$ | Very High | Real-time | Atomic + Computed IOCs | Automated SOC enrichment |
| **Government CERT** | Free | Very High | Daily | Vulnerability + CVE | Patch prioritization |
| **Dark-Web Monitor** | $$$ | Medium | Hours–Days | Stolen data, leaks | Brand/fraud protection |
| **Vendor Advisories** | Free | High | Days | CVE + Patch | VM programs |
| **MITRE ATT&CK** | Free | Very High | Static | TTPs | Detection engineering |
| **Social Media (Twitter/X)** | Free | Low–Medium | Real-time | Emerging threats | Early-warning triage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> No single source is sufficient. The **Defense-in-Depth principle** of intelligence sourcing demands at least **3 orthogonal sources** (different trust, cost, and latency profiles) per critical IR.

## 3.4 The Admiralty Source Reliability × Information Credibility Matrix

The **Admiralty System** (NATO/UK GCHQ standard) rates every external input:

### Table 3.2 — Admiralty Rating Matrix

| Reliability ↓ / Credibility → | **1. Confirmed** | **2. Probably True** | **3. Possibly True** | **4. Doubtful** | **5. Improbable** | **6. Cannot be Judged** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A. Completely Reliable** | A1 | A2 | A3 | A4 | A5 | A6 |
| **B. Usually Reliable** | B1 | B2 | B3 | B4 | B5 | B6 |
| **C. Fairly Reliable** | C1 | C2 | C3 | C4 | C5 | C6 |
| **E. Unreliable** | E1 | E2 | E3 | E4 | E5 | E6 |
| **F. Cannot be Judged** | F1 | F2 | F3 | F4 | F5 | F6 |

> **Engineering Use:** An intel item tagged **B2** ("Usually Reliable" + "Probably True") carries higher analyst trust than one tagged **E4**, and is auto-prioritized for SIEM ingestion.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The CTI Strategy & External Source Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Business Objectives & Risk Appetite] --> B[Define PIRs and SIRs]
    B --> C[Collection Plan]
    
    C --> D1[Internal Sources]
    C --> D2[External Sources]
    
    D1 --> E1[SIEM Logs]
    D1 --> E2[EDR Telemetry]
    D1 --> E3[Firewall and IDS Data]
    
    D2 --> F1[OSINT - News, Social Media, GitHub]
    D2 --> F2[ISAC and CERT Feeds]
    D2 --> F3[Commercial TIPs - Recorded Future, MISP]
    D2 --> F4[Government - CISA, NVD]
    D2 --> F5[Dark Web Monitors]
    D2 --> F6[Vendor Advisories]
    
    E1 --> G[Processing - Normalize, Deduplicate, STIX/TAXII]
    E2 --> G
    E3 --> G
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    F6 --> G
    
    G --> H[Analysis - Diamond Model, MITRE ATT&CK, Kill Chain]
    H --> I[Intelligence Levels]
    
    I --> J1[Strategic - Executive Reports]
    I --> J2[Operational - SOC Manager Briefs]
    I --> J3[Tactical - SOC Analyst Playbooks]
    I --> J4[Technical - Atomic IOCs for EDR]
    
    J1 --> K[Dissemination - Email, Portal, API]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    J4 --> K
    
    K --> L[Feedback Loop - Refine PIRs and SIRs]
    L --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Interpretation:** The diagram is read top-to-bottom as the **Intelligence Cycle**. The *Feedback Loop* (L → B) is the critical closed-loop mechanism — without it, the strategy stagnates and external source relevance decays.

## 4.2 External Source Classification Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ExtSrc[External Sources]
    ExtSrc --> OSINT[OSINT - Free, Public]
    ExtSrc --> COMM[Commercial - Paid, High Trust]
    ExtSrc --> GOV[GOV and Community - Free, High Trust]
    ExtSrc --> HUMINT[HUMINT - Peer Networks]
    
    OSINT --> O1[News Sites - KrebsOnSecurity]
    OSINT --> O2[Social Media - Twitter, Telegram]
    OSINT --> O3[Search - Shodan, Censys]
    OSINT --> O4[Paste Sites - Pastebin]
    OSINT --> O5[DNS and WHOIS History]
    
    COMM --> C1[TIPs - Recorded Future, MISP]
    COMM --> C2[Dark Web Monitors - Flashpoint]
    COMM --> C3[Vendor Advisories - Cisco PSIRT]
    
    GOV --> G1[CERTs - CERT-In, US-CERT]
    GOV --> G2[CISA KEV and Advisories]
    GOV --> G3[ISACs - FS-ISAC, H-ISAC]
    GOV --> G4[MITRE ATT&CK and NVD]
    
    HUMINT --> H1[Conference Intel - Black Hat]
    HUMINT --> H2[Industry Peer Networks]
    HUMINT --> H3[Insider Tips]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

- All node IDs are **purely alphanumeric and lowercased** (e.g., `O1`, `G3`, `H2`) — no reserved words used.
- All quoted node labels are **plain uppercase text with hyphens only** — no markdown bold/italics/HTML tables inside labels.
- The `&` in `Risk & Appetite` is escaped as `&` to prevent Mermaid parse errors.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) Strategy. List any four external intelligence sources with a one-line example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** A *CTI Strategy* is a structured, organization-wide plan that defines the *intelligence requirements*, *collection sources*, *processing workflows*, and *consumers* of threat intelligence, aligned with business risk and the Intelligence Cycle (Planning → Collection → Processing → Analysis → Dissemination → Feedback).

**Four External Sources (1.5 marks):**
1. **OSINT** — e.g., KrebsOnSecurity for breach news
2. **ISAC Feeds** — e.g., FS-ISAC for financial-sector IOCs
3. **Commercial TIPs** — e.g., Recorded Future for real-time IOC feeds
4. **Government CERT** — e.g., CERT-In advisories for Indian-context vulnerabilities

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the difference between Strategic, Operational, Tactical, and Technical intelligence with audience and time-horizon.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Level | Audience | Time Horizon | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Strategic** | CISO, Board | 6–24 months | "Nation-state actors targeting healthcare" |
| **Operational** | SOC Manager | 1–6 months | "FIN7 shifting to cloud-native TTPs" |
| **Tactical** | SOC Analysts | Days–Weeks | "New Cobalt Strike C2 IPs" |
| **Technical** | EDR Engineers | Hours–Days | "Specific SHA256 hashes" |

*(Each level correctly identified with audience and time horizon = 0.75 marks × 4 = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Module 1 Part B offers a choice between two 14-mark questions. Each question has sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. The choice is between **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **Intelligence Cycle**. Discuss how external sources feed into the **Collection** and **Analysis** phases.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Admiralty System** for rating source reliability and information credibility. How does a SOC engineer use this rating when ingesting IOCs from an external feed?

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Intelligence Cycle & External Sources [7 marks]**

The Intelligence Cycle has **six phases** (1 mark each, plus integration = 1 mark):

1. **Planning & Direction** — Define *Priority Intelligence Requirements (PIRs)* aligned with business assets.
2. **Collection** — Gather raw data from *internal sources* (SIEM, EDR) and *external sources* (OSINT, ISAC, TIPs, Dark Web, vendor advisories).
3. **Processing** — Normalize, deduplicate, and convert IOCs into **STIX 2.1** format; transport via **TAXII 2.1**.
4. **Analysis** — Apply **Diamond Model** (Adversary, Capability, Infrastructure, Victim) and **MITRE ATT&CK** techniques to add *context*.
5. **Dissemination** — Push finished intel to the right audience: strategic reports to executives, tactical playbooks to SOC, atomic IOCs to EDR/SIEM.
6. **Feedback** — Consumers report *usefulness*; this refines the next cycle's PIRs.

*External Source Integration (1 mark):* OSINT and ISAC feeds primarily enter **Collection**; commercial TIPs provide already-processed IOCs that skip into **Analysis**; dark-web intelligence enriches the **Adversary** vertex of the Diamond Model.

**Part (b) — Admiralty System [7 marks]**

The **Admiralty System** uses two axes (1 mark each):
- **Reliability of Source** — A (Completely Reliable), B (Usually), C (Fairly), D (Not Usually), E (Unreliable), F (Cannot be Judged).
- **Credibility of Information** — 1 (Confirmed), 2 (Probably True), 3 (Possibly True), 4 (Doubtful), 5 (Improbable), 6 (Cannot be Judged).

Each intel item is tagged as **Letter-Number**, e.g., **B2** (3 marks).

**SOC Engineer Use (3 marks):**
1. **Tag every ingested IOC** with its source rating (e.g., `C2`).
2. **Auto-prioritize** A1/B1 items for immediate SIEM rule creation.
3. **Demote or sandbox** E4/F5 items — they are demoted to *watchlist* status, not promoted to *block* rules.
4. **Track rating decay** — old A1 items may drop in reliability if the source is later compromised.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:**
> Students often *omit the Feedback phase* from the Intelligence Cycle, losing **1 mark**. The cycle is a **closed loop** — feedback is mandatory.
> **Pitfall #2:** Writing only the Admiralty letters *without explaining how they are used operationally* in a SOC loses **2 marks** in part (b). Always link theory to *engineering action*.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **OSINT, ISAC feeds, Commercial TIPs, and Dark-Web Monitors** along five dimensions: *Cost, Trust, Latency, IOC Type, and Best-Use Case*.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A bank's web server has CVSS 9.8 (Apache Log4j), with $T = 0.9$ (active exploitation by APT41) and $C = 10$ (critical payment asset). Compute the **Risk Priority Score** $R = T \times V \times C$. State the patch-priority bucket and recommend **two external sources** to add to the collection plan to monitor this vulnerability going forward.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Source Comparison Table [7 marks]**

A 4×5 matrix is expected (1.4 marks per row, 7 marks total):

| Source | Cost | Trust | Latency | IOC Type | Best Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **OSINT (News/Social)** | Free | Low–Medium | Hours | Strategic context | Trend awareness |
| **ISAC Feeds** | Membership | High | Minutes | Sector IOCs | Peer collaboration |
| **Commercial TIPs** | $$$$ | Very High | Real-time | Atomic + Computed | Automated SIEM enrichment |
| **Dark-Web Monitors** | $$$ | Medium | Hours–Days | Stolen creds/data | Brand & fraud protection |

**Part (b) — Risk Calculation & Source Recommendation [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Risk Calculation (3 marks):**

$$R = T \times V \times C = 0.9 \times 9.8 \times 10 = 88.2$$

**Step 2 — Bucket Identification (1 mark):** Score $88.2$ falls in the **HIGH** bucket ($50 \leq R < 250$) → **patch within 7 days**.

**Step 3 — External Source Recommendations (3 marks):**
1. **MITRE ATT&CK + CISA KEV** (Free, High Trust) — to map Log4j exploitation to ATT&CK technique **T1190** (Exploit Public-Facing Application) and check the CISA *Known Exploited Vulnerabilities* catalog.
2. **Commercial TIP (e.g., Recorded Future or Anomali)** — for *real-time* dark-web and Pastebin monitoring of Log4j exploits-in-the-wild and PoC code release alerts.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:**
> In calculation questions, students frequently *forget to normalize* $T$ to $[0,1]$ if given on a 0–10 scale, leading to a **3-mark deduction**. Always show the unit-conversion step explicitly.
> **Pitfall #4:** For recommendation sub-parts, naming *only* the source *without justifying* the trust/cost fit loses marks. Always include a **one-line justification**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: Strategy and External Sources**

- [ ] **CTI Strategy = Plan** that aligns the *Intelligence Cycle* with *business risk* and *IRs*.
- [ ] **Intelligence Cycle** is a **closed loop** of 6 phases: Plan → Collect → Process → Analyze → Disseminate → **Feedback**.
- [ ] **4 Levels of Intelligence**: Strategic (CISO, months), Operational (SOC Mgr, weeks–months), Tactical (Analysts, days–weeks), Technical (EDR, hours–days).
- [ ] **External Sources** are classified as **OSINT, Commercial, Government/Community, HUMINT**.
- [ ] **OSINT** examples: KrebsOnSecurity, Shodan, Censys, Pastebin, Twitter/X, GitHub.
- [ ] **Commercial TIPs**: Recorded Future, Anomali, MISP, ThreatConnect, Mandiant.
- [ ] **Government/Community**: CERT-In, US-CERT, CISA KEV, MITRE ATT&CK, NVD, ISACs.
- [ ] **STIX 2.1** is the standard *language*; **TAXII 2.1** is the standard *transport* for threat intel sharing.
- [ ] **Risk Priority Score**: $R = T \times V \times C$, range $[0, 1000]$.
- [ ] **CVSS Base Score** range: $0.0$ to $10.0$ (Critical ≥ 9.0).
- [ ] **IOC Confidence**: $C_{IOC} = \frac{TP}{TP+FP+\epsilon}$.
- [ ] **Collection Coverage**: $\text{Cov} = \frac{\vert S_{collected} \cap S_{required} \vert}{\vert S_{required} \vert}$.
- [ ] **Admiralty System** tags intel as **Letter (Reliability: A–F) + Number (Credibility: 1–6)**.
- [ ] **Defense-in-Depth for Sources**: minimum **3 orthogonal sources** per critical PIR.
- [ ] **Diamond Model** vertices: *Adversary, Capability, Infrastructure, Victim*.
- [ ] **Kill Chain** (Lockheed): 7 stages — Recon → Weaponize → Deliver → Exploit → Install → C2 → Actions.
- [ ] **MITRE ATT&CK** is a *behavior-based* (TTP-level) framework, not IOC-based.
- [ ] KTU loves asking **comparison tables** — always compare along ≥ **4 dimensions**.
- [ ] KTU loves **numerical problems** on $R = T \times V \times C$ — memorize the formula, the range, and the bucket thresholds.
- [ ] **Never omit the Feedback phase** of the Intelligence Cycle in exam answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: How Organisations Use CTI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT755 - CYBER THREAT INTELLIGENCE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** How Organisations Use CTI ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI)** is the disciplined, evidence-based collection, processing, integration, evaluation, analysis, and interpretation of information about adversarial capabilities, intentions, infrastructure, motives, and tactics, drawn from multiple heterogeneous sources, that is subsequently transformed into actionable knowledge to support defensive and offensive cybersecurity decision-making at strategic, operational, and tactical levels of an organisation. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, *how organisations use CTI* refers to the structured consumption patterns of intelligence products by Security Operations Centres (SOCs), Computer Security Incident Response Teams (CSIRTs), executive leadership, risk management, and engineering teams to **reduce uncertainty, prioritise defensive investment, accelerate incident response, and proactively hunt for adversary presence** within the enterprise attack surface. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The three primary consumption levels of CTI are: > 1. **Strategic CTI** — consumed by C-suite / Board for long-term risk posture. > 2. **Operational CTI** — consumed by SOC managers / Threat Hunters for campaign tracking. > 3. **Tactical CTI** — consumed by SOC analysts / SIEM engineers for rule creation and alert tuning. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Intelligence Model Imagine a large multi-speciality **hospital** (the *organisation*) facing constant biological threats — viruses, bacteria, drug-resistant superbugs (the *threat actors*). The hospital does not treat every patient blindly; it relies on a layered intelligence system: | Hospital Element | CTI Equivalent | |------------------|----------------| | WHO disease bulletins, epidemic forecasts | Strategic CTI (geopolitical, sector threat reports) | | Hospital infection-control team tracking outbreaks | Operational CTI (campaigns, threat actor TTPs) | | Lab reports — specific bacterium, antibiotic resistance | Tactical CTI (IOCs, IPs, hashes, CVEs) | | Triage nurse prioritising critical patients | SOC analyst triaging alerts using CTI | | Hospital Director deciding annual budget | CISO deciding security spend using CTI | > [!NOTE] > **Intuition in one line:** Just as a doctor uses lab reports (raw data) + clinical knowledge (context) + patient history (environment) to prescribe a cure, an organisation uses **raw threat data + adversary context + business risk profile** to prescribe a defence. CTI is the "clinical knowledge" that converts raw indicator noise into a precise prescription. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics Organisations Track - **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** — average time between compromise and detection. **Industry benchmark: ~204 days (IBM 2023).** - **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)** — average time between detection and containment. **Industry benchmark: ~73 days.** - **False Positive Rate (FPR)** — proportion of alerts that are benign, targeted for reduction using tactical CTI. - **Threat Coverage %** — fraction of the MITRE ATT&CK techniques for which the organisation has detection coverage. - **Intelligence Confidence Score (1–5)** — reliability rating inherited from the Admiralty System (NATO). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework ### 2.1 The Six Canonical Use-Cases of CTI in Organisations Organisations consume CTI across **six interlocking use-cases**. Each use-case answers a specific business question: 1. **Risk-Based Prioritisation** — "Which threats actually matter to *us*?" 2. **Proactive Defence Engineering** — "What controls should we build before the attack?" 3. **Detection Engineering** — "What signatures and behavioural rules do we need?" 4. **Incident Response Acceleration** — "How do we contain this faster?" 5. **Threat Hunting** — "Where is the adversary hiding that our tools missed?" 6. **Executive Decision Support** — "What should the Board fund next quarter?" ### 2.2 Structured Logic Steps of CTI Consumption The organisational CTI workflow follows a **closed-loop pipeline** (also known as the *F3EAD cycle* — Find, Fix, Finish, Exploit, Analyse, Disseminate — adapted from military HUMINT): - **Step 1 — Requirement Definition:** Stakeholders (CISO, SOC lead, Fraud team) submit intelligence requirements (PIRs — Priority Intelligence Requirements). - **Step 2 — Collection:** Pull data from OSINT, ISACs, commercial feeds (Recorded Future, Mandiant), internal logs, dark-web forums. - **Step 3 — Processing:** Normalise, deduplicate, enrich (geo-IP, WHOIS, sandbox detonation). - **Step 4 — Analysis:** Apply the **Diamond Model of Intrusion Analysis** (Adversary, Capability, Infrastructure, Victim) and **MITRE ATT&CK** mapping. - **Step 5 — Dissemination:** Push tailored products to the right consumer (CEO briefing vs. SIEM rule). - **Step 6 — Feedback:** Consumers report utility → refines future requirements. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters for KTU:** Question papers often test whether students understand *who* consumes *which level* of CTI. Memorise the mapping: **Strategic → CISO/Board**, **Operational → SOC Manager/Threat Hunter**, **Tactical → SOC Analyst/SIEM Engineer**. ### 2.3 The Three Intelligence Layers (Detailed) | Layer | Consumer | Format | Time Horizon | Example Question | |-------|----------|--------|--------------|------------------| | **Strategic** | Board, CISO, Legal | Whitepaper, Briefing slide | Months–Years | "Will ransomware-as-a-service reshape our industry's risk profile in 2025?" | | **Operational** | SOC Manager, IR Lead, Threat Hunter | Threat report, Campaign profile | Weeks–Months | "What is APT-29's current campaign against healthcare?" | | **Tactical** | SOC Analyst, Detection Engineer, Network Admin | STIX/TAXII feed, IOC list, YARA rule | Hours–Days | "Block this IP — it is a C2 for Emotet variant X." | ### 2.4 KTU Framework Cheat-Sheet | Construct | Definition | Application in Organisation | |-----------|------------|----------------------------| | **IOC** (Indicator of Compromise) | Atomic artefact (hash, IP, domain) showing intrusion | Block at firewall / EDR | | **IOA** (Indicator of Attack) | Behavioural pattern showing intent | Detect in SIEM via correlation | | **TTP** (Tactics, Techniques, Procedures) | Adversary behaviour mapped to ATT&CK | Build resilient defences, hunt | | **PIR** (Priority Intelligence Requirement) | Top-priority question intelligence must answer | Directs collection effort | | **Diamond Model** | Intrusion analysis via 4 vertices: Adversary, Capability, Infrastructure, Victim | Link campaigns across intrusions | | **Kill Chain** (Lockheed Martin) | 7-stage model: Recon → Weaponisation → Delivery → Exploit → Install → C2 → Actions | Gap analysis of controls | | **ATT&CK** (MITRE) | Globally accessible knowledge base of adversary behaviour | Coverage mapping, detection engineering | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** In real-world deployments, ATT&CK-based detection engineering has been shown to reduce dwell time by up to **60%** when combined with threat-hunting programmes (Mandiant M-Trends 2024). The framework allows organisations to objectively measure defensive maturity. ### 2.5 The CTI Maturity Model (Kurta Model, Simplified) Organisations typically evolve through **four maturity stages** in their CTI consumption: - **Stage 1 — Reactive:** Intelligence consumed ad-hoc; no dedicated team. - **Stage 2 — Proactive:** Threat feeds integrated into SIEM; IOC blocking automated. - **Stage 3 — Operationalised:** Dedicated CTI team; ATT&CK coverage metrics tracked; hunt team active. - **Stage 4 — Predictive:** CTI fused with business risk; adversary emulation (BAS — Breach & Attack Simulation) drives purple-team exercises. ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — From Raw Data to Organisational Action ### 3.1 The Analytical Pipeline (Exhaustive Walk-through) Below is the rigorous, end-to-end transformation of a *raw observation* into an *organisational decision*: $$ \text{Raw Signal} \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{Step 1: Collection}\;}\; \text{Data Point} \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{Step 2: Validation}\;}\; \text{Verified Indicator} \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{Step 3: Contextualisation}\;}\; \text{Enriched IOC} \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{Step 4: Analysis}\;}\; \text{TTP-Mapped Intelligence} \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{Step 5: Production}\;}\; \text{Tailored Product} \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{Step 6: Consumption}\;}\; \text{Defensive Action} $$ Each transition is governed by explicit criteria and quality controls. ### 3.2 Worked Analytical Example **Scenario:** A SOC analyst at *Bank Kerala* observes repeated DNS queries to `secure-update-portal[.]xyz` from an internal workstation. Apply the organisational CTI workflow step-by-step. **Step 1 — Collection (Data Acquisition):** The DNS logs (internal source) and a passive DNS feed (external source) are aggregated. The domain `secure-update-portal[.]xyz` is flagged because its entropy score is **high (4.2)** and registration age is **< 30 days** — both classic DGA-like features. $$ \text{Entropy}(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i $$ For the domain string above, character distribution analysis yields $H \approx 4.2$ bits/char, far exceeding typical benign-domain average of $H \approx 2.8$ bits/char. **Step 2 — Validation (False-Positive Reduction):** Cross-reference the domain against **Threat-Intel platforms (VirusTotal, AlienVault OTX)** and **internal allow-list**. The platform returns **15/70 vendors flagging malicious**, and the domain is **NOT** in the allow-list. $$ \text{Confidence}_{\text{final}} = \left( \text{Vendor} \times 0.4 \right) + \left( \text{Behavioural} \times 0.3 \right) + \left( \text{Contextual} \times 0.3 \right) $$ $$ \text{Confidence}_{\text{final}} = \left( \frac{15}{70} \times 100 \times 0.4 \right) + \left( 70 \times 0.3 \right) + \left( 80 \times 0.3 \right) $$ $$ \text{Confidence}_{\text{final}} = 8.57 + 21.0 + 24.0 = 53.57 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Round to } 54\,\% $$ A confidence score of **≥ 70%** is generally required for automated blocking. At 54%, this becomes a **Tactical alert** requiring human triage. **Step 3 — Contextualisation (IOC Enrichment):** WHOIS lookup reveals: - Registrar: anonymised - Hosting ASN: AS-*** — known bullet-proof hoster - GeoIP: Eastern Europe - Associated samples: 3 malware binaries (Emotet variant B) **Step 4 — Analysis (TTP Mapping using MITRE ATT&CK):** The behaviour and binaries map to: - **T1071.004 — Application Layer Protocol: DNS** (C2 channel) - **T1568.002 — Dynamic Resolution** (DGA-style domain) - **T1059 — Command and Scripting Interpreter** (downloader payload) Adversary cluster correlation using the **Diamond Model** identifies the cluster as **FIN12 / Emotet malspam affiliate**, matching prior campaign **CAMP-2024-117** seen against two other Indian banks. **Step 5 — Production (Tailored Intelligence Products):** | Consumer Level | Product Delivered | Format | |---------------|-------------------|--------| | Strategic | "Emotet resurgence targeting Indian BFSI sector — risk elevation to HIGH" | 2-page exec PDF | | Operational | Campaign profile: FIN12 TTPs, infrastructure list, victimology | 10-page STIX 2.1 bundle | | Tactical | Block-list: 412 domains, 18 IPs, 3 file hashes; YARA rule: `Emotet_BankKerala_2024` | STIX/TAXII feed, SIEM rule | **Step 6 — Consumption (Organisational Action):** - **Tactical consumer (SIEM):** Auto-block domains at DNS firewall; ingest YARA into EDR. - **Operational consumer (SOC Manager):** Initiate **Threat Hunt** across all endpoints using the new YARA rule. - **Strategic consumer (CISO):** Approves emergency budget of ₹35 lakhs for advanced DNS-layer security; updates Board on emerging BFSI threat. ### 3.3 Decision Matrix — Who Consumes What? | Adversary Activity Observed | Intelligence Produced | Primary Consumer | Defensive Action | |------------------------------|----------------------|------------------|------------------| | New malware sample submitted | Atomic IOC (hash, IP) | SOC Analyst (Tactical) | EDR block, SIEM alert | | Sustained phishing wave against finance dept | Campaign profile | SOC Manager (Operational) | Email gateway rule, user awareness | | Ransomware group targets Indian healthcare | Sector threat forecast | CISO, Board (Strategic) | Cyber-insurance review, budget reallocation | | Nation-state actor breaches peer organisation | Attribution + TTP set | CISO + Legal + PR (Strategic) | Risk register update, regulatory disclosure | | Insider trading from leaked data | Adversary infrastructure map | IR Lead (Operational) | DLP tuning, legal hold | ### 3.4 The Intelligence Confidence Scoring (Admiralty / NATO) The **Admiralty System** rates intelligence on two axes: **Source Reliability (A–F)** and **Information Credibility (1–6)**. Organisations use this for *gating* automated actions. $$ \text{Decision Gate} = \begin{cases} \text{Automated Block} & \text{if } \text{Reliability} \in \{A, B\} \;\land\; \text{Credibility} \in \{1, 2, 3\} \\ \text{Analyst Triage} & \text{if } \text{Reliability} \in \{C, D\} \;\lor\; \text{Credibility} \in \{4\} \\ \text{Watch Only} & \text{if } \text{Reliability} \in \{E, F\} \;\lor\; \text{Credibility} \in \{5, 6\} \end{cases} $$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Organisational CTI Consumption Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB src1[Open Source Feeds
OSINT Blogs RSS] src2[Commercial TI Vendors
Recorded Future Mandiant] src3[ISAC and Industry Sharing
FS ISAC HFCERT] src4[Internal Telemetry
SIEM EDR Firewall Logs] src5[Dark Web and Deep Web
Forums Marketplaces] coll[CTI Collection Engine
STIX TAXII Ingestion] proc[Processing and Normalisation
Dedupe Enrichment] ana[Analysis Layer
Diamond Model ATT and CK] prod[Intelligence Production
Strategic Operational Tactical] c1[CISO and Board
Strategic Decisions] c2[SOC Manager and IR Lead
Operational Actions] c3[SOC Analyst and SIEM
Tactical Blocks] c4[Risk and Compliance
Regulatory Mapping] fb[Feedback Loop
PIRs Refinement] src1 --> coll src2 --> coll src3 --> coll src4 --> coll src5 --> coll coll --> proc proc --> ana ana --> prod prod --> c1 prod --> c2 prod --> c3 prod --> c4 c1 --> fb c2 --> fb c3 --> fb c4 --> fb fb --> coll ``` ### 4.2 CTI Use-Case Mapping to Organisational Functions ```mermaid flowchart LR cti[CTI Core Engine] uc1[Risk Based Prioritisation] uc2[Detection Engineering] uc3[Incident Response] uc4[Threat Hunting] uc5[Strategic Planning] uc6[Fraud and Brand Protection] fn1[Risk Management Team] fn2[SIEM and SOC Engineering] fn3[CSIRT and IR Team] fn4[Red Team and Hunters] fn5[Executive Leadership] fn6[Finance and Marketing Security] cti --> uc1 --> fn1 cti --> uc2 --> fn2 cti --> uc3 --> fn3 cti --> uc4 --> fn4 cti --> uc5 --> fn5 cti --> uc6 --> fn6 ``` ### 4.3 The Three-Layer CTI Consumption Pyramid ```mermaid flowchart TB s1[STRATEGIC LAYER
CISO and Board
Months to Years Horizon
Threat Forecasts Sector Trends] o1[OPERATIONAL LAYER
SOC Manager and Hunters
Weeks to Months Horizon
Campaign Profiles TTP Maps] t1[TACTICAL LAYER
SOC Analysts and SIEM
Hours to Days Horizon
IOCs IOAs STIX Feeds] s1 --> o1 o1 --> t1 t1 -.feedback to.-> s1 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]** **Q1. Define Cyber Threat Intelligence and list its three primary levels of consumption in an organisation. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) is the evidence-based collection, processing, and analysis of information about adversaries — their capabilities, intents, infrastructure, and TTPs — transformed into actionable knowledge to support cybersecurity decision-making. The three primary levels of consumption are: 1. **Strategic CTI** — high-level, long-horizon intelligence consumed by the CISO and Board for risk posture and investment decisions. 2. **Operational CTI** — campaign- and adversary-level intelligence consumed by SOC managers, IR leads, and threat hunters for planning defences and proactive hunts. 3. **Tactical CTI** — atomic-indicator intelligence (IOCs, hashes, IPs) consumed by SOC analysts and detection engineers for SIEM/EDR rule creation and blocking. *(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the three levels with consumer mapping.)* --- **Q2. List any three use-cases of CTI in a modern enterprise. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** 1. **Risk-based prioritisation of vulnerabilities and threats** — helping the organisation focus on adversaries and CVEs that *actually* target its sector. 2. **Detection engineering** — creation of SIEM correlation rules, EDR signatures, and YARA rules informed by adversary TTPs. 3. **Incident response acceleration** — using threat actor playbooks and known infrastructure to triage and contain intrusions rapidly. 4. *(Optional third alternative)* **Threat hunting** — proactively searching for adversary presence using hypothesis-driven hunts based on CTI. *(1 mark per use-case, any three.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]** ### **Question A (14 Marks):** **(a) Explain in detail the Strategic, Operational, and Tactical levels of CTI consumption in organisations. For each level, state the consumer, the format of the product, and one example. (7 Marks)** **(b) Discuss how the MITRE ATT&CK framework is used by organisations for detection engineering and threat hunting. Illustrate with one real-world technique. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **(a) The Three Levels of CTI Consumption:** | Level | Consumer | Format | Time Horizon | Example | |-------|----------|--------|--------------|---------| | **Strategic** | CISO, Board, Legal, Risk Committee | Executive briefing slides, sector risk reports, whitepapers | 6 months – 3 years | A report stating "Ransomware-as-a-service will increase 35% YoY targeting Indian BFSI in 2025" | | **Operational** | SOC Manager, IR Lead, Threat Hunters | Threat-actor profiles, campaign reports, ATT&CK TTP mappings | 2 weeks – 6 months | A FIN12 campaign profile mapping their current infrastructure, target sectors, and TTPs | | **Tactical** | SOC Analyst, Detection Engineer, Network Admin | STIX/TAXII feeds, IOC block-lists, YARA rules, Sigma rules | Hours – 1 week | An IP address `192.0.2.45` flagged as Emotet C2, distributed as a SIEM rule | The three levels are **hierarchical and complementary**: tactical feeds inform operational tracking, which in turn informs strategic forecasting. The *same* piece of raw data is repackaged differently for each audience — a process called **intelligence tailoring**. *(3 marks for table; 2 marks for hierarchy; 2 marks for examples.)* **(b) MITRE ATT&CK in Detection Engineering & Threat Hunting:** The **MITRE ATT&CK** (Adversarial Tactics, Techniques, and Common Knowledge) framework is a curated knowledge base of adversary behaviour, organised into **14 tactic categories** (e.g., Initial Access, Execution, Persistence) containing **200+ techniques** and **400+ sub-techniques**. Organisations use ATT&CK in three primary ways: 1. **Coverage Mapping:** SOC teams map their existing SIEM/EDR rules to ATT&CK techniques to identify blind spots. A typical "ATT&CK Navigator" heatmap shows techniques as red (no detection) → green (full detection). 2. **Detection Engineering:** Analysts author Sigma/YARA rules aligned to specific technique IDs (e.g., T1059.001 — PowerShell). This ensures every rule is tied to a known adversary behaviour rather than a one-off signature. 3. **Threat Hunting:** Hunt teams generate hypotheses from ATT&CK — e.g., "Is any endpoint executing `regsvr32` with the `/s` flag and remote URL (T1218.010)?" — and search telemetry for evidence. **Illustrative example — Technique T1071.001 (Application Layer Protocol: Web):** Adversaries use HTTP/HTTPS for C2 to blend with normal web traffic. The detection-engineering approach is to build a rule that flags **processes making outbound HTTPS connections to newly registered domains (NRDs) with high entropy**, an indicator of DGA-based C2. The hunt hypothesis: "An attacker may have compromised a workstation and is beaconing out via HTTPS every 60 seconds to a DGA domain." Analysts then search proxy logs for these patterns. **Outcome:** By mapping controls to ATT&CK, organisations can quantitatively measure defensive maturity and prioritise engineering effort on the techniques their specific adversaries (identified via CTI) most likely to use. *(3 marks for ATT&CK concept; 2 marks for the three uses; 2 marks for the worked example.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** **(a) Describe the CTI lifecycle as applied within an organisation. Include all six stages with a one-line description of each. (7 Marks)** **(b) Explain how organisations use CTI for risk-based decision making. Discuss with reference to the Diamond Model of Intrusion Analysis. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **(a) The CTI Lifecycle (Six Stages):** 1. **Planning and Direction** — Define Priority Intelligence Requirements (PIRs) based on organisational risk, sector, and threat landscape. *Example:* "Identify ransomware groups actively targeting Kerala's BFSI sector." 2. **Collection** — Gather raw data from OSINT, commercial feeds, ISACs, internal logs, dark-web sources, and human intelligence. 3. **Processing and Exploitation** — Normalise, deduplicate, translate, and enrich the data. Convert PDFs to structured STIX; resolve IPs via WHOIS/GeoIP; detonate samples in sandbox. 4. **Analysis and Production** — Apply analytical frameworks (Diamond Model, Kill Chain, ATT&CK) to produce finished intelligence tailored to consumer needs. 5. **Dissemination and Integration** — Deliver products to the right consumer via the right channel: SIEM, EDR, email, executive dashboards, STIX/TAXII. 6. **Feedback and Review** — Consumers report utility; PIRs and collection sources are refined; lessons are fed back to Stage 1. The lifecycle is **iterative**, not linear — feedback from Stage 6 constantly re-shapes Stage 1. *(1 mark per stage × 6 = 6 marks; 1 mark for the iterative nature.)* **(b) CTI for Risk-Based Decision Making & the Diamond Model:** Risk-based decision making in cybersecurity requires answering: *"Which threats pose unacceptable risk to my specific organisation, and where should I invest to reduce that risk?"* CTI enables this by **fusing threat probability with business impact**. **The Risk Equation:** $$ \text{Risk} = \text{Threat Likelihood} \times \text{Vulnerability Exposure} \times \text{Business Impact} $$ CTI primarily informs the **Threat Likelihood** term — by identifying *who* is targeting *us*, *how*, and *with what capability*. The **Diamond Model** (Caltagirone, Pendergast, Betz, 2013) supports this by representing any intrusion as a 4-vertex event: - **Adversary** — Who is attacking? (e.g., FIN12, Lazarus, hacktivist group) - **Capability** — What malware/tool is used? (e.g., Emotet, Cobalt Strike) - **Infrastructure** — What C2, domains, IPs? (e.g., `secure-update-portal[.]xyz`) - **Victim** — Who is targeted? (e.g., Indian BFSI, healthcare) **How an organisation uses the Diamond Model:** 1. **Pivot Analysis:** From one known vertex, the analyst pivots to identify related vertices.
