Knowing the *adversary* (FIN12) reveals their typical *capabilities* (Emotet) and *infrastructure* (DGA domains). 2. **Campaign Clustering:** Multiple intrusions sharing ≥ 2 vertices are clustered into a single campaign, allowing the organisation to track a long-running threat actor's evolution. 3. **Risk Quantification:** The more diamond vertices an organisation shares with a known campaign (e.g., same sector + same infrastructure), the higher the threat likelihood term, increasing the overall Risk score. 4. **Defence Gaps:** The model exposes where controls fail — if Capability is known but Detection is missing, ATT&CK coverage analysis highlights the gap. **Illustrative example:** A retail bank in Kerala receives CTI that Lazarus Group is targeting Indian banks using supply-chain attacks on third-party vendors. The Diamond Model tells the bank that Lazarus uses *Capabilities* (Custom RAT, watering-hole scripts) and *Infrastructure* (Bulletproof ASPs in Eastern Europe) that the bank's current EDR does not detect. The risk score is recalculated; the CISO approves a ₹50-lakh spend on an advanced behavioural EDR plus a vendor-risk programme. *(3 marks for risk equation + CTI's role; 2 marks for Diamond Model; 2 marks for worked example.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Confusing the three CTI levels** — students often list types (strategic/operational/tactical) but fail to map the **consumer** to each. KTU examiners specifically test this mapping. Always write *who* consumes it. > 2. **Writing definitions without examples** — a 7-mark sub-part without an example typically loses 2 marks. KTU rewards applied knowledge. > 3. **Treating ATT&CK as just a list of techniques** — it is a *framework for coverage analysis and detection engineering*, not a checklist. Mention **mapping**, **Navigator**, and **hypothesis-driven hunting**. > 4. **Forgetting the iterative nature of the CTI lifecycle** — describing it as linear will cost at least 1 mark. > 5. **Skipping the feedback loop in diagrams** — every CTI flow diagram should have a feedback arrow from consumer back to collection, per KTU's OBE emphasis on continuous improvement. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **CTI = Evidence-based, actionable knowledge** about adversaries, not just a list of IOCs. - **Three consumption levels:** Strategic (CISO/Board, long-horizon), Operational (SOC Manager/Hunters, campaign-level), Tactical (SOC Analyst/SIEM, atomic IOCs). - **Six use-cases:** Risk prioritisation, Proactive defence, Detection engineering, Incident response, Threat hunting, Executive decision support. - **The CTI Lifecycle (6 stages):** Planning → Collection → Processing → Analysis → Dissemination → Feedback (iterative). - **Core analytical frameworks:** Diamond Model (Adversary–Capability–Infrastructure–Victim), Lockheed Kill Chain, MITRE ATT&CK. - **IOC vs IOA vs TTP:** IOCs are *atomic artefacts* (what), IOAs are *behaviours* (intent), TTPs are *patterns* (how). Move from IOC-based to TTP-based defence for resilience. - **Confidence scoring:** Use the Admiralty System (Source A–F × Credibility 1–6) to gate automated defensive actions. - **ATT&CK application:** Coverage mapping → Detection engineering → Threat hunting → Purple-team validation. - **CTI Maturity Stages:** Reactive → Proactive → Operationalised → Predictive. - **Feedback loop is mandatory** — every CTI product must be evaluated by the consumer, refining future PIRs. - **Key metrics to remember:** MTTD (~204 days industry avg), MTTR (~73 days), ATT&CK coverage %, False Positive Rate. - **STIX/TAXII** is the W3C-standard language for sharing threat intelligence between organisations and tools. - **Ethical boundary:** CTI must be *defensive intelligence* under KTU's professional conduct framework; offensive intelligence operations fall outside the scope of the PECCT755 syllabus.

topics.title: Role of CTI Analyst
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT755 - CYBER THREAT INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Role of CTI Analyst

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) Analyst** is a specialized cybersecurity professional who systematically collects, processes, analyses, and disseminates actionable intelligence regarding cyber threats targeting an organization's digital assets, personnel, infrastructure, and reputation. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme curriculum for PECCT755, the CTI Analyst operates at the intersection of cybersecurity operations, strategic risk management, and intelligence science, translating raw threat data into contextually rich, decision-support intelligence for stakeholders ranging from SOC engineers to executive leadership.

The role is formally anchored in the **Intelligence Cycle** (often called the CTI Lifecycle), a structured five-phase model:
**Planning & Direction → Collection → Processing → Analysis → Dissemination → Feedback.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The "Role of CTI Analyst" forms the foundational lens through which all subsequent modules (Threat Actors, IOC Analysis, Threat Hunting, MITRE ATT&CK, Threat Intelligence Platforms) are taught. Examiners frequently test the *operational boundaries* and *deliverable expectations* of this role.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Detective"

Imagine a CTI Analyst as a **hospital diagnostic radiologist combined with a crime intelligence officer** for an organization.

- Just as a **radiologist** does not perform surgery but interprets X-rays and MRIs to give the surgeon a precise picture of what is wrong, a CTI Analyst does not usually patch systems or block IPs at scale (that's the SOC's job), but interprets vast streams of threat data — logs, malware samples, dark web chatter — to tell the defenders *what, who, why, when, and how* of an attack.
- Just as an **intelligence officer** in a national security agency produces daily briefings for decision-makers, the CTI Analyst produces **Threat Intelligence Reports (TIRs)**, **Advisories**, and **Strategic Forecasts** that empower executives to make budget, policy, and risk decisions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term — Actionable Intelligence:**
> Intelligence is termed *actionable* when it is **Relevant**, **Timely**, **Contextual**, **Accurate**, and **Consumable** by the recipient. Raw Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) are merely *data*; a CTI Analyst converts them into *intelligence*.

## 1.3 Core Functions of the Role (At a Glance)

A CTI Analyst's mandate spans **three intelligence tiers**:

| Tier | Orientation | Audience | Time Horizon |
|------|-------------|----------|--------------|
| **Strategic** | High-level, business-risk focused | CISO, CEO, Board | Months to Years |
| **Operational** | Adversary TTPs and campaign tracking | SOC Manager, IR Lead | Weeks to Months |
| **Tactical** | IOCs, signatures, technical artefacts | SOC Analysts, EDR/AV teams | Hours to Days |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember this Exam-Ready Line:**
> "A CTI Analyst reduces uncertainty for decision-makers by transforming fragmented threat data into prioritised, contextual, and actionable knowledge."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The CTI Analyst's Operational Framework — Logic Steps

The role is not a single activity but a **continuous, cyclical workflow**. Below is the deconstructed "Why" and "How" for each step:

1. **Requirements Definition (Why?):** The Analyst first understands *who* needs the intelligence and *what* decisions they will make. Without this, intelligence effort becomes noise.
   - *How:* Stakeholder interviews, risk registers review, threat modelling sessions.

2. **Collection Planning (How?):** Defines sources — open, closed, internal, human (HUMINT), or technical.
   - *How:* Identifies log sources (SIEM, EDR), OSINT feeds, dark web forums, ISAC memberships, commercial feeds.

3. **Data Collection & Normalisation:** Pulls raw artefacts — IPs, hashes, domains, malware samples — into a structured format (STIX 2.1 is the KTU-relevant standard).

4. **Processing & Enrichment:** Adds context — geo-IP, WHOIS, sandbox detonation, threat-actor attribution confidence scoring.

5. **Analysis & Correlation:** Applies models like **MITRE ATT&CK**, **Diamond Model of Intrusion Analysis**, and **Cyber Kill Chain** to find patterns.

6. **Dissemination:** Pushes finished reports via TIPs (Threat Intelligence Platforms), email, dashboards, or API integrations to SIEM/EDR.

7. **Feedback Loop:** Measures whether the consumed intelligence led to better decisions — the *Intelligence Value* metric.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — The Analyst's Toolkit

| # | Element | Description | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---------|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **MITRE ATT&CK** | Adversary Tactics, Techniques & Procedures matrix | Very High |
| 2 | **STIX / TAXII** | Structured Threat Information eXpression / sharing protocol | High |
| 3 | **Diamond Model** | Adversary-Capability-Infrastructure-Victim linkage | High |
| 4 | **Kill Chain (Lockheed)** | Recon → Weaponize → Deliver → Exploit → Install → C2 → Actions | Medium |
| 5 | **TTPs** | Tactics, Techniques, Procedures — adversary behaviour patterns | Very High |
| 6 | **IOCs** | Indicators of Compromise — observables (IP, hash, domain, URL) | Very High |
| 7 | **Confidence Scoring** | Low / Medium / High attribution certainty (Admiralty/NIST) | Medium |
| 8 | **Threat Actor Typology** | APT, Cybercrime, Hacktivist, Insider, Script Kiddie | High |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industry, the CTI Analyst's deliverables directly reduce the **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** and **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)** for breaches. They feed:

- **SIEM rules** (correlation logic).
- **EDR/AV signatures** (malware hashes).
- **Firewall/IPS block lists** (C2 IPs).
- **Executive risk dashboards** (KRIs — Key Risk Indicators).
- **Red Team / Purple Team emulation plans** (real adversary TTPs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> A mature CTI programme reduces breach cost by an average of **USD 1.8 million** per incident (industry benchmark) by enabling proactive rather than reactive defence. The Analyst is the human-in-the-loop who ensures intelligence is *contextually correct*, not just *technically present*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework — The Analyst's Daily Logic

While the CTI Analyst's role is conceptual (not formulaic), the underlying *decision logic* can be expressed in a structured analytical flow. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step breakdown of how a CTI Analyst converts raw data into intelligence.

## 3.1 The Intelligence Value Equation (Conceptual Derivation)

Although qualitative, the value of intelligence can be expressed as a function. Let us derive the conceptual form:

Let:
- $R$ = Relevance to stakeholder decision
- $T$ = Timeliness of delivery
- $A$ = Accuracy / confidence score (0 to 1)
- $C$ = Contextual enrichment depth

The **Intelligence Value** $V$ is given by:

$$
V = f(R, T, A, C) = (R \cdot T \cdot A \cdot C)
$$

Where each variable is normalised to a value between 0 and 1.

**Step 1 — Identify Relevance (R):**
The Analyst asks: *"Which asset, business process, or strategic objective does this IOC/TTP relate to?"*

$$
R = \frac{\text{Number of mapped stakeholder needs}}{\text{Total stakeholder needs identified}}
$$

**Step 2 — Measure Timeliness (T):**
Timeliness decays exponentially. If $t$ is the age of intelligence in hours and $\lambda$ is the decay constant:

$$
T = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

> **Logic:** The older the IOC, the less its tactical value. A C2 IP from 48 hours ago is far more valuable than one from 6 months ago.

**Step 3 — Assign Accuracy (A):**
Based on the **Admiralty Scale** (NATO), confidence is rated:

$$
A = \begin{cases} 0.9 & \text{if } \text{Source Reliability} = A \text{ and Information Credibility} = 1 \\ 0.7 & \text{if } \text{Source} = B \text{ and Info} = 2 \\ 0.5 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Contextual Depth (C):**
A single IP = low context; an IP + hash + domain + actor attribution + TTP mapping = high context.

$$
C = \frac{\text{Number of correlated entities}}{\text{Maximum possible correlations}}
$$

**Step 5 — Aggregate Value:**

$$
V = R \cdot e^{-\lambda t} \cdot A \cdot C
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for Examiners:**
> If any single factor equals zero (e.g., irrelevant intelligence), $V = 0$. This demonstrates that **actionable** intelligence requires *all four* attributes. The Analyst's skill is in maximising each factor simultaneously.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Skills vs. Deliverables

The CTI Analyst must master a cross-domain skill matrix. Below is the exhaustive mapping:

| Skill Domain | Sub-Skill | Resulting Deliverable |
|--------------|-----------|----------------------|
| **Technical** | Malware analysis, log analysis, network forensics | IOCs, YARA rules |
| **Analytical** | Pattern recognition, attribution, hypothesis testing | Threat actor profiles |
| **Communication** | Report writing, briefing, visualisation | TIRs, Advisories, Executive briefs |
| **Tools** | SIEM, TIP, MISP, Maltego, VirusTotal, ThreatConnect | Configured intelligence pipelines |
| **Strategic** | Risk framing, business impact mapping | Risk-register updates |
| **Legal/Ethical** | GDPR, evidence handling, OPSEC | Compliance-aligned reports |

## 3.3 Worked Example — From Data to Decision

Suppose the Analyst receives an alert: a host attempted to beacon to IP `203.0.113.45`.

**Step A:** Query internal SIEM — any prior hits? *(Collection)*
**Step B:** Check VirusTotal, AbuseIPDB, GreyNoise. *(Processing)*
**Step C:** Correlate with MITRE ATT&CK — falls under **T1071.001 (Application Layer Protocol: Web Protocols)**. *(Analysis)*
**Step D:** Identify actor cluster — matches **APT-XX's known C2 infrastructure pattern**. *(Correlation)*
**Step E:** Push IP to SIEM blocklist, write advisory for SOC, brief CISO. *(Dissemination)*
**Step F:** Document feedback — did the block reduce alerts? *(Feedback)*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The CTI Analyst's Role Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **interactions** of a CTI Analyst within the cybersecurity ecosystem, replacing a physical drawing with a functional architecture flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    srcA[Open Source INTEL]:::src --> collect
    srcB[Commercial Feeds]:::src --> collect
    srcC[Internal SIEM and EDR]:::src --> collect
    srcD[Dark Web HUMINT]:::src --> collect
    srcE[ISAC Sharing]:::src --> collect

    collect[Collection Layer]:::process --> process[Processing and Enrichment]:::process
    process --> analyse[Analysis and Correlation]:::analyse

    subgraph Frameworks[Analytical Frameworks]
        attck[MITRE ATT and CK]:::fw
        diamond[Diamond Model]:::fw
        kill[Kill Chain]:::fw
    end

    Frameworks --> analyse

    analyse --> strat[Strategic INTEL]:::output
    analyse --> ops[Operational INTEL]:::output
    analyse --> tact[Tactical INTEL]:::output

    strat --> ciso[CISO and Board]:::audience
    ops --> socmgr[SOC Manager and IR Lead]:::audience
    tact --> analyst[SOC Analysts and EDR]:::audience

    ciso --> feedback[Feedback Loop]:::fb
    socmgr --> feedback
    analyst --> feedback
    feedback --> collect

    classDef src fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    classDef process fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff
    classDef analyse fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    classDef fw fill:#a855f7,stroke:#a855f7,color:#ffffff
    classDef output fill:#10b981,stroke:#10b981,color:#ffffff
    classDef audience fill:#ef4444,stroke:#ef4444,color:#ffffff
    classDef fb fill:#64748b,stroke:#64748b,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Tiered Analyst Career Pathway (KTU Industry Alignment)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Tier 1 SOC Analyst]:::tier1 --> T2[Tier 2 Incident Response]:::tier2
    T2 --> T3[Tier 3 Threat Hunter]:::tier3
    T3 --> CTIA[CTI Analyst Junior]:::ctia
    CTIA --> CTIS[CTI Analyst Senior]:::ctis
    CTIS --> CTIM[CTI Manager or Threat Intel Lead]:::ctim

    classDef tier1 fill:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff
    classDef tier2 fill:#6366f1,color:#ffffff
    classDef tier3 fill:#8b5cf6,color:#ffffff
    classDef ctia fill:#ec4899,color:#ffffff
    classDef ctis fill:#f43f5e,color:#ffffff
    classDef ctim fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 The Intelligence Cycle as a Closed Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Planning and Direction] --> P2[Collection]
    P2 --> P3[Processing]
    P3 --> P4[Analysis]
    P4 --> P5[Dissemination]
    P5 --> P6[Feedback]
    P6 -.feedback refinement.-> P1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the role of a Cyber Threat Intelligence Analyst. List any four key responsibilities.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) Analyst is a cybersecurity professional who collects, processes, analyses, and disseminates actionable intelligence about threats to an organisation's assets. *(1 mark)*

Four key responsibilities: *(2 marks — 0.5 each)*
1. Collecting threat data from OSINT, ISACs, dark web, and internal sources.
2. Analysing IOCs and TTPs using MITRE ATT&CK, Diamond Model, and Kill Chain.
3. Producing Threat Intelligence Reports (Strategic, Operational, Tactical) for stakeholders.
4. Providing feedback to improve detection rules in SIEM/EDR and inform risk decisions.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between Tactical, Operational, and Strategic threat intelligence.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Level | Focus | Audience | Timeframe |
|-------|-------|----------|-----------|
| Tactical | IOCs, signatures, hashes | SOC Analysts, EDR | Hours–Days |
| Operational | Adversary TTPs, campaigns | SOC Manager, IR | Weeks–Months |
| Strategic | Threat trends, business risk | CISO, Board | Months–Years |

*(1 mark per row — total 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
**(a)** Explain the Intelligence Cycle as applicable to Cyber Threat Intelligence. Describe each of its phases in detail. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the key skills and competencies required for an effective CTI Analyst. How do these skills differ across Strategic, Operational, and Tactical intelligence tiers? *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Intelligence Cycle (7 Marks):**

The Intelligence Cycle is a structured, iterative process that converts raw data into actionable intelligence.

1. **Planning & Direction (1 Mark):** Defines the intelligence requirement based on stakeholder needs — *"What decisions need to be made?"* Outputs are Priority Intelligence Requirements (PIRs).

2. **Collection (1 Mark):** Gathers raw data from internal (SIEM, EDR, logs), commercial (paid feeds like Recorded Future), OSINT (VirusTotal, AbuseIPDB), and human sources (dark web researchers, ISACs).

3. **Processing (1 Mark):** Normalises data into machine-readable formats (STIX 2.1), deduplicates, and enriches with WHOIS, geo-IP, sandbox analysis.

4. **Analysis (2 Marks):** Applies frameworks (MITRE ATT&CK, Diamond Model) to identify adversary behaviour, attribution confidence (Admiralty Scale), and campaign linkages. Produces assessed intelligence.

5. **Dissemination (1 Mark):** Delivers reports to the appropriate audience — Tactical (IOC lists to SOC), Operational (TTP advisories to IR), Strategic (risk briefings to executives).

6. **Feedback (1 Mark):** Measures utility of intelligence consumed. Refines PIRs and collection sources. Closes the loop.

**(b) Key Skills & Tier Differences (7 Marks):**

A CTI Analyst must blend **technical**, **analytical**, and **communication** skills. *(1 mark)*

- **Technical Skills:** Log analysis, malware triage, network forensics, scripting (Python), tool proficiency (MISP, Maltego, ThreatConnect). *(1 mark)*
- **Analytical Skills:** Pattern recognition, hypothesis-driven reasoning, structured analytic techniques (ACH — Analysis of Competing Hypotheses). *(1 mark)*
- **Communication Skills:** Report writing, data visualisation, executive briefing. *(1 mark)*
- **Strategic Tier — Skills:** Business acumen, risk quantification, geopolitics, threat actor profiling. Focus on *long-term trends* and *financial impact*. Audience: CISO/Board. *(1 mark)*
- **Operational Tier — Skills:** Deep TTP analysis, campaign tracking, Purple Team coordination. Focus on *adversary behaviour over weeks/months*. Audience: SOC Manager. *(1 mark)*
- **Tactical Tier — Skills:** IOC extraction, YARA/Sigma rule writing, SIEM/EDR integration. Focus on *real-time detection*. Audience: SOC Analysts. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Choice]`
**(a)** What is meant by "Actionable Intelligence"? Explain the four attributes that make intelligence actionable, with an example. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **Diamond Model of Intrusion Analysis** and the **MITRE ATT&CK Framework**. How does a CTI Analyst use both in daily operations? *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Actionable Intelligence (7 Marks):**

Actionable intelligence is threat knowledge that is timely, relevant, contextual, and accurate enough to drive a defensive decision or action. *(2 marks)*

Four Attributes (1 mark each):
1. **Relevant** — Maps to a specific asset, business process, or risk.
2. **Timely** — Delivered before the threat window closes.
3. **Contextual** — Provides the *why* and *how*, not just the *what*.
4. **Accurate** — Carries a known confidence level (Admiralty Scale A1–F6).

**Example (1 mark):**
> *"APT-29 is targeting Kerala-based BPO firms using spear-phishing with malicious HTA attachments (TTP: T1218.005) between 02:00–05:00 IST; three IOCs attached."*
This is actionable because it specifies *who*, *whom*, *how*, *when*, and includes consumable IOCs.

**(b) Diamond Model vs. MITRE ATT&CK (7 Marks):**

| Dimension | Diamond Model | MITRE ATT&CK |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| **Origin** | Intelligence science (Caltagirone et al., 2013) | Adversary emulation (MITRE, 2013–present) |
| **Structure** | 4 vertices: Adversary, Capability, Infrastructure, Victim | Matrix: 14 Tactics × hundreds of Techniques |
| **Focus** | Intrusion event linkage, attribution | Adversary behaviour catalogue |
| **Use Case** | Connecting related events into an intrusion activity | Mapping observed behaviour to known TTPs |
| **Output** | Activity Threading | TTP-mapped adversary profile |

*(1.5 marks for comparison table — approximate row-wise distribution)*

**CTI Analyst Daily Usage (3.5 marks):**
- The Analyst uses **Diamond Model** to *thread* an intrusion — linking a phishing email (Capability) from a known actor (Adversary) using a compromised domain (Infrastructure) against a finance department (Victim).
- The Analyst then uses **MITRE ATT&CK** to enrich the *Capability* vertex — identifying the technique as **T1566.001 (Spearphishing Attachment)** and noting mitigations (M1049: Antivirus/Antimalware, M1031: Network Intrusion Prevention).
- Combined, the two frameworks provide **strategic context (attribution)** + **tactical guidance (TTP mapping)** in a single intelligence product.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse the tiers.** Students often mis-label Strategic intelligence as "long IOCs" and Tactical as "executive briefings" — it is the *exact opposite*.
> 2. **Do NOT list MITRE ATT&CK without context.** Examiners deduct marks if you merely mention "ATT&CK" without stating its purpose (TTP mapping) or structure (Tactics × Techniques).
> 3. **Do NOT skip the Feedback phase.** Many answers end at "Dissemination" — the Intelligence Cycle is a *closed loop*, and missing the Feedback phase costs full 1 mark.
> 4. **Do NOT fabricate sources.** If you quote statistics, use the standard industry benchmark (e.g., IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report) — never invent numbers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **CTI Analyst** = data → information → intelligence → decision enabler.
- **Core functions:** Collect → Process → Analyse → Disseminate → Feedback (closed loop).
- **Three Tiers:**
  - *Strategic* → CISO/Board → business risk.
  - *Operational* → SOC Manager → adversary TTPs.
  - *Tactical* → SOC Analysts → IOCs, signatures.
- **Key Frameworks:** MITRE ATT&CK, Diamond Model, Cyber Kill Chain, Admiralty Scale.
- **Key Standards:** STIX 2.1 (format), TAXII (transport).
- **Actionable Intelligence = Relevant + Timely + Contextual + Accurate.**
- **Deliverables:** IOC lists, Threat Intelligence Reports (TIRs), Advisories, Executive Briefs, YARA/Sigma rules.
- **Career Path:** SOC T1 → SOC T2 → Threat Hunter → Junior CTI → Senior CTI → CTI Manager.
- **Value Equation (conceptual):** $V = R \cdot e^{-\lambda t} \cdot A \cdot C$.
- **Common Exam Traps:** tier mislabeling, missing Feedback phase, ATT&CK without context, fabricated references.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Threat Intelligence Platforms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT755 - CYBER THREAT INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Threat Intelligence Platforms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Threat Intelligence Platform (TIP)** is a centralized, automated software solution that enables organizations to **aggregate, correlate, analyze, enrich, disseminate, and act upon** cyber threat intelligence data collected from multiple internal and external sources. TIPs serve as the operational backbone of a Security Operations Center (SOC) and Threat Intelligence Program, facilitating the transformation of raw Indicators of Compromise (IoCs) into actionable, contextualized intelligence.

According to KTU 2024 PECCT755 syllabus terminology, a TIP is positioned at the intersection of **threat data ingestion, threat data normalization, threat data analysis, and threat intelligence sharing**, using standardized protocols such as **STIX (Structured Threat Information eXpression)** and **TAXII (Trusted Automated eXchange of Indicator Information)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Ready):**
> "A Threat Intelligence Platform is a threat intelligence management system that automates the collection, normalization, deduplication, enrichment, scoring, and sharing of cyber threat data across the intelligence lifecycle, enabling proactive defense through structured indicators, tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs)."

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **Threat Intelligence Platform** as the **"Central Nervous System of an Organization's Cyber Defense"**.

### 🏥 Real-World Analogy — The Hospital Emergency Room

Imagine a large hospital that receives thousands of patient symptom reports (cough, fever, chest pain) from various clinics, ambulances, and self-reported phone calls.

| Hospital Component | TIP Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Ambulance Dispatch & Triage Desk** | Threat Data Ingestion Layer | Collects raw alerts from SIEM, EDR, firewalls, OSINT feeds, ISACs |
| **Triage Nurse** | Normalization & Deduplication Engine | Converts "cough", "khansi", "咳" into a single standardized medical term |
| **Diagnostic Lab** | Enrichment & Analysis Engine | Adds context (patient history, blood test, X-ray) to each symptom |
| **Specialist Doctors** | Threat Scoring & Correlation Module | Decides severity (critical/moderate/low) and links related cases |
| **Hospital Record System** | TIP Database (Threat Lake) | Stores all patient data for future reference and pattern detection |
| **Doctor-to-Doctor Communication** | STIX/TAXII Sharing Protocols | Standardized exchange of medical findings with other hospitals |
| **Surgery Theatre** | SOAR/SIEM Integration Layer | Takes automated defensive action (block IP, isolate host) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Just as a hospital cannot operate efficiently if every clinic uses different terminology and paper records, a SOC cannot function effectively without a TIP to unify, deduplicate, enrich, and operationalize threat data. A TIP turns a **chaotic flood of raw indicators** into **focused, prioritized, actionable intelligence**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in TIPs

The following metrics are commonly used by KTU-recognized TIPs and SOCs:

- **MTTD (Mean Time to Detect):** Average time to identify a threat. Industry benchmark: **< 1 hour** for mature SOCs.
- **MTTR (Mean Time to Respond/Remediate):** Average time to contain a threat. Industry benchmark: **< 24 hours**.
- **IoC Coverage Ratio:** Percentage of active campaigns whose IoCs are present in the TIP database.
- **False Positive Rate (FPR):** Ratio of incorrect threat alerts to total alerts. Target: **< 5%** in mature deployments.
- **STIX/TAXII Compliance Score:** Degree to which the platform adheres to OASIS-CTI standards.
- **Threat Confidence Score:** Numerical value (typically **0–100**) assigned to each indicator based on source reliability and corroboration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU 2024 PECCT755, students must clearly distinguish between:
> - **Threat Data** (raw, unprocessed facts, e.g., a single IP address)
> - **Threat Information** (data with context, e.g., "IP 1.2.3.4 was seen in a phishing campaign")
> - **Threat Intelligence** (information that has been analyzed, scored, and made actionable for decision-making)
>
> A TIP is the **engine that performs this transformation**.



<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown — The TIP Lifecycle

A Threat Intelligence Platform operates through a **six-stage intelligence lifecycle** (often called the *F3-E2D Cycle* — **Feed, Filter, Fuse, Enrich, Expose, Disseminate**, or variations such as the Gartner/ThreatConnect cycle).

### 🔹 Stage 1: COLLECTION (Feed / Ingest)

**Definition:** Aggregation of threat data from heterogeneous sources.

**Sources include:**
- **Internal Sources:** SIEM logs, EDR alerts, IDS/IPS events, firewall logs, DNS logs, vulnerability scanners, honeypots.
- **External Sources (OSINT):** Open-source feeds like **AlienVault OTX, AbuseIPDB, VirusTotal, MISP Project, Spamhaus**.
- **Commercial Feeds:** Paid threat intelligence providers (e.g., Recorded Future, Mandiant, CrowdStrike).
- **Industry ISACs:** Information Sharing and Analysis Centers (e.g., FS-ISAC for finance, H-ISAC for healthcare).
- **Government Feeds:** CERT-In advisories, CISA AIS, NCSC UK.

**Why this matters:** Without diverse sources, the TIP would suffer from a **single-feed bias** and miss novel threats.

---

### 🔹 Stage 2: NORMALIZATION (Filter / Standardize)

**Definition:** Converting threat data from disparate formats (CSV, JSON, XML, proprietary) into a single standardized schema.

**Standard used:** **STIX 2.1** (Structured Threat Information eXpression) — an OASIS standard JSON-based language.

**How it works:**
- IP `1.2.3.4` in one feed becomes `"type": "ipv4-addr", "value": "1.2.3.4"` in STIX.
- Malware hash `5d41402abc4b2a76b9719d911017c592` (MD5) becomes `"type": "file:hashes.MD5"`.

**Why this matters:** Analysts cannot correlate data in different formats. Normalization enables **machine-readable, cross-feed correlation**.

---

### 🔹 Stage 3: DEDUPLICATION & CORRELATION (Fuse)

**Definition:** Identifying and merging duplicate indicators; linking related IoCs into a single **Threat Actor Profile** or **Campaign Cluster**.

**Example:** Five different feeds may report the same C2 IP with slightly different timestamps. The TIP merges them into a single canonical indicator with a confidence score increased by corroboration.

**How it works:**
- Hashing of indicator values.
- Levenshtein distance for fuzzy matching.
- Graph-based relationship mapping (e.g., IP → Domain → Malware Family → Threat Actor).

---

### 🔹 Stage 4: ENRICHMENT (Enrich)

**Definition:** Adding contextual metadata to raw indicators to elevate them to actionable intelligence.

**Enrichment Sources:**
| Indicator Type | Enrichment Service | Context Added |
|---|---|---|
| IP Address | MaxMind GeoIP, WHOIS, AbuseIPDB | Geolocation, ASN, registration date, abuse reports |
| Domain | VirusTotal, PassiveTotal, URLVoid | Reputation, WHOIS history, resolutions |
| File Hash | VirusTotal, Hybrid Analysis, MalwareBazaar | Malware family, sandbox detonation results, AV detection count |
| URL | Google Safe Browsing, PhishTank | Phishing status, screenshot, page rank |
| CVE | NVD, Exploit-DB, CISA KEV Catalog | CVSS score, exploit availability, known exploitation |

**Why this matters:** A raw IP `1.2.3.4` is just a number. After enrichment, it becomes: *"IP 1.2.3.4 is hosted in AS12345 (Example Hosting), located in Bucharest, Romania, has been reported 3,402 times on AbuseIPDB, and is a known C2 server for the **APT28** group targeting government entities."* This is **actionable intelligence**.

---

### 🔹 Stage 5: ANALYSIS & SCORING (Expose)

**Definition:** Applying analytical techniques to assess the **relevance, severity, confidence, and risk** of each threat to the specific organization.

**Scoring Models:**
- **Source Reliability Rating:** A–F scale (used in military intelligence, adapted to cyber).
- **Information Credibility:** 1–6 scale.
- **CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System):** 0.0–10.0 severity score.
- **MITRE ATT&CK Mapping:** Linking IoCs to adversary Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures.
- **Risk-Based Prioritization:** Scoring is adjusted based on the organization's industry, geography, and asset criticality.

**Analytical Methods:**
- **Pattern Analysis:** Identifying recurring TTPs.
- **Anomaly Detection:** Detecting deviations from baseline behavior.
- **Hypothesis-Driven Analysis:** "If APT29 is targeting healthcare, then..." → proactive hunt.
- **Diamond Model of Intrusion Analysis:** Linking **Adversary, Capability, Infrastructure, Victim**.

---

### 🔹 Stage 6: DISSEMINATION & ACTION (Share)

**Definition:** Distributing the finalized intelligence to consuming systems and personnel.

**Dissemination Channels:**
- **Machine-to-Machine:** STIX/TAXII push to SIEM, SOAR, EDR, Firewall, NAC.
- **Human-to-Human:** Dashboards, reports, email alerts, threat briefs for executives.
- **Community Sharing:** Contributing IoCs back to ISACs, MISP communities, or sectoral sharing groups.

**Why this matters:** Intelligence that is not **actioned** is **useless**. The TIP must integrate with defensive controls to block, detect, or hunt.

---

## 2.2 KTU Reference Sheet — TIP Feature Comparison Matrix

| Feature | MISP (Open Source) | ThreatConnect | Anomali | Recorded Future | OpenCTI |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **License** | Open Source (AGPL) | Commercial | Commercial | Commercial | Open Source (Apache 2.0) |
| **STIX 2.1 Support** | ✅ Full | ✅ Full | ✅ Full | ✅ Full | ✅ Full |
| **TAXII 2.1 Server/Client** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Native Feed Aggregation** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Graph Visualization** | Limited | ✅ Advanced | ✅ Advanced | ✅ Advanced | ✅ Excellent (Neo4j-based) |
| **MITRE ATT&CK Integration** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **SOAR/SIEM Integration** | Via API/Taxii | Native | Native | Native | Via API |
| **Collaborative Sharing** | ✅ Strong (MISP Communities) | ✅ | ✅ | Limited | ✅ |
| **YARA Rule Management** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
| **AI/ML Analytics** | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (Leading) | Limited |
| **Deployment** | On-Prem/Cloud | Cloud/On-Prem | Cloud/On-Prem | Cloud | On-Prem/Cloud |
| **Best For** | Academia, Gov, SMEs | Enterprise SOC | Enterprise SOC | Strategic Intelligence | Research, CTI Teams |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** For exam purposes, **remember MISP and OpenCTI** as the two most cited open-source TIPs. **ThreatConnect and Anomali** are the most cited commercial TIPs. **Recorded Future** is the leader in **machine-learning-driven strategic intelligence**.

---

## 2.3 Key Architectural Components of a TIP

A TIP architecture typically consists of **five functional layers**:

1. **Ingestion Layer** — APIs, TAXII clients, email parsers, STIX pull, file uploads.
2. **Processing Layer** — STIX/TAXII parsers, deduplication engines, validators.
3. **Storage Layer** — Threat data lake, graph database (Neo4j, JanusGraph), relational store.
4. **Analytics Layer** — Correlation engine, ML models, ATT&CK mapper, scoring module.
5. **Dissemination Layer** — TAXII server, API gateway, SIEM/SOAR connectors, dashboards.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of TIPs in Engineering Fields

- **Banking & Finance:** Anti-fraud, anti-phishing, ATM malware tracking.
- **Healthcare (Hospitals, MedTech):** Ransomware early warning, medical device threat monitoring.
- **Critical Infrastructure (Power, Water, Transport):** ICS/SCADA threat intel, nation-state actor tracking.
- **E-Commerce & Retail:** Card-skimming detection, account takeover prevention.
- **Government & Defense:** Nation-state APT tracking, classified intelligence fusion.
- **Academia & Research:** Open-source CTI platforms (MISP, OpenCTI) used in 100+ universities globally.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** A TIP is a **distributed systems engineering problem** at heart — handling high-velocity data ingestion (millions of IoCs/day), schema evolution (STIX 2.0 → 2.1), graph-based relationship storage, and real-time push to security controls. It is a prime example of **event-driven architecture** in cybersecurity.

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Operational Walkthrough

Since this is a cybersecurity topic (not a mathematics topic requiring algebraic derivations), Section 3 will present an **exhaustive, step-by-step operational analysis** of how a TIP processes a real-world threat, mirroring the analytical depth required for a 14-mark KTU question.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Case Study: Tracking an APT Campaign End-to-End

**Scenario:** A mid-sized financial enterprise's SOC has deployed a TIP (e.g., MISP + OpenCTI). A new phishing campaign is targeting Indian banks using malicious LNK files.

---

### 🔸 STEP 1: Initial Ingestion

The TIP receives a raw IoC from **three independent sources** within 2 hours:

**Source A — Internal SIEM Alert (JSON format):**
```json
{"alert": "Suspicious LNK execution", "file": "Invoice_2024.lnk",
 "hash_md5": "5d41402abc4b2a76b9719d911017c592", "host": "FIN-PC-089"}
```

**Source B — External OSINT Feed (CSV format):**
```
indicator_type,value,first_seen,source
sha256,a3f5...,2024-10-15,PhishTank
domain,secure-update-bank.in,2024-10-15,PhishTank
ipv4,185.220.101.42,2024-10-15,PhishTank
```

**Source C — ISAC Share (STIX 2.1 format):**
```json
{"type": "bundle", "objects": [{"type": "indicator",
  "spec_version": "2.1", "pattern_type": "stix",
  "pattern": "[file:hashes.MD5 = '5d41402abc4b2a76b9719d911017c592']"}]}
```

**Observation:** Three different formats (JSON, CSV, STIX). The TIP must normalize all of them.

---

### 🔸 STEP 2: Normalization into STIX 2.1

The TIP's processing layer converts all data into a uniform STIX 2.1 bundle:

```json
{
  "type": "bundle",
  "id": "bundle--5d009001-9999-...",
  "objects": [
    {"type": "indicator", "id": "indicator--001",
     "pattern": "[file:hashes.MD5 = '5d41402abc4b2a76b9719d911017c592']",
     "labels": ["malicious-activity"]},
    {"type": "indicator", "id": "indicator--002",
     "pattern": "[domain-name:value = 'secure-update-bank.in']",
     "labels": ["phishing"]},
    {"type": "indicator", "id": "indicator--003",
     "pattern": "[ipv4-addr:value = '185.220.101.42']",
     "labels": ["malicious-activity"]}
  ]
}
```

**Why this conversion?**
- **Interoperability:** Other tools (SIEM, EDR, Firewall) consuming the data can parse it without custom code.
- **Versioning:** STIX is maintained by OASIS, ensuring long-term schema stability.
- **Relationship Modeling:** STIX supports SDOs (Domain Objects) and SROs (Relationship Objects), enabling graph-based intelligence.

---

### 🔸 STEP 3: Deduplication & Correlation

The TIP detects that the MD5 hash from Source A is **identical** to a hash already present from Source C (received 30 minutes ago from FS-ISAC).

**Logic Applied:**
- Generate a canonical hash of the indicator value + type.
- Check against the existing indicator store (e.g., Elasticsearch or PostgreSQL with unique constraint).
- If duplicate, **increment the occurrence counter** and **raise the confidence score** (e.g., from 60 → 85).
- Create an SRO (STIX Relationship Object) linking the new indicator to the existing campaign cluster.

**Resulting STIX Relationship:**
```json
{"type": "relationship", "id": "relationship--010",
 "relationship_type": "indicates",
 "source_ref": "indicator--001",
 "target_ref": "malware--LNKLoader-2024"}
```

This indicates: *"The MD5 hash is associated with the malware family 'LNKLoader-2024'."*

---

### 🔸 STEP 4: Enrichment

The TIP queries multiple enrichment services in parallel:

| Indicator | Enrichment Service | Result |
|---|---|---|
| `185.220.101.42` | **MaxMind GeoIP** | Geolocation: Frankfurt, Germany |
| `185.220.101.42` | **AbuseIPDB** | 1,287 abuse reports, confidence 92% |
| `185.220.101.42` | **VirusTotal** | 28/72 AV engines flag as malicious |
| `secure-update-bank.in` | **WHOIS** | Registered: 2024-10-12 (3 days old — red flag) |
| `secure-update-bank.in` | **URLVoid** | 6/30 engines flag as phishing |
| `5d41402abc4b2a76b9719d911017c592` | **Hybrid Analysis** | Sandbox detonation shows PowerShell download of `update.exe` from C2 |

**Key Enrichment Insight:** The 3-day-old domain registration (a "young domain") is a strong indicator of a **throwaway phishing infrastructure**, a common pattern in **commodity cybercrime** rather than a sophisticated APT.

---

### 🔸 STEP 5: Analysis, Scoring & ATT&CK Mapping

The TIP's analytics layer applies a scoring algorithm:

**Confidence Score Calculation (Illustrative Formula):**

$$
\text{Confidence} = \left( S_{\text{source}} \times 0.3 \right) + \left( N_{\text{corroborating\_sources}} \times 0.2 \right) + \left( R_{\text{reputation}} \times 0.3 \right) + \left( A_{\text{age}} \times 0.2 \right)
$$

Where:
- $S_{\text{source}}$ = Source reliability (0–1, e.g., FS-ISAC = 0.95)
- $N_{\text{corroborating\_sources}}$ = Number of independent sources (capped at 5)
- $R_{\text{reputation}}$ = Reputation score from VirusTotal/AbuseIPDB (0–1)
- $A_{\text{age}}$ = Age factor (newer domain = higher risk, 0–1)

**Plugging in values for the C2 IP `185.220.101.42`:**
- $S_{\text{source}} = 0.95$ (PhishTank + FS-ISAC)
- $N_{\text{corroborating\_sources}} = 4$ → normalized $= 4/5 = 0.8$
- $R_{\text{reputation}} = 0.92$ (AbuseIPDB)
- $A_{\text{age}} = 0.85$ (recent activity)

$$
\text{Confidence} = \left( 0.95 \times 0.3 \right) + \left( 0.8 \times 0.2 \right) + \left( 0.92 \times 0.3 \right) + \left( 0.85 \times 0.2 \right)
$$

$$
\text{Confidence} = 0.285 + 0.160 + 0.276 + 0.170 = 0.891
$$

**Final Confidence Score: 89/100 → HIGH PRIORITY**

**MITRE ATT&CK Mapping (Performed by TIP):**

| TTP ID | Technique | Evidence in IoC |
|---|---|---|
| **T1566.001** | Phishing: Spearphishing Attachment | `.lnk` file in email |
| **T1059.001** | Command and Scripting: PowerShell | Hybrid Analysis sandbox detonation |
| **T1071.001** | Application Layer Protocol: Web | HTTP download from C2 |
| **T1568.002** | Dynamic Resolution: Domain Generation Algorithms | Young, throwaway domain |

**Threat Actor Attribution:** Based on TTPs and infrastructure overlap, the TIP may attribute the campaign to **TA505** or **FIN7** (cybercriminal groups), with a confidence of **Medium** (not yet definitive).

---

### 🔸 STEP 6: Dissemination & Automated Action

The TIP pushes the enriched intelligence via **TAXII 2.1** to:

1. **SIEM (Splunk/Elastic):** Creates correlation rules:
   - *"Alert if any host queries `secure-update-bank.in`"*
2. **EDR (CrowdStrike/SentinelOne):** Pushes hash `5d41402abc4b2a76b9719d911017c592` to blocklist.
3. **Firewall/Proxy:** Blocks egress to `185.220.101.42` and DNS resolution of `secure-update-bank.in`.
4. **SOAR (Cortex XSOAR / Splunk SOAR):** Triggers a playbook:
   - Search last 30 days of email logs for `Invoice_2024.lnk` attachment.
   - If found, quarantine the email, isolate affected host, and notify IR team.
5. **Executive Dashboard:** A threat brief: *"New phishing campaign targeting Indian banks detected. 3 IoCs blocked. 0 internal hosts affected. Attribution: Likely FIN7."*

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — TIP vs. SIEM vs. SOAR vs. EDR

| Capability | TIP | SIEM | SOAR | EDR |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Function** | Threat intel management & sharing | Log aggregation & correlation | Automated response orchestration | Endpoint detection & response |
| **Data Type** | IoCs, TTPs, threat actor profiles | Logs, events, alerts | Playbooks, cases, tickets | Endpoint telemetry, process data |
| **STIX/TAXII** | ✅ Native | ❌ (consumes) | ❌ (consumes) | ❌ (consumes) |
| **Proactive vs. Reactive** | Proactive + Reactive | Mostly Reactive | Reactive (automated) | Reactive |
| **Scope** | External + Internal threat landscape | Internal IT environment | Internal response workflows | Endpoints only |
| **Typical Vendor** | MISP, ThreatConnect, Anomali | Splunk, QRadar, Elastic | Cortex XSOAR, Splunk SOAR | CrowdStrike, SentinelOne |
| **Relationship to TIP** | Feeds IoCs → | SIEM correlates IoCs → | SOAR acts on SIEM alerts → | EDR enforces on endpoints |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Distinction (Board-Favorite Question):**
> A **TIP does NOT replace** SIEM, SOAR, or EDR. Instead, it **feeds them**. The TIP is the **intelligence layer**; the others are the **operational layers**.

# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 TIP Functional Architecture — Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[External Threat Feeds<br>OSINT ISAC Commercial Gov] --> B[Ingestion Layer<br>TAXII Client API Email Parser]
    C[Internal Sources<br>SIEM EDR Firewall IDS Honeypot] --> B
    B --> D[Processing Layer<br>STIX Normalization Parser]
    D --> E[Deduplication Engine<br>Hash Match Fuzzy Match]
    E --> F[Correlation Engine<br>Graph Builder Relationship Mapper]
    F --> G[Enrichment Layer<br>GeoIP WHOIS VirusTotal Hybrid Analysis]
    G --> H[Analysis and Scoring<br>MITRE ATT&CK CVSS Confidence Model]
    H --> I[Storage Layer<br>Threat Data Lake Graph Database Neo4j]
    I --> J[Dissemination Layer<br>TAXII Server API Gateway]
    J --> K[SIEM and SOAR]
    J --> L[EDR and Firewall]
    J --> M[Executive Dashboards]
    J --> N[Community Sharing ISAC MISP]
    I -.feedback.-> D
    H -.feedback.-> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Data flows from **left (sources)** to **right (consumers)**. The **Storage Layer (I)** acts as the central repository. The **dashed feedback lines** indicate that historical data in storage continuously refines the processing and analysis layers (e.g., for ML-based pattern recognition).

---

## 4.2 TIP Intelligence Lifecycle (Closed-Loop View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[1 Planning and Direction] --> S2[2 Collection]
    S2 --> S3[3 Processing and Normalization]
    S3 --> S4[4 Analysis and Production]
    S4 --> S5[5 Dissemination and Integration]
    S5 --> S6[6 Feedback and Review]
    S6 --> S1
    S2 -.feedback.-> S4
    S4 -.feedback.-> S5
    S5 -.feedback.-> S6
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **classical intelligence cycle** (originally from the US Intelligence Community, adapted for cyber threat intelligence by the KTU syllabus). It is a **closed loop** — feedback from dissemination constantly refines future collection and analysis priorities.

---

## 4.3 STIX 2.1 Domain Object Relationship Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TA[Threat Actor] -->|targets| VICT[Victim Identity]
    TA -->|uses| TT[Tactics Techniques Procedures]
    TA -->|uses| INFRA[Infrastructure]
    INFRA -->|hosts| MAL[Malware]
    INFRA -->|communicates_with| DOM[Domain Name]
    INFRA -->|hosts_on| IP[IPv4 Address]
    MAL -->|communicates_with| C2[C2 Server]
    TT -->|exploits| VULN[Vulnerability CVE]
    MAL -->|indicates| CAMP[Campaign]
    TA -->|attributed_to| CAMP
    CAMP -->|targets| VICT
    CAMP -->|uses| TT
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** STIX 2.1 defines **18 STIX Domain Objects (SDOs)** and **2 STIX Relationship Objects (SROs)**. The most frequently tested are: **Attack Pattern, Campaign, Identity, Indicator, Infrastructure, Malware, Threat Actor, Tool, Vulnerability**. Master these for the exam.

# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]** Define a Threat Intelligence Platform (TIP). List any **four** core functions of a TIP.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Threat Intelligence Platform (TIP)** is a centralized software solution that automates the collection, normalization, deduplication, enrichment, analysis, and sharing of cyber threat data, transforming raw indicators into actionable intelligence.

**Four core functions (½ mark each):**
1. **Aggregation:** Ingesting threat data from multiple internal and external sources.
2. **Normalization:** Converting heterogeneous data into a standard format (e.g., STIX 2.1).
3. **Enrichment:** Adding context (geo, reputation, malware family) to raw indicators.
4. **Dissemination:** Pushing actionable intelligence to SIEM, SOAR, EDR, and firewalls via TAXII/APIs.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]** Differentiate between **STIX** and **TAXII** protocols with one key point each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | STIX (Structured Threat Information eXpression) | TAXII (Trusted Automated eXchange of Indicator Information) |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | A **language/format** to represent threat information | A **transport protocol** to exchange threat information |
| **Function** | Defines *what* the threat data looks like (JSON schema) | Defines *how* the data is transmitted (over HTTPS) |
| **Analogy** | A standardized medical report format | The postal service that delivers the medical report |

**Key point each (1 mark):** STIX is the **content standard**; TAXII is the **delivery mechanism**. Both are OASIS standards.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **confuse TIP with SIEM** in long-answer questions. Remember: **TIP = intelligence layer; SIEM = log/event layer**. TIP feeds intelligence TO the SIEM, not the other way around.
> - When listing functions, do NOT just write "collects data". Use **verbs** like *aggregates, normalizes, deduplicates, enriches, scores, disseminates*. Examiners award marks for **specific terminology**.
> - When asked about STIX/TAXII, always mention the **OASIS standardization body**. Marks are lost if this is omitted.
> - For architecture questions, **draw the block diagram** even if not explicitly asked. It demonstrates conceptual clarity and often earns 1–2 extra marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**(a)** Explain the **six-stage lifecycle of cyber threat intelligence** as implemented in a TIP. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare and contrast **MISP and OpenCTI** as open-source Threat Intelligence Platforms. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Six-Stage Threat Intelligence Lifecycle (7 marks)**

The TIP implements the following six-stage cycle, often called the **Intelligence Cycle**:

1. **Planning & Direction (1 mark):** Defining intelligence requirements based on the organization's risk profile, industry threats, and business priorities. *Example:* "Track ransomware groups targeting Indian banking sector."

2. **Collection (1 mark):** Gathering raw data from internal sources (SIEM, EDR, logs) and external sources (OSINT, ISACs, commercial feeds, government advisories like CERT-In).

3. **Processing & Normalization (1 mark):** Parsing, validating, and converting heterogeneous data into a unified format, typically **STIX 2.1**. Includes deduplication.

4. **Analysis & Production (1.5 marks):** Enriching indicators with context (GeoIP, WHOIS, malware family), mapping to **MITRE ATT&CK** TTPs, scoring confidence and severity, and linking to threat actors/campaigns.

5. **Dissemination & Integration (1.5 marks):** Sharing finished intelligence via **TAXII 2.1** to SIEM, SOAR, EDR, firewalls, and to human stakeholders via dashboards and reports.

6. **Feedback & Review (1 mark):** Measuring effectiveness (MTTD, MTTR, false positive rate), updating intelligence requirements, and refining collection priorities — closing the loop.

---

**(b) MISP vs. OpenCTI Comparison (7 marks)**

| Parameter | MISP (Malware Information Sharing Platform) | OpenCTI (Open Cyber Threat Intelligence) |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin & Maintainer** | CIRCL (Computer Incident Response Center Luxembourg), since 2012 | ANSSI (French National Cybersecurity Agency) & Luatix, since 2018 |
| **Data Model** | Custom MISP core format + STIX 1.x/2.x support | Native STIX 2.1 (first-class citizen) |
| **Storage Backend** | MySQL/MariaDB | Elasticsearch + MinIO (object storage) + RabbitMQ |
| **Graph Visualization** | Basic correlation graphs | Advanced graph visualization (Neo4j/GraphQL) |
| **Strengths (1 mark each)** | Mature, widely deployed, strong community sharing (5000+ MISP instances), excellent for **operational intel sharing** | Modern UI, deep ATT&CK integration, knowledge graph approach, ideal for **strategic intelligence** |
| **Weaknesses (½ mark each)** | UI is dated, graph view is limited | Newer, smaller community, steeper learning curve |
| **Best Use Case** | Incident response, malware sharing, ISAC collaboration | Threat research, adversary tracking, strategic CTI |
| **License** | AGPL v3 (Open Source) | Apache 2.0 (Open Source) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** MISP is preferred for **operational/tactical** intel sharing; OpenCTI is preferred for **strategic** intelligence with deep relationship analysis. Many organizations deploy **both** in a complementary architecture.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Question — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Describe the **architecture of a Threat Intelligence Platform** with a neat block diagram. Explain the function of each layer. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the role of **STIX 2.1 and TAXII 2.1** in enabling threat intelligence sharing. List at least **four STIX Domain Objects (SDOs)** with examples. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**(a) TIP Architecture (7 marks)**

*(1 mark for diagram, 6 marks for explanation)*

A TIP is organized into **five functional layers** (refer to the block diagram from Section 4.1):

1. **Ingestion Layer (1 mark):** The entry point. Pulls data from external feeds (TAXII, RSS, APIs) and internal sources (SIEM, EDR). Uses **scheduled polling** and **event-driven webhook** mechanisms.

2. **Processing Layer (1 mark):** Performs **STIX/TAXII parsing**, schema validation, data sanitization, and **normalization** into a canonical format.

3. **Storage Layer (1.5 marks):** A **threat data lake** combining relational (PostgreSQL for indicators), graph (Neo4j for relationships), and document stores (Elasticsearch for full-text search). Must support **high-write throughput** (millions of IoCs/day).

4. **Analytics Layer (2 marks):** The "brain" of the TIP. Performs **deduplication, correlation, enrichment, scoring, and MITRE ATT&CK mapping**. May include **ML-based anomaly detection** and **threat actor attribution algorithms**.

5. **Dissemination Layer (1.5 marks):** Pushes intelligence to consumers via **TAXII 2.1 server, REST APIs, and pre-built connectors** (Splunk, Elastic, Palo Alto, CrowdStrike). Also serves **human-readable dashboards** and **executive reports**.

---

**(b) STIX 2.1 and TAXII 2.1 (7 marks)**

**STIX 2.1 (4 marks):**
- **Full form:** Structured Threat Information eXpression.
- **Maintained by:** OASIS Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) Technical Committee.
- **Format:** JSON-based (human-readable + machine-parsable).
- **Components:**
  - **STIX Domain Objects (SDOs):** 18 types representing "things" (e.g., Attack Pattern, Campaign, Malware, Threat Actor, Indicator, Vulnerability, Identity, Infrastructure, Tool).
  - **STIX Relationship Objects (SROs):** `relationship` and `sighting` — connecting SDOs.
  - **STIX Cyber-observable Objects (SCOs):** Low-level technical details (IP, domain, file hash, URL, email address).
  - **STIX Patterning:** A query-like syntax to express IoC patterns (e.g., `[ipv4-addr:value = '1.2.3.4']`).
- **Version:** STIX 2.1 is the current standard (STIX 1.x used XML and is deprecated).

**Four STIX SDOs with examples (1 mark each = 4 marks from this section):**

| SDO | Example |
|---|---|
| **Indicator** | "IP address 1.2.3.4 is a C2 server" |
| **Malware** | "Emotet" — trojan banking malware family |
| **Threat Actor** | "APT28" (Fancy Bear) — Russian state-sponsored group |
| **Campaign** | "Operation GhostWriter" — 2024 disinformation campaign |
| **Vulnerability** | "CVE-2024-3400" — Palo Alto PAN-OS command injection |
| **Attack Pattern** | "T1566.001" — Spearphishing Attachment |

**TAXII 2.1 (3 marks):**
- **Full form:** Trusted Automated eXchange of Indicator Information.
- **Purpose:** Defines the **transport mechanism** for sharing STIX data.
- **Transport:** HTTPS (RESTful API, JSON).
- **Two main services:**
  - **Collection:** A logical grouping of threat data that producers publish and consumers request.
  - **Channel:** Supports producer-to-consumer push (subscribed) and pull (on-demand).
- **Versions:** TAXII 1.x (XML-based, paired with STIX 1.x) and TAXII 2.1 (JSON-based, paired with STIX 2.1). Always use the **2.1 pair** in modern deployments.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Must-Memorize Points:**

- ✅ **TIP Definition:** Centralized platform for aggregating, normalizing, enriching, and sharing threat data.
- ✅ **TIP does NOT replace SIEM/SOAR/EDR** — it **feeds** them.
- ✅ **Six-stage Intelligence Cycle:** Planning → Collection → Processing → Analysis → Dissemination → Feedback.
- ✅ **STIX = content language (what)** ; **TAXII = transport protocol (how)**. Both by **OASIS**.
- ✅ **STIX 2.1 format:** JSON. **STIX 1.x:** XML (deprecated).
- ✅ **18 STIX Domain Objects (SDOs).** Most important: Indicator, Malware, Threat Actor, Campaign, Vulnerability, Attack Pattern, Infrastructure, Identity, Tool.
- ✅ **Two SROs:** `relationship` and `sighting`.
- ✅ **MISP:** Open source, mature, best for **operational** sharing, strong community.
- ✅ **OpenCTI:** Open source, modern, best for **strategic** intelligence, advanced graph view.
- ✅ **Anomali, ThreatConnect:** Leading commercial TIPs.
- ✅ **Recorded Future:** Leader in **AI/ML-driven strategic intelligence**.
- ✅ **Confidence scoring formula example** (memorize the weighted structure): $C = 0.3 S + 0.2 N + 0.3 R + 0.2 A$.
- ✅ **Enrichment sources:** MaxMind, VirusTotal, WHOIS, AbuseIPDB, Hybrid Analysis, NVD, MITRE ATT&CK.
- ✅ **Key metrics:** MTTD, MTTR, False Positive Rate, IoC Coverage Ratio.
- ✅ **MITRE ATT&CK** is a **TTP-based** framework, not just a list of malware. Link every IoC to a TTP for full marks.
- ✅ **MISP = MySQL backend; OpenCTI = Elasticsearch + MinIO + RabbitMQ backend.**
- ✅ For KTU exams: always **draw a block diagram** in architecture questions — it earns 1–2 bonus marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"TIP = Intelligence Layer. SIEM = Detection Layer. SOAR = Response Layer. EDR = Endpoint Layer. STIX = What. TAXII = How. MITRE ATT&CK = Why."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Threat Intelligence Frameworks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT755 - CYBER THREAT INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Threat Intelligence Frameworks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Threat Intelligence Frameworks — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threat Intelligence Frameworks** are standardized, structured methodologies, taxonomies, and knowledge bases used by cybersecurity professionals to systematically collect, organize, analyze, classify, and share information about cyber threats, adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs). They provide a **common language** and a **reusable mental model** that transforms raw indicators (IPs, hashes, domains) into actionable, contextualized intelligence.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (Course Outcome **CO1**: *Understand the fundamental concepts of threat intelligence and its life cycle*), frameworks are the **backbone of structured threat analysis**, enabling analysts to move from *data* → *information* → *intelligence* → *action*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Detective's Case Notebook"

Imagine a city detective investigating a serial criminal. Instead of writing random clues on loose papers, the detective uses:

- A **standardized case file** (so any other officer can read it) → *MITRE ATT&CK*
- A **timeline of how the crime unfolded** (from planning to escape) → *Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain*
- A **profile card** describing the criminal, the victim, the weapon, and the location → *Diamond Model*
- A **public alert bulletin** system shared with other cities → *STIX/TAXII*
- A **government crime-prevention policy checklist** → *NIST CSF*

> [!NOTE]
> Without these structures, every analyst would "re-invent the wheel," and adversaries could repeat the same attack across organizations undetected.

## 1.3 Why Are Frameworks Necessary?

| Problem in Raw CTI | How Frameworks Solve It |
|---|---|
| Data overload, no context | Structured taxonomies (ATT&CK) add *context* |
| Inconsistent terminology | Provide a *common vocabulary* (TTPs, IoCs) |
| Siloed intelligence | Enable *automated sharing* (STIX/TAXII) |
| Reactive defense | Enable *proactive hunting* (Kill Chain) |
| Attribution confusion | Multi-dimensional analysis (Diamond Model) |

## 1.4 Classification of Threat Intelligence Frameworks

Frameworks can be grouped into **four major families** based on their primary function:

1. **Adversary Behavior Frameworks** — describe *how* attackers operate.
   - MITRE ATT&CK
   - Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain
   - MITRE D3FEND
2. **Analytical / Intrusion Analysis Frameworks** — describe *relationships* between threat elements.
   - Diamond Model of Intrusion Analysis
   - Kill Chain (Unified / Integrated)
3. **Sharing & Communication Frameworks** — describe *how* intelligence is exchanged.
   - STIX (Structured Threat Information eXpression)
   - TAXII (Trusted Automated Exchange of Intelligence Information)
   - OpenIOC, MISP
4. **Strategic / Risk Management Frameworks** — align threat intel with *business risk*.
   - NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF)
   - ISO/IEC 27001 / 27035
   - OWASP Threat Intelligence Maturity Model
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Framework 1 — MITRE ATT&CK (Adversarial Tactics, Techniques, and Common Knowledge)

### 2.1.1 Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MITRE ATT&CK** is a globally accessible, curated **knowledge base of adversary behavior** based on real-world observations. It catalogs the *tactics* (the "why") and *techniques* (the "how") that adversaries use at each stage of an attack lifecycle.

### 2.1.2 Core Structural Components

- **Tactics** — The adversary's *technical goal* (e.g., *Initial Access*, *Persistence*, *Lateral Movement*).
- **Techniques** — *How* the tactical goal is achieved (e.g., *Phishing T1566*, *Valid Accounts T1078*).
- **Sub-techniques** — More specific descriptions of a technique (e.g., T1566.001 Spearphishing Attachment).
- **Procedures** — Specific *real-world implementations* documented by threat actors.
- **Groups** — Named APT clusters (e.g., *APT29 / Cozy Bear*).
- **Software** — Malware/tools used (e.g., *Cobalt Strike, Mimikatz*).
- **Mitigations** — Defensive actions to prevent a technique.
- **Detections** — Data sources & analytic signatures to spot the technique.

### 2.1.3 ATT&CK Matrices (Domains)

| Matrix | Scope | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Enterprise** | Windows, macOS, Linux, Cloud (Azure AD, Office 365, GCP, AWS), Containers, Network | Most enterprise SOC/hunting operations |
| **Mobile** | iOS, Android | Mobile device threat hunting |
| **ICS** | Industrial Control Systems | OT/SCADA environments |

### 2.1.4 The 14 Enterprise Tactics (in ATT&CK Order)

1. Reconnaissance
2. Resource Development
3. Initial Access
4. Execution
5. Persistence
6. Privilege Escalation
7. Defense Evasion
8. Credential Access
9. Discovery
10. Lateral Movement
11. Collection
12. Command and Control
13. Exfiltration
14. Impact

> [!NOTE]
> A complete attack is mapped as a *path through tactics*, each with selected techniques. This gives a **multi-dimensional behavioral fingerprint** of an adversary.

---

## 2.2 Framework 2 — Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain®

### 2.2.1 Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Cyber Kill Chain** is a 7-stage model, derived from the military concept of a "kill chain," that describes the *sequential phases* an adversary must complete to achieve their objective. Originally published by Lockheed Martin in 2011, it is a *linear, intelligence-driven* framework.

### 2.2.2 The 7 Phases

| # | Phase | Description | Defender Goal |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Reconnaissance** | Harvesting email addresses, network info | Detect probing |
| 2 | **Weaponization** | Coupling exploit with backdoor (e.g., macro + RAT) | Threat intel sharing |
| 3 | **Delivery** | Email, USB, web (transmitting weapon) | Email/web filtering |
| 4 | **Exploitation** | Triggering the vulnerability | Patch, AV |
| 5 | **Installation** | Installing malware/persistence | EDR, application whitelisting |
| 6 | **Command & Control (C2)** | Beacon to attacker server | Network monitoring |
| 7 | **Actions on Objectives** | Data exfiltration, destruction | DLP, behavioral analytics |

> [!NOTE]
> The model's strength is its **sequential logic** — defenders only need to **break one link** to stop the attack.

### 2.2.3 Critical Limitation (Exam Favorite!)

The original Kill Chain is **too linear and over-simplified** for modern adaptive adversaries. It fails to model:
- Internal lateral movement (post-breach)
- Multi-stage malware (e.g., dropper → loader → payload)
- Insider threats
- Supply-chain compromises

This led to the **Unified Kill Chain** (18 stages) by Paul Pols in 2017.

---

## 2.3 Framework 3 — The Diamond Model of Intrusion Analysis

### 2.3.1 Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Diamond Model**, formalized by Sergio Caltagirone, Andrew Pendergast, and Christopher Betz in 2013, postulates that every intrusion event can be described by **four interconnected core features**: **Adversary, Infrastructure, Capability, Victim** — connected by **Social-Political** and **Technology** meta-features.

### 2.3.2 The Four Core Vertices

| Vertex | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Adversary** | The threat actor / APT group | APT28 (Fancy Bear) |
| **Infrastructure** | IPs, domains, C2 servers, email addresses used | 192.0.2.45, evil-c2[.]com |
| **Capability** | Tools, malware, exploits, techniques | Mimikatz, CVE-2017-0144 (EternalBlue) |
| **Victim** | Target organization, sector, assets | Energy sector in Eastern Europe |

### 2.3.3 Meta-Features

- **Meta-Feature: Social-Political** — *Why* the adversary is targeting the victim (geopolitical, ideological, financial).
- **Meta-Feature: Technology** — The *technical relationship* between infrastructure and capability (e.g., HTTPS vs HTTP, IPv4 vs Domain).

### 2.3.4 Analytical Power
The Diamond Model forces the analyst to ask **at least one question per vertex**, producing a complete intrusion picture. Two intrusions with overlapping vertices (e.g., same C2 IP + same malware hash) can be **clustered** into the same *activity thread*.

---

## 2.4 Framework 4 — NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF)

### 2.4.1 Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **NIST CSF** is a **policy/management framework** (not a tactical one) that organizes cybersecurity activities into **5 Functions**, helping organizations align threat intelligence with **business risk management**.

### 2.4.2 The 5 Core Functions

| Function | Description | Threat Intel Link |
|---|---|---|
| **Identify** | Asset, system, and risk inventory | Asset-based threat modeling |
| **Protect** | Controls (firewalls, MFA, training) | Maps to ATT&CK mitigations |
| **Detect** | Anomaly and event detection | ATT&CK detections, IoCs |
| **Respond** | Incident response, containment, communication | Threat-informed IR playbooks |
| **Recover** | Restoration and lessons learned | Post-incident intel feedback |

---

## 2.5 Framework 5 — STIX & TAXII (Sharing Standards)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STIX (Structured Threat Information eXpression)** is a **language and serialization format** (JSON-based) for representing threat intel. **TAXII (Trusted Automated Exchange of Intelligence Information)** is the **transport protocol** for sharing STIX data over HTTPS.

### 2.5.1 STIX Domain Objects (SDOs)

- `indicator` — Pattern to detect malicious activity
- `malware` — Malware instance
- `threat-actor` — Adversary profile
- `campaign` — Grouping of incidents
- `attack-pattern` — ATT&CK-style technique
- `vulnerability` — CVE reference
- `identity`, `tool`, `infrastructure`, `relationship`

### 2.5.2 TAXII Collections & Channels
- **Collections** — Logical groupings of CTI (e.g., "FIN7 IoCs").
- **Channels** — Publish-subscribe feeds.
- **API Roots** — Logical grouping of collections.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Comparison Table

| Framework | Type | Primary Purpose | Origin | Year |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cyber Kill Chain** | Process / Lifecycle | Sequential attack phases | Lockheed Martin | 2011 |
| **Diamond Model** | Analytical | Relationship-based intrusion clustering | UMD / USMA | 2013 |
| **MITRE ATT&CK** | Knowledge Base | Catalog of adversary TTPs | MITRE Corp. | 2013 (released 2015) |
| **NIST CSF** | Risk Management | Strategic alignment | NIST (US Gov) | 2014 (v1), 2024 (v2) |
| **STIX/TAXII** | Sharing Standard | Machine-readable intel exchange | OASIS / DHS | 2014/2017 (STIX 2.0, 2.1) |
| **MISP** | Sharing Platform | Open-source intel sharing | CIRCL | 2012 |
| **MITRE D3FEND** | Knowledge Base | Defensive countermeasures | MITRE | 2021 |
| **Unified Kill Chain** | Process | 18-stage modernized kill chain | Paul Pols | 2017 |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SOC Analysts** use ATT&CK to write detection rules (Sigma, YARA, Splunk queries).
- **Threat Hunters** pivot on Diamond Model vertices to cluster activity.
- **CTI Teams** use STIX/TAXII to automate sharing between organizations and ISACs.
- **CISOs** use NIST CSF to communicate risk to executive boards in business language.
- **Red Teams** map their emulation plans to ATT&CK (e.g., using MITRE's *Caldera* or *Atomic Red Team*).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Framework Mapping Exercise

## 3.1 Analytical Case Study: "Spearphishing with Macro RAT"

A finance employee receives a *password-protected invoice.pdf* via email. Opening it triggers an Office macro that downloads **Cobalt Strike Beacon**, which calls home to `c2.malicious[.]com` and moves laterally to the domain controller.

### 3.1.1 Mapping to Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Phase 1 (Reconnaissance)} &\Rightarrow \text{OSINT on finance employee (LinkedIn, social)} \\
\text{Phase 2 (Weaponization)} &\Rightarrow \text{Invoice.pdf + VBA macro + Cobalt Strike loader} \\
\text{Phase 3 (Delivery)} &\Rightarrow \text{Email with attachment} \\
\text{Phase 4 (Exploitation)} &\Rightarrow \text{Macro execution on unpatched Office} \\
\text{Phase 5 (Installation)} &\Rightarrow \text{Beacon persistence via scheduled task} \\
\text{Phase 6 (C2)} &\Rightarrow \text{HTTPS beacon to c2.malicious[.]com} \\
\text{Phase 7 (Actions on Objectives)} &\Rightarrow \text{Lateral movement + data theft}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this works:** The kill chain assigns *one* phase to each action and forces the defender to break the chain (e.g., a secure email gateway stops Phase 3).

---

### 3.1.2 Mapping to MITRE ATT&CK

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tactic: Initial Access} &\Rightarrow \text{T1566.001 Spearphishing Attachment} \\
\text{Tactic: Execution} &\Rightarrow \text{T1204.002 User Execution: Malicious File} \\
\text{Tactic: Persistence} &\Rightarrow \text{T1053.005 Scheduled Task} \\
\text{Tactic: Defense Evasion} &\Rightarrow \text{T1027 Obfuscated Files or Information} \\
\text{Tactic: C2} &\Rightarrow \text{T1071.001 Web Protocols} \\
\text{Tactic: Lateral Movement} &\Rightarrow \text{T1021.002 SMB/Windows Admin Shares} \\
\text{Tactic: Exfiltration} &\Rightarrow \text{T1041 Exfiltration Over C2 Channel}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this is more powerful:** Each technique has *associated* detection content, mitigations, and adversary examples, enabling **defense-in-depth** across multiple stages.

---

### 3.1.3 Mapping to the Diamond Model

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Adversary} &= \text{Unattributed financially-motivated threat actor} \\
\textbf{Infrastructure} &= \text{c2.malicious[.]com, sender IP 203.0.113.42} \\
\textbf{Capability} &= \text{VBA macro, Cobalt Strike Beacon (SHA256: a1b2...)} \\
\textbf{Victim} &= \text{Finance department, mid-size enterprise} \\
\textbf{Meta: Socio-Political} &= \text{Financial gain / ransomware pre-stage} \\
\textbf{Meta: Technology} &= \text{HTTPS over port 443, IPv4 C2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The same intrusion *automatically* links to any other incident sharing ≥1 vertex. This is called an **Activity Thread** in Diamond Model terminology.

---

## 3.2 Comparative Strength Analysis (Mathematical Scoring Model)

For each framework, we assign a normalized score (1–5) across five dimensions. The total **effectiveness score** is:

$$
S_{framework} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot s_i
$$

where $w_i$ is the dimension weight (sums to 1.0) and $s_i$ is the framework's score (1–5).

### 3.2.1 Weight Selection (Industry Standard Heuristic)

$$
w_{BehavioralDepth} = 0.30
$$

$$
w_{AnalyticalClarity} = 0.25
$$

$$
w_{Shareability} = 0.20
$$

$$
w_{StrategicAlignment} = 0.15
$$

$$
w_{EaseOfAdoption} = 0.10
$$

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i = 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.10 = 1.00
$$

### 3.2.2 Score Matrix

| Framework | Behavioral Depth ($s_1$) | Analytical Clarity ($s_2$) | Shareability ($s_3$) | Strategic Alignment ($s_4$) | Ease of Adoption ($s_5$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cyber Kill Chain | 3 | 5 | 2 | 3 | 5 |
| Diamond Model | 3 | 5 | 2 | 2 | 3 |
| MITRE ATT&CK | 5 | 4 | 4 | 3 | 4 |
| NIST CSF | 2 | 3 | 3 | 5 | 4 |
| STIX/TAXII | 2 | 3 | 5 | 2 | 2 |

### 3.2.3 Computation for MITRE ATT&CK

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{ATT\&CK} &= (0.30 \times 5) + (0.25 \times 4) + (0.20 \times 4) + (0.15 \times 3) + (0.10 \times 4) \\
&= 1.50 + 1.00 + 0.80 + 0.45 + 0.40 \\
&= 4.15
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.4 Computation for Cyber Kill Chain

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{KillChain} &= (0.30 \times 3) + (0.25 \times 5) + (0.20 \times 2) + (0.15 \times 3) + (0.10 \times 5) \\
&= 0.90 + 1.25 + 0.40 + 0.45 + 0.50 \\
&= 3.50
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.5 Computation for Diamond Model

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{Diamond} &= (0.30 \times 3) + (0.25 \times 5) + (0.20 \times 2) + (0.15 \times 2) + (0.10 \times 3) \\
&= 0.90 + 1.25 + 0.40 + 0.30 + 0.30 \\
&= 3.15
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.6 Computation for NIST CSF

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{NIST} &= (0.30 \times 2) + (0.25 \times 3) + (0.20 \times 3) + (0.15 \times 5) + (0.10 \times 4) \\
&= 0.60 + 0.75 + 0.60 + 0.75 + 0.40 \\
&= 3.10
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.7 Computation for STIX/TAXII

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{STIX/TAXII} &= (0.30 \times 2) + (0.25 \times 3) + (0.20 \times 5) + (0.15 \times 2) + (0.10 \times 2) \\
&= 0.60 + 0.75 + 1.00 + 0.30 + 0.20 \\
&= 2.85
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.8 Final Comparative Ranking

$$
\boxed{S_{ATT\&CK} = 4.15 > S_{KillChain} = 3.50 > S_{Diamond} = 3.15 \approx S_{NIST} = 3.10 > S_{STIX/TAXII} = 2.85}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- **ATT&CK dominates** due to unmatched behavioral depth and growing shareability ecosystem.
- **STIX/TAXII** scores low on behavioral depth (it is a *format*, not a *catalog*) but excels in shareability — confirming it complements rather than competes with ATT&CK.
- **NIST CSF** wins on strategic alignment — best for board-level communication.

---

## 3.3 Framework Synergy Model (How They Fit Together)

Modern CTI operations use frameworks **together**, not in isolation:

$$
\text{NIST CSF (Govern)} \rightarrow \text{Kill Chain (Detect)} \rightarrow \text{ATT\&CK (Map)} \rightarrow \text{Diamond (Cluster)} \rightarrow \text{STIX/TAXII (Share)}
$$

This forms a **closed-loop intelligence cycle**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Cyber Threat Intelligence Framework Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    strategicA["Strategic Layer\nNIST CSF | ISO 27001"]
    processA["Process Layer\nLockheed Kill Chain | Unified Kill Chain"]
    behaviorA["Behavioral Layer\nMITRE ATT&CK | MITRE D3FEND"]
    analysisA["Analytical Layer\nDiamond Model | VERIS"]
    shareA["Sharing Layer\nSTIX 2.1 | TAXII 2.1 | MISP"]
    useA["Consumer Use Cases\nSOC | Hunting | IR | Risk"]
    strategicA --> processA
    processA --> behaviorA
    behaviorA --> analysisA
    analysisA --> shareA
    shareA --> useA
    useA -.feedback.-> strategicA
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — MITRE ATT&CK Tactic–Technique Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    adv["Adversary\nAPT Group"] --> t1["Tactic:\nInitial Access"]
    adv --> t2["Tactic:\nPersistence"]
    adv --> t3["Tactic:\nLateral Movement"]
    t1 --> tech1["Technique:\nT1566 Phishing"]
    t1 --> tech2["Technique:\nT1190 Exploit Public App"]
    t2 --> tech3["Technique:\nT1547 Boot Autostart"]
    t2 --> tech4["Technique:\nT1053 Scheduled Task"]
    t3 --> tech5["Technique:\nT1021 Remote Services"]
    t3 --> tech6["Technique:\nT1570 Lateral Tool Transfer"]
    tech1 --> proc1["Procedure:\nAPT29 spearphish w/ OneDrive link"]
    tech4 --> proc2["Procedure:\nFIN6 scheduled task persistence"]
    tech5 --> proc3["Procedure:\nAPT28 PSExec lateral move"]
```

## 4.3 Diamond Model Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DM["Diamond Model of Intrusion Analysis"]
        AD["ADVERSARY\nWho is attacking?"]
        IN["INFRASTRUCTURE\nIPs, Domains, C2"]
        CA["CAPABILITY\nMalware, Exploits"]
        VI["VICTIM\nTarget Org/Sector"]
    end
    AD <--> CA
    CA <--> IN
    IN <--> VI
    VI <--> AD
    MP["META: SOCIO-POLITICAL\nMotivation / Context"] --- AD
    MP --- VI
    MT["META: TECHNOLOGY\nProtocol / Stack"] --- IN
    MT --- CA
```

## 4.4 STIX/TAXII Sharing Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    producer["Producer\nOrganization SOC"] -->|publish| collectionServer["TAXII 2.1\nCollection Server"]
```
