# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: The Mitigating Risks from Threat Vectors — End-to-End Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Assets and Threat Vectors] --> B[Assess Inherent Risk L times I]
    B --> C{Risk Appetite Threshold Met}
    C -- No --> D[Select Risk Response Strategy]
    C -- Yes --> E[Document and Accept Residual Risk]
    D --> F[Apply Layered Controls Admin Technical Physical]
    F --> G[Compute Residual Risk and ROI]
    G --> H{Control Justified ROI greater than 0}
    H -- Yes --> I[Deploy and Document Controls]
    H -- No --> J[Re-evaluate Strategy or Accept Risk]
    I --> K[Continuous Monitoring SIEM SOAR Threat Hunt]
    K --> L[Periodic Review Annual or Post Incident]
    L --> A
    E --> K
    J --> K
```

**Figure 4.1 — Interpretation:** This flowchart captures the *continuous, cyclical* nature of threat-vector risk mitigation. The most important feature is the **feedback loop** from `K` (Monitor) and `L` (Review) back to `A` (Identify), emphasizing that the process is never "finished". The branch at `C` (Risk Appetite Threshold) demonstrates the *Risk Acceptance* strategy path, and the branch at `H` (Control Justified) is the *Cost-Benefit Decision Gate* that prevents the deployment of financially unsound controls.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Defense-in-Depth Onion Model for Threat Vector Mitigation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph L1[Outer Layer Perimeter Defense]
        D1[Firewalls IDS IPS DDoS Scrubbing]
    end
    subgraph L2[Network Layer Segmentation]
        D2[VLANs Zero Trust NAC Internal Firewalls]
    end
    subgraph L3[Identity Layer Authentication and Authorization]
        D3[MFA RBAC PAM IAM]
    end
    subgraph L4[Host Layer Endpoint Protection]
        D4[EDR Antivirus Application Allow Listing]
    end
    subgraph L5[Application Layer Secure SDLC]
        D5[Input Validation WAF Parameterized Queries]
    end
    subgraph L6[Data Layer Protection]
        D6[Encryption at Rest and In Transit DLP Tokenization]
    end
    subgraph L7[Core Asset]
        ASSET[Critical Data and Systems]
    end
    D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4 --> D5 --> D6 --> ASSET
```

**Figure 4.2 — Interpretation:** This is the classic **Defense-in-Depth** (or "Castle Approach") diagram. The adversary must breach *all six layers* to reach the core asset. Each layer mitigates a *different category* of threat vector:

- L1 blocks *external network-based* vectors (DDoS, port scanning).
- L2 mitigates *lateral movement* vectors after initial breach.
- L3 stops *credential-based* vectors (credential stuffing, privilege escalation).
- L4 catches *malware and endpoint* vectors (RATs, ransomware).
- L5 mitigates *application-layer* vectors (SQLi, XSS, IDOR).
- L6 protects the *data* itself even if all other layers fail (encryption makes stolen data useless).

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Risk Response Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Identified Threat Vector with Inherent Risk] --> Q1{Is Asset Critical to Mission}
    Q1 -- No --> ACC[Risk Acceptance Document and Monitor]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Can Risk Be Eliminated by Stopping the Activity}
    Q2 -- Yes --> AVO[Risk Avoidance Discontinue the Activity]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is Cost of Mitigation Greater than Asset Value}
    Q3 -- Yes --> ACC
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Is Cyber Insurance Available and Cost Effective}
    Q4 -- Yes --> TRA[Risk Transfer Cyber Insurance or MSSP]
    Q4 -- No --> MIT[Risk Reduction Apply Layered Controls]
    MIT --> RES[Compute Residual Risk]
    RES --> Q5{Residual Risk within Appetite}
    Q5 -- Yes --> DONE[Document and Monitor]
    Q5 -- No --> MIT
```

**Figure 4.3 — Interpretation:** This decision tree operationalizes the **four canonical risk responses** (Reduce, Transfer, Avoid, Accept) into a logical algorithm. It is a *highly-valuable diagram to memorize* for KTU board exams because the decision logic mirrors the exact sequence of questions a GRC (Governance, Risk, Compliance) professional asks when evaluating a threat vector.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define a threat vector. Differentiate between threat vector and threat actor with one example each.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]`

**Model Answer:**

A **threat vector** is the *pathway or method* by which an adversary attempts to gain unauthorized access to a system or data. It is the *mechanism* of attack.

A **threat actor** is the *entity (person, group, or organization)* that initiates the attack. It is the *originator* of the attack.

**Example:**
- Threat vector: *Spear-phishing email* containing a malicious attachment.
- Threat actor: *APT-29 (Cozy Bear)*, a nation-state hacking group.

**Differentiation:** The actor (APT-29) *uses* the vector (phishing email) to deliver the payload. Multiple actors can use the same vector, and one actor can use multiple vectors.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for definition of threat vector, 1 mark for definition of threat actor, 1 mark for example + differentiation.)*

### Question A2: List and briefly explain the four canonical risk response strategies.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]`

**Model Answer:**

The four canonical risk response strategies are:

1. **Risk Reduction (Mitigation):** Apply controls (administrative, technical, physical) to lower the likelihood or impact of the threat. *Example: Deploying MFA to reduce credential-theft risk.*
2. **Risk Transfer:** Shift the financial impact to a third party. *Example: Purchasing a cyber insurance policy to cover breach costs.*
3. **Risk Avoidance:** Eliminate the risk by discontinuing the activity that creates it. *Example: Discontinuing use of an unsupported legacy operating system.*
4. **Risk Acceptance:** Formally acknowledge and operate with the residual risk, typically when it falls below the organization's risk appetite. *Example: Accepting the low risk of an employee's USB drive being lost in a small office.*

*(Examiner's Note: 1.5 marks for naming all four, 1.5 marks for the brief explanations with examples.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Defense-in-Depth** strategy for mitigating threat vectors. List any **six layers** with one control per layer. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Defense-in-Depth is a layered security strategy in which multiple independent controls are deployed across different architectural layers so that the failure of any *one* control does not result in total compromise of the asset. The strategy is analogous to a medieval castle with multiple concentric defenses.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Layer 1: Perimeter] → [Layer 2: Network] → [Layer 3: Identity]
       ↓
[Layer 4: Host] → [Layer 5: Application] → [Layer 6: Data]
       ↓
[Critical Asset]
```

**The Six Layers and One Control per Layer:**

| Layer | Purpose | Example Control |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Perimeter Defense** | Block external network-based threat vectors at the edge. | Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW) with deep-packet inspection. |
| **2. Network Segmentation** | Prevent lateral movement if perimeter is breached. | Internal VLANs, Zero-Trust Network Access (ZTNA). |
| **3. Identity & Authentication** | Ensure only authorized users gain access. | Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) on all privileged accounts. |
| **4. Host / Endpoint Protection** | Detect and block malware on user devices. | Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) on all endpoints. |
| **5. Application Layer** | Mitigate code-level attacks (SQLi, XSS). | Web Application Firewall (WAF) + secure SDLC practices. |
| **6. Data Protection** | Protect the data itself even if all other layers fail. | AES-256 encryption at rest and TLS 1.3 encryption in transit. |

**Why it Works:** An attacker must defeat *all six layers* to reach the core asset, exponentially raising the cost and complexity of a successful attack.

---

**Q. (b)** An organization identifies a **ransomware threat vector** targeting its file servers. The asset value is **₹/S 50,00,000**, the exposure factor is **0.6**, and the ransomware event is expected to occur **once every 2 years**. Calculate the **SLE, ARO, and ALE** for this threat vector. If a new EDR solution costing **₹/S 4,00,000 per year** is deployed and reduces the occurrence rate to **once every 10 years** (with the same SLE), determine whether the EDR is financially justified. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Identify Given Data**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AV &= \text{\textcurrency}50{,}00{,}000 \\
EF &= 0.6 \\
ARO_{\text{before}} &= 0.5 \text{ (one event per 2 years)} \\
C_{\text{EDR}} &= \text{\textcurrency}4{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate SLE**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SLE &= AV \times EF \\
&= \text{\textcurrency}50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6 \\
&= \text{\textcurrency}30{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: State ARO**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ARO_{\text{before}} &= 0.5 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Inherent ALE**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ALE_{\text{before}} &= SLE \times ARO_{\text{before}} \\
&= \text{\textcurrency}30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 \\
&= \text{\textcurrency}15{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate Residual ALE After EDR Deployment**

The new occurrence rate is once every 10 years:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ARO_{\text{after}} &= 0.1 \text{ per year} \\
ALE_{\text{after}} &= SLE \times ARO_{\text{after}} \\
&= \text{\textcurrency}30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1 \\
&= \text{\textcurrency}3{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Calculate Annual Savings**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings} &= ALE_{\text{before}} - ALE_{\text{after}} \\
&= \text{\textcurrency}15{,}00{,}000 - \text{\textcurrency}3{,}00{,}000 \\
&= \text{\textcurrency}12{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Calculate Net Benefit (Savings Minus Control Cost)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Benefit} &= \text{Savings} - C_{\text{EDR}} \\
&= \text{\textcurrency}12{,}00{,}000 - \text{\textcurrency}4{,}00{,}000 \\
&= \text{\textcurrency}8{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8: Calculate ROI**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ROI &= \dfrac{\text{Net Benefit}}{C_{\text{EDR}}} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{\text{\textcurrency}8{,}00{,}000}{\text{\textcurrency}4{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% \\
&= 200\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since the ROI is a **strongly positive 200%** and the net annual benefit is **₹/S 8,00,000**, the EDR solution is **financially justified** and should be deployed.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the **NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1** risk assessment methodology as applied to mitigating threat vectors. List the steps and explain each with one sentence. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The **NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1** *Guide for Conducting Risk Assessments* defines a structured, repeatable methodology for assessing risks from threat vectors. The methodology consists of the following steps:

1. **Step 1 — System Characterization:** Define the scope of the assessment, identify the assets, system boundaries, and the operational environment.
2. **Step 2 — Threat Identification:** Identify potential threat sources (actors) and the corresponding threat vectors they may employ, using sources like threat intelligence feeds and historical incident data.
3. **Step 3 — Vulnerability Identification:** Identify weaknesses in the system that could be exploited by the identified threat vectors (e.g., via vulnerability scanners, penetration tests, and code review).
4. **Step 4 — Control Analysis:** Review the existing controls (administrative, technical, physical) and assess their effectiveness in mitigating the identified threat vectors.
5. **Step 5 — Likelihood Determination:** Estimate the probability that a given threat vector will successfully exploit a vulnerability, considering threat capability, control effectiveness, and vulnerability characteristics.
6. **Step 6 — Impact Analysis:** Determine the adverse impact (financial, reputational, operational, regulatory) that would result if the threat vector is realized.
7. **Step 7 — Risk Determination:** Compute the overall risk as a function of threat, vulnerability, likelihood, and impact (commonly $IR = L \times I$).
8. **Step 8 — Control Recommendations:** Recommend additional or enhanced controls to bring the residual risk to within the organization's risk appetite.
9. **Step 9 — Results Documentation:** Document the assessment in a formal risk assessment report for stakeholders, auditors, and regulators.

**Why this matters:** This methodology ensures that mitigation decisions are *data-driven*, *defensible*, and *aligned with business risk appetite* — a core expectation of mature CTI programs.

---

**Q. (b)** A financial services firm identifies the following three threat vectors with the given qualitative likelihood (L), impact (I), and current control effectiveness (C) values. Compute the **inherent risk**, **residual risk**, and **risk reduction percentage** for each. Recommend a prioritized mitigation order. *(7 marks)*

| Threat Vector | L | I | C |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phishing email | 5 | 3 | 0.40 |
| SQL injection on customer portal | 3 | 5 | 0.60 |
| Insider data theft | 2 | 5 | 0.20 |

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Compute Inherent Risk (IR = L × I)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IR_{\text{phishing}} &= 5 \times 3 = 15 \\
IR_{\text{SQLi}} &= 3 \times 5 = 15 \\
IR_{\text{insider}} &= 2 \times 5 = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Residual Risk (RR = IR × (1 − C))**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RR_{\text{phishing}} &= 15 \times (1 - 0.40) = 15 \times 0.60 = 9.0 \\
RR_{\text{SQLi}} &= 15 \times (1 - 0.60) = 15 \times 0.40 = 6.0 \\
RR_{\text{insider}} &= 10 \times (1 - 0.20) = 10 \times 0.80 = 8.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Risk Reduction Percentage (RR% = C × 100)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RR\%_{\text{phishing}} &= 0.40 \times 100 = 40\% \\
RR\%_{\text{SQLi}} &= 0.60 \times 100 = 60\% \\
RR\%_{\text{insider}} &= 0.20 \times 100 = 20\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Summary Table**

| Threat Vector | IR | RR | RR% | Mitigation Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Phishing | 15 | 9.0 | 40% | **1 (highest residual risk)** |
| Insider data theft | 10 | 8.0 | 20% | **2 (second-highest residual risk)** |
| SQL injection | 15 | 6.0 | 60% | **3 (lowest residual risk)** |

**Step 5: Recommended Prioritization Logic**

- **Phishing** has the *highest residual risk* (9.0) and the *lowest control effectiveness* (40%), making it the top priority.
- **Insider data theft** has the *second-highest residual risk* (8.0) and the *weakest existing controls* (20%), making it the second priority — especially concerning for a *financial* firm.
- **SQL injection** has the *lowest residual risk* (6.0) because the existing WAF is already 60% effective. It is the lowest priority for new investment, but the WAF should be *continuously tuned* to push effectiveness toward 80%+.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1 — Forgetting Units:** Many students write $SLE = 50,00,000 \times 0.6 = 30,00,000$ but forget the currency symbol. *Always include units in every numerical answer — losing this costs 0.5 to 1 mark.*
> 2. **Common Mistake #2 — Mis-Ordering Risk Priority:** Students often rank by *inherent risk* alone. The board expects ranking by *residual risk* (post-control), because the question is *where to spend the next mitigation dollar*. A vector with high inherent risk but already-strong controls is *less urgent* than a vector with moderate inherent risk and weak controls.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3 — Skipping the Recommendation:** In 14-mark questions, the model answer explicitly demands a *recommendation* (deploy / do not deploy, prioritize in which order). Students who stop at the numerical computation lose 2–3 marks for "lack of business judgment".
> 4. **Common Mistake #4 — Confusing SLE with ALE:** $SLE$ is the loss from *one* event. $ALE$ is the loss *per year*. Mixing them up is a guaranteed zero for the calculation sub-part.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5 — Forgetting the Mitigation Lifecycle:** When asked "Is mitigation a one-time project?", students often answer "yes". The board expects: *"No, it is a continuous lifecycle — Identify, Assess, Mitigate, Implement, Monitor, Review — because new vulnerabilities appear daily and controls degrade over time."*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Pin This Section Before the Exam**

- [ ] **Threat Vector vs. Threat Actor:** Vector = *pathway* (phishing, SQLi). Actor = *originator* (APT, insider).
- [ ] **Risk = f(Threat, Vulnerability, Impact).** Memorize this function; it is the seed of every other formula.
- [ ] **Inherent Risk (IR) = L × I** (qualitative) or **ALE = SLE × ARO** (quantitative).
- [ ] **Residual Risk (RR) = IR × (1 − C_eff)** — the risk *after* controls.
- [ ] **Risk Reduction % = C_eff × 100** (algebraic identity — the *most* important shortcut in the chapter).
- [ ] **Four Risk Responses:** **R**educe, **T**ransfer, **A**void, **A**ccept (mnemonic: *RTAA* or *"Reduce The Attack Area"*).
- [ ] **Risk Transfer ≠ Risk Elimination:** Insurance pays for the loss; the incident still happens.
- [ ] **Risk Avoidance is the *only* strategy that fully eliminates risk** (by removing the activity).
- [ ] **ROI of a control = (Savings − Cost) / Cost × 100.** A positive ROI justifies the control.
- [ ] **Defense-in-Depth = 6 layers** (Perimeter → Network → Identity → Host → Application → Data). *Memorize the order.*
- [ ] **NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1 = 9-step methodology** (System Char → Threat ID → Vuln ID → Control Analysis → Likelihood → Impact → Risk Determination → Control Recommendations → Documentation).
- [ ] **Common Threat Vectors to Know Cold:** Phishing, Credential Stuffing, SQLi, Ransomware, Insider Threat, Supply Chain, Unpatched Software, DDoS, Lateral Movement, Cloud Misconfiguration.
- [ ] **ALE is the metric used in *boardroom* decisions**; IR/RR are used in *engineering* decisions. Know which to use when.
- [ ] **Mitigation is a *lifecycle*, not a *project*.** Use the **Identify → Assess → Mitigate → Implement → Monitor → Review** loop.
- [ ] **One mitigation per threat vector is *never* enough** — always propose *layered* (admin + technical + physical) controls in the answer.
- [ ] **Control effectiveness (C_eff) is the single most important variable** in any cost-benefit analysis. If a question gives C_eff, *most* of the answer hinges on using it correctly.
- [ ] **KTU exam "trigger words" and their expected responses:**
  - *"Differentiate"* → 2 clear definitions + 1 distinguishing sentence.
  - *"Explain with diagram"* → diagram (1 mark) + 4–6 explanation points (4 marks) + 1 real-world example (1 mark).
  - *"Calculate"* → 5–8 explicit steps, each with a one-line *conversion logic* sentence, units on every number.
  - *"Recommend"* → 1 mitigation strategy + 1 supporting justification + 1 monitoring/validation step.
  - *"Justify"* → a control is justified if **ROI > 0** *or* **residual risk is within risk appetite** — state *both* for full marks.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 — Topic Notes: Mitigating Risks from Threat Vectors**
> *These notes are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education framework and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels (Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze, Evaluate). Practice both the Part A and Part B questions under timed conditions to maximize board-exam readiness.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scanning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT755 - CYBER THREAT INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Scanning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Scanning — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI)** and the **PTES (Penetration Testing Execution Standard)**, **Scanning** is defined as the second phase of a penetration test (preceded by reconnaissance and succeeded by enumeration/gaining access). It is the systematic, automated, and methodical process of probing target systems, networks, hosts, and services to identify **live hosts**, **open ports**, **running services**, **protocol versions**, **operating system fingerprints**, and **known vulnerabilities**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECCT755 Module 2):**
> *Scanning is the pre-attack phase in which an adversary actively transmits specially crafted packets to a target system to extract intelligence — such as topology, host availability, exposed services, OS identity, and exploit vectors — that supports subsequent exploitation and intrusion planning.*

The process is governed by the following formal classifications:

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Host Discovery** | Determining which IP addresses in a given subnet correspond to live/active machines. |
| **Port Scanning** | Identifying the **state** (open, closed, filtered) of TCP/UDP ports on a target host. |
| **Service / Version Detection** | Probing open ports to determine the application software and its version banner. |
| **OS Fingerprinting** | Inspecting TCP/IP stack behavior to infer the target's operating system. |
| **Vulnerability Scanning** | Comparing discovered service versions against CVE databases to flag exploitable weaknesses. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Burglar Survey"

Imagine a burglar approaching a residential colony at night. Before forcing any door:

1. **He walks the perimeter** — checking which houses have lights on (Host Discovery / Ping Sweep).
2. **He jiggles every door handle and window latch** — finding unlocked entry points (Port Scanning).
3. **He looks at the doorbell nameplate and mailbox stickers** to identify the owner (Banner Grabbing / Service Enumeration).
4. **He notices the car parked outside** to guess the family type (OS Fingerprinting).
5. **He checks the lock brand** to see if he has the right skeleton key (Vulnerability Scanning).

Scanning in cybersecurity is **exactly this survey** — but conducted electronically, across thousands of "houses" (hosts) in milliseconds, using crafted packets instead of hands.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric to Remember:** The standard well-known TCP port range is **0–1023**, registered ports span **1024–49151**, and dynamic/private ports use **49152–65535**. KTU examiners frequently test this numerical range.

## 1.3 Why Scanning Matters in CTI

- **Threat Surface Mapping:** Reveals the **attack surface** — the sum total of all points where an unauthorized user can attempt to enter or extract data.
- **Strategic Intelligence:** Scanning results feed SIEM/SOAR platforms, providing tactical indicators of compromise (IoCs).
- **Defensive Posture:** Blue teams use the same techniques to audit and harden their infrastructure.
- **Compliance Auditing:** Standards like **PCI-DSS 11.3**, **NIST 800-53 RA-5**, and **ISO 27001 A.12.6.1** mandate regular vulnerability scanning.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Scanning Methodology (PTES-Compliant Phases)

A disciplined scanning engagement proceeds through the following structured phases:

### Phase 1 — Network Enumeration (Pre-Scan)
Determine the live network range using **ICMP Echo Requests**, **ARP Requests** (on LANs), and **TCP SYN/ACK** packets to default ports. Tools: `nmap -sn`, `fping`, `arp-scan`.

### Phase 2 — Host Discovery
Identify active hosts. A host is considered "up" when it responds to at least one probe. Nmap's default behavior is to send **ICMP echo**, **TCP SYN to port 443**, **TCP ACK to port 80**, and **ICMP timestamp** in parallel.

### Phase 3 — Port Scanning
The technical core of scanning. Targets all **65,536 TCP** and **65,536 UDP** ports per host. Categorized by **TCP flag manipulation** techniques.

### Phase 4 — Service & Version Detection
Open ports are probed with **probe signatures** (e.g., `GET / HTTP/1.0\r\n\r\n` for HTTP, `SSH-2.0-OpenSSH_7.9` for SSH). The response is matched against Nmap's `nmap-service-probes` database of over **18,000 signatures**.

### Phase 5 — OS Fingerprinting
Two primary methods:
- **Active:** Send crafted packets (SYN, FIN, bogus flag combinations), analyze responses — uses Nmap's `nmap-os-db` with **6,000+ fingerprints**.
- **Passive:** Sniff network traffic silently, analyze TTL, window size, DF bit, TOS — used by tools like `p0f` and `Ettercap`.

### Phase 6 — Vulnerability Mapping
Cross-reference identified service versions with **CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures)**, **CWE (Common Weakness Enumeration)**, and **NVD (National Vulnerability Database)** to flag potential exploits.

## 2.2 TCP Three-Way Handshake — Foundation of Scanning

Every reliable TCP scan is grounded in the **TCP 3-WHS** mechanics. The state transitions of the connection are crucial for understanding scan responses:

$$
\text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN, Seq=x}} \text{Server}
$$

$$
\text{Server} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN+ACK, Seq=y, Ack=x+1}} \text{Client}
$$

$$
\text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{ACK, Seq=x+1, Ack=y+1}} \text{Server}
$$

**Established state** = port is **OPEN**. If the server replies with **RST/ACK**, the port is **CLOSED**. No response typically means **FILTERED** (firewall dropping packets).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Scan Classification Table

| Scan Type | Packet Flags Sent | Open Port Response | Closed Port Response | Stealth Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **TCP Connect (`-sT`)** | Full 3-WHS via `connect()` syscall | SYN+ACK → completes handshake | RST/ACK | Low (logged) |
| **SYN Stealth / Half-Open (`-sS`)** | SYN only | SYN+ACK → attacker sends RST | RST/ACK | High |
| **FIN Scan (`-sF`)** | FIN | **No response** (RFC 793) | RST/ACK | High |
| **NULL Scan (`-sN`)** | No flags set | **No response** | RST/ACK | High |
| **Xmas Scan (`-sX`)** | FIN + PSH + URG (lit like a Christmas tree) | **No response** | RST/ACK | High |
| **ACK Scan (`-sA`)** | ACK only | RST (state: unfiltered) | RST (state: unfiltered) | Firewall mapping |
| **Window Scan (`-sW`)** | ACK with window probe | RST with non-zero window | RST with zero window | OS detection |
| **Maimon Scan (`-sM`)** | FIN+ACK | No response / RST | RST/ACK | Evasion |
| **Idle/Zombie Scan (`-sI`)** | Spoofed SYN via idle host | SYN+ACK observed at zombie | RST/ACK observed at zombie | Maximum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RFC 793 Compliance Rule:** FIN, NULL, and Xmas scans exploit the fact that RFC 793 mandates an **RST** response to any segment without **SYN, RST, or ACK** flags **when the port is closed**. **Microsoft Windows** does NOT follow RFC 793 — it sends RST for both open and closed ports, making these scans **unreliable against Windows hosts.**

## 2.4 UDP Scanning Theory

UDP scanning is fundamentally slower because UDP is **connectionless** — no handshake. The scanner sends a UDP packet and waits:

$$
\text{If response is ICMP Port Unreachable (Type 3, Code 3)} \Rightarrow \text{Port is CLOSED}
$$

$$
\text{If response is UDP packet (e.g., DNS, SNMP)} \Rightarrow \text{Port is OPEN}
$$

$$
\text{If no response after retries}} \Rightarrow \text{Port is FILTERED or open|filtered}
$$

**Mathematical Time Estimate:**
$$
T_{\text{UDP scan}} \approx N_{\text{ports}} \times \left( T_{\text{retry}} + T_{\text{timeout}} \right)
$$

A full UDP scan of all 65,535 ports typically takes **~18 hours** vs. a TCP SYN scan of **~5 minutes**.

## 2.5 Scan Performance Formula

For KTU calculations, the theoretical scan time for `H` hosts × `P` ports with parallelism `W` and rate `R` packets/sec is:

$$
T_{\text{scan}} = \frac{H \times P}{W \times R}
$$

**Boundary conditions:**
- $H \geq 1$, $P \leq 65535$, $W \geq 1$, $R \leq$ line capacity.
- For **TCP Connect**: $R$ is bounded by kernel `connect()` rate (~1000/s).
- For **SYN Stealth**: $R$ bounded by raw socket rate (~100,000/s with `-T5`).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Scanning Application |
|---|---|
| **Offensive Security** | Penetration testing, red-team engagements, APT reconnaissance. |
| **Defensive Security** | Continuous vulnerability assessment, attack surface management. |
| **Compliance** | PCI-DSS quarterly scans, ISO 27001 audits, SOC 2 reports. |
| **Cloud Security** | AWS Inspector, Azure Defender — cloud-native scanning services. |
| **OT/ICS Security** | Passive asset discovery on SCADA networks without disruption. |
| **Bug Bounty** | Reconnaissance to discover scope-eligible assets. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: TCP State Transition During a SYN Stealth Scan

**Given:** Attacker sends a single SYN packet to target port `P` on host `H`.

**Goal:** Determine the port state based on the response packet observed.

### Step 1 — Attacker Transmits the Probe

The attacker crafts a TCP segment with the following header fields:

$$
\text{TCP Header: } \{ \text{Src Port: random}, \text{Dst Port: } P, \text{Seq: } x, \text{Ack: } 0, \text{Flags: SYN}, \text{Win: } 65535, \text{TTL: } 64 \}
$$

### Step 2 — Target TCP/IP Stack Processing

The target's kernel inspects the destination port `P`. Three logical branches occur:

$$
\text{If } P \in \text{listening sockets} \rightarrow \text{Kernel allocates TCB, replies SYN+ACK}
$$

$$
\text{If } P \notin \text{listening sockets} \rightarrow \text{Kernel replies RST+ACK immediately}
$$

$$
\text{If packet dropped by firewall} \rightarrow \text{No response (timeout)} 
$$

### Step 3 — Attacker's Classification Logic

Upon receiving a response (or timeout), the scanner classifies the state:

$$
\text{State}(P) = \begin{cases} \text{OPEN} & \text{if response} = \text{SYN+ACK} \\ \text{CLOSED} & \text{if response} = \text{RST+ACK} \\ \text{FILTERED} & \text{if response} = \text{none (after retries)} \\ \text{OPEN|FILTERED} & \text{if no response (FIN/NULL/Xmas/UDP)} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 4 — Attacker Tears Down the Half-Open Connection

For SYN scan, the attacker sends **RST** to avoid completing the handshake (hence "half-open"):

$$
\text{Attacker} \xrightarrow{\text{RST, Seq=x+1}} \text{Target}
$$

The target's TCB is deallocated; no entry appears in connection logs of services that only log established connections.

## 3.2 Derivation: Idle/Zombie Scan (Most Stealthy Method)

The Idle scan uses a **zombie host** with predictable IP ID incrementation to bounce probes, achieving **complete anonymity** of the attacker.

### Step 1 — Probe the Zombie to Record IP ID

$$
\text{Attacker} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN+ACK (spoofed target IP)}} \text{Zombie}
$$

$$
\text{Zombie} \xrightarrow{\text{RST, IPID}=I_1} \text{Target (sees source = attacker)}
$$

The attacker observes IP ID = $I_1$ by sniffing or using a network tap.

### Step 2 — Spoofed Probe to Target

$$
\text{Attacker} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN (Source IP = Zombie)}} \text{Target}
$$

### Step 3 — Analyze Target's Response to Zombie

$$
\text{If target port is OPEN:} \quad \text{Target} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN+ACK}} \text{Zombie} \rightarrow \text{Zombie} \xrightarrow{\text{RST, IPID}=I_2=I_1+2} \text{Target}
$$

$$
\text{If target port is CLOSED:} \quad \text{Target} \xrightarrow{\text{RST}} \text{Zombie} \rightarrow \text{No zombie response, IPID}=I_1+1 \text{ (from other traffic)}
$$

### Step 4 — Re-Probe the Zombie

$$
\text{Attacker} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN+ACK}} \text{Zombie} \rightarrow \text{Attacker observes IPID}=I_3
$$

### Step 5 — Decision Logic

$$
\text{If } I_3 - I_1 = 2 \Rightarrow \text{Target port is OPEN}
$$

$$
\text{If } I_3 - I_1 = 1 \Rightarrow \text{Target port is CLOSED or FILTERED}
$$

This is the **mathematical foundation** of the most anonymous scan possible — the target's logs only ever record the zombie's IP, not the attacker's.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: TCP Header Flag Bits

The 6-bit "Reserved" + 6-bit "Code Bits" portion of the TCP header (offset 13–14 in a 20-byte TCP header) determines scan behavior:

| Flag Bit | Position | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **URG** | Bit 5 | Urgent pointer field valid |
| **ACK** | Bit 4 | Acknowledgment field valid |
| **PSH** | Bit 3 | Push function — deliver data immediately |
| **RST** | Bit 2 | Reset the connection |
| **SYN** | Bit 1 | Synchronize sequence numbers |
| **FIN** | Bit 0 | No more data from sender (graceful close) |

**Composite flag combinations for scans:**

$$
\text{Xmas} = \text{FIN} \cup \text{PSH} \cup \text{URG} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Byte value} = 0x29 = 41_{10}
$$

$$
\text{NULL} = \emptyset \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Byte value} = 0x00
$$

$$
\text{SYN+ACK} = \text{SYN} \cup \text{ACK} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Byte value} = 0x12 = 18_{10}
$$

## 3.4 Countermeasure Mathematics: Detection Probability

A network IDS detects a scan using the **Bayes' theorem** model. Given prior probability of scanning $P(S)$ and likelihood ratio:

$$
P(S \mid \text{Alert}) = \frac{P(\text{Alert} \mid S) \cdot P(S)}{P(\text{Alert} \mid S) \cdot P(S) + P(\text{Alert} \mid \neg S) \cdot P(\neg S)}
$$

For an Nmap scan, $P(\text{Alert} \mid S) \approx 0.92$ (very high detection), while for an Idle scan, $P(\text{Alert} \mid S) \approx 0.05$ due to the absence of attacker-side packets.

## 3.5 Scan Optimization: Timing Templates

Nmap provides 6 timing templates that govern parallelization and rate:

| Template | Flag | Packets/sec approx | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Paranoid | `-T0` | 1 per 5 min | IDS evasion |
| Sneaky | `-T1` | 1 per 15 sec | IDS evasion |
| Polite | `-T2` | ~100/s | Reduce network load |
| Normal | `-T3` | ~1,000/s | Default |
| Aggressive | `-T4` | ~10,000/s | Modern networks |
| Insane | `-T5` | ~100,000/s | Fast local nets |

The mathematical relationship is exponential: $R_{n+1} \approx 10 \times R_n$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Scanning Methodology — Top-Level Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Scope and Targets] --> B[Phase 1: Network Enumeration]
    B --> C[Phase 2: Host Discovery - Ping Sweep]
    C --> D{Any Live Hosts Found?}
    D -- No --> E[Report Empty Scope]
    D -- Yes --> F[Phase 3: Port Scanning]
    F --> G[Phase 4: Service Version Detection]
    G --> H[Phase 5: OS Fingerprinting]
    H --> I[Phase 6: Vulnerability Mapping]
    I --> J[Phase 7: Generate Intelligence Report]
    J --> K[End: Feed to Exploitation or Defense]
```

## 4.2 TCP SYN Stealth Scan — Packet Exchange Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Attacker
    participant T as Target Host
    participant F as Firewall
    A->>T: SYN Packet (Dst Port: 80)
    alt Port OPEN
        T-->>A: SYN + ACK Response
        A->>T: RST (Tear Down Half-Open)
        Note over A,T: Port classified as OPEN
    else Port CLOSED
        T-->>A: RST + ACK Response
        Note over A,T: Port classified as CLOSED
    else Packet DROPPED by Firewall
        F->>F: Packet Silently Dropped
        Note over A,T: Port classified as FILTERED
    end
```

## 4.3 Idle Scan Architecture — Tri-Party Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AttackerZone
        AT[Attacker Machine]
    end
    subgraph ZombieZone
        ZM[Idle Zombie Host with Predictable IP ID]
    end
    subgraph TargetZone
        TG[Target System]
    end

    Step1[Step 1: Probe Zombie to Record IPID]
    Step2[Step 2: Send Spoofed SYN to Target via Zombie IP]
    Step3[Step 3: Target Replies to Zombie]
    Step4[Step 4: Re-Probe Zombie to Compare IPID]
    Step5[Step 5: Decision Logic OPEN vs CLOSED]

    AT --> Step1
    Step1 --> ZM
    Step2 --> TG
    TG --> Step3
    Step3 --> ZM
    ZM --> Step4
    Step4 --> AT
    AT --> Step5
```

## 4.4 Scan Technique Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[Network Scanning Techniques]
    Root --> TCP[TCP-Based Scans]
    Root --> UDP[UDP-Based Scans]
    Root --> Sp[Specialty Scans]

    TCP --> Conn[Connect Scan -sT]
    TCP --> Syn[SYN Stealth Scan -sS]
    TCP --> Fin[FIN Scan -sF]
    TCP --> Null[NULL Scan -sN]
    TCP --> Xmas[Xmas Scan -sX]
    TCP --> Ack[ACK Scan -sA]
    TCP --> Win[Window Scan -sW]
    TCP --> Maimon[Maimon Scan -sM]

    UDP --> UdpBasic[UDP Scan -sU]
    Sp --> Idle[Idle Scan -sI]
    Sp --> Prot[Protocol Scan -sO]
    Sp --> Ftp[FTP Bounce Scan -b]
```

## 4.5 Evasion vs. Detection Trade-off Matrix (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Evasion[Evasion Technique] --> Frag[IP Fragmentation -f]
    Evasion --> Decoy[Decoy Scan -D]
    Evasion --> Spoof[Source Port Spoofing --source-port]
    Evasion --> Timing[Timing Manipulation -T0 or -T1]
    Evasion --> Mtu[MTU Specification --mtu]
    Evasion --> Macro[MAC Address Spoofing --spoof-mac]

    Detection[Detection Countermeasure] --> IDS[Signature-Based IDS Snort Suricata]
    Detection --> HIDS[Host-Based Firewall]

    Detection --> Honey[Honeypots and Tarpits]

    Evasion -. competes with .-> Detection
```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PECCT755]**
**Explain the concept of "half-open" or SYN stealth scanning. Why is it preferred over a full TCP connect scan?**

**Model Answer:**
A SYN stealth scan, also called a half-open scan, is a port scanning technique in which the attacker sends only a single TCP **SYN** packet to the target port instead of completing the full three-way handshake. If the port is **open**, the target replies with **SYN+ACK**, and the attacker immediately sends an **RST** to tear down the half-open connection before the handshake completes.

It is preferred over the TCP Connect scan for the following reasons:
1. **Stealth:** It is not logged by the target application because the connection is never fully established. Most application-level logs (e.g., web server logs) only record **completed** handshakes.
2. **Speed:** It bypasses the kernel's `connect()` syscall overhead, allowing raw socket manipulation and much higher packet-per-second rates.
3. **Resource Efficiency:** It does not allocate a full Transmission Control Block (TCB) on the target, minimizing evidence of scanning activity.
4. **Privilege Requirement:** It requires **raw socket** access (root/admin) but avoids the high-overhead user-space connect calls.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PECCT755]**
**Differentiate between active and passive OS fingerprinting with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Active OS Fingerprinting | Passive OS Fingerprinting |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Sends specially crafted packets to the target and analyzes responses. | Silently sniffs network traffic without injecting packets. |
| **Detection Risk** | High — the target can detect and log the probe. | Very low — virtually undetectable. |
| **Speed** | Fast — single round-trip per probe. | Slow — must wait for natural traffic. |
| **Tools** | Nmap (`-O`), Xprobe2, SinFP. | **p0f**, Ettercap, Satori. |
| **Example Probe** | Sending a SYN packet with specific TTL, window size, and sequence number to trigger a stack-specific response. | Capturing an outgoing TCP SYN from the host and analyzing TTL (=64 → Linux, =128 → Windows), window size, DF bit, and TCP options order. |
| **Accuracy** | High (~95% with Nmap's database of 6,000+ signatures). | Moderate (~80%, depends on traffic volume). |

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PECCT755 Module 2]**

**(a)** With neat packet exchange diagrams, explain the working of **TCP Connect scan** and **FIN scan**. Compare their responses for open and closed ports. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**TCP Connect Scan:**
This scan uses the operating system's `connect()` system call to attempt a full TCP three-way handshake with the target port.

- **Open Port Behavior:** Target sends **SYN+ACK** → Attacker completes with **ACK** → Connection established → Attacker closes with **FIN**. Application logs the connection.
- **Closed Port Behavior:** Target sends **RST+ACK** immediately → Attacker aborts the connection.
- **Filtered Port Behavior:** No response due to firewall drop; connection attempt times out.

**FIN Scan:**
This scan exploits **RFC 793**, which states that a closed port must respond with **RST** to any incoming segment that does **not contain SYN, RST, or ACK flags**. An open port should silently drop such packets.

- **Open Port Behavior:** No response from the target (the packet is discarded by the listening socket).
- **Closed Port Behavior:** Target replies with **RST+ACK**.
- **Filtered Port Behavior:** No response (firewall drop).

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | TCP Connect | FIN Scan |
|---|---|---|
| Flag sent | SYN | FIN |
| Open port response | SYN+ACK | No response |
| Closed port response | RST+ACK | RST+ACK |
| Works against Windows | Yes | **No** (Windows ignores RFC 793) |
| Requires root | No | Yes (raw sockets) |
| Detection | Easy (logged) | Hard (stealth) |

---

**(b)** Describe the **Idle/Zombie scan** technique in detail. Explain how IP ID incrementation is used to determine the port state. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Idle scan**, pioneered by **Antirez (Salvatore Sanfilippo)** in 1998 and implemented in Nmap via `-sI`, is the most anonymous TCP port scan possible. The attacker uses a third-party "zombie" host whose **IP Identification (IP ID)** field increments predictably (e.g., one increment per packet sent by the host, regardless of destination).

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Step 1 — Probe the zombie directly to record its current IP ID:**
 The attacker sends a **SYN+ACK** packet to the zombie. Since the zombie did not initiate a connection, it replies with **RST**. The attacker notes the IP ID value, say $I_1$.

2. **Step 2 — Send a spoofed SYN packet to the target, pretending to be the zombie:**
 The attacker crafts a TCP SYN with source IP = zombie's IP, destination IP = target. This packet is routed to the target, but the target's response (if any) goes to the zombie.

3. **Step 3 — Observe the zombie's reaction:**
 - If the target port is **OPEN**: the target sends SYN+ACK to the zombie. The zombie, not expecting this, replies with **RST**. The zombie's IP ID is now $I_1 + 2$ (one increment for the unsolicited SYN+ACK it received, one for the RST it sent).
 - If the target port is **CLOSED**: the target sends RST to the zombie. The zombie discards it silently. The zombie's IP ID remains effectively $I_1$ (or $I_1 + 1$ if other unrelated traffic occurred).

4. **Step 4 — Re-probe the zombie:**
 The attacker again sends SYN+ACK to the zombie and reads the new IP ID, say $I_3$.

5. **Step 5 — Decision Logic:**
 - **If $I_3 - I_1 = 2$ → Port is OPEN**
 - **If $I_3 - I_1 = 1$ → Port is CLOSED or FILTERED**

**Advantages:** Complete attacker anonymity, no packets originate from the attacker's IP.
**Disadvantages:** Requires an idle zombie host with a predictable IP ID; modern OSes randomize IP ID by default (Linux ≥4.0), making this scan difficult against current systems.

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PECCT755 Module 2 — Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **UDP scanning** methodology. Why is it slower than TCP scanning, and what are the response interpretations? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

UDP scanning (`nmap -sU`) is used to identify open UDP services. Since UDP is **connectionless**, the scanner cannot rely on a handshake; instead, it interprets ICMP responses.

**Procedure:**
For each of the 65,535 UDP ports on the target, the scanner sends a UDP packet. If the port is **open**, the application is expected to reply with a UDP payload (e.g., DNS query response on port 53). If the port is **closed**, the host should reply with **ICMP Type 3, Code 3 (Port Unreachable)**.

**Response Interpretation Table:**

| Response | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| UDP packet received | Port **OPEN** |
| ICMP Port Unreachable (Type 3, Code 3) | Port **CLOSED** |
| ICMP Administratively Prohibited (Type 3, Code 13) | Port **FILTERED** |
| No response (after retries) | Port **OPEN\|FILTERED** |

**Why UDP Scanning Is Slower:**
1. **ICMP rate-limiting:** Most operating systems limit ICMP error messages to ~1 per second to prevent flood attacks. A full scan of 65,535 ports thus takes ~18 hours.
2. **No handshake confirmation:** TCP scans use SYN+ACK as immediate acknowledgment; UDP scans often rely on timeouts.
3. **Payload ambiguity:** Many UDP services use proprietary protocols, so a "no response" might be an open port running an unknown service.

**Mitigation Techniques:** Run UDP scans in parallel (`--min-parallelism`), use service-version detection (`-sV`), and combine with TCP SYN scan for a complete picture.

---

**(b)** What is **banner grabbing**? Explain its methodology for HTTP and FTP services. Discuss countermeasures against banner grabbing. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Banner grabbing** is the technique of connecting to an open port and reading the **service greeting banner** that the application transmits upon connection. The banner often reveals the **software name, version, and patch level** — information critical for vulnerability mapping.

**HTTP Banner Grabbing:**
The attacker connects to port 80 or 443 and sends:
```
HEAD / HTTP/1.0\r\n\r\n
```
The server replies with headers such as:
```
Server: Apache/2.4.41 (Ubuntu)
X-Powered-By: PHP/7.4.3
```
Tools: `curl -I`, `Netcat (nc)`, `Nmap -sV`, `httprint`.

**FTP Banner Grabbing:**
Simply opening a TCP connection to port 21 returns the FTP greeting:
```
220 ProFTPD 1.3.5rc1 Server ready.
```
Tools: `nc -v target 21`, `Nmap -sV`, `telnet`.

**Countermeasures:**
1. **Banner Customization:** Modify `httpd.conf` (`ServerTokens Prod` for Apache) or `nginx.conf` (`server_tokens off`) to show only "Apache" without version.
2. **Strip Identifying Headers:** Remove `X-Powered-By`, `Server`, `Via` headers.
3. **Reverse Proxy / Load Balancer:** Place services behind a proxy that obscures backend identity.
4. **Service Hardening:** Disable FTP (use SFTP), use TLS for all services.
5. **Honeypots:** Deploy deceptive services that mislead attackers.
6. **Network Filtering:** Restrict direct internet exposure using firewalls and WAFs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Do not confuse** "Service Detection" (`-sV`) with "Banner Grabbing." Service detection uses **probe-response matching against a signature database**, whereas banner grabbing is the simpler act of reading the literal greeting text. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this conflation.
> 2. **Idle scan answers** must include the IP ID increment mathematics. Stating only "uses a zombie" without the $\Delta IPID = 2$ logic loses 3 marks.
> 3. **Windows incompatibility of FIN/NULL/Xmas scans** is a frequently-tested point — students often forget to mention RFC 793 non-compliance of Microsoft stacks.
> 4. **Port range numerics** (0–1023, 1024–49151, 49152–65535) are mandatory factual recall; wrong ranges cost marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Scanning** is **Phase 2** of penetration testing (after reconnaissance, before exploitation), focused on extracting **live host, port, service, OS, and vulnerability** intelligence.
- **TCP Connect scan** completes the full 3-WHS — loud but does not require root.
- **SYN Stealth scan** is the most commonly used scan — half-open, fast, root-required, low detection.
- **FIN, NULL, and Xmas scans** exploit **RFC 793's closed-port RST rule**; they are **ineffective against Windows** systems.
- **ACK scan** is used to map **firewall rule sets**, not to determine port openness.
- **Idle/Zombie scan** uses an **IP ID increment of 2** to indicate an open port — the most anonymous scan method.
- **UDP scan** is inherently slow due to **ICMP rate-limiting** (typically ~1 ICMP error/second).
- **Banner grabbing** extracts **service version** info — mitigated by stripping headers and using proxies.
- **OS fingerprinting** uses **TCP/IP stack behavioral analysis** (TTL, window size, DF bit, options order).
- **Nmap timing templates** scale from `-T0` (paranoid, IDS evasion) to `-T5` (insane, fast LAN).
- **Standard port ranges**: **Well-known 0–1023**, **Registered 1024–49151**, **Dynamic 49152–65535**.
- **Evasion techniques** include **fragmentation (`-f`), decoy scans (`-D`), source-port spoofing, and timing manipulation**.
- **Countermeasures** include **firewalls, IDS/IPS, honeypots, banner stripping, and rate-limiting**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Scanning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT755 - CYBER THREAT INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Scanning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) and ethical hacking, **Scanning** is the second phase of the standard cyber kill chain (preceded by Reconnaissance and followed by Enumeration/Gaining Access). It refers to the systematic, methodical probing of target systems, networks, hosts, or applications to collect intelligence regarding their operational posture, including live hosts, open/closed/filtered ports, running services, protocol versions, and potential vulnerabilities.

**Types of Scanning** classifies the methodologies attackers and defenders use to interrogate digital assets. KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes scanning into the following primary typologies:

1. **Network Scanning** — Identifying live hosts, IP ranges, and topology.
2. **Port Scanning** — Probing TCP/UDP ports to determine service availability.
3. **Vulnerability Scanning** — Automated detection of known CVEs and misconfigurations.
4. **Application/Service Scanning** — Probing specific software (e.g., HTTP, FTP, SSH).
5. **Web Application Scanning** — OWASP-aligned testing of web layers (XSS, SQLi surfaces).
6. **Wireless Scanning** — Discovery of Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and RF assets.
7. **OS Fingerprinting** — Stack-based operating system identification.
8. **Banner Grabbing / Version Scanning** — Reading service banners for version disclosure.
9. **Stealth / Evasive Scanning** — Techniques designed to bypass IDS/IPS.
10. **Compliance Scanning** — Auditing systems against regulatory baselines (PCI-DSS, ISO 27001).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECCT755 Module 2):** Students are expected to differentiate between *Active* and *Passive* scanning, classify scan types based on TCP flag manipulation, and identify the tool-of-the-trade for each category (e.g., Nmap for port scanning, Nikto for web scanning, OpenVAS for vulnerability scanning).

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Burglar vs. Security Guard" Analogy

Imagine a large office complex with 500 rooms (representing the **65,535 TCP/UDP ports** on every networked computer).

- **Network Scanning** is the burglar walking around the perimeter of the building, checking which lights are ON (live hosts) and which are OFF (dead hosts).
- **Port Scanning** is the burglar trying every door to see which ones are *unlocked* (OPEN), *locked from inside* (CLOSED), or have a *silent alarm* (FILTERED by a firewall).
- **Vulnerability Scanning** is calling a locksmith and asking, "Of the unlocked doors, which ones have a known broken lock model on the recall list?"
- **Application Scanning** is leaning against an open door and asking, "Hey, what software are you running, and what version?"
- **Banner Grabbing** is reading the welcome mat that says "Apache 2.4.41 — Built 2019."
- **Stealth Scanning** is the burglar knocking gently so the security guard inside (IDS) thinks it was just the wind.
- **Compliance Scanning** is a fire marshal checking if the building meets the 2024 Kerala Fire Safety Code.

Every scan is a **question**; the network's response is the **answer** — and that answer is the *intelligence* used in subsequent attack/defense decisions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** The standard port range probed is **0 to 65,535** for both TCP and UDP. The first **1,024** ports are *Well-Known Ports* (assigned by IANA), **1,025–49,151** are *Registered*, and **49,152–65,535** are *Dynamic/Private*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The TCP Three-Way Handshake — The Foundation of Most Scans

To understand *Types of Scanning*, you must first understand the TCP handshake, because every TCP scan manipulates it.

$$\text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN}} \text{Server}$$
$$\text{Client} \xleftarrow{\text{SYN+ACK}} \text{Server}$$
$$\text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{ACK}} \text{Server} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Connection Established}$$

A scan's intelligence value is derived from the **flags** set in the TCP header and the **response pattern** the target emits.

## 2.2 Detailed Breakdown of Scan Types

### A. Network Scanning (Host Discovery)
- **Goal:** Identify which IP addresses in a subnet are alive.
- **Techniques:**
  - **ICMP Echo (Ping Sweep):** Sends ICMP Type 8 (Echo Request) → expects Type 0 (Echo Reply).
  - **ARP Scan:** Operates at Layer 2; used inside local networks; almost always succeeds because ARP is rarely blocked.
  - **TCP SYN Ping:** Sends SYN to port 80/443; a SYN+ACK means host is alive.
  - **UDP Ping:** Sends UDP packet to a closed port → ICMP Port Unreachable means host is alive.

### B. Port Scanning (The Core of Scanning)
- **TCP Connect Scan (`-sT`):** Completes the full three-way handshake. Logged in target logs. **No root required** on attacker.
- **SYN Scan / Half-Open Scan (`-sS`):** Sends SYN, receives SYN+ACK, then sends **RST** instead of ACK. Faster, stealthier. **Requires root.**
- **FIN Scan (`-sF`):** Sends FIN flag. RFC 793 states closed ports must reply with RST; open ports silently drop.
- **NULL Scan (`-sN`):** Sends packet with **NO flags** set. Same behavior pattern as FIN.
- **XMAS Scan (`-sX`):** Sends packet with FIN, PSH, and URG flags all set (lit up "like a Christmas tree").
- **ACK Scan (`-sA`):** Sends ACK. Used to map firewall **rule sets** (stateful vs. stateless) — returns RST for unfiltered, ICMP for filtered.
- **Window Scan (`-sW`):** Like ACK but examines TCP Window size to differentiate open/closed.
- **Maimon Scan (`-sM`):** Sends FIN+ACK; exploits BSD stack behavior on closed ports.
- **Idle Scan / Zombie Scan (`-sI`):** Spoofs source IP as a "zombie" host; the attacker infers open ports based on the zombie's IPID increments. The **most stealthy** technique.
- **UDP Scan (`-sU`):** Sends empty UDP headers. Open ports respond with the service data; closed ports reply with **ICMP Port Unreachable** (Type 3, Code 3). Slow because ICMP is rate-limited.

### C. Vulnerability Scanning
- **Authenticated Scan:** Uses valid credentials to log in and inspect deep configuration (registry, patch level).
- **Unauthenticated Scan:** External view only; simulates an outside attacker with no credentials.
- **Active Vulnerability Scan:** Sends crafted probes to actively trigger and detect flaws.
- **Passive Vulnerability Scan:** Taps network traffic; identifies versions and CVEs from observed packets (no injection).

### D. Application / Service Scanning
- Probes a specific service after port discovery (e.g., `amap`, `nmap -sV`).
- Uses **service probes** containing malformed/edge-case requests to elicit version-disclosing error messages.

### E. Web Application Scanning
- Fuzzes HTTP parameters, headers, and inputs to detect XSS, SQLi, LFI, RFI.
- Tools: **Nikto, Burp Suite Pro, OWASP ZAP, Acunetix**.

### F. Wireless Scanning
- Discovers SSIDs, encryption types (WPA2/WPA3/WEP), signal strength, and rogue APs.
- Tools: **Kismet, Aircrack-ng, Wifite**.

### G. OS Fingerprinting
- **Active:** Sends crafted packets; analyzes responses (TTL, DF-bit, window size).
  - Tool: **Nmap `-O`**
- **Passive:** Sniffs traffic and identifies OS from observed packet signatures.
  - Tool: **p0f**

### H. Stealth / Evasive Scanning
- Combines techniques: **Idle scans, fragmentation (`-f`), decoy scans (`-D`), timing tricks (`-T0` to `-T5`), source-port spoofing.**

### I. Compliance Scanning
- Maps target posture against hardened baselines (CIS Benchmarks, DISA STIGs).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM MANDATE:** Memorize the **response pattern matrix** below — it is the single most-asked concept in KTU board exams for this topic.

| Scan Type | Packet Sent (Flags) | Open Port Response | Closed Port Response |
|-----------|---------------------|--------------------|----------------------|
| TCP Connect | SYN → SYN+ACK → ACK | SYN+ACK (full handshake) | RST |
| SYN (Half-Open) | SYN | SYN+ACK (attacker sends RST) | RST |
| FIN | FIN | (No response / silent drop) | RST |
| NULL | (No flags) | (No response) | RST |
| XMAS | FIN + PSH + URG | (No response) | RST |
| ACK | ACK | RST | RST (unfiltered) / ICMP unreachable (filtered) |
| UDP | Empty UDP | UDP response from service | ICMP Type 3 Code 3 |
| Idle (Zombie) | SYN with spoofed source | IPID increment of zombie +2 | IPID increment of zombie +1 |

**RTT / Scan Time Estimation Formula (KTU High-Yield):**

$$T_{scan} = N_{ports} \times R_{max} \times \frac{1}{P_{parallel}}$$

Where:
- $T_{scan}$ = total scan duration (seconds)
- $N_{ports}$ = number of ports probed
- $R_{max}$ = maximum round-trip time observed (seconds)
- $P_{parallel}$ = parallelism level (concurrent probes)

**Stealth Metric — Detection Probability:**

$$P_{detect} = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

Where $\lambda$ is the IDS detection rate (events/sec) and $t$ is the scan duration. **Shorter, fragmented, decoy-heavy scans minimize $P_{detect}$.**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Idle (Zombie) Scan IPID Logic

The Idle Scan is the most analytically rigorous scan type in the KTU syllabus. The following derivation shows **how an attacker infers port state from a zombie host's IPID counter**.

**Step 1 — Probe the Zombie (Pre-test):**
The attacker sends a SYN+ACK to the zombie and receives a RST.

$$\text{Zombie RST packet} \Rightarrow \text{IPID} = X_{n}$$

**Step 2 — Attacker Sends Spoofed SYN to Target:**
The attacker forges a SYN packet to the target with **source IP = zombie's IP**.

$$\text{Attacker} \xrightarrow[\text{Source: Zombie IP}]{\text{SYN}} \text{Target Port } P$$

**Step 3 — Target's Reaction:**

**Case A — Port P is OPEN:**
Target sends SYN+ACK **to the zombie**. The zombie, not expecting this, replies with a **RST** to the target.

$$\Rightarrow \text{Zombie's IPID increments to } X_{n} + 1$$
(One unsolicited SYN+ACK → one RST → IPID +1)

**Case B — Port P is CLOSED:**
Target sends RST **to the zombie**. The zombie ignores unsolicited RSTs and does **not** respond. IPID unchanged.

$$\Rightarrow \text{Zombie's IPID remains } X_{n}$$

**Step 4 — Attacker Probes the Zombie (Post-test):**
The attacker sends another SYN+ACK to the zombie and receives its RST.

$$\text{Zombie RST packet} \Rightarrow \text{IPID} = X_{n+1}$$

**Step 5 — Inference Logic:**

$$\Delta \text{IPID} = X_{n+1} - X_{n}$$

| Observed Δ IPID | Inference |
|-----------------|-----------|
| $\Delta = 1$ | Port P is **CLOSED** (zombie only replied to the post-test SYN+ACK) |
| $\Delta = 2$ | Port P is **OPEN** (zombie replied to both the target's SYN+ACK AND the post-test) |

This two-step check completely blinds the target to the attacker's true IP — the target's logs show scans **originating from the zombie**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Scan

The KTU board exam often asks: *"Given a scenario, which scan would you use?"* Use this decision matrix.

| Scenario Constraint | Recommended Scan | Justification |
|---------------------|------------------|---------------|
| No root privileges | TCP Connect (`-sT`) | Only scan that doesn't need raw sockets |
| Maximum stealth required | Idle / Zombie | Completely blinds target to attacker IP |
| Behind a stateful firewall | ACK / Window | Maps firewall rules without triggering stateful inspection |
| Need to scan UDP services | UDP Scan (`-sU`) | UDP has no flags; relies on ICMP responses |
| Identify OS of remote host | Active OS fingerprint (`-O`) | Stack-based; needs raw responses |
| Cannot send packets (read-only) | Passive scan / p0f | Uses sniff-only approach |
| Web-layer vulnerability discovery | Web App Scan (Nikto/ZAP) | Understands HTTP, AJAX, REST, GraphQL |
| Auditing against CIS | Compliance scan (Nessus/Qualys) | Compares to baseline ruleset |
| Find rogue wireless APs | Wireless scan (Kismet) | Operates at RF layer, not IP |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Scan Time Estimation

**Problem:** A penetration tester is scanning a /24 subnet (254 hosts) targeting all 1,000 most common ports. The average RTT is **120 ms**, and the Nmap parallelism is set to **50**. Calculate the total scan time.

**Solution:**

$$T_{scan} = N_{ports} \times R_{max} \times \frac{1}{P_{parallel}}$$

$$\Rightarrow T_{scan} = 1{,}000 \times 0.120 \times \frac{1}{50}$$

$$\Rightarrow T_{scan} = 120 \times 0.02$$

$$\Rightarrow T_{scan} = 2.4 \text{ seconds}$$

If parallelism is reduced to 10 (to evade IDS rate detection):

$$T_{scan} = 1{,}000 \times 0.120 \times \frac{1}{10} = 12 \text{ seconds}$$

**Trade-off demonstrated:** Lowering parallelism **increases stealth** (lower $P_{detect}$) but **increases** $T_{scan}$ — a classic security trade-off.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->


## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: TCP Scan Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Attacker sends probe to target port] --> Q1{What flag is set?}
    Q1 -->|SYN| R1{Target replies?}
    R1 -->|SYN+ACK| O1[Port is OPEN - attacker sends RST]
    R1 -->|RST| C1[Port is CLOSED]
    Q1 -->|FIN or NULL or XMAS| R2{Target replies?}
    R2 -->|No response| O2[Port is OPEN RFC 793 silent drop]
    R2 -->|RST| C2[Port is CLOSED]
    Q1 -->|ACK| R3{Target replies?}
    R3 -->|RST| F1[Unfiltered - stateful inspection]
    R3 -->|ICMP unreachable| F2[Filtered - stateless firewall]
    Q1 -->|Empty UDP| R4{Target replies?}
    R4 -->|UDP data| O3[Port is OPEN]
    R4 -->|ICMP Type 3 Code 3| C3[Port is CLOSED]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Idle Scan Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Attacker -->|1 Probe IPID| Zombie
    Attacker -->|2 Spoofed SYN with Zombie IP| Target
    Target -->|3a If OPEN, sends SYN+ACK to Zombie| Zombie
    Zombie -->|3b Replies RST, IPID increments| Target
    Target -->|4a If CLOSED, sends RST to Zombie| Zombie
    Zombie -->|4b Ignores, IPID unchanged| Target
    Attacker -->|5 Post-test probe IPID| Zombie
    Zombie -->|6 RST with new IPID| Attacker
    Attacker -->|Delta analysis| Result{Delta IPID}
    Result -->|1| Closed[Port CLOSED]
    Result -->|2| Open[Port OPEN]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q1. Differentiate between Active and Passive scanning with one example for each.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
- **Active Scanning** involves directly sending probes/packets to the target to elicit responses (e.g., Nmap SYN scan). The target is *aware* of the interaction.
- **Passive Scanning** involves capturing and analyzing existing traffic without injecting any packets (e.g., p0f for OS detection, Wireshark for service identification). The target is *unaware*.
- Active scanning is faster but noisier; passive is stealthier but limited to observed traffic.

---

**Q2. What is Stealth Scanning? List two techniques to achieve it.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
**Stealth Scanning** refers to scan methodologies designed to minimize detection by Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS), firewalls, and audit logs while still extracting intelligence.
Two techniques:
1. **Idle/Zombie Scan (`nmap -sI <zombie>`):** Spoofs the source IP as a "zombie" host; the target's logs attribute the scan to the zombie, not the real attacker.
2. **Packet Fragmentation (`-f`):** Splits the TCP header across multiple tiny IP fragments to bypass simple packet inspection firewalls that only reassemble full packets above a size threshold.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
> 
> **(a)** Explain in detail the different types of TCP port scanning techniques with the packet flags used and the corresponding target responses. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Describe the Idle (Zombie) Scan in detail. Explain how an attacker uses a zombie host to determine whether a port is open or closed, with the IPID increment logic. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Q3(A)(a):

**1. TCP Connect Scan (`-sT`):**
- Sends a complete SYN → receives SYN+ACK → sends ACK.
- Fully establishes the connection; logged by the target.
- No raw socket privilege required.

**2. SYN Scan / Half-Open Scan (`-sS`):**
- Sends SYN → receives SYN+ACK → sends RST (instead of completing handshake).
- Faster, less logged, requires root.

**3. FIN Scan (`-sF`):**
- Sends packet with only FIN flag.
- Per RFC 793: closed port → RST; open port → silent drop.

**4. NULL Scan (`-sN`):**
- Sends packet with **no flags** set.
- Same response logic as FIN (RFC 793).

**5. XMAS Scan (`-sX`):**
- Sends packet with FIN + PSH + URG flags (packet "lights up like a Christmas tree").
- Same RFC 793 response logic as FIN/NULL.

**6. ACK Scan (`-sA`):**
- Sends ACK flag only.
- Used for firewall mapping: RST = unfiltered; ICMP unreachable = filtered.

**7. Window Scan (`-sW`):**
- Variant of ACK; uses TCP window field to differentiate OS responses.

**8. Idle Scan (`-sI`):** (Discussed below)

### Model Solution for Q3(A)(b):

(Refer to Section 3.1 derivation.)

The attacker uses a third-party "zombie" host with a predictable, incrementing IP Identification (IPID) counter. The two-phase IPID probe reveals:
- Δ IPID = 1 → Port CLOSED
- Δ IPID = 2 → Port OPEN

**Advantages:**
- True anonymity — the target logs the zombie's IP, not the attacker's.
- Bypass whitelists that trust the zombie host.
- Highly stealthy, no direct connection from attacker to target.

**Disadvantages:**
- Requires a suitable idle zombie (must have predictable IPID increment and be reachable).
- Slower (multiple round-trips per port).
- Modern OSes randomize IPID, breaking this technique.

---

> **Q3 (B). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model — Internal Choice Alternative]**
> 
> **(a)** Explain the types of vulnerability scanning. Discuss the difference between authenticated and unauthenticated scanning. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Explain the techniques used in OS fingerprinting and banner grabbing with relevant examples. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Q3(B)(a):

**Types of Vulnerability Scanning:**

1. **Active vs. Passive**
   - *Active:* Sends crafted probes to actively trigger and detect flaws (e.g., OpenVAS, Nessus active scan).
   - *Passive:* Taps network traffic, identifies CVE-prone versions from observed packets (e.g., Tenable PVS).

2. **Authenticated vs. Unauthenticated**
   - *Authenticated:* Uses valid credentials to log in; can inspect deep configuration (patch level, registry, file permissions). Produces low false positives.
   - *Unauthenticated:* Simulates an external attacker with no credentials. Faster but only sees the external-facing surface; higher false positives.

3. **Internal vs. External**
   - *Internal:* Originates from inside the network; tests lateral movement risk.
   - *External:* Originates from outside; tests perimeter defenses.

### Model Solution for Q3(B)(b):

**OS Fingerprinting:**
- **Active:** Sends crafted packets (e.g., FIN probe, bogus flag, ICMP echo) to elicit differing responses from various OS TCP/IP stacks. Analyzes TTL, DF-bit, window size, TOS, etc. Tool: **Nmap `-O`**.
- **Passive:** Sniffs packets from the target and identifies OS based on observed packet characteristics (TTL, MSS, DF-bit, signature matching). Tool: **p0f**.

**Banner Grabbing:**
- Connects to an open port and reads the service's initial greeting or response to a specific command.
- Examples:
  - `nc -v target 80` → reveals "Apache/2.4.41 (Ubuntu)"
  - `telnet target 25` → reveals "220 mail.target.com ESMTP Postfix"
  - HTTP Server header → reveals `Server: nginx/1.18.0`
- Helps identify the service, version, and associated CVEs.
- Countermeasure: Strip or customize banners in production services.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Loss:**
> 1. ❌ Writing "FIN scan is used to bypass firewall" — partially correct. The *real* reason is RFC 793's silent-drop behavior. Examiners expect the **RFC 793 reasoning**, not vague "stealth" answers.
> 2. ❌ Confusing *SYN scan* with *ACK scan* in the response matrix. SYN expects SYN+ACK for open; ACK **always** expects RST (it's probing the firewall, not the port's open state).
> 3. ❌ Stating that Idle Scan "spoofs the attacker's IP" — **wrong**. It spoofs the **zombie's IP** as the source. The attacker never appears in the target's logs.
> 4. ❌ Omitting the Δ IPID = 1 vs. 2 logic for Idle Scan — this is a guaranteed **3-mark question** in KTU and missing it loses 50% of marks on that sub-part.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU ESE:**

- ✅ **Three port states** (from attacker's perspective): **OPEN, CLOSED, FILTERED**.
- ✅ **RFC 793 Rule:** Closed TCP ports MUST respond with RST to any unsolicited segment; open ports silently drop segments with no flags set — this is the foundation of FIN/NULL/XMAS scans.
- ✅ **SYN Scan is the DEFAULT and most common** scan in Nmap; requires root.
- ✅ **TCP Connect Scan** is the fallback when root is unavailable.
- ✅ **UDP Scan** relies on ICMP Type 3 Code 3 (Port Unreachable) to detect closed ports; very slow.
- ✅ **Idle Scan** is the **most stealthy**; uses IPID analysis of a zombie; **no trace of attacker IP** on target.
- ✅ **ACK Scan** does NOT determine open/closed ports — it maps **firewall rule sets**.
- ✅ **XMAS = FIN + PSH + URG** (all flags set).
- ✅ **NULL = no flags at all**.
- ✅ **Authenticated vulnerability scans** = lower false positives, deeper visibility.
- ✅ **Passive scanning** = sniff-only, no injection, used by p0f and Tenable PVS.
- ✅ **Web Application Scanners** (Nikto, ZAP, Burp) target the HTTP layer, not the network layer.
- ✅ **Stealth Techniques:** fragmentation (`-f`), decoys (`-D`), source-port spoofing (`--source-port 53`), timing (`-T0` paranoid to `-T5` insane).
- ✅ **Compliance scanning** maps posture against **CIS Benchmarks, NIST, PCI-DSS**.
- ✅ **Default tools:** Nmap (port/OS), OpenVAS/Nessus (vulnerability), Nikto (web), Kismet (wireless), p0f (passive OS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In the 14-mark question, examiners expect a **table** showing scan type → flag → open response → closed response. Presenting this as a clean matrix in your answer script **secures full marks** because it matches the official valuation key format.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
