topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT755 - CYBER THREAT INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Port Scanning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Port Scanning** is a foundational network reconnaissance technique in cyber threat intelligence wherein an attacker (or security analyst) systematically transmits crafted packets to a range of TCP/UDP port numbers on a target host to enumerate the **state** of each port (open, closed, filtered, or unfiltered), the running **services**, their **versions**, and the underlying **operating system fingerprint**.

In RFC-aligned terminology (RFC 793 – TCP, RFC 768 – UDP), every network service on an IP-addressable host listens for incoming connections on a logical **port** identified by a 16-bit unsigned integer. The total addressable port space is therefore:

$$
\text{Total Ports} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ ports}
$$

These are partitioned into three IANA-defined ranges:

| Port Range | Category | Typical Allocation |
|---|---|---|
| 0 – 1023 | **Well-Known Ports** | HTTP (80), HTTPS (443), SSH (22), FTP (21), SMTP (25), DNS (53) |
| 1024 – 49151 | **Registered Ports** | Application-specific services (e.g., MySQL 3306, RDP 3389) |
| 49152 – 65535 | **Dynamic / Ephemeral Ports** | Used by clients as source ports during outbound connections |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 2: Reconnaissance & Scanning), port scanning is positioned as the **second phase of the Cyber Kill Chain** (post-footprinting and pre-vulnerability analysis). It transitions an attacker from "knowing the target exists" to "knowing the attack surface."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Building Inspector

Imagine you are a **security inspector** walking around a large office building. You cannot enter, but you want to know which **doors and windows** are:

- **Wide open** (an employee is actively using that entrance)
- **Locked from the inside** (a service listening but refusing unknown clients)
- **Bricked up** (the door physically does not exist)
- **Guarded by a sentry** (a firewall drops your knock silently)

You walk around knocking on each door and window — sometimes a polite full conversation (TCP Connect scan), sometimes a quick half-tap (SYN scan), sometimes you tap a *meaningless rhythm* (FIN/NULL/XMAS scans) to see who is fooled.

> **The "knock" is a network packet, the "door" is a TCP/UDP port, the "guard" is a host-based or network firewall, and the building is the target host.**

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters to a KTU student:** Port scanning is the *first* technical exercise in penetration testing labs (Kali Linux + Nmap). Almost every vulnerability scanner (Nessus, OpenVAS, Qualys) uses port scan results as input for the deeper vulnerability phase.

## 1.3 Port States — The Four Cardinal Answers

Every port on a target can return one of the canonical states defined in the Nmap / TCP RFC framework:

| State | Meaning | Packet Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **open** | Application is actively accepting TCP connections or UDP datagrams. | Target replies with **SYN-ACK** (TCP) or **ICMP Port Unreachable** absent (UDP). |
| **closed** | Port is reachable but no service is listening. | Target replies with **RST** (TCP) or **ICMP Port Unreachable** (UDP). |
| **filtered** | A firewall/IDS is blocking the probe. | No response, or **ICMP Administrative Prohibited** (Type 3, Code 13). |
| **unfiltered** | Port is reachable but the probe type cannot determine open/closed (typical of ACK scans). | **RST** returned. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners love asking the difference between `closed` and `filtered`. The single-line answer: **closed = the host itself rejected the probe; filtered = something in between (firewall/IDS) prevented the probe from reaching the host.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The TCP Three-Way Handshake — Prerequisite for Scan Logic

Almost every port scan technique manipulates the **TCP three-way handshake**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Client} &\xrightarrow{\text{SYN (seq=x)}} \text{Server} \\
\text{Client} &\xleftarrow{\text{SYN-ACK (seq=y, ack=x+1)}} \text{Server} \\
\text{Client} &\xrightarrow{\text{ACK (seq=x+1, ack=y+1)}} \text{Server}
\end{aligned}
$$

The result is a fully established connection (state = **ESTABLISHED**). Port scan techniques selectively complete, abort, or fabricate parts of this handshake to elicit different responses from the target.

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Port Scanning Techniques

### 2.2.1 TCP Connect Scan (`-sT`)
- **Mechanism:** Full OS-level `connect()` system call completes the 3-way handshake.
- **Pro:** No raw socket privilege required; always works.
- **Con:** Highly noisy — completes a full TCP session, easily logged on the target.
- **Detection footprint:** Maximum.

### 2.2.2 SYN Scan / Half-Open Scan (`-sS`)
- **Mechanism:** Attacker sends SYN, receives SYN-ACK, then sends **RST** instead of ACK.
- **Pro:** Connection never fully established — target's application does not log the connection.
- **Con:** Requires raw socket privilege (root).
- **Detection footprint:** Low. This is the **default scan** in Nmap when run as root.

### 2.2.3 FIN Scan (`-sF`)
- **Mechanism:** Sends a TCP segment with only the **FIN** flag set (no SYN, no ACK).
- **Logic:** RFC 793 states: a closed port must respond with RST to any non-SYN packet; an open port silently **drops** the packet.
- **Pro:** Bypasses basic stateless firewalls that filter only SYN packets.
- **Con:** Ineffective against Windows targets (Windows violates RFC 793 by sending RST regardless).

### 2.2.4 NULL Scan (`-sN`)
- **Mechanism:** Sends a TCP segment with **no flags** set.
- **Logic:** Same RFC 793 differential — closed → RST, open → drop.

### 2.2.5 XMAS Scan (`-sX`)
- **Mechanism:** Sends a TCP segment with **FIN + PSH + URG** flags set (the segment "lights up like a Christmas tree").
- **Logic:** Same RFC 793 differential as FIN/NULL.

### 2.2.6 ACK Scan / Firewall Map (`-sA`)
- **Mechanism:** Sends a packet with only the **ACK** flag set.
- **Logic:** Does NOT determine open vs. closed; it determines **filtered vs. unfiltered**. Useful for mapping firewall rule sets.
- **Response:** RST → unfiltered; no response (or ICMP unreachable) → filtered.

### 2.2.7 UDP Scan (`-sU`)
- **Mechanism:** Sends empty UDP datagrams to each port.
- **Logic:** Open → no response (or service-specific reply); closed → **ICMP Port Unreachable (Type 3, Code 3)**.
- **Pro:** UDP services are heavily overlooked in hardening (DNS, SNMP, TFTP).
- **Con:** Very slow; ICMP rate-limiting by hosts; unreliable.

### 2.2.8 Idle / Zombie Scan (`-sI`)
- **Mechanism:** Spoofs source IP using a "zombie" host with predictable IPID sequence numbers. The attacker infers target port state by observing the zombie's IPID increment.
- **Pro:** Attacker IP never touches the target; near-perfect anonymity.
- **Con:** Requires finding a suitable idle zombie host.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Although port scanning is qualitative, the following quantitative relations are **exam-favorite** numerical problems.

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_{\text{ports}} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | Total TCP or UDP ports | Compute scan duration |
| 2 | $N_{\text{probes}} = (P_{\text{range}}) \times (N_{\text{hosts}})$ | Number of probes in a scan | Bandwidth estimation |
| 3 | $T_{\text{scan}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{probes}}}{R_{\text{rate}}}$ | Scan time = probes ÷ rate | $R_{\text{rate}}$ in probes/sec |
| 4 | $P_{\text{detect}} = 1 - (1-p)^N$ | Cumulative detection probability over $N$ probes, each independently detected with probability $p$ | IDS/evasion math |
| 5 | $\lambda_{\text{ICMP-limit}}$ | ICMP unreachable rate-limit (RFC 1812: ~80/sec) | Explains why UDP scan is slow |
| 6 | $\Delta_{\text{IPID}}$ | IPID increment between two probes | Idle/Zombie scan inference |
| 7 | $N_{\text{hands}} = 3$ | Packets in TCP 3-way handshake | Connect scan cost |
| 8 | $N_{\text{packets}} = 2$ | Packets in SYN (half-open) scan | Stealth scan cost |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Defensive use:** Continuous external port scanning (via Tenable Nessus, Qualys VMDR, Rapid7 InsightVM) feeds the **attack surface management (ASM)** pipeline.
- **SIEM/IDS correlation:** Splunk, Snort, Suricata, and Zeek generate alerts on burst patterns matching port-scan signatures (e.g., Snort SID 1:1228 for SYN scans).
- **Compliance:** PCI-DSS Requirement 11.3 mandates **quarterly** external port scans against the CDE (Cardholder Data Environment).
- **Threat intelligence:** Tools like Shodan, Censys, and ZoomEye perform **passive port enumeration** of the entire IPv4 internet, providing CTI analysts with external exposure telemetry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cumulative Probability of Detection by an IDS

A common KTU numerical problem states: *Each probe in a port scan is independently detected by the IDS with probability $p$. What is the probability that the scan is detected after $N$ probes?*

**Step 1 — Define the complementary event.**
Let $A$ be the event "scan is detected at least once." It is easier to first compute $A^c$ = "scan evades detection on every single probe."

**Step 2 — Probability of a single missed probe.**
If a single probe is detected with probability $p$, it is missed with probability $(1-p)$.

**Step 3 — Independence across $N$ probes.**
Because probes are independent (a standard assumption unless a stateful IDS correlates them),

$$
P(A^c) = (1-p)^{N}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the complement rule.**

$$
P(A) = 1 - P(A^c) = 1 - (1-p)^{N}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example (KTU style).**
Given $p = 0.05$ and $N = 60$ probes (a small SYN scan against a /24 host's top 60 ports):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(A) &= 1 - (1-0.05)^{60} \\
&= 1 - (0.95)^{60} \\
&= 1 - 0.04609 \\
&\approx 0.9539 \\
&\approx 95.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Even a 5% per-probe detection rate yields a 95% overall detection rate after 60 probes — explaining why **stealth scans** (slower, randomized) are used in mature red-team operations.

## 3.2 Derivation: Time to Complete a Full TCP SYN Scan

**Step 1 — Variables.**
Let $N_h$ = number of target hosts, $N_p$ = ports per host, $R$ = probe rate in probes/sec, $k$ = average retries per filtered port (typically 1, sometimes 2).

**Step 2 — Base number of probes.**

$$
N_{\text{base}} = N_h \times N_p
$$

**Step 3 — Effective probes with retries.**

$$
N_{\text{eff}} = N_{\text{base}} \times k
$$

**Step 4 — Time formula.**

$$
T = \frac{N_{\text{eff}}}{R}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked example.**
Scan a /24 subnet (254 usable hosts) for the **top 1000 TCP ports** at 1000 probes/sec, with 1 retry:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{base}} &= 254 \times 1000 = 254{,}000 \\
N_{\text{eff}} &= 254{,}000 \times 1 = 254{,}000 \\
T &= \frac{254{,}000}{1000} = 254 \text{ seconds} \approx 4.23 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

If you add 1 retry ($k=2$) and drop to 100 probes/sec (stealth mode):

$$
T = \frac{254{,}000 \times 2}{100} = 5080 \text{ seconds} \approx 84.7 \text{ min}
$$

## 3.3 Idle (Zombie) Scan — IPID Inference Logic

Let $Z$ be the zombie, $T$ be the target, $A$ be the attacker.

**Round 1:** Attacker → Zombie: SYN/ACK.
Zombie (un-requested) sends RST. Attacker records zombie IPID = $I_1$.

**Round 2:** Attacker **spoofs** a SYN packet with source IP = Z, destination = T:port $p$.
- If $p$ is **open** on T → T sends SYN-ACK to Z → Z replies with RST → Z's IPID **increments by 2** (one for the unexpected SYN-ACK, one for the attacker's earlier probe).
- If $p$ is **closed** on T → T sends RST to Z → Z **silently drops** it → Z's IPID **increments by 1** (only the attacker's probe).

**Round 3:** Attacker → Zombie: SYN/ACK again. Records $I_2$.

$$
\Delta I = I_2 - I_1 =
\begin{cases}
3 & \text{if port } p \text{ is open on } T \\
2 & \text{if port } p \text{ is closed on } T
\end{cases}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Scan Technique vs. Target Behavior

| Scan Type | Flag(s) Sent | Open Port Reply | Closed Port Reply | Filtered Reply | Stealth Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| TCP Connect | SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK | Completes handshake | RST/ACK | No response | ★☆☆☆☆ |
| SYN (Half-Open) | SYN → RST | SYN-ACK | RST/ACK | No response | ★★★☆☆ |
| FIN | FIN | Dropped (no reply) | RST/ACK | No response | ★★★★☆ |
| NULL | (none) | Dropped | RST/ACK | No response | ★★★★☆ |
| XMAS | FIN+PSH+URG | Dropped | RST/ACK | No response | ★★★★☆ |
| ACK | ACK | RST (always) | RST | No response / ICMP | ★★☆☆☆ |
| UDP | (empty UDP) | UDP reply or silent | ICMP Type 3 Code 3 | No response / ICMP | ★★★☆☆ |
| Idle/Zombie | (spoofed SYN) | IPID+2 vs. +1 | IPID+1 | No response | ★★★★★ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 TCP Three-Way Handshake — Attacker's View

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant A as Attacker
    participant T as Target Port
    A->>T: SYN (seq=x)
    alt Port Open
        T-->>A: SYN-ACK (seq=y, ack=x+1)
        A->>T: RST (half-open scan) OR ACK (full connect)
    else Port Closed
        T-->>A: RST-ACK
    else Port Filtered
        T--xA: No Response
    end
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PECCT755]`
**Differentiate between SYN scan and TCP Connect scan. Why is SYN scan preferred for stealth reconnaissance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | TCP Connect Scan | SYN Scan |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Completes full 3-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) | Sends SYN, receives SYN-ACK, then sends RST (no ACK) |
| Privilege | Any user (uses OS `connect()`) | Requires raw socket (root/admin) |
| Logging on target | Connection completes → application/server logs the session | Connection never established → usually NOT logged by application |
| Stealth | High noise footprint | Low noise footprint (half-open) |

**Why SYN is stealthier (1 mark):** Because the TCP connection is never fully established, most application-level services (web servers, SSH daemons) do not write the half-open attempt to their access logs, making it harder for defenders to identify the source of the scan.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PECCT755]`
**Explain the difference between a "closed" port and a "filtered" port. What kind of response does an attacker observe in each case for a TCP SYN probe?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Closed port (1 mark):** The host receives the SYN probe and replies with a **TCP RST (Reset)** segment, indicating the port exists but no service is listening.
- **Filtered port (1 mark):** A firewall, ACL, or IDS between the attacker and the host drops the packet. The attacker observes **no response at all** (or sometimes an ICMP Administrative Prohibited message, Type 3 Code 13).
- **Differentiation (1 mark):** A `closed` port responds with RST; a `filtered` port produces silence. The presence of a reply is the key discriminator.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, PECCT755]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the TCP three-way handshake. Show how a **SYN (half-open) scan** manipulates this handshake to enumerate open ports. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** List and briefly explain **five different port scanning techniques**. Compare their **stealth level** and **information disclosed**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) TCP Three-Way Handshake and SYN Scan (7 marks):**

The TCP three-way handshake is the standardized procedure (RFC 793) used to establish a reliable connection between a client and a server:

1. **Step 1 — SYN:** The client sends a TCP segment with the **SYN** flag set and an initial sequence number (e.g., $x$). This is a request to synchronize sequence numbers and open a connection.
2. **Step 2 — SYN-ACK:** The server, if it has a service listening on that port, replies with a segment having both **SYN** and **ACK** flags set, acknowledging the client's $x$ by sending $ack = x+1$ and offering its own sequence number $y$.
3. **Step 3 — ACK:** The client sends a final segment with the **ACK** flag set and $ack = y+1$. The connection enters the **ESTABLISHED** state on both sides.

**How SYN scan manipulates this handshake (4 marks):**

In a SYN (half-open) scan, the attacker **intentionally omits the third step**:
- The attacker sends the **SYN** packet (Step 1).
- If the port is **open**, the target replies with **SYN-ACK** (Step 2). The attacker then immediately sends a **TCP RST** instead of the final ACK. The connection is torn down before becoming ESTABLISHED, so the target application does not log the connection.
- If the port is **closed**, the target replies with **RST/ACK**, telling the attacker the port has no listener.
- If the port is **filtered**, the attacker receives **no response** at all (the packet was dropped by a firewall).

```
Attacker            Target
   |  --SYN(x)---->  |
   |                 |  (port open)
   |  <--SYN-ACK--   |
   |  -----RST---->  |   (Attacker aborts; no ESTABLISHED state)
   |                 |
   |  --SYN(x)---->  |
   |                 |  (port closed)
   |  <----RST-----  |
```

This half-open behavior is what makes SYN scanning stealthy and the **default scan in Nmap** when run with root privileges.

---

**(b) Five Port Scanning Techniques (7 marks):**

**1. TCP Connect Scan (`-sT`)** *(1.5 marks)*
- Performs a full 3-way handshake using the OS `connect()` syscall.
- Does not require raw-socket privilege.
- **Stealth:** Very low — every connection is completed and logged by the target application.
- **Information disclosed:** Open vs. closed ports (filtered inferred by timeout).

**2. SYN / Half-Open Scan (`-sS`)** *(1.5 marks)*
- Sends SYN, on SYN-ACK responds with RST; never completes handshake.
- Requires raw-socket privilege.
- **Stealth:** High — application does not log the half-open attempt.
- **Information disclosed:** Open, closed, filtered.

**3. FIN Scan (`-sF`)** *(1 mark)*
- Sends a packet with only the FIN flag set.
- Relies on RFC 793: closed ports send RST; open ports silently drop the packet.
- **Stealth:** Very high — bypasses stateless packet filters.
- **Information disclosed:** Open vs. closed (not filtered reliably). **Windows violation:** Windows sends RST regardless, so FIN scan misreports.

**4. NULL Scan (`-sN`)** *(1 mark)*
- Sends a TCP segment with **no flags** set.
- Same RFC 793 differential as FIN.
- **Stealth:** Very high; rarely detected by signature-based IDS.

**5. XMAS Scan (`-sX`)** *(1 mark)*
- Sends a packet with **FIN + PSH + URG** flags lit ("lit like a Christmas tree").
- Same RFC 793 differential.
- **Stealth:** Visually obvious on the wire but bypasses naïve firewall rules; **modern IDS detect it trivially**.

**6. (Bonus) ACK Scan (`-sA`)** *(not required but adds depth)*
- Sends ACK packets; used for **firewall rule mapping** (filtered vs. unfiltered), not open vs. closed.

**Comparative table (final 1 mark):**

| Scan | Open Port Reveal | Closed Port Reveal | Stealth | Privilege |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Connect | Yes (full) | Yes (RST) | Low | User |
| SYN | Yes (SYN-ACK) | Yes (RST) | High | Root |
| FIN | Inferred (drop) | Yes (RST) | Very High | Root |
| NULL | Inferred (drop) | Yes (RST) | Very High | Root |
| XMAS | Inferred (drop) | Yes (RST) | Very High | Root |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PECCT755]` *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a)** Describe the **Idle (Zombie) Scan** technique. Explain step-by-step how an attacker can enumerate open ports on a target without ever sending a packet from their own IP address. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss **countermeasures and detection methods** for port scanning. How can a defender use **Snort/Suricata rules**, **firewall rate-limiting**, and **port scan detection heuristics** to mitigate reconnaissance? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Idle (Zombie) Scan — Step-by-Step (7 marks):**

The Idle Scan, originally described by Antirez (creator of Nmap), uses a third-party "zombie" host with a **predictable IP Identification (IPID) counter** to probe a target. The attacker's IP never appears in the target's logs.

**Prerequisite (1 mark):** The zombie must be idle (no other traffic) and use an OS that increments IPID by 1 for every packet it sends (older Windows, older Linux, some printers).

**Step 1 — Probe the Zombie (1 mark):**
The attacker sends a **SYN-ACK** to the zombie. The zombie, having no matching session, replies with a **RST**. The attacker records the zombie's IPID = $I_1$.

**Step 2 — Spoofed SYN to Target (2 marks):**
The attacker crafts a TCP SYN packet with:
- Source IP = **Zombie's IP** (spoofed)
- Destination IP = Target's IP, Destination Port = $p$

The attacker sends this to the target.

**Step 3 — Target's Response (Inferred) (2 marks):**
- If port $p$ is **open** on the target → target sends **SYN-ACK to the zombie** (thinking the zombie initiated the connection) → zombie, not expecting it, replies with **RST** to the target → zombie's IPID increments by **1** due to this extra RST.
- If port $p$ is **closed** → target sends **RST to the zombie** → zombie silently drops unsolicited RSTs → zombie's IPID does **not** increment.
- If port $p$ is **filtered** → target sends nothing → zombie's IPID does not increment.

**Step 4 — Re-probe the Zombie (1 mark):**
The attacker again sends a SYN-ACK to the zombie and records $I_2$.

**Step 5 — Inference:**

$$
\Delta I = I_2 - I_1 =
\begin{cases}
2 & \text{(port closed or filtered — IPID incremented only by attacker's probe)} \\
3 & \text{(port open — IPID incremented by attacker's probe + zombie's RST to target)}
\end{cases}
$$

> **Key advantage:** The target's logs show packets from the zombie's IP, **not** the attacker's IP — providing strong anonymity.

---

**(b) Countermeasures & Detection (7 marks):**

**1. Network-Based IDS/IPS Signatures (2 marks):**
Snort and Suricata include built-in port-scan preprocessors. Example Snort rules:

- `alert tcp $EXTERNAL_NET any -> $HOME_NET any (msg:"SCAN SYN"; flags:S; threshold: type both, track by_src, count 50, seconds 10; sid:1000001;)`
- `alert tcp $EXTERNAL_NET any -> $HOME_NET any (msg:"SCAN FIN"; flags:F; sid:1000002;)`

These rules trigger when an excessive number of SYN (or FIN, NULL, XMAS) packets originate from a single source within a short window.

**2. Firewall Rate-Limiting & Stateful Inspection (2 marks):**
- Modern stateful firewalls (iptables, pfSense, Cisco ASA) track half-open connections per source IP. A burst of SYNs without completing the handshake triggers a **SYN-flood / scan detection**.
- Configurable thresholds (`--syn-rate-limit` in iptables) drop or tarpit (delay) the source.
- Example: `iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --syn -m limit --limit 10/s --limit-burst 20 -j DROP`

**3. Port Scan Detection Heuristics (2 marks):**
- **Vertical scan detection:** Single source IP probes many ports on one host → suspicious.
- **Horizontal scan detection:** Single source IP probes the same port across many hosts → worm or attacker sweep.
- **Block detection:** Source probes many hosts across many ports → "port scan" pattern.

**4. Active Response (1 mark):**
- **Port knocking:** Service remains invisible until the client sends a specific sequence of closed-port knocks.
- **Tarpitting:** Firewall deliberately delays RSTs, slowing the attacker's scan.
- **Blackholing:** Automatically null-route the source IP for a configurable duration.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing `closed` and `filtered`:** Students frequently write "filtered means the port is closed." This loses 1–2 marks. *Correct:* filtered means a firewall/IDS is in the path; the host itself may have the port open.
> 2. **Forgetting the Windows anomaly in FIN/NULL/XMAS scans:** Not mentioning that **Windows violates RFC 793** by responding with RST to all such probes loses 1 mark on stealth-scan questions.
> 3. **Skipping the third packet in SYN scan:** Many students draw only SYN and SYN-ACK in the SYN scan diagram, omitting the attacker's RST that aborts the handshake. The RST is the *defining* packet of a half-open scan.
> 4. **No mention of raw-socket privilege:** SYN and Idle scans require root. Forgetting this loses 1 mark when comparing scan techniques.
> 5. **Idle scan IPID math:** Writing $\Delta I = 1$ for closed and $\Delta I = 2$ for open is the *common wrong answer*. The correct values are **2 and 3** because the attacker's first probe (Step 1) already accounts for the +1 baseline.
> 6. **Generic answers on countermeasures:** Listing "use a firewall" without specifying Snort SIDs, iptables rate-limit syntax, or vertical/horizontal heuristics loses at least 2 marks in 14-mark questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Port Scanning**

- **Definition:** Active reconnaissance technique that probes TCP/UDP ports to determine their **state** (open / closed / filtered / unfiltered).
- **Port range:** 0–65535 (Well-known 0–1023, Registered 1024–49151, Dynamic/Ephemeral 49152–65535).
- **TCP Connect Scan:** Full 3-way handshake, no privilege required, **noisy**, logged by target.
- **SYN (Half-Open) Scan:** RST after SYN-ACK, requires root, **stealthy**, default in Nmap.
- **FIN / NULL / XMAS Scans:** Exploit RFC 793 differential (closed → RST, open → drop). **Bypasses simple firewalls. Fails against Windows.**
- **ACK Scan:** Maps **filtered vs. unfiltered**; does not determine open/closed.
- **UDP Scan:** Empty UDP datagram; closed → ICMP Type 3 Code 3; very slow due to ICMP rate-limiting (RFC 1812: ~80/sec).
- **Idle (Zombie) Scan:** Spoofed SYN via idle zombie; IPID increment of 2 (closed) vs. 3 (open); perfect anonymity.
- **Detection math:** $P(\text{detect}) = 1 - (1-p)^{N}$ for $N$ independent probes, per-probe detection probability $p$.
- **Scan time math:** $T = (N_h \times N_p \times k) / R$ where $k$ = retries, $R$ = probe rate.
- **Countermeasures:** Stateful firewalls, IDS/IPS signatures (Snort, Suricata), rate-limiting, port knocking, tarpitting, blackholing.
- **Compliance link:** PCI-DSS 11.3 mandates quarterly external port scans; ISO 27001 A.12.6.1 mandates technical vulnerability management.
- **Tools (KTU lab favorites):** **Nmap**, **Masscan**, **Zmap** (internet-scale), **Hping3**, **Netcat**.
- **Exam mantra:** "Closed = RST received. Filtered = silence. Open = SYN-ACK received (TCP) or service-specific reply (UDP)."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Protocol Analyser
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT755 - CYBER THREAT INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Network Protocol Analyser

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Network Protocol Analyser** (also called a **Packet Sniffer**, **Network Sniffer**, or **Protocol Decoder**) is a software or hardware tool that intercepts, logs, decodes, and analyses network traffic flowing across a digital communication channel. It operates by placing the host's Network Interface Card (NIC) into **promiscuous mode**, enabling it to capture every packet traversing the medium, not merely those addressed to the local host.

In the context of **Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI)**, a protocol analyser is classified as a **passive reconnaissance and network forensics instrument**, mapped to the **MITRE ATT&CK Technique T1040 – Network Sniffing**, used extensively for threat hunting, incident response, malware traffic analysis, and exfiltration detection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PECCT755 Module 2)**
> The Network Protocol Analyser forms the foundational layer of **network-based threat intelligence gathering**. It is the primary tool used by SOC analysts and CTI professionals to extract Indicators of Compromise (IoCs) such as IP addresses, domain names, protocol anomalies, and payload signatures from live or captured network flows.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a highly secure post office where every letter and parcel that passes through is photocopied, the envelope metadata (sender, receiver, postmark, weight, route) is recorded into a logbook, and the contents are catalogued — all without the senders or receivers ever knowing. A network protocol analyser performs exactly this function in the digital realm.

| Real-World Analogy | Network Protocol Analyser Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Postal sorting facility | Network switch / router |
| Envelope address | IP header (source/destination) |
| Postal stamp & date | Protocol header fields (TTL, timestamp) |
| Letter contents | Packet payload (data) |
| Photocopy machine | Packet capture buffer |
| Inspector's logbook | Decoded trace file (.pcap / .pcapng) |
| Inspector with X-ray vision | Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) engine |

### 1.3 Key Terminology

- **Packet**: A formatted unit of data carried across a packet-switched network, consisting of a **header** (control information) and a **payload** (actual data).
- **Capture**: The act of intercepting and storing raw bytes from a network interface.
- **Decode**: The process of parsing the captured bytes against known protocol specifications (RFCs) to display human-readable fields.
- **Promiscuous Mode**: An NIC operational mode in which the card accepts *all* frames it sees on the wire, bypassing the destination MAC address filter.
- **pcap / pcapng**: Standard packet capture file formats (libpcap format) used to store captured traffic for offline analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Performance Metric**: Modern protocol analysers are benchmarked in terms of **capture rate (packets per second)**, **drop rate (percentage of frames missed)**, and **decode latency (microseconds per frame)**. A typical enterprise tool like Wireshark can capture up to **~1 Gbps** on commodity hardware.

### 1.4 Classification of Protocol Analysers

| Class | Examples | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Software-based** | Wireshark, tcpdump, TShark | General analysis, education, SOC operations |
| **Hardware-based** | NetScout, Fluke Networks OptiView | High-speed carrier-grade (10/40/100 Gbps) |
| **Cloud-based** | VPC Flow Logs, AWS Traffic Mirroring | Cloud-native forensics |
| **Inline / Active** | Suricata (IDS mode), Zeek | Threat detection + blocking |
| **Passive / Tap-based** | Wireshark + hardware tap | Stealthy observation, no interference |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Protocol Analyser

A network protocol analyser functions through a layered architecture, each component performing a distinct role in the capture-decode-display pipeline.

#### Step 1: Hardware Layer — Promiscuous Mode Activation
The NIC's driver is instructed via OS APIs (e.g., Linux `socket(PF_PACKET, ...)`) to accept all frames. The NIC's MAC address filter is disabled at the hardware register level.

#### Step 2: Kernel Layer — Packet Capture Driver
A kernel-level library (e.g., **libpcap** on Unix/Linux, **Npcap/WinPcap** on Windows) creates a raw socket and copies incoming frames from kernel space into a userspace circular buffer using zero-copy techniques such as `mmap()` to minimize context-switch overhead.

#### Step 3: Decode Engine — Protocol Dissection
The captured bytes are parsed against a stack of protocol dissectors arranged in a tree structure. Each dissector identifies its protocol by inspecting the appropriate header offset (e.g., the 13th byte of an Ethernet frame for EtherType, the 10th byte of an IP header for protocol number).

#### Step 4: Statistics & Display Layer
Decoded information is rendered in a GUI (e.g., Wireshark's three-pane view: Packet List, Packet Details, Packet Bytes) or emitted as text via CLI tools (e.g., `tcpdump -X`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for CTI**: Understanding this pipeline allows an analyst to know *where* in the chain a tool might fail — for example, a NIC in non-promiscuous mode will silently drop unicast traffic not addressed to it, missing **lateral movement** indicators that would otherwise be visible.

### 2.2 Two Categories of Filters

- **Capture Filters (BPF — Berkeley Packet Filter syntax)**: Applied *before* capture to reduce volume; operates at the kernel/driver level. Example: `tcp port 80 and host 192.168.1.10`.
- **Display Filters**: Applied *after* capture to refine the view; offers richer protocol-aware expressions. Example: `http.request.uri contains "cmd.exe"`.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarises all essential formulas, constants, and reference data required for this topic. The notation `\vert` is used in place of the vertical pipe to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula / Constant | Unit / Value | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Packet size** | $L_{packet} = L_{header} + L_{payload}$ | Bytes | Determines buffer sizing |
| 2 | **Maximum Ethernet payload (MTU)** | $MTU_{Ethernet} = 1500$ | Bytes | Largest unfragmented IP datagram |
| 3 | **Maximum IP datagram (incl. header)** | $L_{max}^{IP} = 65535$ | Bytes | IPv4 total length field ceiling |
| 4 | **IPv4 header length** | $IHL = 4 \times \vert IHL\_field \vert$ bits | Bytes (20–60) | Locates payload start |
| 5 | **TCP header minimum length** | $L_{header}^{TCP} = 20$ | Bytes (20–60 with options) | Locates TCP payload |
| 6 | **UDP header fixed length** | $L_{header}^{UDP} = 8$ | Bytes | Locates UDP payload |
| 7 | **Capture throughput** | $T_{cap} = \dfrac{N_{pkts}}{t_{capture}}$ | Packets/sec | Tool performance benchmarking |
| 8 | **Drop rate** | $D_{rate} = \dfrac{N_{dropped}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Capture fidelity metric |
| 9 | **Bandwidth utilization** | $U = \dfrac{L_{payload} \times 8}{RTT \times B}$ | Ratio (0–1) | Capacity planning |
| 10 | **Capture buffer occupancy** | $B_{occ} = \dfrac{N_{captured} \times \bar{L}_{packet}}{B_{max}}$ | Fraction | Memory planning |
| 11 | **Standard well-known port range** | $0 \le p \le 1023$ | Port number | Service identification |
| 12 | **Registered port range** | $1024 \le p \le 49151$ | Port number | Application services |
| 13 | **Dynamic / ephemeral port range** | $49152 \le p \le 65535$ | Port number | Client-side source ports |
| 14 | **Bit-to-byte conversion** | $N_{bytes} = \dfrac{N_{bits}}{8}$ | Bytes | Packet-size calculations |
| 15 | **Encoding ratio (compression viewpoint)** | $\eta = \dfrac{L_{payload}}{L_{header} + L_{payload}}$ | Ratio (0–1) | Protocol efficiency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**: All the above metrics are evaluated by Wireshark's **Statistics** menu (I/O graphs, Conversations, Endpoints, Expert Infos) and form the quantitative basis of any **network forensic report** submitted as part of CTI deliverables.

### 2.4 Promiscuous vs Monitor Mode — A Key Distinction

| Feature | Promiscuous Mode | Monitor Mode (RF Monitor) |
|---|---|---|
| Layer | OSI Layer 2 (Data Link) | OSI Layer 1 (Physical) |
| Use case | Wired Ethernet sniffing | Wi-Fi (802.11) frame capture |
| Captures | All Ethernet frames on segment | 802.11 management + control + data frames |
| Hardware support | Universal | Requires chipset + driver support (e.g., Atheros, Ralink) |
| Detection risk | Low on switched networks with port mirroring | Detectable via Wi-Fi anomaly detectors |

### 2.5 Use Cases in Cyber Threat Intelligence

1. **Threat Hunting**: Searching for known IoCs (malicious IPs, domains, hashes) inside historical pcaps.
2. **Incident Response**: Reconstructing the timeline of an attack (e.g., Cobalt Strike beacon traffic, DNS tunneling).
3. **Malware Traffic Analysis**: Identifying C2 (Command & Control) callbacks by examining beacon intervals and payload patterns.
4. **Exfiltration Detection**: Spotting large outbound flows, unusual protocol-port pairings (e.g., DNS-over-HTTP).
5. **Lateral Movement Detection**: Observing SMB, RDP, WinRM, and PsExec traffic across internal subnets.
6. **Baseline Establishment**: Profiling "normal" traffic to later detect deviations (anomaly-based detection).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivative Derivation: Locating the Application Payload

A foundational skill tested in KTU examinations is locating the exact byte offset of the application-layer payload in a captured packet. The following exhaustive derivation shows how a protocol analyser computes this offset.

#### Given
A captured Ethernet/IPv4/TCP frame with the following observed header field values:
- IPv4 `IHL` field = `5` (in 32-bit words)
- TCP `Data Offset` field = `5` (in 32-bit words)

#### To Find
The byte offset of the first byte of the application payload, measured from the start of the Ethernet frame.

#### Step 1 — Establish the fixed Ethernet header size
The Ethernet II frame header (destination MAC + source MAC + EtherType) is fixed.

$$L_{eth} = 6 + 6 + 2 = 14 \text{ bytes}$$

#### Step 2 — Convert IPv4 IHL field from words to bytes
The `IHL` field counts 32-bit words. The KTU board expects the conversion explicitly.

$$L_{IPv4} = IHL \times 4 = 5 \times 4 = 20 \text{ bytes}$$

#### Step 3 — Convert TCP Data Offset from words to bytes
TCP's `Data Offset` field also counts 32-bit words.

$$L_{TCP} = DataOffset \times 4 = 5 \times 4 = 20 \text{ bytes}$$

#### Step 4 — Compute the cumulative header length

$$L_{header}^{total} = L_{eth} + L_{IPv4} + L_{TCP} = 14 + 20 + 20 = 54 \text{ bytes}$$

#### Step 5 — Payload begins immediately after the TCP header
Because Wireshark and tcpdump count bytes from the start of the frame (offset 0), the first application payload byte is located at:

$$\text{offset}_{payload} = L_{header}^{total} = 54 \text{ bytes}$$

#### Step 6 — Generalised formula for any IP/TCP frame

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{offset}_{payload} &= L_{eth} + (IHL_{IP} \times 4) + (DataOffset_{TCP} \times 4) \\
&= 14 + 4 \times \vert IHL_{IP} \vert + 4 \times \vert DataOffset_{TCP} \vert
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: This offset is the single most important quantity for a CTI analyst. It is the byte index at which the protocol analyser begins reading the application-layer protocol (HTTP, TLS, DNS, SMB, etc.), and is the same index a YARA rule or Snort signature would target.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Capture Drop Rate

#### Given
A SOC engineer runs `tcpdump` on a 1 Gbps link for 60 seconds and observes the following:
- Total frames presented by the NIC: $N_{total} = 2{,}500{,}000$
- Frames recorded into the pcap: $N_{captured} = 2{,}200{,}000$

#### Step 1 — Compute the number of dropped frames

$$N_{dropped} = N_{total} - N_{captured} = 2{,}500{,}000 - 2{,}200{,}000 = 300{,}000 \text{ frames}$$

#### Step 2 — Apply the drop-rate formula from the cheat sheet

$$D_{rate} = \dfrac{N_{dropped}}{N_{total}} \times 100 = \dfrac{300{,}000}{2{,}500{,}000} \times 100 = 12\%$$

#### Step 3 — Interpretation
A drop rate above **1–2 %** is considered unacceptable in forensic capture contexts, as it may cause the loss of critical IoCs. The engineer should either reduce the capture filter scope, increase the ring buffer, or migrate to a hardware tap with line-rate capture.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Protocol-to-Port Mapping for CTI

The following matrix is essential for protocol-analyser-based threat hunting. Each row links an application protocol to its standard transport-layer port, the protocol dissector Wireshark uses, and a representative CTI use case.

| Application Protocol | Default Port | Transport | Wireshark Dissector | CTI Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| HTTP | 80 | TCP | `http` | Detect web-shell uploads, plaintext credentials |
| HTTPS / TLS | 443 | TCP | `tls` | Inspect SNI / JA3 fingerprint for C2 detection |
| DNS | 53 | UDP/TCP | `dns` | Identify DNS tunneling, DGA domains |
| SMB / CIFS | 445 | TCP | `smb` | Detect lateral movement, EternalBlue exploits |
| RDP | 3389 | TCP | `rdp` | Identify brute-force / unauthorized remote access |
| SSH | 22 | TCP | `ssh` | Detect unauthorised remote administration |
| FTP | 20, 21 | TCP | `ftp` | Locate plaintext exfiltration |
| SMTP | 25 | TCP | `smtp` | Trace phishing email delivery |
| Kerberos | 88 | UDP/TCP | `kerberos` | Spot Kerberoasting, Pass-the-Ticket |
| LDAP | 389, 636 | TCP | `ldap` | Detect AD enumeration |
| RDP-over-TLS | 3389 | TCP | `rdp` + `tls` | Encrypted session analysis |
| WinRM | 5985, 5986 | TCP | `http` / `tls` | Lateral movement via PSRemoting |

### 3.4 Bandwidth Utilization Derivation

A protocol analyser measures bandwidth utilisation by sampling the packet stream over a fixed time window. Given $N_{pkts}$ packets with average size $\bar{L}_{packet}$ bytes captured over a period $\Delta t$ seconds:

$$B_{util} = \dfrac{N_{pkts} \times \bar{L}_{packet} \times 8}{\Delta t \times B_{link}} \quad \text{(dimensionless ratio)}$$

where $B_{link}$ is the nominal link capacity in bits per second. Multiplying by 100 yields percentage utilisation. A sustained $B_{util} > 0.70$ is the classical heuristic threshold for triggering capacity and security reviews.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram: Protocol Analyser Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the end-to-end functional architecture of a software-based network protocol analyser (Wireshark-class) and its interaction with the captured network.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph netA[Network Segment Under Observation]
        hostA[Source Host A]
        hostB[Destination Host B]
        switchX[Switch with Port Mirroring SPAN]
        hostA --> switchX
        switchX --> hostB
    end

    subgraph hostC[Analyst Workstation]
        nicA[NIC in Promiscuous Mode]
        drvA[Packet Capture Driver libpcap or Npcap]
        ringA[Kernel Ring Buffer mmap]
        decA[Protocol Dissector Engine]
        guiA[Wireshark GUI 3 Pane View]
        statA[Statistics Engine I O Graphs Conversations]
        filA[Capture and Display Filters BPF]
    end

    subgraph outA[Analyst Output Artifacts]
        outP[pcap or pcapng Trace File]
        outI[IoC Report IPs Domains Hashes]
        outR[Forensic Timeline]
    end

    switchX -. mirrored copy .-> nicA
    nicA --> drvA
    drvA --> ringA
    ringA --> decA
    decA --> guiA
    decA --> statA
    filA -. pre filter .-> drvA
    filA -. post filter .-> decA
    guiA --> outP
    statA --> outI
    guiA --> outR
```

### 4.2 Layered Decoding Sequence

The protocol dissector engine works as a strict cascade, where each layer hands the remaining bytes to the next layer using the offset logic derived in Section 3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawA[Raw Bytes from NIC] --> ethA[Ethernet II Dissector offset 0 to 13]
    ethA --> ipA[IPv4 Dissector offset 14 to 14 plus IHL x 4 minus 1]
    ipA --> tcpA[TCP Dissector offset after IP to after IP plus DataOffset x 4 minus 1]
    ipA --> udpA[UDP Dissector offset after IP to after IP plus 8 minus 1]
    ipA --> icmpA[ICMP Dissector offset after IP to after IP plus 8 minus 1]
    tcpA --> httpA[HTTP TLS SMTP etc Dissector]
    udpA --> dnsA[DNS SNMP NTP etc Dissector]
    icmpA --> pingA[ICMP Echo Request Reply Display]
    httpA --> dispA[Decoded Output in Packet Pane]
    dnsA --> dispA
    pingA --> dispA
```

### 4.3 CTI Workflow — From Capture to Indicator

The following block diagram shows the operational workflow by which a protocol analyser feeds the CTI pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Network Capture pcap] --> normA[Normalisation and Protocol Parsing]
    normA --> extrA[Extraction of Network Indicators]
    extrA --> extrB[IP Addresses]
    extrA --> extrC[Domain Names and SNI Strings]
    extrA --> extrD[File Hashes via HTTP TLS transfer]
    extrA --> extrE[JA3 JA3S TLS Fingerprints]
    extrB --> enrichA[Threat Intelligence Enrichment]
    extrC --> enrichA
    extrD --> enrichA
    extrE --> enrichA
    enrichA --> tdbA[Threat Intel Platform MISP OpenCTI]
    tdbA --> detA[Detection Rules YARA Suricata Sigma]
    detA --> blockA[Preventive Controls Firewall EDR]
```

### 4.4 Filter Decision Tree (BPF vs Display Filters)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qA[Need to reduce captured data volume?] -->|Yes| bpfA[Use Capture Filter BPF Syntax]
    qA -->|No| bpfB[Use Display Filter]
    bpfA --> exA[Example tcp port 443 and host 10.0.0.5]
    bpfB --> exB[Example tls.handshake.extensions_server_name contains evil.com]
    exA --> perfA[Pro Faster smaller file]
    exB --> flexA[Pro Richer expressions no data loss]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Note**: All node identifiers above are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `hostA`, `decA`, `bpfA`) to comply with Mermaid v10+ parser requirements. Reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) are never used as standalone node names. Labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text to prevent syntax breakage.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

Define a **Network Protocol Analyser**. List any two popular open-source protocol analysers.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Network Protocol Analyser** is a tool (software or hardware) that captures, decodes, and analyses network traffic by placing the network interface into promiscuous mode, thereby recording packets traversing the medium regardless of their destination MAC address.

*(1 mark for definition)*

Two popular open-source protocol analysers are:
1. **Wireshark** (GUI-based, cross-platform)
2. **tcpdump** (CLI-based, Unix/Linux)

*(1 mark each for the two correct examples)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**

Differentiate between **promiscuous mode** and **monitor mode** in a network interface card.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Promiscuous Mode | Monitor Mode |
|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer | Layer 2 (Data Link) | Layer 1 (Physical) |
| Frame types captured | All Ethernet frames on the segment | 802.11 management, control, and data frames |
| Typical use | Wired sniffing | Wireless (Wi-Fi) sniffing |
| Hardware dependency | Standard NIC support | Requires chipset/driver support (e.g., Atheros) |

*(2 marks for the table; 1 mark for the concluding use-case distinction)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**: Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each** (a) and (b). You are expected to provide diagrams, examples, and point-wise structure to score full marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain the architecture and working of a Network Protocol Analyser with a neat block diagram. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A network protocol analyser operates through a four-stage pipeline: **promiscuous-mode capture → kernel-level buffering → protocol dissection → analyst-facing display**. It enables passive observation of traffic without injecting packets onto the wire.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
[Network Segment] → [SPAN/Tap] → [NIC – Promiscuous Mode]
        → [Capture Driver (libpcap/Npcap)]
        → [Kernel Ring Buffer]
        → [Dissector Engine]
        → [Display GUI / CLI]
        → [Output: .pcap / Statistics / Reports]
```

**Stage-wise Explanation (4 marks):**

1. **Promiscuous Mode Activation**: The NIC's MAC-address filtering is disabled at the register level so that it accepts every frame on the segment, irrespective of the destination MAC.
2. **Capture Driver**: A kernel-mode library (libpcap on Linux/macOS, Npcap on Windows) creates a raw socket. It employs `mmap()` zero-copy techniques to push frames into userspace efficiently.
3. **Dissector Engine**: The captured bytes are parsed by a tree of protocol dissectors. The Ethernet dissector first locates the EtherType, then hands control to the IP dissector, which in turn invokes TCP, UDP, or ICMP dissectors, and finally application-layer dissectors (HTTP, DNS, TLS).
4. **Display & Statistics**: Decoded results are presented in a three-pane Wireshark view, with filters (BPF capture filters, display filters), expert warnings, and I/O graphs to support CTI analysis.

**Conclusion (marks consolidated):** The analyser thus functions as a non-intrusive window into all network communications.

---

**(b) Discuss the various types of filters used in Wireshark with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Filters in Wireshark are expressions that restrict the packets that are either captured or displayed, allowing analysts to focus on relevant traffic during threat hunting.

**1. Capture Filters (BPF Syntax) (3 marks):**
Capture filters operate at the kernel/driver layer and prevent non-matching packets from being written to disk. They use the **Berkeley Packet Filter (BPF)** syntax.

- `host 192.168.1.10` — captures all traffic to/from a specific host.
- `tcp port 443` — captures only TLS-encrypted traffic.
- `not arp and not broadcast` — eliminates non-essential noise.
- `src net 10.0.0.0/8 and dst port 22` — captures SSH traffic from a corporate subnet.

**2. Display Filters (3 marks):**
Display filters operate on already-captured traffic and offer rich, protocol-aware expressions.

- `http.request.method == POST` — isolates HTTP POST requests (often used for exfiltration).
- `dns.qry.name contains "malicious"` — flags suspicious DNS lookups.
- `tls.handshake.extensions_server_name == "evil.com"` — detects C2 callback domains via SNI.
- `ip.src == 10.0.0.5 && tcp.flags.syn == 1` — scans from a specific source.
- `frame contains "cmd.exe"` — searches for plaintext command signatures.

**Conclusion (marks consolidated):** Capture filters reduce storage overhead; display filters enhance analytical precision — both are indispensable in CTI workflows.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]

**(a) Describe the TCP/IP packet structure and explain how a network protocol analyser decodes each layer. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**TCP/IP Packet Structure (3 marks):**
A TCP/IP packet (more precisely, an Ethernet/IPv4/TCP frame) consists of nested headers:

1. **Ethernet II Header (14 bytes)**: Destination MAC (6) + Source MAC (6) + EtherType (2).
2. **IPv4 Header (20–60 bytes)**: Version, IHL, Total Length, TTL, Protocol, Source IP, Destination IP, etc.
3. **TCP Header (20–60 bytes)**: Source Port, Destination Port, Sequence Number, Acknowledgement Number, Data Offset, Flags (SYN, ACK, FIN, RST), Window Size, Checksum, Urgent Pointer.
4. **Application Payload (variable)**: HTTP, TLS, SMTP, etc.

**Decoding Process (4 marks):**
The dissector engine performs layer-by-layer parsing:

- **Step 1**: The Ethernet dissector reads the first 14 bytes and uses the EtherType to identify IPv4 (`0x0800`).
- **Step 2**: The IPv4 dissector reads the next `IHL × 4` bytes, locates the **Protocol** field, and invokes the TCP dissector if the protocol number is `6`.
- **Step 3**: The TCP dissector reads `DataOffset × 4` bytes, extracts flags and ports, and then determines the application-layer protocol (e.g., HTTP for port 80, TLS for port 443).
- **Step 4**: The application dissector parses the remaining payload, displaying requests, responses, headers, and bodies in human-readable form.
- Each dissector reports errors via the **Expert Info** subsystem (e.g., malformed TCP checksum, retransmission).

This cascade mirrors the OSI model's encapsulation and is the basis for **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)**.

---

**(b) Explain the role of Network Protocol Analysers in Cyber Threat Intelligence with at least four use cases. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Role (1 mark):**
Protocol analysers are the **primary data source** for network-derived threat intelligence, providing the raw observations from which Indicators of Compromise (IoCs) are extracted and from which detection rules are crafted.

**Use Case 1 — Threat Hunting (1.5 marks):**
Analysts replay historical pcap files and apply YARA/Snort-compatible display filters to search for traffic patterns matching known adversary Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures (TTPs) from the MITRE ATT&CK framework.

**Use Case 2 — Incident Response (1.5 marks):**
Following a breach, the responder captures traffic from the affected subnet, reconstructs the attack timeline (initial access, lateral movement, exfiltration), and produces forensic evidence admissible in legal proceedings.

**Use Case 3 — Malware C2 Detection (1.5 marks):**
Beaconing behaviour (regular intervals, fixed packet sizes) and JA3/JA3S TLS fingerprints are extracted from the pcap to identify command-and-control callbacks, even when the payload is encrypted.

**Use Case 4 — Exfiltration & Data Leakage Detection (1.5 marks):**
Outbound flows exceeding baseline thresholds, unusual protocol-port pairings (e.g., DNS-over-HTTP), and large HTTP POST bodies are flagged as potential exfiltration channels.

**Conclusion (marks consolidated):** Thus, protocol analysers convert raw network telemetry into actionable, shareable threat intelligence.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse promiscuous mode with monitor mode** — mixing the two will cost you 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Always specify the offset unit** when discussing headers (e.g., "20 bytes" rather than "5 words"). The board penalises ambiguous units.
> 3. **Mention pcap/pcapng** as the file format — students often forget the on-disk artifact and lose the application mark.
> 4. **In Part B answers, include a block diagram** even if not explicitly asked. It typically earns 1–2 bonus marks and demonstrates conceptual clarity.
> 5. **Avoid using the term "hacking tool"** in your definitions. Always frame protocol analysers as **defensive, forensic, or intelligence-gathering** instruments — this aligns with KTU's ethical-usage stance.
> 6. **BPF syntax errors** in filter examples (e.g., using `==` instead of literal match) are a common silent deduction. Use `contains` for substring matches and `==` for exact matches.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Network Protocol Analyser** = tool that captures, decodes, and analyses network traffic by placing the NIC into **promiscuous mode**.
- Key open-source tools: **Wireshark** (GUI), **tcpdump** (CLI), **TShark** (CLI of Wireshark).
- MITRE ATT&CK mapping: **T1040 – Network Sniffing**.
- Two filter classes: **Capture Filters (BPF, pre-capture, kernel-level)** vs **Display Filters (post-capture, dissector-aware)**.
- Standard pcap file format: **.pcap** (libpcap) and **.pcapng** (next-generation, supports multi-interface and metadata).
- Fixed header sizes to memorise: **Ethernet = 14 bytes**, **IPv4 = 20–60 bytes**, **TCP = 20–60 bytes**, **UDP = 8 bytes**.
- Payload offset formula: `14 + 4 × IHL + 4 × DataOffset` (for IPv4/TCP).
- Standard port ranges: **0–1023** (well-known), **1024–49151** (registered), **49152–65535** (ephemeral).
- Promiscuous mode operates at **OSI Layer 2**; monitor mode at **OSI Layer 1** (used for Wi-Fi).
- Always use protocol analysers in **defensive/forensic** contexts with **written authorisation** — unauthorised sniffing is illegal under the **IT Act, 2000 (India)** and equivalent statutes globally.
- Drop rate above **1–2%** indicates capture pipeline issues and possible loss of forensic evidence.
- CTI value chain: **Capture → Normalise → Extract IoCs → Enrich → Correlate → Detect → Block**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vulnerability Assessment Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT755 - CYBER THREAT INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vulnerability Assessment Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Vulnerability Assessment (VA)** is the systematic, methodical, and repeatable process of identifying, classifying, prioritizing, and remediating security weaknesses (vulnerabilities) in an information system's hardware, software, network infrastructure, and procedural controls. It is formally defined in **NIST SP 800-115 (Technical Guide to Information Security Testing and Assessment)** as a *non-intrusive* examination intended to discover weaknesses that could be exploited by a threat actor.

**Vulnerability Assessment Tools (VAT)** are specialized software applications, frameworks, and utilities engineered to automate and orchestrate the vulnerability assessment lifecycle. They perform active probing, passive network analysis, credentialed configuration inspection, signature matching, and behavioral heuristic analysis to discover, enumerate, and report on security flaws. Formally, they are categorized under the broader umbrella of **Security Content Automation Protocol (SCAP)**-compliant components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECCT755 / Module 2):**
> Vulnerability Assessment Tools encompass scanners, exploit frameworks, and intelligence platforms that map an organization's attack surface, correlate findings against public vulnerability databases (CVE, NVD, CWE), and produce quantified risk scores using the **Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS)**. The output of VATs is a critical input to the **Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI)** pipeline.

### 1.2 Core Sub-Concepts (Atomic Definitions)

- **Vulnerability:** A weakness in an information system that could be exploited or triggered by a threat source. Quantified using the **Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE)** identifier — e.g., `CVE-2017-0144` (EternalBlue).
- **Threat:** Any circumstance or event with the potential to adversely impact organizational operations, assets, or individuals via an information system.
- **Asset:** A resource of value requiring protection — hardware, software, data, personnel, or processes.
- **Attack Surface:** The sum total of all points (ports, services, APIs, user inputs) where an unauthorized user can attempt to enter, extract data from, or cause damage to an environment.
- **Risk:** The qualitative or quantitative measure of the likelihood and impact of a threat exploiting a vulnerability. Expressed as:
  $$\text{Risk} = f(\text{Threat}, \text{Vulnerability}, \text{Impact})$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🏥 The Hospital Health Checkup Analogy**
>
> Imagine your computer network is a **large hospital building**.
> - **Vulnerability Assessment** is the annual full-body health checkup of that hospital. The doctors (the scanning tool) walk room-by-room (port-by-port), check every organ (service), every electrical wire (configuration), every locked door (patch level), and write a report saying: *"Room 204 has a faulty heart valve — fix it within 30 days."*
> - **Penetration Testing** is the *stress test* — they actually try to make the patient collapse to see if the building can survive a real attack.
> - **Vulnerability Assessment Tools** are the *automated diagnostic machines* (MRI, blood-test analyzer, ECG) that perform these checks faster, more consistently, and at scale than any human auditor could.
>
> A hospital never skips its annual health checkup — and neither should an enterprise skip its **quarterly VA scans**.

### 1.4 Industry-Standard Metrics & Frameworks

The following standards are **mandatory references** for any KTU 2024 examiner evaluating answers on VATs:

| Standard | Governing Body | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **CVE** | MITRE Corporation | Unique ID for every publicly known vulnerability |
| **NVD** | NIST | Enriches CVE entries with CVSS scores & metadata |
| **CWE** | MITRE Corporation | Categorization of weakness *types* (e.g., buffer overflow) |
| **CVSS** | FIRST.org | Numerical 0.0–10.0 severity scoring |
| **SCAP** | NIST | Protocol for automated vuln management |
| **OVAL** | MITRE | Open standard for vulnerability checks |
| **CPE** | NIST | Naming scheme for IT products (e.g., `cpe:/o:microsoft:windows_10`) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Vulnerability Assessment Tools

Vulnerability Assessment Tools can be classified along **five orthogonal axes** that the KTU examiner frequently tests:

#### 2.1.1 By Deployment Architecture
- **Active Scanners:** Inject crafted probe packets into the target. Examples: **Nessus, Qualys VM, OpenVAS**.
- **Passive Scanners:** Mirror network traffic and analyze it without injecting packets. Examples: **Tenable Nessus Network Monitor, Cisco Stealthwatch**.
- **Agent-Based Scanners:** A lightweight daemon is installed on the host; reports back to a central management server. Examples: **Qualys Cloud Agent, Tenable Nessus Agent**.
- **Agentless / Credentialed Scanners:** Authenticate via SSH, SNMP, WMI, or APIs to read system state remotely. Examples: **Nessus (credentialed mode), Rapid7 InsightVM**.

#### 2.1.2 By Assessment Layer
- **Network-Based Scanners:** Examine network-layer services — open ports, banner grabbing, protocol fuzzing. Example: **Nmap, Nessus**.
- **Host-Based Scanners:** Inspect OS-level artifacts — registry, file integrity, patch level. Example: **OpenVAS, Microsoft Defender Vulnerability Management**.
- **Application-Layer Scanners (DAST):** Black-box test of web applications. Example: **OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite Scanner, Acunetix**.
- **Source-Code Scanners (SAST):** Static analysis of source code. Example: **SonarQube, Checkmarx, Semgrep**.
- **Database Scanners:** Audit SQL/NoSQL configurations. Example: **SQLmap, DbProtect**.

#### 2.1.3 By Source Code Availability
- **Open-Source:** OpenVAS, OWASP ZAP, Nmap, Nikto, SQLmap, WPScan.
- **Commercial / Proprietary:** Nessus Professional, Qualys VMDR, Rapid7 InsightVM, Burp Suite Enterprise, Tenable.io.

#### 2.1.4 By Scope of Discovery
- **Authenticated (Credentialed) Scan:** Logged-in scan; reads actual patch level and configuration. Lowest false-positive rate.
- **Unauthenticated (Non-Credentialed) Scan:** External attacker's view; only surface-level services are visible. High false-positive rate but represents true external exposure.

#### 2.1.5 By Output & Integration
- **Reporting-Focused Tools:** Generate executive PDF reports (Nessus, Qualys).
- **Exploitation Frameworks:** Combine discovery with weaponization (Metasploit).
- **CI/CD Integrated Tools:** Inline SAST/DAST in DevOps pipelines (Snyk, GitHub Advanced Security).

### 2.2 The Vulnerability Assessment Lifecycle (NIST SP 800-115)

A formal VA engagement follows **four iterative phases**:

1. **Planning Phase**
   - Define scope (which subnets, hosts, applications are in-scope).
   - Obtain written authorization (Rules of Engagement — RoE).
   - Identify tools, testing window, blackout dates.
   - Map the asset inventory and classify data sensitivity.

2. **Discovery Phase**
   - **Network Mapping:** Use ICMP sweeps, ARP scans, and TCP/UDP port scans.
   - **Service Enumeration:** Banner grabbing, version detection, SNMP walking.
   - **Vulnerability Identification:** Match identified software versions against the tool's vulnerability feed (which is synchronized daily with the NVD).

3. **Attack Phase (Controlled Validation)**
   - **Active Probing:** Send crafted packets to trigger vulnerable code paths.
   - **Credentialed Inspection:** Read `/etc/shadow`, `regedit`, `systeminfo` to detect missing patches.
   - **False-Positive Reduction:** Cross-reference findings with manual verification or auxiliary tools.

4. **Reporting Phase**
   - Executive summary (risk-prioritized).
   - Technical findings (CVE, CVSS, evidence screenshots, remediation steps).
   - Re-test plan and remediation tracking dashboard.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all high-yield formulas, scores, and constants** the KTU examiner expects in any VAT-related numerical or descriptive answer. Memorize these before the exam.

| # | Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = T \times V \times I$ | Risk equation (qualitative) | Dimensionless index |
| 2 | $\text{CVSS Base} \in [0.0,\ 10.0]$ | Common Vulnerability Scoring System base score | **0.0** = None, **10.0** = Critical |
| 3 | $\text{Impact SubScore (ISS)} = 1 - [(1-C)(1-I)(1-A)]$ | Combined impact on Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability | Decimal, $0 \le \text{ISS} \le 1$ |
| 4 | $\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times \text{ISS}$ | Overall impact component | $\text{Impact} \in [0,\ 6.42]$ |
| 5 | $\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI$ | Likelihood the vuln can be exploited | $\text{Exploitability} \in [0,\ 10]$ |
| 6 | $\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundUp}\left(\min\left[\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\ 10\right]\right)$ | When scope unchanged | $0 \le \text{BaseScore} \le 10$ |
| 7 | $\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundUp}\left(\min\left[1.08 \times (\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability}),\ 10\right]\right)$ | When scope **CHANGED** | $0 \le \text{BaseScore} \le 10$ |
| 8 | $C,\ I,\ A \in \{0.0,\ 0.22,\ 0.56\}$ | Impact metric values: None / Low / High | $0.0, 0.22, 0.56$ respectively |
| 9 | $AV \in \{0.85,\ 0.62,\ 0.55,\ 0.20\}$ | AttackVector: N, A, L, P | Network, Adjacent, Local, Physical |
| 10 | $AC \in \{0.77,\ 0.44\}$ | AttackComplexity: L, H | Low, High |
| 11 | $PR \in \{0.85,\ 0.62,\ 0.27\}$ | PrivilegesRequired: N, L, H | None, Low, High |
| 12 | $UI \in \{0.85,\ 0.62\}$ | UserInteraction: N, R | None, Required |
| 13 | $\text{Port Scan Rate} = \frac{\text{Total Ports Scanned}}{\text{Time (s)}}$ | Tool performance metric | Ports / second |
| 14 | $\text{Coverage} = \frac{\text{Hosts Scanned}}{\text{Total Hosts in Scope}} \times 100\%$ | Scan completeness | Percentage |
| 15 | $\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | False Positive Rate | $0 \le \text{FPR} \le 1$ |
| 16 | $\text{FNR} = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | False Negative Rate | $0 \le \text{FNR} \le 1$ |
| 17 | $\text{MTTR} = \text{Mean Time To Remediate}$ | Remediation efficiency | Hours / days |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Notation Pitfall:** In CVSS equations, $C$, $I$, $A$ denote the *Impact* metrics (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) — **not** the metric values themselves. The actual numeric values ($0.0$, $0.22$, $0.56$) are plugged in. Do not confuse them in the answer sheet.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Vulnerability Assessment Tools are not academic curiosities. They are the **operational backbone of every modern Security Operations Center (SOC)** and feed into:

- **Risk Management Frameworks (RMF)** — the U.S. federal government's mandatory NIST RMF mandates continuous VA scanning (Step 5: Assess).
- **Regulatory Compliance:** PCI-DSS Req. 11.2, HIPAA §164.308(a)(1), ISO 27001 Control A.12.6.1, and India's **CERT-In Directions 2022** all require periodic VA.
- **DevSecOps Pipelines:** Tools like **Snyk, Trivy, Grype** are embedded directly in CI/CD to scan every commit and container image.
- **Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) Loop:** The *Intelligence Cycle* (Planning → Collection → Analysis → Dissemination → Feedback) receives raw vulnerability data from VATs and converts it into actionable IOCs (Indicators of Compromise).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — CVSS Base Score (First-Principles)

The CVSS Base Score is the foundational metric used by every commercial and open-source VAT to prioritize findings. Below is the **complete, line-by-line derivation** following the **CVSS v3.1 Specification (FIRST.org)**.

> [!NOTE]
> The CVSS Base Score is computed from **8 metrics** belonging to two sub-groups: *Exploitability* (4 metrics) and *Impact* (3 metrics), plus the *Scope* (1 metric).

#### Step 1 — Define the Impact Sub-Score (ISS)

The **Impact Sub-Score (ISS)** is the geometric mean of the impact on the three CIA triad properties — Confidentiality ($C$), Integrity ($I$), and Availability ($A$). It is defined as:

$$
\text{ISS} = 1 - \left[ (1 - C) \times (1 - I) \times (1 - A) \right]
$$

**Logic:** If *any one* of the three CIA properties is fully compromised (value $= 1.0$), the product of the other two unused factors is $0$, so the ISS $\to 1.0$, signaling total impact.

#### Step 2 — Apply the Multiplication Constant (6.42)

The raw ISS is converted into the *Impact* component used in the final score:

$$
\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times \text{ISS}
$$

**Logic:** The constant **6.42** is an empirically derived scaling factor chosen by the CVSS Special Interest Group (SIG) to align the *Impact* component with the *Exploitability* component on the same 0–10 severity scale.

#### Step 3 — Boundary Condition on Impact

If the **Scope is Unchanged**, and if the resulting $\text{Impact} \le 0$, the Base Score collapses to $0$:

$$
\text{If } \text{Impact} \le 0 : \text{ BaseScore} = 0
$$

#### Step 4 — Compute the Exploitability Component

The **Exploitability** component captures the *ease* with which the vulnerability can be triggered:

$$
\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI
$$

**Logic:** The constant **8.22** is a second empirical scaling factor. $AV$ (AttackVector), $AC$ (AttackComplexity), $PR$ (PrivilegesRequired), and $UI$ (UserInteraction) are taken from the metric value lookup tables given in Section 2.3.

#### Step 5 — Final Base Score Assembly

Two cases exist based on the **Scope** metric ($S$):

**Case A — Scope Unchanged ($S = U$):**

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundUp}\left( \min\left[ (\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability}),\ 10 \right] \right)
$$

**Case B — Scope Changed ($S = C$):**

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundUp}\left( \min\left[ 1.08 \times (\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability}),\ 10 \right] \right)
$$

**Logic of the 1.08 factor:** When the exploit *escapes* the vulnerable component and affects resources beyond its security authority, an additional 8% bonus is awarded to reflect the broader blast radius.

#### Worked Numerical Example

> **Given:** A web server vulnerability with the following CVSS v3.1 metrics —
> - $AV = 0.55$ (Local)
> - $AC = 0.44$ (High)
> - $PR = 0.85$ (None)
> - $UI = 0.85$ (None)
> - $C = 0.22$ (Low)
> - $I = 0.22$ (Low)
> - $A = 0.22$ (Low)
> - $S = U$ (Unchanged)

**Step A — Compute ISS:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ISS} &= 1 - \left[ (1 - 0.22) \times (1 - 0.22) \times (1 - 0.22) \right] \\
&= 1 - \left[ 0.78 \times 0.78 \times 0.78 \right] \\
&= 1 - \left[ 0.474552 \right] \\
&= 0.525448
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step B — Compute Impact:**

$$
\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times 0.525448 = 3.373376
$$

**Step C — Compute Exploitability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Exploitability} &= 8.22 \times 0.55 \times 0.44 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 \\
&= 8.22 \times 0.174469 \\
&= 1.434135
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step D — Assemble Base Score (Scope Unchanged):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BaseScore} &= \text{roundUp}\left( \min\left[ 3.373376 + 1.434135,\ 10 \right] \right) \\
&= \text{roundUp}\left( \min\left[ 4.807511,\ 10 \right] \right) \\
&= \text{roundUp}\left( 4.807511 \right) \\
&= 4.9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verdict:** $\text{BaseScore} = 4.9 \rightarrow$ **Medium Severity (CVSS v3.1).**

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Risk Priority Index (RPI) Used by VATs

Most commercial scanners (Nessus, Qualys) output a **Risk Priority Number** derived as:

$$
\text{RPI} = \text{CVSS Base} \times \text{Asset Criticality} \times \text{Exploit In The Wild (EITW)}
$$

Where:
- $\text{Asset Criticality} \in \{0.5,\ 1.0,\ 1.5,\ 2.0\}$ — classified as Low / Medium / High / Mission-Critical.
- $\text{EITW} \in \{1.0,\ 1.5\}$ — based on whether Metasploit modules, public PoCs, or active exploitation by ransomware gangs (e.g., LockBit) are observed.

**Numerical Example:**

> A Critical CVSS 9.8 vulnerability (EternalBlue, $CVE$-$2017$-$0144$) is found on a **Mission-Critical database server (AC = 2.0)** with **active exploitation in the wild (EITW = 1.5, LockBit affiliates)**.

$$
\text{RPI} = 9.8 \times 2.0 \times 1.5 = 29.4
$$

This score, being above the threshold of $20$, triggers an **emergency patch SLA of 24 hours** in most organizations.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Scanner Coverage Metric

The effectiveness of a VA tool is judged by the **Coverage** of the scan:

$$
\text{Coverage \%} = \left( \frac{\text{Hosts Successfully Scanned}}{\text{Total Hosts In Scope}} \right) \times 100
$$

**Reliability Adjustment:** A scan is only considered "successful" if it returned at least 80% of the expected port/service count, otherwise the host is marked as *unreached* and re-scanned.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — False-Positive Trade-Off

A scanner's accuracy is the harmonic mean of its Precision and Recall:

$$
F_1 = 2 \times \frac{\text{Precision} \times \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}
$$

Where:
- $\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$
- $\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$

A high $F_1$ (close to 1.0) indicates that the scanner is both **precise** (few false positives) and **comprehensive** (few false negatives). Tenable Nessus typically reports an $F_1 \ge 0.92$ in production environments.

    G -- Yes --> H["Perform Authenticated Deep Inspection"]

    I --> J["Phase 3 Attack Active Probing"]
