## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Vulnerability Assessment Tools. Differentiate between credentialed and non-credentialed scans.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Vulnerability Assessment Tools (VAT) are automated software applications that systematically identify, classify, and report security weaknesses in an information system's hardware, software, and configuration, by comparing observed state against known vulnerability databases such as the **CVE / NVD**.

**Differentiation (1.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Credentialed Scan | Non-Credentialed Scan |
|---|---|---|
| Authentication | Logs into the target with valid credentials | No credentials used |
| Viewpoint | Internal / administrative | External / attacker's view |
| False Positives | Low | High |
| Patch Detection | Accurate (reads patch DB) | Inferential (only via banner) |
| Speed | Slower | Faster |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is CVSS? List the three metric groups in CVSS v3.1.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**CVSS Definition (1.5 marks):** The **Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS)** is an open framework maintained by **FIRST.org** for communicating the characteristics and severity of software vulnerabilities. It produces a numerical score from **0.0 to 10.0**, where higher values indicate greater severity.

**Three Metric Groups (1.5 marks):**

1. **Base Metrics** — Intrinsic properties of the vulnerability that are constant over time and across environments (e.g., Attack Vector, Complexity, Impact).
2. **Temporal Metrics** — Properties that change over time (e.g., Exploit Code Maturity, Remediation Level, Report Confidence).
3. **Environmental Metrics** — Properties tied to a specific user environment (e.g., Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability Requirements, Modified Base metrics).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Solutions (14 Marks Each)

---

## ❓ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2, PECCT755]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **vulnerability assessment lifecycle as per NIST SP 800-115**. List and describe each phase with at least two activities per phase.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks)** With a neat diagram, describe the **architecture of a typical commercial vulnerability scanner** (Nessus/Qualys). Explain the role of the plugin feed, credentialed inspection module, and reporting engine.

---

### Solution to Q.A(a) — VA Lifecycle (7 marks)

The **NIST SP 800-115** standard, titled *"Technical Guide to Information Security Testing and Assessment"*, formalizes the vulnerability assessment process into **four sequential phases** with iterative feedback:

**Phase 1 — Planning (1.5 marks):**
- Define the **scope, objectives, and limitations** of the assessment — which IP ranges, applications, and business units are in-scope.
- Obtain **written authorization** (Rules of Engagement) from management to avoid legal issues.
- Identify the **testing window, blackout dates** (e.g., month-end payroll processing), and emergency contacts.
- Conduct a **pre-assessment survey** of the target environment using prior network diagrams and asset inventories.

**Phase 2 — Discovery (2 marks):**
- **Network Mapping:** Identify live hosts via ICMP echo sweeps, ARP scans, and TCP SYN probes.
- **Service Enumeration:** Use banner grabbing, version detection, and protocol-specific probes (e.g., SNMP walks, SMB enumeration).
- **Vulnerability Identification:** Cross-reference discovered software versions against the tool's **plugin feed** (e.g., Nessus has 200,000+ plugins) synchronized daily with **NVD**.
- **Asset Correlation:** Map findings to the **Configuration Management Database (CMDB)** to flag business-critical assets.

**Phase 3 — Attack / Validation (2 marks):**
- **Active Probing:** Send crafted packets to trigger vulnerable code paths in real services.
- **Credentialed Inspection:** Authenticate via SSH/WMI/SNMP and read OS-level state (e.g., `systeminfo`, `rpm -qa`, registry keys) to detect missing patches.
- **False-Positive Triangulation:** Manually verify a sample of findings using secondary tools (e.g., `searchsploit` for known exploits) to ensure the scanner is not over-reporting.

**Phase 4 — Reporting / Post-Assessment (1.5 marks):**
- Generate an **Executive Summary** with risk-prioritized counts (Critical / High / Medium / Low).
- Produce a **Technical Report** with evidence — CVE IDs, CVSS scores, screenshots of successful probes, and **step-by-step remediation** (e.g., *"Upgrade OpenSSL to version 1.1.1w"*).
- Define the **remediation SLAs** (e.g., Critical: 7 days, High: 30 days, Medium: 90 days).
- Conduct a **lessons-learned** review and update the next assessment's plan.

---

### Solution to Q.A(b) — Scanner Architecture (7 marks)

A commercial vulnerability scanner (Nessus / Qualys VMDR) is architecturally a **distributed client–server system** with five layers:

**Layer 1 — Management Console (1 mark):**
- Web-based GUI (HTTPS) used by analysts to launch scans, view results, configure policies, and generate compliance reports.
- Supports multi-user RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) — e.g., Admin, Auditor, Read-Only.

**Layer 2 — Scan Orchestrator (1 mark):**
- The **central scheduler** that distributes scan jobs across multiple scan engines.
- Handles the **plugin feed update** (downloading ~10,000+ new checks per quarter from NVD).
- Manages **credentials vault** (encrypted with AES-256) for credentialed scans.

**Layer 3 — Scan Engine (2 marks):**
- The workhorse — performs the actual port scans, plugin execution, and vulnerability detection.
- **Plugin Feed Module:** Each vulnerability check is a *plugin* — a self-contained script (NASL for Nessus) that knows how to detect one specific flaw. The feed is the *vulnerability signature database*.
- **Credentialed Inspection Module:** SSHes/WMI-connects to the host and runs native commands (`systeminfo`, `apt list --upgradable`) to get a definitive patch list.
- **Active Probe Module:** Sends SYN, ACK, FIN, NULL, XMAS packets; performs banner grabbing, version detection, and protocol fuzzing.

**Layer 4 — Knowledge Base (1 mark):**
- Stores raw scan data, asset inventory, and historical trend analytics.
- Indexes findings by **CVE / CWE / CPE** for easy correlation with external intelligence feeds.

**Layer 5 — Reporting & Integration (2 marks):**
- Generates **PDF, CSV, and HTML reports** with executive dashboards.
- Exposes **REST APIs** for integration with **SIEM** (Splunk, QRadar), **SOAR** (Cortex XSOAR), and **ticketing systems** (Jira, ServiceNow).
- Supports compliance templates for **PCI-DSS, HIPAA, ISO 27001, CIS Benchmarks**.

> **Diagram Reference:** See Section 4.2 (Architecture of a Commercial Vulnerability Scanner).

---

## ❓ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2, PECCT755]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** What is the **Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS)?** Derive the **base score** for a vulnerability with the following metrics: $AV = 0.62$ (Adjacent), $AC = 0.77$ (Low), $PR = 0.62$ (Low), $UI = 0.85$ (None), $C = 0.56$ (High), $I = 0.56$ (High), $A = 0.56$ (High), Scope = Unchanged.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the following tools in a tabular format: **Nessus, OpenVAS, Qualys, OWASP ZAP, and Burp Suite**. Cover aspects of license type, primary use-case, credentialed scan support, and report output format.

---

### Solution to Q.B(a) — CVSS Base Score Derivation (7 marks)

**CVSS Definition (2 marks):**
The **Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS)** is an open industry standard, maintained by **FIRST.org**, that provides a numerical (0.0–10.0) severity score for software vulnerabilities. The score is composed of three metric groups: **Base** (intrinsic, time/environment-independent), **Temporal** (changes over time), and **Environmental** (specific to user environment).

**Derivation (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute the Impact Sub-Score (ISS):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ISS} &= 1 - \left[ (1 - C) \times (1 - I) \times (1 - A) \right] \\
&= 1 - \left[ (1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56) \right] \\
&= 1 - \left[ 0.44 \times 0.44 \times 0.44 \right] \\
&= 1 - 0.085184 \\
&= 0.914816
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Impact component:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Impact} &= 6.42 \times \text{ISS} \\
&= 6.42 \times 0.914816 \\
&= 5.873118
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Exploitability component:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Exploitability} &= 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI \\
&= 8.22 \times 0.62 \times 0.77 \times 0.62 \times 0.85 \\
&= 8.22 \times 0.252443 \\
&= 2.075081
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary condition (Impact > 0 ✓), Scope = Unchanged:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BaseScore} &= \text{roundUp}\left( \min\left[ \text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\ 10 \right] \right) \\
&= \text{roundUp}\left( \min\left[ 5.873118 + 2.075081,\ 10 \right] \right) \\
&= \text{roundUp}\left( \min\left[ 7.948199,\ 10 \right] \right) \\
&= \text{roundUp}\left( 7.948199 \right) \\
&= 7.95
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verdict:** Base Score = **7.95** → **High Severity** vulnerability.

---

### Solution to Q.B(b) — Tool Comparison (7 marks)

| # | Tool | License | Primary Use Case | Credentialed Scan | Report Format |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nessus (Tenable)** | Commercial (Pro) / Free (Essentials) | General-purpose enterprise VA — networks, hosts, cloud | Yes — SSH, WMI, SNMP | PDF, HTML, CSV, NessusDB |
| 2 | **OpenVAS (Greenbone)** | Open-Source (GPL) | Free alternative to Nessus; network/host VA | Yes — SSH, SMB | PDF, HTML, XML |
| 3 | **Qualys VMDR** | Commercial (SaaS) | Cloud-native enterprise VA with continuous monitoring | Yes — agent + agentless | PDF, CSV, API (JSON) |
| 4 | **OWASP ZAP** | Open-Source (Apache 2.0) | Web application DAST — black-box and passive | Limited (form auth, session) | HTML, JSON, XML, PDF |
| 5 | **Burp Suite** | Commercial (Pro) / Free (Community) | Web app penetration testing — DAST + manual proxy | Yes (via session handling rules) | HTML, XML |

> **Examiner's Note (1 mark bonus):** Students should also mention that **Nessus & Qualys** lead in **enterprise network scanning**, while **ZAP & Burp Suite** dominate the **web application DAST** niche. The open-source champion across both niches is **OpenVAS / ZAP**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Vulnerability Assessment Tools Answers**
>
> 1. **CVSS Confusion:** Students frequently write **CVSS v2** constants (e.g., using $7.5$ for Impact constant) in a **v3.1** question. The constants $6.42$ and $8.22$ are **v3.1-specific**. KTU 2024 mandates **CVSS v3.1** — using wrong version constants costs full marks.
>
> 2. **Confusing VA with Pentest:** A common error is describing vulnerability exploitation, payload delivery, or post-exploitation — these are **penetration testing** activities, not VA. VA is *discovery and reporting*; pentest is *exploitation simulation*. Mixing them up is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Scope = Changed Multiplier 1.08:** In CVSS derivation questions, students often forget to apply the **1.08 bonus** when Scope = Changed. Always re-read the Scope metric before final assembly.
>
> 4. **Reporting Phase Neglect:** Students describe only Discovery + Attack and skip the **Reporting phase** of NIST SP 800-115. Examiners specifically test this. Always include the four phases: Planning → Discovery → Attack → Reporting.
>
> 5. **Naming Tools without Context:** Listing "Nessus, Qualys, OpenVAS" without explaining *why* they fit the question (e.g., credentialed vs. un-credentialed, network vs. host layer) loses application marks. Always justify the tool choice.
>
> 6. **No Round-Up in CVSS:** The `roundUp` function in CVSS rounds to **one decimal place** (e.g., $4.807511 \to 4.8$, not $5$). A common error is rounding to the nearest integer. Use the formula: $\lceil x \times 10 \rceil / 10$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Vulnerability Assessment Tools**

- ✅ **Vulnerability Assessment** = systematic identification, classification, and prioritization of security weaknesses. It is **non-intrusive** and **not** exploitation.
- ✅ **NIST SP 800-115** defines the **4 phases**: **P**lanning → **D**iscovery → **A**ttack → **R**eporting (mnemonic: *P-D-A-R*).
- ✅ **CVE** = unique vulnerability identifier (MITRE). **CWE** = weakness type. **CPE** = product name. **NVD** = enriched CVE database.
- ✅ **CVSS v3.1 Base Score** uses constants **6.42** (Impact) and **8.22** (Exploitability), plus a **1.08** scope-changed bonus.
- ✅ **Credentialed scans** are slow but **low false-positive**; **non-credentialed scans** are fast but represent the external attacker's view (high FP).
- ✅ **Network scanners:** Nmap, Nessus, Qualys. **Host scanners:** OpenVAS. **Web (DAST):** OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite. **SAST:** SonarQube, Semgrep. **Database:** SQLmap.
- ✅ **OpenVAS** is the leading open-source enterprise VA. **Nessus** is the most widely deployed commercial scanner. **Qualys** is the leading cloud-native SaaS scanner.
- ✅ **Risk** = $f(\text{Threat}, \text{Vulnerability}, \text{Impact})$. **CVSS** measures the vulnerability's severity, *not* the organization's risk.
- ✅ **RPI** (Risk Priority Index) = $\text{CVSS} \times \text{Asset Criticality} \times \text{EITW}$ — the formula used by modern scanners for remediation prioritization.
- ✅ **VA Tools feed the CTI loop** at the *Collection* phase; the enriched data drives detection engineering, incident response, and strategic threat briefings.
- ✅ **Compliance mandates** VA: PCI-DSS 11.2, HIPAA §164.308, ISO 27001 A.12.6, CERT-In Directions 2022, NIST RMF Step 5.
- ✅ **Memorize** the CVSS metric value tables (AV, AC, PR, UI, C, I, A) — KTU 2024 often asks derivations using these values.
- ✅ **Key command-line tools** to know: `nmap -sS -sV -O`, `nikto -h`, `sqlmap -u`, `searchsploit`, `gvm-start` (OpenVAS), `nessuscli`.
- ✅ The **OWASP Top 10** and **CWE Top 25** are *priority lists* that VATs use to focus scans on the most common vulnerability classes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application Security and Testing: Characteristics of Security Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT755 - CYBER THREAT INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Application Security and Testing: Characteristics of Security Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security Architecture** is a comprehensive, unified design framework that describes the structure, behavior, and inter-relationship of an application's security controls, components, and processes. It integrates **policy**, **mechanism**, and **assurance** to ensure confidentiality, integrity, and availability (**CIA Triad**) of information assets across the entire **Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)**.

In the context of **Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI)** and the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus, the *characteristics* of a security architecture refer to the foundational design principles (often traced to **Saltzer and Schroeder's 1975 design principles** and the **NIST SP 800-160** systems security engineering guidelines) that govern *how* security controls are conceptualized, layered, and enforced within an application.

These characteristics are **non-negotiable engineering principles** that, when violated, introduce systemic vulnerabilities exploitable by threat actors.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bank Vault"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Designing a Modern Bank Building**

Imagine you are designing a **high-security bank building**:
- It does not have just **one** lock on the front door. It has a perimeter wall, a guard, a mantrap, CCTV, biometric access, an inner vault, time-locked safes, and an alarm response team.
- The teller who handles cash does **not** have access to the vault (separation of duties).
- If the power goes out, the doors **default to locked**, not open.
- The door lock design is **publicly documented**, so everyone knows how it works — yet it is mathematically impossible to bypass without the key.

A *security architecture* for an application follows **exactly the same philosophy**. Each "lock and guard" maps to a software control (firewall, WAF, input validator, RBAC, encrypted vault, SIEM alert, etc.). The **characteristics** are the *rules* for placing these controls.

---

## 1.3 The CIA Triad — The Foundational Goal

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every characteristic of security architecture ultimately serves to protect one or more pillars of the **CIA Triad**:

| Pillar | Definition | Example Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Preventing unauthorized disclosure | AES-256 encryption, access control lists |
| **Integrity** | Preventing unauthorized modification | SHA-256 hashing, digital signatures |
| **Availability** | Ensuring timely, reliable access | DDoS mitigation, redundant clusters |

---

## 1.4 The 8 Core Characteristics (KTU 2024 High-Yield List)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 8 Characteristics of Security Architecture (Saltzer–Schroeder / NIST Aligned):**

1. **Least Privilege**
2. **Defense in Depth**
3. **Fail Secure (Fail Safe Defaults)**
4. **Economy of Mechanism**
5. **Complete Mediation**
6. **Open Design**
7. **Separation of Privilege (Duties)**
8. **Psychological Acceptability**

These eight form the **de-facto KTU board examination syllabus** for this topic. Each is examined for **3-mark definitions** and **14-mark design explanations**.

---

## 1.5 Application Security & Testing — Where Architecture Meets Verification

> [!NOTE]
> **Application Security Testing** is the *empirical validation* that the security architecture is correctly implemented. The two dominant paradigms are:
> - **SAST (Static Application Security Testing)** — analyzes source code without execution.
> - **DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing)** — black-box testing of a running application.
> - **IAST (Interactive Application Security Testing)** — hybrid instrumentation-based.

The characteristics of security architecture dictate **what** is being tested, while application security testing answers **whether** the architecture is correctly realized in code.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of the 8 Characteristics

> [!NOTE]
> Each characteristic is a **design rule** — a constraint placed upon the architect at the whiteboard *before* any code is written.

### 2.1.1 Least Privilege (Principle of Least Privilege — PoLP)
- **Definition:** Every user, process, or system component is granted **only the minimum permissions** necessary to perform its intended function — and *no more*.
- **Why:** Limits the *blast radius* of a compromise. A compromised low-privilege account cannot exfiltrate the customer database.
- **How:** Implement Role-Based Access Control (**RBAC**) or Attribute-Based Access Control (**ABAC**). Default-deny in firewall rules. Use temporary Just-in-Time (JIT) elevation for administrative tasks.
- **Real-world:** A junior bank clerk can view account balances but cannot initiate wire transfers. AWS IAM policies grant `s3:GetObject` but not `s3:DeleteObject`.

### 2.1.2 Defense in Depth
- **Definition:** Security is implemented as **multiple, overlapping, independent layers** such that the failure of one layer does not result in total compromise.
- **Why:** No single control is 100% effective against a determined adversary. Redundancy reduces probability of total breach.
- **How:** Layered controls across **perimeter → network → host → application → data** (e.g., firewall + IDS + WAF + input validation + parameterized queries + encryption at rest).
- **Real-world:** Medieval castle architecture — moat, wall, gatehouse, inner bailey, keep, royal vault.

### 2.1.3 Fail Secure (Fail Safe Defaults)
- **Definition:** When a system component fails, it must **default to a secure state** — denying access rather than granting it.
- **Why:** Failure modes are inevitable. Insecure failure creates an attacker-controllable condition.
- **How:** Auth services that go offline should reject all requests, not allow all. Door locks that lose power should lock, not unlock.
- **Real-world:** Bank vault's time-lock defaults to "locked" if the pneumatic seal fails.

### 2.1.4 Economy of Mechanism (Keep It Simple)
- **Definition:** Security mechanisms should be **as simple and small as possible**. Complexity is the enemy of security.
- **Why:** Every additional line of code is a potential bug. Complex mechanisms are harder to audit, test, and reason about.
- **How:** Prefer proven cryptographic libraries (OpenSSL, libsodium) over custom implementations. Avoid security-by-obscurity.
- **Real-world:** Using a standard TLS 1.3 handshake rather than a custom 47-step proprietary protocol.

### 2.1.5 Complete Mediation
- **Definition:** Every access to every object must be **checked for authorization on every access** — no exceptions, no caching of stale decisions.
- **Why:** A permission change (e.g., employee termination) must propagate to *all* subsequent checks. A cached "yes" from yesterday is a vulnerability.
- **How:** Re-validate session permissions on every request. Invalidate caches on role change. Avoid client-side authorization checks.
- **Real-world:** Hotel keycards are re-validated against the central lock system on every door swipe, even if the card was issued yesterday.

### 2.1.6 Open Design
- **Definition:** The security of a mechanism should **not depend on the secrecy of its design or implementation**. The mechanism may be public; only secrets (keys, passwords) remain private — **Kerckhoffs's Principle (1883)**.
- **Why:** Obscure systems are NOT more secure. They simply delay reverse engineering and prevent peer review.
- **How:** Use open cryptographic standards (AES, RSA, TLS). Publish your threat model. Conduct public bug bounties.
- **Real-world:** AES-256's algorithm is fully public; its security rests solely on the 256-bit key. The algorithm has withstood 20+ years of public cryptanalysis.

### 2.1.7 Separation of Privilege (Separation of Duties)
- **Definition:** A critical operation should require **two or more independent parties** to complete, preventing unilateral abuse of power.
- **Why:** A single malicious insider cannot complete a sensitive action alone. Reduces fraud and coercion attacks.
- **How:** Two-person integrity (TPI) rules, dual-control key custody, four-eyes principle for code deployment, multi-signature cryptocurrency wallets.
- **Real-world:** Nuclear launch requires two authorized personnel turning two separate keys simultaneously.

### 2.1.8 Psychological Acceptability
- **Definition:** Security mechanisms should **not unreasonably burden the user** or operator. If security is too painful, users will work around it.
- **Why:** Humans are the weakest link. A security control that frustrates users will be bypassed, disabled, or ignored.
- **How:** Single Sign-On (SSO) reduces password fatigue. Biometric authentication is faster than 12-character passwords. Clear UX for security warnings.
- **Real-world:** Mandating password rotation every 7 days causes users to write passwords on sticky notes — a *worse* outcome than a 90-day rotation policy.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Risk, Hashing, and Cryptographic Strength**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Description | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Annualized Loss Expectancy** | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | Expected yearly loss from a threat | Currency (₹) |
| 2 | **Single Loss Expectancy** | $SLE = AV \times EF$ | Loss from one occurrence | Currency (₹) |
| 3 | **Exposure Factor** | $EF = \frac{Loss}{Asset Value}$ | Fraction of asset lost | Dimensionless $(0 \le EF \le 1)$ |
| 4 | **Annualized Rate of Occurrence** | $ARO$ | Number of occurrences per year | events/year |
| 5 | **Cost-Benefit of Control** | $CBA = ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - ACS$ | Net benefit of implementing control | Currency (₹) |
| 6 | **Annual Cost of Safeguard** | $ACS$ | Yearly cost to maintain control | Currency (₹) |
| 7 | **Bit Strength of Hash** | $2^n$ | Operations to brute-force $n$-bit hash | Operations |
| 8 | **Birthday Bound** | $O(2^{n/2})$ | Birthday-attack complexity for $n$-bit hash | Operations |
| 9 | **Shannon Entropy** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Information content / password strength | bits |
| 10 | **RSA Key Strength** | $n = p \times q$ | Modulus length for $k$-bit security | bits (e.g., $n \ge 2048$) |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> These characteristics are **not academic abstractions**. They are operationalized in:
> - **NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5** — US federal security control catalog
> - **ISO/IEC 27001:2022** — Information Security Management Systems
> - **OWASP SAMM v2** — Software Assurance Maturity Model
> - **MITRE ATT&CK** — Adversary tactic mapping used to validate defense-in-depth coverage
> - **PCI-DSS v4.0** — Payment card industry compliance explicitly requires defense in depth and least privilege

A Cyber Threat Intelligence analyst uses these characteristics to **assess the architectural maturity** of a target organization's applications and to **recommend remediations** aligned with industry best practice.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Quantitative Risk Analysis using Security Architecture Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A fintech application stores customer credit-card data worth ₹50,00,000. A SQL Injection vulnerability (failure of *Economy of Mechanism*, *Complete Mediation*, and *Least Privilege*) is discovered. Industry data shows this is exploited 4 times per year on average. If a control (parameterized queries + WAF) is implemented, the exploitation rate drops to 0.5/year, and the Exposure Factor drops from 80% to 10%. The control costs ₹80,000/year to maintain.

**Given Data:**
- Asset Value $AV = \text{₹}50{,}00{,}000$
- Exposure Factor (before) $EF_{before} = 0.80$
- Exposure Factor (after) $EF_{after} = 0.10$
- $ARO_{before} = 4$ per year
- $ARO_{after} = 0.5$ per year
- Annual Cost of Safeguard $ACS = \text{₹}80{,}000$

### Step 1 — Compute Single Loss Expectancy (Before)

$$
SLE_{before} = AV \times EF_{before}
$$

$$
SLE_{before} = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.80 = \text{₹}40{,}00{,}000
$$

**Interpretation:** A single successful SQL Injection attack destroys 80% of the asset's value (₹40 Lakhs).

### Step 2 — Compute Annualized Loss Expectancy (Before)

$$
ALE_{before} = SLE_{before} \times ARO_{before}
$$

$$
ALE_{before} = 40{,}00{,}000 \times 4 = \text{₹}1{,}60{,}00{,}000
$$

**Interpretation:** Without the control, the organization expects to lose ₹1.6 Crore per year to this threat.

### Step 3 — Compute Single Loss Expectancy (After Control)

$$
SLE_{after} = AV \times EF_{after}
$$

$$
SLE_{after} = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10 = \text{₹}5{,}00{,}000
$$

**Interpretation:** With the control, each successful attack only destroys 10% of the asset (₹5 Lakhs) — because the WAF catches most payloads and parameterized queries neutralize the attack surface.

### Step 4 — Compute Annualized Loss Expectancy (After Control)

$$
ALE_{after} = SLE_{after} \times ARO_{after}
$$

$$
ALE_{after} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 = \text{₹}2{,}50{,}000
$$

**Interpretation:** With the control, expected annual loss drops to ₹2.5 Lakhs — a **98.4% reduction**.

### Step 5 — Compute Cost-Benefit of the Control

$$
CBA = ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - ACS
$$

$$
CBA = 1{,}60{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}50{,}000 - 80{,}000
$$

$$
CBA = 1{,}56{,}70{,}000
$$

**Interpretation:** Implementing the control yields a **net annual benefit of ₹1.567 Crore**. The control is overwhelmingly justified. The architect correctly applied the principles of **Defense in Depth** (WAF + parameterized queries) and **Economy of Mechanism** (reusing a well-tested library) to recover massive residual risk.

### Step 6 — Verify ROI Sanity Check

$$
ROI = \frac{CBA}{ACS} \times 100\%
$$

$$
ROI = \frac{1{,}56{,}70{,}000}{80{,}000} \times 100\% = 1958.75\%
$$

A **1958% ROI** is exceptionally strong. This is the kind of quantitative justification a KTU 14-mark answer must present.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Threat → Principle Violation → Architectural Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Favorite — Map Each OWASP Top 10 Threat to the Violated Principle and the Required Architectural Control:**

| OWASP Threat | Violated Security Principle | Required Architectural Control |
|---|---|---|
| **A01 — Broken Access Control** | Least Privilege, Complete Mediation | RBAC, server-side authorization, re-check every request |
| **A02 — Cryptographic Failures** | Open Design, Economy of Mechanism | Use TLS 1.3, AES-256, vetted cryptographic libraries |
| **A03 — Injection (SQLi/XSS)** | Complete Mediation, Economy of Mechanism | Parameterized queries, output encoding, WAF |
| **A04 — Insecure Design** | Defense in Depth, Separation of Privilege | Threat modeling, secure design patterns, peer review |
| **A05 — Security Misconfiguration** | Fail Secure Defaults, Economy of Mechanism | Hardened baselines, default-deny, automated config scanning |
| **A06 — Vulnerable Components** | Open Design | SCA (Software Composition Analysis), dependency patching |
| **A07 — Auth Failures** | Complete Mediation, Least Privilege | MFA, session invalidation, password hashing (bcrypt/Argon2) |
| **A08 — Software/Data Integrity** | Separation of Privilege | Code signing, SLSA framework, CI/CD pipeline controls |
| **A09 — Logging/Monitoring Failures** | Psychological Acceptability, Defense in Depth | SIEM integration, tamper-evident audit logs |
| **A10 — SSRF** | Least Privilege, Complete Mediation | Egress filtering, deny-by-default outbound rules |

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Shannon Entropy for a Password

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** Psychological Acceptability demands that password policies be measurable. Shannon entropy quantifies strength.

For a password drawn from an alphabet of size $N$ (e.g., $N = 94$ printable ASCII), with length $L$, assuming uniform random distribution:

$$
H_{max} = L \times \log_2(N)
$$

For a 12-character password from 94 ASCII characters:

$$
H = 12 \times \log_2(94)
$$

$$
\log_2(94) = \frac{\ln 94}{\ln 2} = \frac{4.5433}{0.6931} \approx 6.554
$$

$$
H = 12 \times 6.554 = 78.65 \text{ bits}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 12-character fully random ASCII password provides ~78.65 bits of entropy — well above the **NIST SP 800-63B** recommended 80-bit minimum for memorized secrets when combined with other factors.

This satisfies **Psychological Acceptability**: a *short but random* password (e.g., `k9#Mq$vL2p@Z`) is more secure and more usable than a long, predictable one (`Password123!Summer`).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Defense in Depth — Layered Security Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the **layered security architecture** of a typical web application. Each layer embodies one or more security characteristics.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer1["LAYER 1: PERIMETER - DDoS Shield, CDN, WAF"]:::l1
    layer2["LAYER 2: NETWORK - NGFW, IDS, IPS, VLANs, mTLS"]:::l2
    layer3["LAYER 3: HOST - OS Hardening, HIDS, EDR, Anti-Malware"]:::l3
    layer4["LAYER 4: APPLICATION - SAST, DAST, IAST, RBAC, Input Validation"]:::l4
    layer5["LAYER 5: DATA - AES-256 Encryption, Hashing, Tokenization, Backups"]:::l5
    layer6["LAYER 6: IDENTITY - MFA, SSO, PAM, Zero Trust"]:::l6
    layer7["LAYER 7: MONITORING - SIEM, SOAR, Threat Intel Feeds, UEBA"]:::l7
    threat["Cyber Threat Actor"]:::threat
    intel["CTI Engine - MITRE ATT&CK Mapping"]:::intel
    audit["Assurance - Audit, Pentest, Red Team"]:::audit

    threat --> layer1
    layer1 --> layer2
    layer2 --> layer3
    layer3 --> layer4
    layer4 --> layer5
    layer5 --> layer6
    layer6 --> layer7
    intel -.feeds.-> layer7
    audit -.validates.-> layer1
    audit -.validates.-> layer2
    audit -.validates.-> layer3
    audit -.validates.-> layer4
    audit -.validates.-> layer5
    audit -.validates.-> layer6
    audit -.validates.-> layer7

    classDef l1 fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef l2 fill:#2c5282,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef l3 fill:#2b6cb0,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef l4 fill:#3182ce,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef l5 fill:#4299e1,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef l6 fill:#63b3ed,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    classDef l7 fill:#7fbbeb,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    classDef threat fill:#c53030,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef intel fill:#d69e2e,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    classDef audit fill:#38a169,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.2 Application Security Testing — SAST vs DAST vs IAST

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dev["Developer Commits Code"]:::dev
    sast["SAST Engine - Static Analysis of Source"]:::sast
    build["CI/CD Build Pipeline"]:::build
    deploy["Deployed Running App"]:::deploy
    dast["DAST Engine - Black-box HTTP Probing"]:::dast
    iast["IAST Agent - Runtime Instrumentation"]:::iast
    sca["SCA - Software Composition Analysis"]:::sca
    report["Consolidated Vulnerability Report"]:::report
    cti["CTI Enrichment - CVE, EPSS, ATT&CK"]:::cti

    dev --> sast
    sast --> sca
    sca --> build
    build --> deploy
    deploy --> dast
    deploy --> iast
    sast --> report
    dast --> report
    iast --> report
    sca --> report
    cti -.enriches.-> report

    classDef dev fill:#4a5568,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef sast fill:#805ad5,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef build fill:#3182ce,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef deploy fill:#319795,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef dast fill:#d53f8c,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef iast fill:#ed64a6,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    classDef sca fill:#9f7aea,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef report fill:#2f855a,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef cti fill:#d69e2e,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
```

---

## 4.3 Mapping Security Principles to Architecture Components

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    principle1["Least Privilege"]:::p1
    principle2["Defense in Depth"]:::p2
    principle3["Fail Secure"]:::p3
    principle4["Economy of Mechanism"]:::p4
    principle5["Complete Mediation"]:::p5
    principle6["Open Design"]:::p6
    principle7["Separation of Privilege"]:::p7
    principle8["Psychological Acceptability"]:::p8

    ctrl1["RBAC, IAM, JIT Elevation"]:::c
    ctrl2["Layered Firewall, WAF, IDS, Encryption"]:::c
    ctrl3["Default-deny ACL, Locked Fallback"]:::c
    ctrl4["Standard TLS, AES, Validated Libraries"]:::c
    ctrl5["Per-request Auth, Re-validated Sessions"]:::c
    ctrl6["Public Algorithm, Secret Key Only"]:::c
    ctrl7["Dual Approval, Multi-Sig, Four-Eyes"]:::c
    ctrl8["SSO, Biometrics, Clear UX"]:::c

    principle1 --> ctrl1
    principle2 --> ctrl2
    principle3 --> ctrl3
    principle4 --> ctrl4
    principle5 --> ctrl5
    principle6 --> ctrl6
    principle7 --> ctrl7
    principle8 --> ctrl8

    classDef p1 fill:#2c5282,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef p2 fill:#2b6cb0,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef p3 fill:#3182ce,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef p4 fill:#4299e1,stroke:#ffffff,color:#000000
    classDef p5 fill:#63b3ed,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    classDef p6 fill:#7fbbeb,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    classDef p7 fill:#90cdf4,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    classDef p8 fill:#bee3f8,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    classDef c fill:#38a169,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.4 Secure SDLC — Security Touchpoints Across Development

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    req["1. Requirements - Threat Modeling, Abuse Cases"]:::a
    des["2. Design - Security Architecture, Pattern Selection"]:::b
    impl["3. Implementation - Secure Coding, Peer Review"]:::c
    ver["4. Verification - SAST, DAST, IAST, SCA"]:::d
    rel["5. Release - Sign, Harden, Config Audit"]:::e
    ops["6. Operations - SIEM, Patch, Monitor, CTI"]:::f
    eol["7. Decommission - Data Wipe, Key Rotation"]:::g

    req --> des --> impl --> ver --> rel --> ops --> eol

    classDef a fill:#744210,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef b fill:#975a16,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef c fill:#b7791f,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef d fill:#d69e2e,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    classDef e fill:#ecc94b,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    classDef f fill:#38a169,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef g fill:#2f855a,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** Security characteristics are *not* an afterthought at the verification stage. They must be specified at **Requirements**, designed at **Design**, coded at **Implementation**, tested at **Verification**, and monitored at **Operations**. This is the essence of **secure-by-design** architecture.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> [!NOTE]
> *Each Part A question is worth 3 marks. Provide the precise, board-exam-ready model answer.*

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define the Principle of Least Privilege. Give one real-world example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP)** states that every user, process, or system component should be granted **only the minimum permissions necessary** to perform its authorized task — and no more.

*Real-world example:* In AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM), a Lambda function that reads objects from an S3 bucket is granted only the `s3:GetObject` permission, not `s3:DeleteObject` or `s3:PutObject`. This ensures that even if the Lambda function's credentials are compromised, the attacker cannot delete or modify bucket contents — the *blast radius* of the breach is constrained to read-only access.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark definition + 1 mark example + 1 mark correct technical detail.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**What is Defense in Depth? Why is a single layer of security insufficient?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Defense in Depth** is a security strategy in which **multiple independent, overlapping layers of controls** are deployed so that the failure of one layer does not result in total system compromise.

A single layer of security is insufficient because:
1. **No control is 100% effective** — every control has a non-zero probability of bypass by a sophisticated adversary.
2. **Zero-day vulnerabilities** can disable a single control type; layered controls reduce correlated failure risk.
3. **Defense in depth assumes breach** — it ensures that even after initial compromise, multiple additional barriers (e.g., network segmentation, MFA, encryption) stand between the attacker and the asset.

*Example:* A WAF alone cannot stop SQL injection if input validation is absent. A WAF + parameterized queries + database least-privilege account + encryption at rest = four independent layers, each mitigating a different aspect of the same threat.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark definition + 2 marks justification.)*

---

## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, Part B carries 14 marks per question. Internal choice is mandatory. Both Question A and Question B are presented below.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]**
**(a)** Explain the **eight characteristics of a security architecture** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Describe **SAST and DAST** as application security testing methodologies. Compare their strengths and weaknesses. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

| # | Principle | Explanation | Example | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Least Privilege** | Grant minimum necessary permissions. | Linux user `www-data` owns only `/var/www`, not `/etc/shadow`. | 1 |
| 2 | **Defense in Depth** | Multiple overlapping security layers. | WAF + IDS + encryption + RBAC. | 1 |
| 3 | **Fail Secure** | Default to a safe state on failure. | Firewall that drops all traffic when its rule engine crashes. | 1 |
| 4 | **Economy of Mechanism** | Keep security design simple and small. | Reusing OpenSSL rather than custom crypto. | 1 |
| 5 | **Complete Mediation** | Re-validate every access, no stale decisions. | Re-checking JWT signature and expiry on every API call. | 1 |
| 6 | **Open Design** | Security does not depend on design secrecy (Kerckhoffs's Principle). | AES-256 algorithm is public; only the key is secret. | 1 |
| 7 | **Separation of Privilege** | Critical ops require multiple parties. | Two-person rule for production database schema migration. | 0.5 |
| 8 | **Psychological Acceptability** | Security UX should not unduly burden users. | SSO via Okta reduces password fatigue. | 0.5 |

*(Marks distributed: ~0.7–1 mark per principle, totaling 7 marks.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**SAST (Static Application Security Testing):**
- **What:** Analyzes application source code, bytecode, or binary *without executing* the program.
- **Tools:** SonarQube, Checkmarx, Fortify, Semgrep.
- **Strengths:** Detects vulnerabilities *early* in SDLC (shift-left); finds bugs before deployment; covers all code paths including unreachable ones; no need for a running environment.
- **Weaknesses:** High false-positive rate; cannot detect runtime/authentication/configuration issues; language-specific.

**DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing):**
- **What:** Performs *black-box* testing against a running application by sending crafted HTTP requests and observing responses.
- **Tools:** OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite, Invicti (Netsparker), Acunetix.
- **Strengths:** Tests the *actual deployed behavior*; finds runtime issues (auth, session, SSRF); language-agnostic; low false positives.
- **Weaknesses:** Requires a running environment; can only test reachable code paths; later in SDLC (more expensive to fix); cannot pinpoint exact line of code.

**Comparison Table (to be drawn in exam):**

| Aspect | SAST | DAST |
|---|---|---|
| Stage | Pre-commit / CI | Post-deploy / Staging |
| Visibility | White-box (source) | Black-box (HTTP) |
| Coverage | All code paths | Reachable code paths |
| Speed | Fast | Slower (crawling) |
| False Positives | Higher | Lower |
| Runtime bugs | Misses | Detects |
| Cost to fix | Low (early) | High (late) |

**Modern Best Practice:** Combine **SAST + DAST + IAST + SCA** in a **DevSecOps pipeline** for comprehensive coverage. This is the *defense-in-depth applied to testing itself.*

*(Marks distributed: SAST explanation 2 + DAST explanation 2 + Comparison 2 + DevSecOps conclusion 1 = 7 marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **layered architecture of Defense in Depth** in a web application. Identify the security principle enforced at each layer. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A banking application stores customer KYC data valued at ₹80,00,000. A privilege-escalation vulnerability gives an attacker full database access. Historical data: $EF = 0.9$, $ARO = 2$/year. A control (RBAC + JIT elevation) reduces $EF$ to $0.1$ and $ARO$ to $0.2$/year. The control costs ₹2,00,000/year. Calculate the **Annual Loss Expectancy before and after**, and the **Cost-Benefit Analysis**. Recommend whether to deploy the control. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Diagram (to be drawn — approximate 3 marks):**

```
[Threat Actor]
      ↓
[Layer 1: Perimeter]     → DDoS Shield, CDN, WAF
      ↓
[Layer 2: Network]        → NGFW, IDS/IPS, VLANs, mTLS
      ↓
[Layer 3: Host]           → OS Hardening, HIDS, EDR
      ↓
[Layer 4: Application]    → SAST/DAST, RBAC, Input Validation
      ↓
[Layer 5: Data]           → AES-256, Hashing, Tokenization
      ↓
[Layer 6: Identity]       → MFA, SSO, PAM, Zero Trust
      ↓
[Layer 7: Monitoring]     → SIEM, SOAR, Threat Intel
```

**Principles at each layer (4 marks):**

- **Layer 1 (Perimeter):** *Defense in Depth* (first line) and *Fail Secure* (DDoS shield defaults to challenge).
- **Layer 2 (Network):** *Least Privilege* (segmentation) and *Defense in Depth*.
- **Layer 3 (Host):** *Economy of Mechanism* (hardened baseline) and *Complete Mediation* (HIDS logs all).
- **Layer 4 (Application):** *Complete Mediation* (every request authorized) and *Economy of Mechanism* (validated libraries).
- **Layer 5 (Data):** *Open Design* (AES algorithm public) and *Separation of Privilege* (key custodianship).
- **Layer 6 (Identity):** *Least Privilege* (RBAC, JIT) and *Psychological Acceptability* (SSO UX).
- **Layer 7 (Monitoring):** *Defense in Depth* (last line of detection) and *Open Design* (logs shared with CTI feeds).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $AV = \text{₹}80{,}00{,}000$
- $EF_{before} = 0.9$, $ARO_{before} = 2$
- $EF_{after} = 0.1$, $ARO_{after} = 0.2$
- $ACS = \text{₹}2{,}00{,}000$

**Step 1 — $SLE_{before}$:**

$$
SLE_{before} = AV \times EF_{before} = 80{,}00{,}000 \times 0.9 = \text{₹}72{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — $ALE_{before}$:**

$$
ALE_{before} = SLE_{before} \times ARO_{before} = 72{,}00{,}000 \times 2 = \text{₹}1{,}44{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — $SLE_{after}$:**

$$
SLE_{after} = AV \times EF_{after} = 80{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1 = \text{₹}8{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — $ALE_{after}$:**

$$
ALE_{after} = SLE_{after} \times ARO_{after} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.2 = \text{₹}1{,}60{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Cost-Benefit Analysis:**

$$
CBA = ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - ACS
$$

$$
CBA = 1{,}44{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}60{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}40{,}40{,}000
$$

**Step 6 — ROI:**

$$
ROI = \frac{1{,}40{,}40{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% = 7020\%
$$

**Recommendation (1 mark):** The control yields a **net positive benefit of ₹1.404 Crore per year** with a **7020% ROI**. It should be **deployed immediately** as a security priority. Additionally, it operationalizes **Least Privilege** and **Complete Mediation** — both critical security characteristics for any banking application under **RBI Cyber Security Framework** and **PCI-DSS v4.0** compliance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔥 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing principles:** Students often *swap* "Fail Secure" with "Fail Open." Remember: **secure systems default to denying access on failure**, not granting it. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting Complete Mediation:** The principle is *not* "cache authorization decisions." It is the opposite — **never cache; re-check every access.** (–1 mark)
> 3. **Miscalculating ALE:** Always compute $SLE$ **before** $ALE$. Skipping the $SLE$ step loses 1 mark. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Forgetting units in cost-benefit:** $CBA$ is in **currency (₹)**, $EF$ is **dimensionless**, $ARO$ is **events/year**. Forgetting units loses 0.5 mark per error.
> 5. **Diagram mistakes in Defense in Depth:** Drawing only *two* layers (e.g., firewall + antivirus) is **incomplete**. KTU expects **at least 5–6 layers** spanning perimeter, network, host, application, data, identity, and monitoring. (–2 marks)
> 6. **Mixing SAST and DAST characteristics:** SAST is *static* (no execution), DAST is *dynamic* (runtime). Confusing them in the comparison table loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — 60-second glance before entering the exam hall:**

- ✅ The **8 characteristics** to memorize: **Least Privilege, Defense in Depth, Fail Secure, Economy of Mechanism, Complete Mediation, Open Design, Separation of Privilege, Psychological Acceptability.**
- ✅ **Saltzer & Schroeder (1975)** is the historical origin; **NIST SP 800-160** is the modern systems-engineering reference.
- ✅ The **CIA Triad** is the *goal*; the 8 characteristics are the *means*.
- ✅ **Defense in Depth** = layered, redundant controls. Use the **7-layer web architecture** (Perimeter → Network → Host → App → Data → Identity → Monitoring).
- ✅ **Fail Secure** ≠ Fail Open. Default to **deny**.
- ✅ **Complete Mediation** = re-check **every** access. Never trust client-side or cached authorization.
- ✅ **Open Design** = security by *mathematics*, not obscurity (Kerckhoffs's Principle).
- ✅ **SAST** = static, white-box, early SDLC, source-level, more false positives.
- ✅ **DAST** = dynamic, black-box, late SDLC, runtime behavior, fewer false positives.
- ✅ **IAST** = hybrid runtime instrumentation (best of both worlds).
- ✅ **SCA** = Software Composition Analysis for third-party/open-source dependency vulnerabilities.
- ✅ **Risk formulas:** $SLE = AV \times EF$; $ALE = SLE \times ARO$; $CBA = ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - ACS$.
- ✅ A **positive CBA** justifies the control. A **negative CBA** means the control is not cost-effective.
- ✅ **Kerckhoffs's Principle (1883):** *"The security of a cryptosystem must not depend on keeping the algorithm secret."*
- ✅ **OWASP Top 10 (2021)** is the industry benchmark for application security testing scope.
- ✅ **Zero Trust Architecture** operationalizes *Complete Mediation* and *Least Privilege* in modern cloud-native systems.
- ✅ **DevSecOps** integrates SAST + DAST + IAST + SCA into the CI/CD pipeline — applying *Defense in Depth* to the *testing process itself*.
- ✅ A **7020% ROI** on a security control is not an exaggeration — quantified risk reduction is what justifies security budgets to management.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Application Security and Testing: Characteristics of Security Architecture** 🎯
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High-Level Architectural Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT755 - CYBER THREAT INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** High-Level Architectural Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI)**, **High-Level Architectural Models** refer to standardized, abstract, layered blueprints that define how threat intelligence data is **collected, processed, analyzed, correlated, and disseminated** across an organization's security ecosystem. These models provide a vendor-agnostic, strategic view of the components, data flows, control boundaries, and feedback loops necessary to operationalize threat intelligence within a Security Operations Center (SOC), Threat Intelligence Platform (TIP), or enterprise defense framework.

The most widely referenced high-level architectural models in CTI include:

- **The CTI Reference Architecture (Layered Model)** — data ingestion, normalization, enrichment, analysis, and dissemination layers.
- **The Intelligence Cycle Architecture** — Planning → Collection → Processing → Analysis → Dissemination → Feedback.
- **The Cyber Kill Chain Architecture** — Lockheed Martin's 7-stage model.
- **The MITRE ATT&CK-based Detection Architecture** — Tactic → Technique → Procedure (TTP) mapping.
- **The Defense-in-Depth Architectural Model** — concentric rings of preventive, detective, and responsive controls.
- **The Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA)** — "never trust, always verify" identity-centric model.
- **The SOC Architectural Models** — Centralized, Distributed, and Hybrid SOC topologies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under Module 3, students must be able to *diagram, differentiate, and critically evaluate* at least four of the above architectural models and explain their inter-relationships with the broader CTI lifecycle.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **High-Level CTI Architecture** as the blueprint of a **hospital's emergency response system**:

- **Triage (Collection Layer)** — just as a hospital receives patients from ambulances, walk-ins, and referrals, the CTI system ingests threat feeds from ISACs, OSINT, commercial vendors, and internal sensors.
- **Laboratory (Processing & Enrichment Layer)** — blood samples and X-rays (raw IOCs) are processed and analyzed. In CTI, raw indicators are parsed, deduplicated, and enriched with context (geo-IP, malware family, threat actor attribution).
- **Specialist Doctors (Analysis Layer)** — radiologists interpret results to diagnose the disease. CTI analysts correlate enriched data to identify campaigns, threat actors, and adversary TTPs.
- **Treatment Plan (Dissemination Layer)** — diagnosis is communicated to surgeons, nurses, and the patient's family. In CTI, actionable intelligence is pushed to SOC analysts, firewall rules, EDR policies, and executive dashboards.
- **Follow-up Clinic (Feedback Layer)** — patient outcomes improve future protocols. CTI feedback refines collection priorities and detection rules.

Just as no single doctor can treat all diseases, **no single security control can stop all threats** — hence the need for *layered, architectural thinking*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Threat Intelligence Architecture:**
> A threat intelligence architecture is the structured arrangement of people, processes, and technology that enables the systematic transformation of raw data into actionable intelligence for proactive cyber defense.

## 1.3 Key Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value / Reference |
|---|---|
| **Intelligence Cycle Stages** | **6** (Planning, Collection, Processing, Analysis, Dissemination, Feedback) |
| **Lockheed Martin Kill Chain Stages** | **7** |
| **MITRE ATT&CK Enterprise Tactics** | **14** |
| **Zero Trust Core Principles** | **3** (Verify explicitly, Least privilege, Assume breach) |
| **Defense-in-Depth Layers** | **7** (Data, Application, Host, Network, Perimeter, Physical, Policies) |
| **STIX Object Types** | **18+** (SDO, SRO, SCO) |
| **TAXII Media Types** | **2** (Collection, Channel) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of the Major Architectural Models

### 2.1.1 The CTI Reference Architecture (5-Layer Model)

The industry-standard high-level architecture for a Threat Intelligence Platform (TIP) consists of **five logical layers**:

1. **Data Sources Layer (Layer 1)** — Ingests raw indicators from:
   - **Internal sources:** SIEM logs, EDR telemetry, firewall logs, DNS logs, IDS/IPS alerts, honeypots.
   - **External sources:** Commercial feeds (Recorded Future, Mandiant), ISAC/ISAO sharing, OSINT (VirusTotal, AbuseIPDB), government (CISA AIS, NCSC), dark web monitoring.
2. **Collection & Ingestion Layer (Layer 2)** — Transport protocols:
   - **STIX 2.x** (Structured Threat Information eXpression) — the data schema.
   - **TAXII 2.x** (Trusted Automated Exchange of Intelligence Information) — the transport protocol.
   - **APIs, RSS, email, FTP, syslog** for legacy sources.
3. **Processing & Normalization Layer (Layer 3)** — Transforms heterogeneous data into a uniform STIX 2.x format; performs **deduplication**, **validation** (e.g., RFC-compliant IP/URL syntax), **whitelisting**, and **TLP (Traffic Light Protocol) tagging**.
4. **Analysis & Enrichment Layer (Layer 4)** — Adds context:
   - **Geolocation**, **WHOIS**, **passive DNS**, **sandbox detonation results**, **threat-actor attribution** (e.g., APT28, Lazarus), **MITRE ATT&CK technique mapping**.
5. **Dissemination & Action Layer (Layer 5)** — Delivers finished intelligence to:
   - **Operational consumers:** SOC analysts, incident responders, threat hunters.
   - **Strategic consumers:** CISOs, executive boards, risk management.
   - **Machine consumers:** SIEM rules, EDR IOCs, SOAR playbooks, firewall blocks, NAC policies.

### 2.1.2 The Intelligence Cycle Architecture

A continuous, closed-loop model originally adapted from the U.S. Intelligence Community (IC) and codified by the **Office of the Director of National Intelligence (ODNI)**:

$$\text{Intelligence Cycle} = f(P, C, P_r, A, D, F)$$

Where:
- $P$ = Planning & Direction
- $C$ = Collection
- $P_r$ = Processing & Exploitation
- $A$ = Analysis & Production
- $D$ = Dissemination & Integration
- $F$ = Feedback

The cycle is **iterative** — feedback from stage 6 re-informs stage 1, making the architecture *self-improving*.

### 2.1.3 The Cyber Kill Chain Architecture (Lockheed Martin, 2011)

A **7-stage attacker-centric model** describing the phases an adversary must traverse to achieve their objective:

| Stage | Phase | CTI Architectural Control |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reconnaissance | OSINT monitoring, dark-web surveillance |
| 2 | Weaponization | Malware sandbox analysis, exploit feed monitoring |
| 3 | Delivery | Email security, web proxies, sandboxing |
| 4 | Exploitation | Patching, vulnerability management, HIPS |
| 5 | Installation | EDR, application whitelisting, HIDS |
| 6 | Command & Control (C2) | DNS sinkholing, network segmentation, NDR |
| 7 | Actions on Objectives | DLP, behavioral analytics, deception tech |

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Insight:** Each Kill Chain stage maps to a **detection-and-response capability** in the CTI architecture. Breaking the chain at *any* stage prevents objective completion — this is the architectural basis of **defense-in-depth**.

### 2.1.4 The MITRE ATT&CK-Based Detection Architecture

**ATT&CK (Adversarial Tactics, Techniques, and Common Knowledge)** is a knowledge base that models adversary behavior post-compromise across three matrices:
- **Enterprise** (Windows, macOS, Linux, Cloud, Network, Containers).
- **Mobile** (Android, iOS).
- **ICS** (Industrial Control Systems).

The architecture follows: **Tactic (the why) → Technique (the how) → Procedure (the specific implementation) → Mitigation → Detection**.

### 2.1.5 The Defense-in-Depth Architecture

A concentric, multi-layer architectural model where controls are stacked so that failure of one layer is compensated by another. The seven canonical layers are:

1. **Policies, Procedures & Awareness (Human Layer)**
2. **Physical Security**
3. **Perimeter (Network Edge)**
4. **Internal Network**
5. **Host (Endpoint)**
6. **Application**
7. **Data**

### 2.1.6 The Zero Trust Architecture (NIST SP 800-207)

Replaces the implicit-trust perimeter model with three core principles:

- **Verify explicitly** — authenticate and authorize *every* transaction based on identity, device, location, and data sensitivity.
- **Least-privilege access** — just-in-time and just-enough-access (JIT/JEA).
- **Assume breach** — design as if the attacker is *already inside*; segment, log, and monitor everything.

The logical components are: **Policy Engine (PE) + Policy Administrator (PA) → Policy Enforcement Point (PEP)**.

### 2.1.7 SOC Architectural Models

| Model | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Centralized SOC** | Single physical location, all analysts, all tools | Large enterprises, regulatory alignment |
| **Distributed SOC** | Multiple regional SOCs with local analysts | Global multinationals, follow-the-sun |
| **Hybrid SOC** | Core in-house + outsourced MDR/MSP | Mid-size orgs balancing cost and 24/7 coverage |
| **Virtual SOC** | Cloud-native, no fixed physical location | SMBs, digital-native firms |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Rule | Mathematical / Logical Form | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Intelligence Cycle Loop** | $IC = \{P \to C \to P_r \to A \to D \to F \to P\}$ | Closed-loop iterative architecture |
| **Defense-in-Depth Coverage** | $C_{total} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} C_i$ | Total coverage = union of all control layers |
| **Single-point-of-failure test** | $\text{Resilience} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - r_i)$ | Probability that *at least one* layer catches the attack |
| **IOC Confidence Score** | $S_{ioc} = w_1 \cdot s_{source} + w_2 \cdot s_{age} + w_3 \cdot s_{correlation}$ | Weighted scoring for indicator trustworthiness |
| **Zero Trust Verification** | $\text{Allow}(req) = \text{PE}(ID, Device, Context, Data)$ | Allow only if Policy Engine permits |
| **Kill Chain Disruption** | $\text{Disruption} = 1$ if any stage blocked, else $0$ | Binary: chain either broken or complete |
| **STIX Object Count** | $\vert \text{STIX Objects} \vert \geq 18$ | Minimum 18 SDOs/SROs/SCOs |
| **Kill Chain Stage Count** | $\vert \text{KC} \vert = 7$ | Lockheed Martin standard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter in engineering?** A CTI architecture is the *operating system* of a modern SOC. Without it, intelligence remains a chaotic data-dump rather than a strategic asset. Senior CTI engineers architect these pipelines; security managers justify budgets using these frameworks; auditors (ISO 27001, SOC 2, PCI-DSS) measure compliance against them.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SIEM/SOAR Engineering:** Architecting Splunk, Elastic, Chronicle, or Sentinel deployments against the 5-layer CTI model.
- **Threat Intelligence Platform (TIP) Selection:** Comparing MISP, Anomali, ThreatConnect, OpenCTI using the reference architecture as a benchmark.
- **Red/Blue Team Operations:** Mapping adversary TTPs to ATT&CK and then to defensive controls.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Mapping PCI-DSS, GDPR, RBI, SEBI, and DPDP Act controls to the Defense-in-Depth architecture.
- **Cloud Security Engineering:** Implementing Zero Trust in AWS, Azure, and GCP using identity-aware proxies and micro-segmentation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Single-Layer vs Multi-Layer Defense Coverage

We mathematically derive why a **layered architecture is superior** to a single-control architecture.

### Step 1 — Define the Problem
Let each control layer $i$ have an **independent detection/prevention probability** $r_i$, where $0 \leq r_i \leq 1$.

### Step 2 — Probability of Failure of a Single Layer
The probability that layer $i$ *fails* to block the attack is:

$$
p_i = 1 - r_i
$$

### Step 3 — Probability That All Layers Fail (Independent)
Assuming layers operate independently (a reasonable architectural assumption in defense-in-depth):

$$
P_{all\_fail} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i = \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - r_i)
$$

### Step 4 — Probability That At Least One Layer Succeeds
Using the **complement rule**:

$$
P_{defense} = 1 - P_{all\_fail} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - r_i)
$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Demonstration
Suppose we have 4 layers, each with $r_i = 0.5$ (50% individual effectiveness).

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{all\_fail} &= (1 - 0.5)^4 = (0.5)^4 = 0.0625 \\
P_{defense} &= 1 - 0.0625 = 0.9375 = 93.75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Four mediocre (50%) controls layered together block 93.75% of attacks — a massive improvement over a single 50% control.

### Step 6 — With Realistic Layered Efficiencies
Let layers be: Firewall ($r_1 = 0.7$), EDR ($r_2 = 0.6$), SIEM correlation ($r_3 = 0.5$), SOC analyst ($r_4 = 0.8$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{all\_fail} &= (1 - 0.7)(1 - 0.6)(1 - 0.5)(1 - 0.8) \\
&= (0.3)(0.4)(0.5)(0.2) \\
&= 0.012 \\
P_{defense} &= 1 - 0.012 = 0.988 = 98.8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Insight:** This is the *quantitative proof* of why high-level architectural models mandate layered controls. A single 70% firewall is far weaker than a 70%-60%-50%-80% layered stack.

## 3.2 Derivation: IOC Confidence Score (Weighted Multi-Source)

A real CTI architecture must rank indicators by trustworthiness. The weighted confidence score is derived as:

$$
S_{ioc} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} w_j \cdot s_j
$$

Where:
- $w_j$ = weight of source $j$ (e.g., government = 0.9, commercial = 0.7, OSINT = 0.5).
- $s_j$ = normalized freshness/age score of the indicator from source $j$ (0 to 1).
- Constraint: $\sum_{j=1}^{k} w_j = 1$

### Worked Example
An IP indicator `198.51.100.42` is observed by three sources:

| Source | Weight $w_j$ | Freshness $s_j$ | Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| CISA AIS | 0.5 | 0.9 | $0.5 \times 0.9 = 0.45$ |
| VirusTotal | 0.3 | 0.6 | $0.3 \times 0.6 = 0.18$ |
| Internal SIEM | 0.2 | 0.8 | $0.2 \times 0.8 = 0.16$ |

$$
S_{ioc} = 0.45 + 0.18 + 0.16 = 0.79
$$

A score of **0.79** indicates **high confidence** — the IP should be auto-blocked in the firewall (action layer of CTI architecture).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Architectural Model Comparison

| Criterion | CTI Ref. Arch. | Intel Cycle | Kill Chain | ATT&CK | Defense-in-Depth | Zero Trust | SOC Models |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Focus** | Data pipeline | Process loop | Adversary path | Adversary behavior | Control layers | Identity & access | Operations |
| **Stage Count** | 5 layers | 6 phases | 7 stages | 14 tactics | 7 layers | 3 principles | 3–4 topologies |
| **Best For** | TIP engineering | Program mgmt. | Detection | Threat hunting | Resilience | Cloud/remote | 24/7 ops |
| **Origin** | Industry | U.S. IC | Lockheed Martin | MITRE (2013) | DoD/NIST | NIST SP 800-207 | SANS/ISACA |
| **Data Schema** | STIX/TAXII | N/A | N/A | STIX/ATT&CK | N/A | N/A | N/A |
| **Feedback Loop** | Yes (Stage 5→1) | Yes (Stage 6→1) | No | Indirect | No | Yes (continuous auth) | Yes |
| **Maturity** | High | High | Mature | Active | Mature | Emerging | Mature |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Evaluation Tip:** When asked to *compare* models, students must mention at least **three** distinguishing criteria (e.g., focus, stages, feedback, schema, use-case) — a common 14-mark question pattern.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
