# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define the term "High-Level Architectural Model" in the context of Cyber Threat Intelligence. List any three such models.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A High-Level Architectural Model in CTI is an **abstract, layered blueprint** that defines how threat intelligence data is collected, processed, analyzed, and disseminated across an organization's security infrastructure. It provides a vendor-neutral, strategic view of components, data flows, and feedback mechanisms.

**Three models (1 mark each):**
1. **CTI Reference Architecture** (5-layer ingestion-to-dissemination model).
2. **The Intelligence Cycle** (6-phase closed loop).
3. **Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain** (7-stage attacker model).

*(Also acceptable: MITRE ATT&CK, Defense-in-Depth, Zero Trust Architecture, SOC models.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between the Cyber Kill Chain and the MITRE ATT&CK framework in 3 points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks = 1 mark per point):**

| # | Cyber Kill Chain | MITRE ATT&CK |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 7 high-level, **pre-compromise** stages. | **14 tactics, 200+ techniques**, focused on **post-compromise** adversary behavior. |
| 2 | Adversary-centric, sequential and linear. | Behavior-centric, non-linear; an attack can use multiple techniques per tactic. |
| 3 | Provides a **macro view** of an attack's lifecycle. | Provides a **micro view** of *how* each technique is executed (procedures). |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** *ESE (End Semester Evaluation) Part B carries 14 marks. Students answer ONE question from an internal choice (A or B). Each 14-mark question has sub-parts of 7 + 7 marks.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**Q.A. (a)** Explain the **5-Layer CTI Reference Architecture** in detail. For each layer, identify **two** data sources, controls, or consumer systems. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The 5-Layer CTI Reference Architecture is the industry-standard blueprint for designing a Threat Intelligence Platform. Each layer has a distinct function:

**Layer 1 — Data Sources (1 mark):**
- *Internal sources:* SIEM logs (e.g., Splunk), EDR telemetry (e.g., CrowdStrike), firewall logs, DNS query logs, IDS/IPS alerts, honeypot captures.
- *External sources:* Commercial feeds (Mandiant, Recorded Future), ISAC/ISAO communities, government feeds (CISA AIS, NCSC), OSINT (VirusTotal, AbuseIPDB), dark-web monitors.

**Layer 2 — Collection & Ingestion (1 mark):**
- Uses **STIX 2.x** as the data schema and **TAXII 2.x** as the transport protocol.
- Legacy ingestion via APIs, RSS, email, FTP, syslog.
- Performs **rate-limiting**, **TLP tagging**, and **source authentication**.

**Layer 3 — Processing & Normalization (1.5 marks):**
- Converts heterogeneous data into uniform STIX 2.x format.
- Performs **deduplication** (removing redundant IOCs), **validation** (RFC syntax for IPs, hashes), **whitelisting** (filtering out known-good internal assets), and **sanitization**.

**Layer 4 — Analysis & Enrichment (1.5 marks):**
- Adds contextual intelligence: **geolocation**, **WHOIS**, **passive DNS**, **malware sandbox detonation** (Cuckoo, Joe Sandbox).
- Performs **threat-actor attribution** (e.g., APT28, Lazarus) and maps indicators to **MITRE ATT&CK techniques**.

**Layer 5 — Dissemination & Action (2 marks):**
- *Operational consumers:* SOC analysts (via TIP dashboards), incident responders, threat hunters.
- *Strategic consumers:* CISOs, executive board, risk management.
- *Machine consumers:* SIEM correlation rules, EDR IOCs, SOAR playbooks, firewall block-lists, NAC policies, email gateway filters.

**Architectural insight:** The architecture is **bidirectional at the feedback plane** — outcomes from Layer 5 inform Layer 2 collection priorities, making it a *living system*.

---

**Q.A. (b)** With a **neat Mermaid/Block diagram**, describe the **Intelligence Cycle** as a high-level architectural model. List **two feedback mechanisms** that close the loop. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```
PLANNING → COLLECTION → PROCESSING → ANALYSIS → DISSEMINATION → FEEDBACK
   ↑                                                                    │
   └─────────────────── feedback loop ──────────────────────────────────┘
```

*Stage definitions (2 marks):*
1. **Planning & Direction** — define intelligence requirements (PIRs), priority intelligence requirements, and consumer needs.
2. **Collection** — acquire raw data from HUMINT, SIGINT, OSINT, and internal telemetry.
3. **Processing & Exploitation** — translate raw data into a usable format (parse, decrypt, normalize).
4. **Analysis & Production** — evaluate sources, assess confidence, integrate information, and produce finished intelligence.
5. **Dissemination & Integration** — deliver intelligence to the right consumer in the right format at the right time.
6. **Feedback** — consumers evaluate the relevance, accuracy, and timeliness of the intelligence received.

**Two feedback mechanisms (2 marks):**
1. **Relevance feedback** — SOC analysts report which IOCs actually triggered detections, allowing collection re-prioritization.
2. **Gap analysis feedback** — when a PIR cannot be answered due to insufficient collection, the architecture triggers new source acquisition or sensor deployment.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]**

**Q.B. (a)** Compare **Defense-in-Depth** and **Zero Trust Architecture** as high-level architectural models. Use a comparison table covering **at least five criteria**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (1.4 marks per criterion):**

| # | Criterion | Defense-in-Depth | Zero Trust Architecture |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Trust Model** | Implicit trust *inside* the perimeter; trust *outside* is denied. | **Zero implicit trust** — verify every transaction, regardless of location. |
| 2 | **Control Structure** | Concentric, layered (7 layers from policies to data). | Identity-centric; PE-PA-PEP logical components. |
| 3 | **Failure Assumption** | Defends against external threats; partial trust in internal network. | **Assumes breach** — designs as if the attacker is already inside. |
| 4 | **Granularity** | Layer-level controls (perimeter, network, host). | Per-request, per-transaction controls (micro-segmentation, JIT access). |
| 5 | **Best Fit Environment** | Traditional on-premise networks, regulated industries. | Cloud, hybrid work, BYOD, Zero-Trust Network Access (ZTNA). |
| 6 | **Origin/Standard** | DoD Orange Book (1983), NIST frameworks. | NIST SP 800-207 (2020), John Kindervag (Forrester, 2010). |
| 7 | **Complementary?** | Yes — Zero Trust can be implemented *within* a Defense-in-Depth framework. | Yes — DoiD layers can be Zero-Trust-enabled. |

**Conclusion (0.2 marks):** The two models are **complementary, not competing** — modern enterprises blend both for optimal resilience.

---

**Q.B. (b)** Describe the **Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain** as a high-level architectural model. For **each of the 7 stages**, identify **one defensive control** that can disrupt it. **(7 marks = 1 mark per stage):**

| Stage | Adversary Action | Defensive Control (CTI Architectural Counter) |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Reconnaissance | Harvesting targets from OSINT/social media | **OSINT monitoring and dark-web surveillance**; takedown of leaked credentials. |
| 2. Weaponization | Coupling exploit with malware payload | **Malware sandbox analysis**; threat intel on emerging CVEs and exploit kits. |
| 3. Delivery | Phishing email, malicious URL, USB drop | **Email security gateway** (anti-phishing), **web proxy** with URL sandboxing. |
| 4. Exploitation | Triggering vulnerability (e.g., CVE) | **Vulnerability management**, **patching**, **HIPS** (Host Intrusion Prevention). |
| 5. Installation | Installing backdoor, persistence | **EDR** (Endpoint Detection & Response), **application whitelisting**, **HIDS**. |
| 6. Command & Control (C2) | Beaconing to attacker server | **DNS sinkholing**, **NDR** (Network Detection & Response), **egress filtering**. |
| 7. Actions on Objectives | Data exfiltration, ransomware | **DLP** (Data Loss Prevention), **behavioral UEBA analytics**, **deception tech**. |

**Key insight:** The Cyber Kill Chain architecture teaches that an attack must **traverse all 7 stages sequentially** — disrupting *any* one stage prevents objective completion. This is the architectural foundation of *early detection* and *proactive defense*.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing STIX with TAXII** — STIX is the *schema*; TAXII is the *transport*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for misuse.
> 2. **Listing Kill Chain stages in the wrong order** — Reconnaissance is **stage 1**, not 6. Order matters.
> 3. **Conflating Zero Trust with Defense-in-Depth** — they are *complementary*, not synonyms. A 14-mark comparison that omits this point loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the feedback loop in the Intelligence Cycle** — Stage 6 is *Feedback*; closing it back to Stage 1 is what makes the architecture "intelligence-driven".
> 5. **Drawing poor diagrams** — Mermaid blocks with unescaped pipes `|`, bold `**`, or reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`) as node IDs will fail to render. Use uppercase alphanumeric text only.
> 6. **No real-world examples** — Examiners award 1–2 marks for naming actual products (MISP, Splunk, CrowdStrike, ATT&CK) or actual threat actors (APT28, Lazarus, FIN7).
> 7. **Missing the ATT&CK "post-compromise" scope** — Kill Chain is *pre-compromise* focused; ATT&CK is *post-compromise* focused. This distinction is frequently asked.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — High-Level Architectural Models (Module 3)**

- **CTI Reference Architecture has exactly 5 layers:** Sources → Ingestion → Processing → Analysis → Dissemination. Memorize the flow.
- **STIX** = schema (the "what"); **TAXII** = transport (the "how"). They are *complementary*, not interchangeable.
- **Intelligence Cycle = 6 phases** ending in **Feedback** that closes the loop to Planning.
- **Cyber Kill Chain = 7 stages**: Reconnaissance, Weaponization, Delivery, Exploitation, Installation, C2, Actions on Objectives.
- **MITRE ATT&CK = 14 Enterprise tactics** and **200+ techniques**; it is **post-compromise** in scope.
- **Defense-in-Depth = 7 layers** from policies/awareness to data; it is **layered and concentric**.
- **Zero Trust = 3 principles** (verify explicitly, least privilege, assume breach) + **PE-PA-PEP** logical model.
- **SOC Models:** Centralized, Distributed, Hybrid, Virtual — choose based on geography, scale, and budget.
- **Quantitative proof of layering:** 4 layers × 50% effectiveness = **93.75%** cumulative defense.
- **IOC Confidence Scoring** uses a weighted sum: $S_{ioc} = \sum w_j \cdot s_j$ with $\sum w_j = 1$.
- **High-yield keywords for 14-mark answers:** STIX/TAXII, TLP, ATT&CK, TTP, ZTNA, PE/PA/PEP, MISP, threat actor attribution.
- **Always close the feedback loop** in your Intelligence Cycle and TIP architecture diagrams — examiners actively look for it.
- **Defense-in-Depth and Zero Trust are complementary**, not competing architectures — a frequent 14-mark trap question.
- **TLP (Traffic Light Protocol) tags:** RED (internal only), AMBER (limited sharing), GREEN (community-wide), WHITE (unrestricted).
- **Architectural patterns to remember:** hub-and-spoke TIP, pipeline (lambda/kappa), broker-based, federated sharing via ISAC/ISAO.
- **Commonly tested CO (Course Outcome):** *CO3 — Design and evaluate threat intelligence architectures for enterprise security operations.*

**All the best for your KTU 2024 End-Semester Examination!** 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Patterns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT755 - CYBER THREAT INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Security Patterns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security Pattern (KTU 2024 Official Definition):** A *Security Pattern* is a reusable, well-proven architectural and design-level solution template that systematically addresses a recurring security problem within a specific context, capturing expert knowledge of how to defend against known categories of cyber threats. Each pattern is documented using a standardized template comprising four mandatory elements: **Context**, **Problem**, **Solution**, and **Consequences** (Schumacher et al. security pattern schema).

A Security Pattern is **not** a finished piece of code or a single algorithm — it is an *architectural knowledge unit* that prescribes the structure, participating components, responsibilities, and interactions required to enforce a specific security property (such as *confidentiality*, *integrity*, *authenticity*, *authorization*, or *non-repudiation*) within a software-intensive system.

The concept was formally introduced by **Yoder and Barcalow (1997)** at PLoP (Pattern Languages of Programs) and later systematized into a catalog of approximately 65+ core patterns by **Schumacher, Fernandez-Buglioni, Hybertson, Schmidt, and Sommerlad (2006)** — the canonical reference adopted by most security engineering curricula, including KTU's elective cybersecurity tracks.

### The AAA Triad — The Foundation of All Security Patterns

> [!NOTE]
> Every security pattern, regardless of category, ultimately enforces one or more of the three fundamental security services known as the **AAA Triad**:
>
> - **A**uthentication — *Who are you?* (Identity verification)
> - **A**uthorization — *What are you allowed to do?* (Access control)
> - **A**uditing — *What did you do, and when?* (Accountability and traceability)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bank Vault Blueprint

Imagine you are an **architect designing 50 bank vaults across a country**. Instead of inventing a new vault for each bank, you design **one master blueprint** (the *pattern*) that any engineer can replicate. This blueprint specifies:

| Real-World Bank Vault | Security Pattern Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The physical **vault room** | The *Context* (where the pattern applies) |
| The **threat of robbery** | The *Problem* (what security risk exists) |
| The **combination lock + steel door + CCTV** | The *Solution* (how the pattern mitigates the threat) |
| The **inconvenience to legitimate staff** | The *Consequences* (trade-offs introduced) |

A *Security Pattern* works identically — it is a **master blueprint** for solving a class of security problems (e.g., "how do I safely authenticate a remote user?") that can be instantiated in any system.

## 1.3 Core Security Metrics & Constants Used in Pattern Evaluation

The following standard security metrics are referenced throughout the official KTU PECCT755 syllabus when comparing the strength of cryptographic and pattern-based solutions:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Cryptographic Key Strength Benchmarks (NIST SP 800-131A Compliant):**
>
> - **Symmetric key (AES):** minimum **128 bits**, recommended **256 bits**
> - **Asymmetric key (RSA/ECC):** minimum **2048 bits** (RSA) or **256 bits** (ECC)
> - **Hash output (SHA-2 family):** **256 bits** (SHA-256) to **512 bits** (SHA-512)
> - **Password entropy threshold for "strong":** **≥ 80 bits**
> - **Session token entropy minimum:** **≥ 64 bits**
> - **OWASP-recommended bcrypt cost factor:** **≥ 12**

These values are **bold** because they appear verbatim in KTU university exam answer keys and are considered non-negotiable factual constants.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mandatory Four-Part Pattern Schema (Schumacher Schema)

Every KTU-grade security pattern **must** be documented using the following four structural elements. This is the exact schema evaluators expect in 14-mark descriptive answers:

1. **Context** — The recurring situation in which the pattern applies (e.g., *a distributed web application with multiple subsystems*).
2. **Problem** — The security threat or design issue to be resolved (e.g., *user credentials are being transmitted in plaintext*).
3. **Solution** — The static structure + dynamic behaviour that resolves the problem (e.g., *wrap credentials in a TLS tunnel and apply the **Authenticator Pattern***).
4. **Consequences** — The trade-offs, side effects, and residual risks (e.g., *single point of failure, latency increase, certificate management overhead*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid (For KTU Viva):** Think **C-P-S-C** = **C**ontext → **P**roblem → **S**olution → **C**onsequences.

## 2.2 The Master Taxonomy of Security Patterns

Security patterns are classified into **seven primary categories** based on the security service they enforce. This taxonomy is the most tested topic in Module 3 university exams.

### Category 1 — Authentication Patterns
Patterns that verify the identity of a principal (user, process, or device).

| Pattern Name | Core Function | KTU-Relevant Detail |
|---|---|---|
| **Authenticator** | Verifies credentials against a credential store | Centralizes all credential validation in one component |
| **Single Sign-On (SSO)** | Allows one authenticated session to access multiple systems | Uses a **Ticket-Granting Ticket (TGT)** in Kerberos, or **SAML/OAuth 2.0 tokens** |
| **Password Authentication** | Validates user-chosen secrets | Must use **salting** + **bcrypt/Argon2** hashing |
| **Biometric Authentication** | Verifies identity via physiological traits | Subject to **False Accept Rate (FAR)** and **False Reject Rate (FRR)** |

### Category 2 — Authorization Patterns
Patterns that enforce access control after authentication.

| Pattern Name | Core Function | KTU-Relevant Detail |
|---|---|---|
| **Authorization Layer** | Abstract access checks into a dedicated middleware | Implements **RBAC** (Role-Based) or **ABAC** (Attribute-Based) |
| **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** | Grants permissions based on roles | Reduces permission explosion; NIST INCITS 359-2012 standard |
| **Capability-Based Access Control** | Issues unforgeable tokens (capabilities) | Token *is* the permission; reduces privilege escalation risk |

### Category 3 — Cryptographic Patterns
Patterns that ensure confidentiality, integrity, and non-repudiation via cryptography.

| Pattern Name | Core Function | KTU-Relevant Detail |
|---|---|---|
| **Secure Logger** | Encrypts and appends audit logs | Uses **HMAC-SHA256** for tamper detection |
| **Encryption Pattern** | Hides data at rest or in transit | AES-256-GCM for symmetric, RSA-2048+ for key exchange |
| **Digital Signature Pattern** | Provides non-repudiation | Uses **private key signing** + **public key verification** |

### Category 4 — Communication Security Patterns

| Pattern Name | Core Function |
|---|---|
| **Secure Channel** | Establishes an encrypted communication path (e.g., TLS 1.3) |
| **Trusted Proxy / Reverse Gateway** | Centralizes encryption termination and request validation |

### Category 5 — Input & Output Validation Patterns

| Pattern Name | Core Function |
|---|---|
| **Input Validator** | Sanitizes all user input at the perimeter (whitelisting preferred over blacklisting) |
| **Output Encoder** | Escapes data before rendering to prevent **XSS** (Cross-Site Scripting) |

### Category 6 — Session Management Patterns

| Pattern Name | Core Function |
|---|---|
| **Session Management** | Generates, stores, validates, and expires session tokens |
| **Session Timeout** | Forces re-authentication after inactivity (typically **15 minutes** for high-security apps) |

### Category 7 — Auditing & Monitoring Patterns

| Pattern Name | Core Function |
|---|---|
| **Audit Trail** | Records security-relevant events with tamper-evidence |
| **Intrusion Detection Pattern** | Implements IDS/IPS hook points at architectural level |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Security Pattern Strength Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **directly assessed** in KTU numerical/descriptive questions on Module 3.

| Metric | Formula | Variable Definitions | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Password Entropy** | $E = L \cdot \log_2(R)$ | $E$ = entropy in bits, $L$ = password length, $R$ = pool size of character set | **bits** |
| **Brute-Force Time** | $T = \dfrac{2^{E}}{H \cdot 2}$ | $T$ = cracking time, $E$ = entropy, $H$ = hash rate (hashes/sec) | **seconds** |
| **Key Space Size** | $S = 2^{k}$ | $S$ = possible keys, $k$ = key length in bits | **dimensionless** |
| **Biometric Crossover Error Rate (CER)** | $CER = \dfrac{FAR + FRR}{2}$ | $FAR$ = False Accept Rate, $FRR$ = False Reject Rate | **percentage** |
| **RSA Modulus Strength** | $n = p \cdot q$ | $n$ = modulus, $p,q$ = large primes of size $k/2$ bits | **bits** |
| **Hash Collision Probability (Birthday)** | $P \approx 1 - e^{-n^{2}/(2 \cdot 2^{L})}$ | $n$ = number of hashes, $L$ = hash output bits | **probability** |
| **BCrypt Effective Cost** | $C_{eff} = 2^{cost}$ | $cost$ = bcrypt work factor (recommended $\geq 12$) | **iterations** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pipe-Symbol Trap:** In answer sheets and even in typed notes, **never** write absolute value as `$|x|$` inside markdown tables — this breaks the table renderer. Always use `$\vert x \vert$` or `$\mid x \mid$`.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Security patterns are the **backbone of modern DevSecOps pipelines**. In industry:
- **Microsoft Azure Architecture Center** publishes an open catalog of cloud-native security patterns.
- **OWASP** (Open Web Application Security Project) maintains the **ASVS** (Application Security Verification Standard) that maps directly to pattern-based defenses.
- **Amazon AWS Well-Architected Framework** has an entire "Security Pillar" organized as patterns.

A KTU graduate who masters these patterns can immediately contribute to **threat modeling (STRIDE)**, **secure code review**, and **architecture risk analysis (ARA)** in any cybersecurity role.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation #1: Password Entropy & Brute-Force Time

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark Part A):** A system uses 8-character passwords drawn from a 94-character printable ASCII set. Calculate the entropy and estimate the time to crack using a GPU capable of $10^{11}$ hashes/second.

**Given Data:**
- Password length: $L = 8$ characters
- Character pool size: $R = 94$ (printable ASCII: 26 lowercase + 26 uppercase + 10 digits + 32 special)
- Attacker hash rate: $H = 10^{11}$ hashes/second

**Step 1 — State the entropy formula.**
The information-theoretic entropy of a uniformly random password is given by:
$$E = L \cdot \log_{2}(R)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**
$$E = 8 \cdot \log_{2}(94)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the logarithm.**
$$\log_{2}(94) = \dfrac{\ln(94)}{\ln(2)} = \dfrac{4.5433}{0.6931} = 6.5546 \text{ bits per character}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by password length.**
$$E = 8 \cdot 6.5546 = 52.437 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the brute-force time formula.**
On average, an attacker must search half the keyspace:
$$T = \dfrac{2^{E}}{H \cdot 2} = \dfrac{2^{E}}{2H}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the keyspace size.**
$$2^{52.437} = 5.94 \times 10^{15} \text{ possible passwords}$$

**Step 7 — Compute cracking time.**
$$T = \dfrac{5.94 \times 10^{15}}{2 \cdot 10^{11}} = \dfrac{5.94 \times 10^{15}}{2 \times 10^{11}}$$

**Step 8 — Final numerical result.**
$$T = 2.97 \times 10^{4} \text{ seconds} \approx 8.25 \text{ hours}$$

**Conclusion:** An 8-character ASCII password is **crackable in less than a single day** by modern GPU hardware — this is the engineering rationale behind NIST's recommendation of **multi-factor authentication** and **password length $\geq 12$ characters** (entropy $\geq 80$ bits).

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation #2: Birthday Attack on Hash Functions

**Problem Statement:** A system uses **SHA-256** for generating session token IDs. How many tokens must be generated before the probability of a collision reaches **1%**?

**Step 1 — State the birthday-bound formula.**
For a hash function with $L$-bit output, the approximate collision probability after $n$ samples is:
$$P(n) \approx 1 - e^{-n^{2}/(2 \cdot 2^{L})}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the parameters.**
$$L = 256 \text{ bits (SHA-256 output)}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $n$ when $P = 0.01$.**
$$0.01 = 1 - e^{-n^{2}/(2^{257})}$$
$$e^{-n^{2}/(2^{257})} = 0.99$$
$$-\dfrac{n^{2}}{2^{257}} = \ln(0.99) \approx -0.01005$$
$$n^{2} = 0.01005 \cdot 2^{257}$$
$$n = \sqrt{0.01005 \cdot 2^{257}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $2^{257}$.**
$$2^{257} \approx 2.31 \times 10^{77}$$

**Step 5 — Final evaluation.**
$$n = \sqrt{0.01005 \cdot 2.31 \times 10^{77}} = \sqrt{2.32 \times 10^{75}} \approx 4.82 \times 10^{37} \text{ tokens}$$

**Conclusion:** SHA-256 is **cryptographically collision-resistant for all practical session volumes** — collisions become statistically significant only at astronomically large sample sizes, which is why **256-bit hashes are NIST-approved for the year 2030 and beyond**.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation #3: Biometric Crossover Error Rate (CER)

**Problem Statement:** A fingerprint scanner has FAR = 0.001% and FRR = 4%. Compute the CER and determine the operational security posture.

**Step 1 — State the CER formula.**
$$CER = \dfrac{FAR + FRR}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Normalize both rates to the same scale.**
$$FAR = 0.001\% = 0.00001$$
$$FRR = 4\% = 0.04$$

**Step 3 — Sum the rates.**
$$FAR + FRR = 0.00001 + 0.04 = 0.04001$$

**Step 4 — Divide by 2.**
$$CER = \dfrac{0.04001}{2} = 0.020005 = 2.0005\%$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**
- A CER of ~2% indicates **excellent biometric performance** (commercial systems target CER < 5%).
- The system should be **tuned to operate at the CER threshold** by adjusting the matching sensitivity knob to balance user convenience (low FRR) against security (low FAR).

**Conclusion:** The fingerprint scanner is suitable for **high-security authentication** such as banking apps and physical access control to data centers.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pattern Selection Decision Table

Use the following matrix to select the appropriate security pattern for a given threat (this is a frequently tested 7-mark question in KTU Module 3):

| Threat Scenario | Recommended Pattern | Auxiliary Pattern | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Replay attack on login form | **Authenticator + Nonce** | Secure Channel (TLS) | Nonce prevents reuse of captured credentials |
| Privilege escalation | **Authorization Layer (RBAC)** | Session Management | Role-checking middleware blocks unauthorized actions |
| Data tampering at rest | **Encryption Pattern (AES-GCM)** | Secure Logger | GCM provides authenticated encryption |
| Insider threat | **Audit Trail** | Separation of Duties | Tamper-evident logs + dual control prevent cover-ups |
| SQL Injection | **Input Validator** | Parameterized Query Pattern | Whitelisting strips malicious payloads |
| Session hijacking via XSS | **Output Encoder + HttpOnly Cookies** | Session Management | Output encoding neutralizes injected scripts |
| Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) | **Secure Channel (TLS 1.3)** | Trusted Proxy | Mutual certificate authentication defeats MitM |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Taxonomy — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Security Patterns] --> B[Authentication Patterns]
    A --> C[Authorization Patterns]
    A --> D[Cryptographic Patterns]
    A --> E[Communication Patterns]
    A --> F[Validation Patterns]
    A --> G[Session Management Patterns]
    A --> H[Auditing and Monitoring Patterns]

    B --> B1[Authenticator]
    B --> B2[Single Sign On]
    B --> B3[Password Authentication]
    B --> B4[Biometric Authentication]

    C --> C1[Authorization Layer]
    C --> C2[Role Based Access Control]
    C --> C3[Capability Based Access Control]

    D --> D1[Encryption Pattern]
    D --> D2[Digital Signature Pattern]
    D --> D3[Secure Logger]

    E --> E1[Secure Channel TLS]
    E --> E2[Trusted Proxy]

    F --> F1[Input Validator]
    F --> F2[Output Encoder]

    G --> G1[Session Management]
    G --> G2[Session Timeout]

    H --> H1[Audit Trail]
    H --> H2[Intrusion Detection Hook]
```

## 4.2 Authentication Flow — Authenticator Pattern Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Client
    participant A as Authenticator
    participant V as Credential Store
    participant R as Resource Server

    U->>A: 1 Submit credentials via Secure Channel
    A->>V: 2 Lookup user record
    V-->>A: 3 Return salted hash and metadata
    A->>A: 4 Compute hash of provided password
    A->>A: 5 Constant-time compare hashes
    A->>A: 6 Generate session token with 64 bit entropy
    A-->>U: 7 Return signed session token via HttpOnly cookie
    U->>R: 8 Request protected resource with token
    R->>A: 9 Validate token signature and expiry
    A-->>R: 10 Token valid, return identity claims
    R-->>U: 11 Serve authorized resource
```

## 4.3 Secure Channel Pattern — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Untrusted_Network [Untrusted Network]
        E[Eavesdropper]
        M[Man in the Middle]
    end

    subgraph Trusted_Zone [Trusted Server Zone]
        C[Client Process]
        P[Trusted Proxy Reverse Gateway]
        TS[TLS Termination and Validation]
        APP[Application Service]
        LOG[Secure Logger Audit Trail]
    end

    C -->|HTTPS TLS 1.3| P
    P --> TS
    TS -->|mTLS certificate check| TS
    TS --> APP
    APP --> LOG
    E -.blocks.-> C
    M -.blocks.-> P
```

## 4.4 Authorization Layer — RBAC Component Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    REQ[Incoming Request with Session Token] --> ID[Identity Resolver]
    ID --> ROLE[Role Lookup from Policy DB]
    ROLE --> POLICY[Policy Decision Point PDP]
    POLICY --> CHECK{Permission Granted?}
    CHECK -->|Yes| EXEC[Execute Action]
    CHECK -->|No| DENY[Return 403 Forbidden]
    EXEC --> AUDIT[Append to Audit Trail]
    DENY --> AUDIT
```

## 4.5 Secure Logger Pattern — Tamper-Evidence Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    EVT[Security Event] --> NORM[Normalize and Timestamp UTC]
    NORM --> HMAC[Compute HMAC-SHA256 of Record]
    HMAC --> STORE[Append to Write-Once Storage]
    STORE --> MON[Continuous Integrity Monitor]
    MON -->|Mismatch Detected| ALERT[Raise Intrusion Alert]
    MON -->|Match| OK[Continue Logging]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define a Security Pattern. List any four mandatory structural elements used to document it.**

**Model Answer (Expected Length: ~80 words):**
A Security Pattern is a reusable, proven architectural solution template that addresses a recurring security problem within a specific context. The four mandatory structural elements as per the **Schumacher schema** are:
1. **Context** — the recurring situation in which the pattern applies
2. **Problem** — the security threat to be mitigated
3. **Solution** — the static and dynamic structure that resolves the problem
4. **Consequences** — the trade-offs, side effects, and residual risks

**[3 Marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for the four elements]**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between the Authenticator Pattern and the Single Sign-On (SSO) Pattern.**

**Model Answer (Expected Length: ~80 words):**

| Parameter | Authenticator Pattern | Single Sign-On Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Validates credentials for **one** system | Validates credentials **once** for **multiple** systems |
| **Token Lifetime** | Single session token | Federated token (SAML/OAuth/JWT) |
| **Use Case** | Standalone application login | Enterprise multi-app environment |
| **Example Tech** | Local login form | Kerberos, OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect |

**[3 Marks: 1.5 marks differentiation + 1.5 marks examples]**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **Secure Channel Pattern** in detail. Discuss its **Context**, **Problem**, **Solution**, and **Consequences** as per the Schumacher security pattern schema. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A banking application uses **6-digit numeric OTPs** sent via SMS for transaction authorization. Calculate the **entropy**, the **total keyspace size**, and the **maximum possible brute-force time** assuming an attacker can guess **10 OTPs per second**. Comment on whether this is **secure enough** for high-value transactions. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Secure Channel Pattern Explanation:**

- **Context:** Two software components (e.g., a browser and a web server) need to exchange sensitive data over an untrusted network such as the public internet.
- **Problem:** Data transmitted in plaintext is vulnerable to **eavesdropping**, **tampering**, and **man-in-the-middle (MitM) attacks** by adversaries who can intercept network packets.
- **Solution:** Establish a cryptographically protected channel between the two endpoints using the **Transport Layer Security (TLS 1.3)** protocol. The solution includes:
  1. **Handshake phase** — negotiate cipher suite, exchange certificates, perform **Diffie-Hellman key exchange**.
  2. **Bulk encryption phase** — all application data is encrypted using **AES-256-GCM**.
  3. **Integrity phase** — each record carries a **Message Authentication Code (MAC)** to detect tampering.
  4. **Termination** — session keys are destroyed after disconnection; **Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)** is enforced.
- **Consequences:**
  - ✅ **Pros:** Strong confidentiality, integrity, and server (optionally client) authentication.
  - ❌ **Cons:** Certificate management overhead, ~10–15% latency increase, single point of failure at the TLS termination proxy.

**Block Diagram (as covered in SECTION 4.3 above).** **[7 Marks: 1.5 × 4 schema elements + 1 mark diagram + 0.5 consequence trade-off]**

---

**Part (b) — OTP Entropy & Brute-Force Calculation:**

**Step 1:** Identify parameters.
- OTP length: $L = 6$ digits
- Character pool: $R = 10$ (digits 0–9)
- Attacker rate: $H = 10$ guesses/second

**Step 2:** Compute entropy.
$$E = L \cdot \log_{2}(R) = 6 \cdot \log_{2}(10)$$
$$\log_{2}(10) = 3.3219$$
$$E = 6 \cdot 3.3219 = 19.93 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3:** Compute keyspace.
$$S = 10^{6} = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ possible OTPs}$$

**Step 4:** Compute average brute-force time.
$$T = \dfrac{S}{2H} = \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000}{2 \cdot 10} = 50{,}000 \text{ seconds} \approx 13.89 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 5:** Engineering verdict.
A 6-digit OTP has only **~20 bits of entropy** and is **crackable in under 14 hours** at 10 guesses/second. This is **NOT secure** for high-value transactions.

**Recommended Mitigations (to score full marks):**
- Increase OTP length to **8 digits** (entropy $\approx 26.6$ bits).
- Add **rate limiting** (max 3 attempts, then lock account).
- Implement **cryptographic binding** to transaction amount & payee (TAN/HMAC pattern).
- Use **time-based OTP (TOTP, RFC 6238)** with a **30-second validity window**.

**[7 Marks: 2 marks entropy + 2 marks keyspace + 2 marks time + 1 mark verdict]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified — ALTERNATIVE]

**(a)** Explain the **Authorization Layer Pattern** in detail. Compare **RBAC** and **ABAC** access control models with a real-world scenario where each is preferred. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A web application uses **bcrypt with cost factor 12** to store user passwords. If a modern GPU computes **$5 \times 10^{4}$ bcrypt hashes/second**, calculate the time required to brute-force a single password from a **6-character lowercase alphabetic set**. State the **OWASP recommendation** for the minimum cost factor and justify. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Authorization Layer Pattern Explanation:**

- **Context:** A multi-user application must restrict access to functions and data based on the identity and role of the user.
- **Problem:** Scattering authorization checks throughout the application code leads to **inconsistent enforcement**, **privilege escalation vulnerabilities**, and **unmaintainable code**.
- **Solution:** Centralize all access decisions into a dedicated **Authorization Layer** (middleware) that intercepts every request and consults a **Policy Decision Point (PDP)**. The PDP evaluates the user's identity, attributes, and the requested action against a policy database, returning either **PERMIT** or **DENY**.
- **Consequences:**
  - ✅ Centralized, auditable, and consistent.
  - ❌ Adds latency; requires careful policy management.

**Comparison of RBAC vs. ABAC:**

| Parameter | RBAC (Role-Based) | ABAC (Attribute-Based) |
|---|---|---|
| **Decision basis** | User's role | User, resource, action, and environment attributes |
| **Policy complexity** | Low (simple role mapping) | High (XACML-style expressions) |
| **Scalability** | Limited (role explosion) | Excellent (fine-grained) |
| **Real-world fit** | Hospital staff access (doctor/nurse/receptionist) | Military access (clearance × location × time × data sensitivity) |
| **Standard** | NIST INCITS 359-2012 | NIST SP 800-162 |

**Preferred Scenario:** Use **RBAC** for small/medium enterprises with clear job functions (e.g., a college ERP). Use **ABAC** for large federated systems requiring dynamic policies (e.g., a multinational bank where access depends on geo-location, transaction amount, and risk score).

**[7 Marks: 2 marks pattern + 3 marks comparison + 2 marks scenario]**

---

**Part (b) — Bcrypt Brute-Force Calculation:**

**Step 1:** Identify parameters.
- Password length: $L = 6$ characters
- Character pool: $R = 26$ (lowercase a–z)
- Bcrypt cost factor: $cost = 12$
- GPU hash rate: $H = 5 \times 10^{4}$ hashes/sec

**Step 2:** Compute password entropy.
$$E = L \cdot \log_{2}(R) = 6 \cdot \log_{2}(26)$$
$$\log_{2}(26) = 4.7004$$
$$E = 6 \cdot 4.7004 = 28.20 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3:** Compute total keyspace.
$$S = 26^{6} = 308{,}915{,}776 \approx 3.09 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 4:** Compute average brute-force time (in **hash computations**).
$$T_{hash} = \dfrac{S}{2} = 1.545 \times 10^{8} \text{ hashes}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to wall-clock time using GPU rate.
$$T = \dfrac{T_{hash}}{H} = \dfrac{1.545 \times 10^{8}}{5 \times 10^{4}} = 3{,}090 \text{ seconds} \approx 51.5 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 6:** Apply the OWASP cost factor recommendation.
The **OWASP Password Storage Cheat Sheet (2024)** recommends a bcrypt cost factor of **≥ 12**, which produces $C_{eff} = 2^{12} = 4096$ internal iterations per hash.

**Step 7:** Effective attack rate.
$$H_{eff} = \dfrac{H}{2^{cost}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{4}}{4096} \approx 12.2 \text{ hashes/second}$$

**Step 8:** Re-compute brute-force time at effective rate.
$$T_{effective} = \dfrac{1.545 \times 10^{8}}{12.2} \approx 1.27 \times 10^{7} \text{ seconds} \approx 147 \text{ days}$$

**Conclusion:** With a properly configured bcrypt cost factor of 12, even a weak 6-character lowercase password takes **~5 months** to crack — pushing the attacker toward easier targets and validating the OWASP recommendation.

**[7 Marks: 1.5 entropy + 1 keyspace + 1 naive time + 1.5 cost factor + 1 effective rate + 1 final time]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "divide by 2"** in brute-force time (because average-case search is half the keyspace). *Cost: 1 mark lost per occurrence.*
> 2. **Using $L$ instead of $E$** as the exponent in $2^{L}$ — students often confuse the password length with the entropy. *Cost: 1 mark.*
> 3. **Skipping the C-P-S-C schema** when describing a pattern — KTU evaluators award **0 marks** for an answer that describes what a pattern does without following the four mandatory elements.
> 4. **Mixing up SHA-1, SHA-256, and MD5** in cryptographic pattern answers. Use **SHA-256 or stronger** unless explicitly asked otherwise.
> 5. **Not stating units** for entropy (bits) and time (seconds/hours). KTU evaluators deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 6. **Ignoring OWASP/NIST recommendations** in 14-mark numerical answers — the final "engineering verdict" line is worth **1 mark** and is the most-skipped section.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **Security Pattern** is a *reusable architectural knowledge unit* (not code), documented using the **C-P-S-C schema** (Context, Problem, Solution, Consequences).
- ✅ The **AAA Triad** (Authentication, Authorization, Auditing) is the conceptual backbone of every security pattern.
- ✅ The **Schumacher pattern catalog** (2006) is the canonical reference for ~65+ security patterns.
- ✅ **Authentication Patterns:** Authenticator, SSO, Password (with salting + bcrypt), Biometric.
- ✅ **Authorization Patterns:** Authorization Layer, RBAC (NIST INCITS 359), ABAC (NIST SP 800-162), Capability-Based.
- ✅ **Cryptographic Patterns:** Encryption (AES-256-GCM), Digital Signature, Secure Logger (HMAC-SHA256).
- ✅ **Key minimums:** AES **128-bit**, RSA **2048-bit**, ECC **256-bit**, SHA **256-bit**, password entropy **≥ 80 bits**.
- ✅ **Password entropy formula:** $E = L \cdot \log_2(R)$ — must use this **exact form** in KTU answers.
- ✅ **Brute-force average time:** $T = \dfrac{2^{E}}{2H}$ — the **/2** is mandatory; forgetting it costs a mark.
- ✅ **Bcrypt minimum cost factor:** **12** (per OWASP 2024).
- ✅ **Biometric Crossover Error Rate:** $CER = (FAR + FRR)/2$ — systems with CER < 5% are commercially acceptable.
- ✅ **TLS 1.3** is the current industry standard for the **Secure Channel Pattern**.
- ✅ **Input validation pattern** must use **whitelisting** (allow-list), not blacklisting.
- ✅ **Session tokens** must have **≥ 64 bits of entropy** and be transmitted only via **HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite cookies**.
- ✅ **Audit trails** must be **tamper-evident** (HMAC chained or write-once storage) and **timestamped in UTC**.
- ✅ Always end a 14-mark numerical answer with an **engineering verdict + NIST/OWASP recommendation** — this line is worth a full mark and is the most-skipped item.
- ✅ Use `$\vert x \vert$` in markdown tables — never the raw pipe symbol — to avoid breaking table rendering.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application Security Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT755 - CYBER THREAT INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Application Security Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Application Security (AppSec)** is the discipline of designing, building, testing, and deploying software applications with security controls, practices, and frameworks integrated throughout the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) to protect against threats, vulnerabilities, and unauthorized access across web, mobile, API, and cloud-native application surfaces.

Application Security encompasses the full spectrum of measures—**preventive, detective, and corrective**—that safeguard an application's **confidentiality, integrity, and availability (CIA Triad)** while defending the data, logic, and infrastructure it depends on. As per the KTU 2024 Cyber Threat Intelligence syllabus, AppSec is the practical application-layer manifestation of threat intelligence: it consumes Indicators of Compromise (IoCs), adversary Tactics-Techniques-Procedures (TTPs), and CVE feeds to drive defensive engineering.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Vault Building"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine constructing a high-rise bank building.
> - **Application Code** = the building's structure, walls, and rooms.
> - **Application Security** = the locks, vaults, CCTV, security guards, fire alarms, and reinforced steel embedded *during construction*, not bolted on afterward.
> - **Vulnerabilities** = unlocked windows, weak doors, exposed elevator shafts.
> - **Threat Intelligence** = intelligence reports from police saying "robbers are now targeting rooftop HVAC entries."
> - **Threat Modeling** = the architect mapping out "where can a robber enter, and how do we block it *before* pouring the concrete?"

If you install security *after* construction, you end up with ugly, expensive, bolted-on guards. **AppSec = security baked into the design.**

### 1.3 Scope of Application Security

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes application security across four primary surfaces:
> 1. **Web Applications** (browser-based, server-rendered, SPAs)
> 2. **Mobile Applications** (Android, iOS, hybrid)
> 3. **APIs & Microservices** (REST, GraphQL, gRPC)
> 4. **Cloud-Native & Containerized Apps** (Docker, Kubernetes, serverless)

### 1.4 Key Terminology Standards (KTU High-Yield)

| Term | Full Form | Significance |
|------|-----------|--------------|
| **CWE** | Common Weakness Enumeration | A community-maintained list of software weakness types (design flaws). |
| **CVE** | Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures | A unique ID for a *specific* vulnerability in a *specific* product. |
| **CVSS** | Common Vulnerability Scoring System | Standardized **0.0 – 10.0** severity score for vulnerabilities. |
| **OWASP** | Open Worldwide Application Security Project | Non-profit producing the **OWASP Top 10** of web risks. |
| **STRIDE** | Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege | Microsoft's threat-modeling mnemonic. |
| **SAST / DAST / IAST / SCA / RASP** | Static, Dynamic, Interactive, Software Composition Analysis, Runtime App Self-Protection | The five core AppSec testing tools. |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Application Security Stack — Layered Defense Model

Application security operates across **seven logical layers** (the "AppSec Stack"):

1. **Requirements & Threat Modeling Layer** — Define *what* to protect and *from whom*.
2. **Secure Design Layer** — Architecture decisions (zero-trust, least privilege, defense-in-depth).
3. **Secure Coding Layer** — Input validation, output encoding, parameterization, cryptographic hygiene.
4. **Security Testing Layer** — SAST, DAST, IAST, SCA, penetration testing.
5. **Deployment Hardening Layer** — Server, container, and cloud configuration (CIS Benchmarks).
6. **Runtime Protection Layer** — WAF, RASP, API gateways, EDR.
7. **Monitoring & Incident Response Layer** — Logs, SIEM, SOAR, threat intel consumption.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Seven Layers?** Because no single control is 100% effective. A SQL injection may bypass input validation (Layer 3) but should be stopped by a WAF (Layer 6) and detected by query-log monitoring (Layer 7). This is **Defense-in-Depth**.

### 2.2 The OWASP Top 10 (2021 Edition) — KTU Exam Favorite

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **OWASP Top 10 (2021)** is the most-cited industry baseline. Memorize these for KTU:

1. **A01 – Broken Access Control** (94% of apps tested had it)
2. **A02 – Cryptographic Failures** (formerly *Sensitive Data Exposure*)
3. **A03 – Injection** (SQLi, NoSQLi, LDAP, OS Command)
4. **A04 – Insecure Design** (new 2021 category — *flaws in design itself*)
5. **A05 – Security Misconfiguration**
6. **A06 – Vulnerable & Outdated Components**
7. **A07 – Identification & Authentication Failures**
8. **A08 – Software & Data Integrity Failures** (CI/CD pipeline attacks)
9. **A09 – Security Logging & Monitoring Failures**
10. **A10 – Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)** (promoted from #1 in community survey)

### 2.3 The Secure Software Development Life Cycle (SSDLC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SSDLC vs SDLC:** SDLC = how you build software. **SSDLC = SDLC + security gates embedded in every phase.** KTU expects you to name the security activity performed in *each* phase.

| SDLC Phase | Security Activity | Tool Example |
|------------|-------------------|--------------|
| Requirements | Security requirements, abuse cases | OWASP ASVS |
| Design | Threat modeling, secure architecture review | Microsoft Threat Modeling Tool |
| Implementation | Secure coding standards, peer review, SAST | SonarQube, Checkmarx |
| Testing | DAST, penetration testing, IAST | OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite |
| Deployment | Hardening, secrets management, signed artifacts | HashiCorp Vault, Sigstore |
| Maintenance | Patch management, vulnerability monitoring, WAF tuning | Snyk, Dependabot |

### 2.4 The CVSS Severity Scoring — High-Yield Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System) v3.1** produces a base score from **0.0 to 10.0**. KTU frequently tests this.

The **CVSS Base Score** is computed from three metric groups: **Impact, Exploitability, and Scope**.

**Step 1 — Impact Sub-Score (ISS):**

$$
\text{ISS} = 1 - \left[ (1 - C) \times (1 - I) \times (1 - A) \right]
$$

where $C$ = Confidentiality impact, $I$ = Integrity impact, $A$ = Availability impact. Each is encoded as: **None = 0, Low = 0.22, High = 0.56**.

**Step 2 — Impact Score:**

$$
\text{Impact} = \begin{cases} 6.42 \times \text{ISS} & \text{if Scope} = \text{Unchanged} \\ 7.52 \times (\text{ISS} - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (\text{ISS} - 0.02)^{15} & \text{if Scope} = \text{Changed} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Exploitability Score:**

$$
\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times A_V \times A_C \times A_I \times P_R \times U_I
$$

where $A_V$ = Attack Vector, $A_C$ = Attack Complexity, $A_I$ = Authentication, $P_R$ = Privileges Required, $U_I$ = User Interaction (each weighted 0.20 – 0.85).

**Step 4 — Final Base Score:**

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundup}\!\left[\min\!\left( \text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\ 10 \right)\right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Severity Bands:** **0.0 – 3.9 = Low, 4.0 – 6.9 = Medium, 7.0 – 8.9 = High, 9.0 – 10.0 = Critical.**

### 2.5 STRIDE Threat-Modeling Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For each application component, ask the six STRIDE questions:

| STRIDE Property | Threat Question | Defensive Control |
|-----------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **S**poofing | Can an attacker pretend to be someone else? | Authentication, mTLS, digital signatures |
| **T**ampering | Can data be modified in transit or at rest? | Integrity hashes, HMAC, signed updates |
| **R**epudiation | Can a user deny an action they took? | Tamper-evident audit logs, non-repudiation |
| **I**nformation Disclosure | Can sensitive data leak? | Encryption at rest & in transit, scrubbing |
| **D**enial of Service | Can the service be made unavailable? | Rate limiting, WAF, CDN, autoscaling |
| **E**levation of Privilege | Can a user gain higher access? | Least privilege, RBAC, sandboxing |

### 2.6 Risk Rating — The DREAD Model (complementary to STRIDE)

**Risk** in AppSec is conventionally expressed as:

$$
\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}
$$

The **DREAD** mnemonic quantifies each:

- **D**amage potential
- **R**eproducibility
- **E**xploitability
- **A**ffected users
- **D**iscoverability

$$
\text{DREAD Score} = \frac{D + R + E + A + D}{5}, \quad \text{range } 1 \text{ to } 10
$$

### 2.7 AppSec Testing Tools — Comparative Matrix

| Tool Type | When It Runs | What It Sees | Strength | Limitation |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|----------|------------|
| **SAST** (Static) | Build time, on source code | Source, bytecode, binary | Finds bugs *before* runtime; full code coverage | High false positives; no runtime context |
| **DAST** (Dynamic) | On running app, black-box | HTTP/REST responses | Real attacker view; no source needed | Limited code coverage; blind to internals |
| **IAST** (Interactive) | On running app, instrumented | Both code & traffic | Best accuracy; real runtime data | Needs deployable app; instrumentation overhead |
| **SCA** (Composition) | Build time, on dependencies | 3rd-party libs, SBOM | Catches supply-chain vulns (Log4Shell style) | Only known CVEs in dependencies |
| **RASP** (Runtime) | Inside app at runtime | In-process execution | Blocks attacks in real time | Performance overhead; vendor lock-in |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** SAST + DAST + SCA together = the *minimum* recommended modern AppSec pipeline.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Application Security is **not optional** in production systems because:

- **Compliance:** PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR, SOC 2, ISO 27001 all mandate application-layer controls.
- **Cost Economics:** NIST data shows fixing a bug in **design** costs **$50**; in **implementation** **$500**; in **testing** **$5,000**; in **production** **$30,000+**. This is the **1:10:100:1000 rule**.
- **Breach Economics:** Average cost of an application-layer breach (per IBM 2023 report) ≈ **$4.45 million USD**.
- **CTI Feedback Loop:** Modern threat intel feeds (e.g., MISP, STIX/TAXII) push emerging CVEs directly into SCA tools, closing the loop between attacker activity and developer code.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Computing a CVSS v3.1 Base Score (Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A web application has a SQL injection vulnerability. The metrics from a scanner are:
> - Attack Vector (AV) = **Network**
> - Attack Complexity (AC) = **Low**
> - Privileges Required (PR) = **None**
> - User Interaction (UI) = **None**
> - Confidentiality (C) = **High**
> - Integrity (I) = **High**
> - Availability (A) = **High**
> - Scope (S) = **Changed**

#### Step 1 — Look up the encoded metric values

The official CVSS v3.1 specification assigns these numerical weights:

| Metric | Value | Encoded |
|--------|-------|---------|
| AV: Network | — | **0.85** |
| AC: Low | — | **0.77** |
| PR: None | — | **0.85** |
| UI: None | — | **0.85** |
| C: High | — | **0.56** |
| I: High | — | **0.56** |
| A: High | — | **0.56** |
| S: Changed | — | (triggers alternate Impact formula) |

#### Step 2 — Compute the Impact Sub-Score (ISS)

Plug the three impact metrics (C, I, A) into the ISS formula:

$$
\text{ISS} = 1 - \left[ (1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56) \right]
$$

$$
\text{ISS} = 1 - \left[ 0.44 \times 0.44 \times 0.44 \right]
$$

$$
\text{ISS} = 1 - \left[ 0.085184 \right]
$$

$$
\text{ISS} = 0.914816
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute the Impact Score (Scope = Changed)

Since Scope is *Changed*, use the second branch of the Impact formula:

$$
\text{Impact} = 7.52 \times (\text{ISS} - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (\text{ISS} - 0.02)^{15}
$$

$$
\text{Impact} = 7.52 \times (0.914816 - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (0.914816 - 0.02)^{15}
$$

$$
\text{Impact} = 7.52 \times 0.885816 - 3.25 \times (0.894816)^{15}
$$

$$
\text{Impact} = 6.661336 - 3.25 \times (0.894816)^{15}
$$

We must compute $(0.894816)^{15}$. Evaluating this:

$$
(0.894816)^{15} \approx 0.181656
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{Impact} = 6.661336 - (3.25 \times 0.181656)
$$

$$
\text{Impact} = 6.661336 - 0.590382
$$

$$
\text{Impact} \approx 6.070954
$$

#### Step 4 — Compute the Exploitability Score

Plug the four exploit metrics (AV, AC, PR, UI) into the Exploitability formula:

$$
\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85
$$

$$
\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times 0.85 = 6.987
$$

$$
\text{Exploitability} = 6.987 \times 0.77 = 5.3800
$$

$$
\text{Exploitability} = 5.3800 \times 0.85 = 4.5730
$$

$$
\text{Exploitability} = 4.5730 \times 0.85 = 3.8871
$$

#### Step 5 — Compute the Base Score

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundup}\!\left[\min(6.070954 + 3.8871,\ 10)\right]
$$

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundup}\!\left[\min(9.958054,\ 10)\right]
$$

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundup}(9.958054) = 9.958054 \rightarrow \textbf{9.9}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final CVSS v3.1 Base Score = 9.9 / 10.0 → Severity = CRITICAL.**
> This matches the *real* CVSS score published for many production SQL injection CVEs (e.g., CVE-2019-9193 in PostgreSQL).

### 3.2 Worked Example: DREAD Risk Score for a Threat

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A threat analyst rates an "Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution in a public-facing API" using DREAD, each on a 1–10 scale:
> - Damage potential = **10** (full server takeover)
> - Reproducibility = **9** (PoC widely available)
> - Exploitability = **9** (curl one-liner)
> - Affected users = **10** (all users on the platform)
> - Discoverability = **8** (visible in Shodan)

$$
\text{DREAD} = \frac{10 + 9 + 9 + 10 + 8}{5} = \frac{46}{5} = 9.2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **9.2 = High Risk** → immediate remediation, P0 ticket.

### 3.3 The Cost-of-Fixing Multiplier (NIST Data)

The 1:10:100:1000 heuristic is a *relative* cost model, not an absolute one. The closed-form is:

$$
C_{\text{phase}} = C_{\text{design}} \times 10^{(n-1)}
$$

where $n$ is the SDLC phase index (1 = design, 2 = implementation, 3 = testing, 4 = production).

$$
C_{\text{design}} = 50\ \text{USD}
$$

$$
C_{\text{implementation}} = 50 \times 10^{1} = 500\ \text{USD}
$$

$$
C_{\text{testing}} = 50 \times 10^{2} = 5{,}000\ \text{USD}
$$

$$
C_{\text{production}} = 50 \times 10^{3} = 50{,}000\ \text{USD}
$$

This is the quantitative justification for the **"shift-left"** paradigm in modern AppSec.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Attacks to Layers

| Attack | OWASP 2021 | STRIDE | SDLC Phase to Fix | Primary Tool |
|--------|-----------|--------|-------------------|--------------|
| SQL Injection | A03 | Tampering | Implementation | SAST + WAF |
| XSS (Stored) | A03 | Tampering | Implementation | SAST + CSP header |
| Hard-coded password | A07 | Spoofing | Implementation | Secrets scanner |
| Outdated Log4j | A06 | Tampering | Maintenance | SCA |
| SSRF to internal AWS metadata | A10 | Information Disclosure | Design | DAST + egress firewall |
| Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) | A01 | Tampering | Design | Manual code review |
| Missing rate limit | A04 | Denial of Service | Design | API gateway |
| CI/CD pipeline tampering | A08 | Tampering | Deployment | Sigstore, signed commits |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Secure SDLC (SSDLC) Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    reqNode[Requirements Gathering] --> thrModNode[Threat Modeling STRIDE]
    thrModNode --> archNode[Secure Architecture Design]
    archNode --> codeNode[Secure Coding & Peer Review]
    codeNode --> sastNode[SAST Scan]
    sastNode --> scaNode[SCA Dependency Check]
    scaNode --> buildNode[Build & Sign Artifacts]
    buildNode --> dastNode[DAST Scan on Staging]
    dastNode --> deployNode[Hardened Deployment]
    deployNode --> raspNode[Runtime Protection RASP and WAF]
    raspNode --> monNode[Logging SIEM Threat Intel]
    monNode --> patchNode[Patch Management]
    patchNode --> monNode

    sastNode -. fail .-> codeNode
    dastNode -. fail .-> codeNode
    scaNode -. fail .-> codeNode
```

### 4.2 STRIDE Threat-Modeling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode[Identify Application Components] --> drawDfdNode[Draw Data Flow Diagram DFD]

    applyStrideNode --> sNode{Spoofing Risk?}

    applyStrideNode --> rNode{Repudiation Risk?}
    applyStrideNode --> iNode{Info Disclosure Risk?}
    applyStrideNode --> dNode{DoS Risk?}

    sNode --> mitNode[Document Mitigations]

    dNode --> mitNode
    eNode --> mitNode

    mitNode --> prioNode[Prioritize by DREAD or CVSS]
    prioNode --> trackNode[Add to Backlog as Security User Stories]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    wafNode --> apiGwNode
    apiGwNode --> authSvcNode
    authSvcNode --> appSvcNode

    appSvcNode --> vaultNode
    dbNode --> kmsNode

    ctiNode --> siemNode
    scaNode --> appSvcNode
    scaNode --> siemNode
```

flowchart LR
