## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Define Application Security. List any four major application security risks as per the OWASP Top 10 (2021).*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Application Security (AppSec)** is the practice of integrating security controls, tools, and processes into every phase of the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) to protect applications from vulnerabilities, threats, and unauthorized access. It covers preventive, detective, and corrective measures across web, mobile, API, and cloud-native surfaces.
>
> **Four OWASP Top 10 (2021) risks:**
> 1. **A01 — Broken Access Control** (e.g., IDOR, missing authorization checks)
> 2. **A02 — Cryptographic Failures** (e.g., storing passwords in plaintext, weak TLS)
> 3. **A03 — Injection** (e.g., SQL injection, OS command injection, XSS)
> 4. **A05 — Security Misconfiguration** (e.g., default credentials, open S3 buckets)
>
> *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four risks.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Differentiate between SAST and DAST. Mention one tool for each.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Parameter | SAST (Static Application Security Testing) | DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing) |
> |-----------|--------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------|
> | **When** | At build time, on source code | On a running application, black-box |
> | **View** | Inside-out (white-box) | Outside-in (black-box) |
> | **Finds** | Code-level bugs, taint flows | Runtime vulns, misconfigs, authz issues |
> | **Needs source** | Yes | No |
> | **Example tool** | **SonarQube / Checkmarx** | **OWASP ZAP / Burp Suite** |
>
> *(1.5 marks for differences, 1.5 marks for the table & tool examples.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT** list OWASP Top 10 items without a *one-line* explanation — the examiner deducts 0.5 marks per unexplained item.
> 2. **Do NOT** confuse SAST and DAST — examiners frequently set "compare and contrast" and award zero if you write a generic paragraph instead of a *table*.
> 3. **Do NOT** write CVSS formulas from memory incorrectly; the official First.org encoding values (0.85, 0.77, 0.56, 0.22) must be **exact**.
> 4. **Always** show units/ranges in formulas. Writing $\text{Risk} = T \times V \times I$ without defining each variable costs you a mark.
> 5. In STRIDE questions, the examiner expects **all six letters** named, a *threat question*, and a *control* per letter.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **Secure Software Development Life Cycle (SSDLC)**. For *each* phase, state the security activity performed and name one supporting tool. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **STRIDE** threat-modeling framework. For each of the six properties, give one example threat and one defensive control. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) SSDLC — 7 marks**
>
> The Secure SDLC embeds security gates into the standard SDLC so vulnerabilities are caught at the earliest, cheapest stage. The phases and their security activities are:
>
> | # | SDLC Phase | Security Activity | Supporting Tool |
> |---|------------|-------------------|-----------------|
> | 1 | **Requirements** | Elicit security requirements (the "what"); write abuse/misuse cases; align with OWASP ASVS. | OWASP ASVS, ISO 27001 A.14 |
> | 2 | **Design** | Threat modeling using STRIDE; secure architecture review; choose authn/authz patterns. | Microsoft Threat Modeling Tool, OWASP SAMM |
> | 3 | **Implementation** | Secure coding standards (CERT, OWASP SCP); peer review; pair programming; secrets scanning. | SonarQube, GitLeaks, Checkmarx (SAST) |
> | 4 | **Testing** | Functional security tests; DAST on staging; IAST; fuzzing; penetration testing. | OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite, Contrast IAST |
> | 5 | **Deployment** | Hardening (CIS Benchmarks); signed artifacts; secrets via Vault; mTLS. | HashiCorp Vault, Sigstore, Cosign |
> | 6 | **Maintenance** | Patch management; SCA on dependencies; continuous monitoring; CTI feed ingestion. | Snyk, Dependabot, MISP |
>
> *Awarding guide: 6 phases × 1 mark each = 6 marks + 1 mark for a concluding note on shift-left / cost multiplier.*
>
> **(b) STRIDE — 7 marks**
>
> STRIDE is Microsoft's mnemonic for systematically identifying threats in a system. For each property, the model asks "can an attacker achieve X?" and prescribes mitigations.
>
> | Property | Example Threat | Defensive Control |
> |----------|----------------|-------------------|
> | **S — Spoofing** | Attacker steals session cookie and impersonates a logged-in user. | Strong authentication (MFA, OAuth2 with PKCE); HttpOnly/Secure cookies; mutual TLS. |
> | **T — Tampering** | Attacker modifies price field in a POST request before checkout. | Input validation; server-side authorization checks; signed requests; HMAC. |
> | **R — Repudiation** | User denies initiating a fund transfer; no logs exist. | Tamper-evident audit logs (write-once); non-repudiation via digital signatures. |
> | **I — Information Disclosure** | Verbose error message leaks DB schema to the attacker. | Generic error pages; disable stack traces in production; encryption at rest & in transit. |
> | **D — Denial of Service** | Attacker floods login endpoint with credential-stuffing requests. | Rate limiting, CAPTCHA, CDN, WAF, autoscaling, CAPTCHA, account lockout. |
> | **E — Elevation of Privilege** | Regular user modifies `/admin/users` URL and gains admin panel. | Role-Based Access Control (RBAC); least privilege; server-side authorization on every endpoint. |
>
> *Awarding guide: 6 rows × 1 mark each = 6 marks + 1 mark for the concluding remark on STRIDE being a *per-component* exercise.*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** What is **CVSS v3.1**? Compute the **Base Score** for a vulnerability with the following metrics, showing every step:

$$
\text{AV}=\text{Network},\ \text{AC}=\text{Low},\ \text{PR}=\text{None},\ \text{UI}=\text{None},\ \text{C}=\text{High},\ \text{I}=\text{High},\ \text{A}=\text{None},\ \text{S}=\text{Unchanged}
$$

State the severity band of the computed score. **(8 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Defense-in-Depth** principle as applied to Application Security. How does a **Web Application Firewall (WAF)** complement **RASP**? **(6 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) CVSS Computation — 8 marks**
>
> **CVSS v3.1** is the **Common Vulnerability Scoring System**, an open standard (maintained by **FIRST.org**) that produces a numerical severity score from **0.0 to 10.0** for a software vulnerability, derived from Impact, Exploitability, and Scope metrics.
>
> **Encoded metric values from the FIRST specification:**
>
> | Metric | Value | Encoded |
> |--------|-------|---------|
> | AV: Network | — | **0.85** |
> | AC: Low | — | **0.77** |
> | PR: None | — | **0.85** |
> | UI: None | — | **0.85** |
> | C: High | — | **0.56** |
> | I: High | — | **0.56** |
> | A: None | — | **0.00** |
> | S: Unchanged | — | (triggers the *first* Impact branch) |
>
> **Step 1 — Impact Sub-Score (ISS):**
>
> $$
> \text{ISS} = 1 - [(1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.00)]
> $$
>
> $$
> \text{ISS} = 1 - [0.44 \times 0.44 \times 1.00] = 1 - 0.1936 = 0.8064
> $$
>
> **Step 2 — Impact (Scope = Unchanged):**
>
> $$
> \text{Impact} = 6.42 \times \text{ISS} = 6.42 \times 0.8064 = 5.177088
> $$
>
> **Step 3 — Exploitability:**
>
> $$
> \text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85
> $$
>
> $$
> = 8.22 \times 0.85 = 6.987
> $$
>
> $$
> = 6.987 \times 0.77 = 5.3800
> $$
>
> $$
> = 5.3800 \times 0.85 = 4.5730
> $$
>
> $$
> = 4.5730 \times 0.85 = 3.8871
> $$
>
> **Step 4 — Base Score:**
>
> $$
> \text{BaseScore} = \text{roundup}\!\left[\min(5.177088 + 3.8871,\ 10)\right] = \text{roundup}(9.064188) = 9.1
> $$
>
> **Severity Band = HIGH** (range 7.0 – 8.9 nominally, but 9.1 lies in the **HIGH–CRITICAL boundary**; using the official FIRST thresholds, 9.1 is **CRITICAL** since 9.0–10.0 = Critical).
>
> *(Awarding: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for encoded values, 6 marks for the four computation steps.)*
>
> **(b) Defense-in-Depth + WAF vs RASP — 6 marks**
>
> **Defense-in-Depth** is the principle that *no single security control is sufficient*. Multiple overlapping layers are deployed so that if one layer fails, the next layer still mitigates the threat. In AppSec, this means combining **perimeter** (WAF, CDN), **application** (RASP, secure code), **data** (encryption, Vault), and **operational** (SIEM, CTI) controls.
>
> **WAF vs RASP — comparison:**
>
> | Aspect | **WAF** (Web Application Firewall) | **RASP** (Runtime Application Self-Protection) |
> |--------|-----------------------------------|------------------------------------------------|
> | **Position** | Perimeter, in front of the app | *Inside* the running application process |
> | **View** | Sees only HTTP traffic | Sees code execution, internal calls, SQL queries |
> | **Strength** | Blocks volumetric / known-pattern attacks before they reach the app | Blocks logic-level attacks (deserialization, SQLi in ORM) with full context |
> | **Limitation** | Blind to encrypted payloads & logic flaws | Performance overhead; instrumentation required |
> | **Complementary?** | **Yes** — WAF filters the noise; RASP catches what slips through. |
>
> *(Awarding: 2 marks for DiD principle, 2 marks for WAF vs RASP table, 2 marks for the concluding "complementarity" sentence.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Definition:** Application Security = security integrated across the *entire* SDLC, not bolted on at the end.
- **Key Standards Bodies:** **OWASP** (Top 10, ASVS, SAMM), **NIST** (SP 800-53, SSDF SP 800-218), **FIRST** (CVSS), **MITRE** (CWE, ATT&CK, CAPEC).
- **OWASP Top 10 (2021) — Memorize the order:** A01 Broken Access Control → A02 Crypto Failures → A03 Injection → A04 Insecure Design → A05 Misconfig → A06 Vulnerable Components → A07 Auth Failures → A08 Integrity Failures → A09 Logging Failures → A10 SSRF.
- **CVSS v3.1 range = 0.0 – 10.0.** Severity bands: Low (0.1–3.9), Medium (4.0–6.9), High (7.0–8.9), Critical (9.0–10.0).
- **Key CVSS encoded values (memorize!):** Network = **0.85**, Low AC = **0.77**, None PR = **0.85**, None UI = **0.85**, High impact = **0.56**, Low impact = **0.22**, None impact = **0.00**.
- **CVSS Impact formulas:** $\text{ISS} = 1 - [(1-C)(1-I)(1-A)]$; then $6.42 \times \text{ISS}$ (Unchanged) or the long polynomial (Changed).
- **STRIDE = 6 properties**; one threat question + one control per property. (S, T, R, I, D, E).
- **DREAD = 5 factors** averaged from 1–10; thresholds: 1–3 Low, 4–7 Medium, 8–10 High.
- **Five Core AppSec Tools:** SAST (code), DAST (running app), IAST (instrumented), SCA (dependencies), RASP (in-process).
- **SSDLC = 6 Phases:** Requirements → Design → Implementation → Testing → Deployment → Maintenance — each with a security gate.
- **Defense-in-Depth:** WAF + RASP + SAST + DAST + SCA + SIEM + Vault + KMS — *all together*.
- **Shift-Left Economics:** Cost multiplier 1× (design) : 10× (impl) : 100× (test) : 1000× (prod).
- **CTI ↔ AppSec Loop:** Threat intel feeds CVE/SCA tools → SCA flags vulnerable library → developer patches → RASP blocks exploit attempt → SIEM logs attempt → feeds back to CTI.
- **Compliance Hooks:** PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR, SOC 2, ISO 27001 — all mandate application-layer security testing.
- **Modern Frameworks:** OWASP SAMM (maturity model), OWASP ASVS (verification standard), NIST SSDF (SP 800-218), BSIMM (industry benchmark).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application Security Standards and Regulations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT755 - CYBER THREAT INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Application Security Standards and Regulations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Application Security Standards and Regulations** refer to a structured set of codified technical specifications, industry best practices, legal mandates, and compliance frameworks designed to govern the confidentiality, integrity, and availability (CIA Triad) of software applications throughout their Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC). These standards provide measurable security baselines that organizations must implement, audit, and continuously validate to protect applications from cyber threats.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Application Security Standards are formalized frameworks (technical, regulatory, or contractual) that prescribe security controls, testing methodologies, secure coding guidelines, and audit requirements to mitigate vulnerabilities in web, mobile, and cloud-native applications.

## 1.2 Core Categorization of Standards

Standards and regulations in application security can be classified into **three primary domains**:

| Domain Type | Purpose | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Voluntary Technical Standards** | Provide implementation-level best practices | OWASP ASVS, OWASP Top 10, NIST SSDF |
| **Industry Compliance Standards** | Mandate security controls for specific sectors | PCI DSS, HIPAA, SOC 2 |
| **Legal/Government Regulations** | Enforce data protection by law | GDPR, IT Act 2000 (India), CCPA |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Building Code"**
> Imagine constructing a high-rise building. Application Security Standards are analogous to the **National Building Code (NBC)**:
> - **OWASP** is like the architect's blueprint (best design practices).
> - **PCI DSS** is like fire-safety regulations for a specific type of building (a mall where credit cards are processed).
> - **GDPR** is like the government law that protects residents' privacy (tenants' data).
> - **ISO 27001** is the international certification that proves the building was constructed using globally accepted safety norms.
>
> Just as a building inspector checks compliance before allowing occupancy, a security auditor checks application compliance before granting certifications or allowing production deployment.

## 1.4 The CIA Triad as the Underpinning Principle

All application security standards fundamentally aim to uphold three pillars, collectively known as the **CIA Triad**:

- **Confidentiality**: Ensuring that sensitive application data (credentials, PII) is accessible only to authorized entities.
- **Integrity**: Guaranteeing that application data and code are not altered maliciously or accidentally.
- **Availability**: Ensuring that applications remain operational and accessible to legitimate users when required.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters in CTI:** A Cyber Threat Intelligence analyst uses these standards as a *benchmark* to measure the defensive posture of an organization's applications against known adversary Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures (TTPs) catalogued in frameworks like MITRE ATT&CK.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Major Application Security Standards — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 OWASP (Open Web Application Security Project)

The **OWASP Foundation** is a non-profit entity that produces freely available articles, methodologies, documentation, tools, and technologies in the field of application security. It is universally regarded as the *de facto* baseline for web application security.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key OWASP Deliverables (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - **OWASP Top 10**: A standard awareness document representing the most critical web application security risks. Updated periodically (2021 is the latest stable version).
> - **OWASP ASVS (Application Security Verification Standard)**: A framework for testing web application technical security controls.
> - **OWASP SAMM (Software Assurance Maturity Model)**: A maturity model that helps organizations assess and improve their software security posture.
> - **OWASP Testing Guide**: A comprehensive methodology for performing application security testing.

### 2.1.2 NIST Cybersecurity Framework & SP 800 Series

The **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** issues Special Publications (SP) that serve as foundational guidance for application security:

- **NIST SP 800-53**: Catalog of security and privacy controls for information systems and organizations.
- **NIST SP 800-63B**: Digital identity guidelines (authentication and password policies).
- **NIST SSDF (SP 800-218)**: Secure Software Development Framework, which defines secure development practices across the SDLC.

### 2.1.3 ISO/IEC 27000 Series

The **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** publishes globally recognized standards. For application security:

- **ISO/IEC 27001**: Specifies requirements for establishing, implementing, and maintaining an Information Security Management System (ISMS).
- **ISO/IEC 27002**: Provides practical guidance on information security controls.
- **ISO/IEC 27034**: Specifically addresses application security, providing a framework for managing security throughout the application lifecycle.
- **ISO/IEC 27017**: Cloud-specific information security controls.
- **ISO/IEC 27018**: Protection of Personally Identifiable Information (PII) in public clouds.

### 2.1.4 PCI DSS (Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard)

**PCI DSS** is a proprietary information security standard administered by the **PCI Security Standards Council** to protect cardholder data. The current version is **PCI DSS v4.0** (released March 2022, mandatory from March 2025).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 6 Core Goals of PCI DSS:**
> 1. Build and Maintain a Secure Network and Systems
> 2. Protect Account Data
> 3. Maintain a Vulnerability Management Program
> 4. Implement Strong Access Control Measures
> 5. Regularly Monitor and Test Networks
> 6. Maintain an Information Security Policy

### 2.1.5 GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation)

**GDPR** is a regulation in European Union (EU) law on data protection and privacy, effective from **25 May 2018**. It applies to all organizations processing the personal data of EU residents, regardless of the organization's location.

Key principles:
- **Lawfulness, Fairness, and Transparency**
- **Purpose Limitation**
- **Data Minimization**
- **Accuracy**
- **Storage Limitation**
- **Integrity and Confidentiality**
- **Accountability**

### 2.1.6 HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act)

A US federal law (1996) that mandates the protection of **Protected Health Information (PHI)**. It comprises:
- **Privacy Rule**
- **Security Rule**
- **Breach Notification Rule**
- **Enforcement Rule**

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet

| Standard | Type | Scope | Origin | Key Focus |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **OWASP Top 10** | Technical | Web Apps | Open Community | Top 10 critical risks |
| **PCI DSS** | Compliance | Card Data | Industry (PCI SSC) | Payment security |
| **HIPAA** | Legal/Regulatory | Healthcare PHI | US Government | Health data privacy |
| **GDPR** | Legal/Regulatory | Personal Data | EU | Data protection rights |
| **ISO 27001** | Certification | Information Security | International | ISMS certification |
| **NIST SSDF** | Technical | Software Dev | US Government | Secure SDLC |
| **SOC 2** | Audit | Service Providers | AICPA | Trust service criteria |

## 2.3 The OWASP Top 10 (2021 Edition) — KTU Mandatory Memorization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 10 Critical Web Application Security Risks (OWASP 2021):**
> 1. **A01: Broken Access Control**
> 2. **A02: Cryptographic Failures**
> 3. **A03: Injection** (SQL, NoSQL, LDAP, OS Command)
> 4. **A04: Insecure Design**
> 5. **A05: Security Misconfiguration**
> 6. **A06: Vulnerable and Outdated Components**
> 7. **A07: Identification and Authentication Failures**
> 8. **A08: Software and Data Integrity Failures**
> 9. **A09: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures**
> 10. **A10: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **For Developers:** Standards like OWASP provide actionable secure coding checklists.
- **For Security Analysts:** Frameworks like NIST SSDF and ISO 27034 provide structured methodologies for threat modelling and risk assessment.
- **For Auditors/Compliance Officers:** Standards like PCI DSS, HIPAA, and SOC 2 define measurable audit criteria.
- **For CTI Professionals:** Mapping application vulnerabilities to standards (e.g., CVE → OWASP Top 10 → MITRE ATT&CK) allows contextualized threat intelligence reporting.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Mapping Matrix

## 3.1 How Standards Map to the SDLC

Application security standards are not applied at a single point in time. They are integrated across the entire **Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)**. Below is a step-by-step analytical breakdown of how multiple standards converge at each phase.

### Step 1: Requirements & Planning Phase

During this initial phase, the organization determines regulatory applicability.

- **GDPR / HIPAA / PCI DSS applicability check** is performed.
- Data classification is conducted to identify which datasets require which level of protection.
- **NIST SP 800-218 (SSDF)** is referenced to define security requirements upfront.

### Step 2: Design Phase

The architectural blueprint is analyzed for security flaws.

- **Threat Modeling** is conducted (often using STRIDE or PASTA frameworks).
- **OWASP SAMM** is used to evaluate the maturity of security practices.
- **ISO/IEC 27034** controls are mapped to design components.

### Step 3: Implementation (Coding) Phase

Developers write the application code.

- **OWASP Secure Coding Practices** checklist is enforced.
- **NIST SSDF** secure coding practices (PO.5, PS.1, PS.2) are followed.
- **Static Application Security Testing (SAST)** tools (e.g., SonarQube, Checkmarx) are used to identify vulnerabilities against standards like OWASP Top 10 and CWE (Common Weakness Enumeration).

### Step 4: Testing Phase

The application undergoes rigorous security validation.

- **Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST)** is performed (e.g., OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite).
- **OWASP Testing Guide** is followed as the standard methodology.
- **OWASP ASVS Level 1, 2, or 3** verification is performed depending on application criticality.

### Step 5: Deployment & Operations Phase

The application is released and monitored.

- **Web Application Firewalls (WAF)** are deployed (PCI DSS Requirement 6.4.1).
- **Continuous monitoring** is performed as required by ISO 27001, SOC 2, and NIST.
- **Patch management** is enforced to address vulnerabilities in components (OWASP A06).

### Step 6: Incident Response & Forensics

When a breach occurs, standards provide a response framework.

- **HIPAA Breach Notification Rule** mandates notification timelines (60 days).
- **GDPR Article 33** requires notification to the supervisory authority within **72 hours**.
- **PCI DSS Requirement 12.10** mandates an incident response plan.

## 3.2 Mathematical Model: Risk-Based Compliance Scoring

A common framework used in standards such as **NIST SP 800-30** and **ISO 27005** for risk assessment is:

$$
R = f(T, V, I)
$$

Where:
- $R$ = Risk Score
- $T$ = Threat Likelihood (probability of attack)
- $V$ = Vulnerability Severity (often measured via CVSS)
- $I$ = Impact (business consequence)

The **Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS)** provides a numerical score reflecting severity, with the base score computed as:

$$
\text{CVSS Base Score} = \text{RoundUp}(\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability}, 10))
$$

Where **Impact** and **Exploitability** are sub-scores derived from metrics like Attack Vector, Attack Complexity, Privileges Required, User Interaction, Scope, Confidentiality Impact, Integrity Impact, and Availability Impact.

## 3.3 CTI Mapping Matrix: Standards → Threats → Defenses

| OWASP Top 10 (2021) | Common CVE Example | MITRE ATT&CK Technique | Primary Standard Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| A01: Broken Access Control | CVE-2021-3156 | T1078 (Valid Accounts) | ISO 27001 A.9, PCI DSS 7 |
| A02: Cryptographic Failures | CVE-2014-3566 (POODLE) | T1573 (Encrypted Channel) | PCI DSS 3, HIPAA Security Rule |
| A03: Injection | CVE-2019-19781 | T1059 (Command & Scripting) | OWASP ASVS V5, NIST SSDF PS.1 |
| A05: Security Misconfig | CVE-2017-9805 | T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing App) | CIS Benchmarks, PCI DSS 2.2 |
| A07: Auth Failures | CVE-2020-1472 (Zerologon) | T1110 (Brute Force) | NIST 800-63B, PCI DSS 8 |

## 3.4 Comparison: PCI DSS v3.2.1 vs v4.0

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** KTU frequently tests knowledge of regulatory updates. The transition from PCI DSS v3.2.1 to v4.0 introduced:
> - **Customized Approach**: Allows entities to implement controls via alternative methods that meet the defined objective.
> - **Enhanced Authentication**: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) required for all access into the CDE (Cardholder Data Environment), not just administrators.
> - **Targeted Risk Analysis**: A formal risk analysis must be performed for each requirement that allows it.
> - **Automated Log Review Mechanisms** are now mandated.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Standards Ecosystem

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]
**"List and briefly explain the three core categories of application security standards with one example for each."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The three core categories of application security standards are:

1. **Voluntary Technical Standards** (1 mark): These are community-driven best practices and technical specifications. They are not legally mandated but are widely adopted.
   - *Example:* **OWASP Top 10** — A standard awareness document listing the ten most critical web application security risks.

2. **Industry Compliance Standards** (1 mark): These are sector-specific mandatory standards enforced by industry bodies or contracts. Non-compliance results in penalties or loss of business.
   - *Example:* **PCI DSS** — Mandates security controls for entities that process, store, or transmit cardholder data.

3. **Legal/Government Regulations** (1 mark): These are statutory laws enacted by governments to protect citizens' data and impose legal liability for non-compliance.
   - *Example:* **GDPR** — EU regulation enforcing data protection and privacy for EU residents, with fines up to 4% of annual global turnover.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]
**"What is OWASP? Explain the significance of the OWASP Top 10 in cyber threat intelligence."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**OWASP (Open Web Application Security Project)** (1 mark) is a non-profit foundation that works to improve software security. It produces freely available articles, methodologies, documentation, and tools such as the OWASP Top 10, OWASP ASVS, and OWASP Testing Guide.

**Significance in CTI** (2 marks):
- It provides a **common taxonomy** for classifying web application vulnerabilities, enabling consistent reporting across organizations.
- CTI analysts use the OWASP Top 10 to **prioritize threat-hunting efforts** by focusing on the most prevalent and impactful risk categories.
- It allows **mapping of CVEs and adversary TTPs to a standardized risk framework**, making intelligence actionable for defensive teams.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A: [14 Marks]

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the OWASP Top 10 (2021 edition) in detail. Discuss how 'Broken Access Control' and 'Injection' attacks are mitigated in modern web applications.**

**Model Answer:**

The **OWASP Top 10 (2021)** is a standard awareness document published by the Open Web Application Security Project that represents the most critical web application security risks. The 2021 edition comprises the following categories (3 marks):

- **A01: Broken Access Control**
- **A02: Cryptographic Failures**
- **A03: Injection**
- **A04: Insecure Design**
- **A05: Security Misconfiguration**
- **A06: Vulnerable and Outdated Components**
- **A07: Identification and Authentication Failures**
- **A08: Software and Data Integrity Failures**
- **A09: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures**
- **A10: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)**

**Mitigation of Broken Access Control (A01)** (2 marks):

Broken Access Control occurs when restrictions on what authenticated users are allowed to do are not properly enforced. Mitigations include:
- Denying access by default (principle of least privilege).
- Implementing centralized access control mechanisms and reusing them across the application.
- Enforcing record ownership using domain-specific unique identifiers.
- Disabling web server directory listings and ensuring metadata files (e.g., `.git`, `.env`) are not deployed.
- Logging access control failures and alerting administrators (aligns with ISO 27001 A.12.4).

**Mitigation of Injection (A03)** (2 marks):

Injection flaws occur when user-supplied data is concatenated into commands or queries without proper sanitization. Mitigations include:
- Using **parameterized queries** (prepared statements) for SQL.
- Using **ORM (Object-Relational Mapping)** frameworks.
- Whitelisting server-side input validation (allow-list).
- Using **LIMIT** and **SQL LIMIT** within SQL queries to prevent mass disclosure in case of injection.
- Using **escape characters** for special elements using their specific syntax (e.g., ESAPI).

**(b) [7 Marks] Describe the PCI DSS standard. List its 6 core goals and explain the significance of version 4.0.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard (PCI DSS)** (1 mark) is an information security standard administered by the **PCI Security Standards Council** to protect cardholder data. It applies to all entities that store, process, or transmit cardholder data, including merchants, processors, acquirers, issuers, and service providers. Compliance is mandatory through contracts with card brands (Visa, Mastercard, etc.).

**The 6 Core Goals (and 12 Requirements) of PCI DSS** (3 marks):

| Goal | Associated Requirements |
|---|---|
| 1. Build and Maintain a Secure Network and Systems | Req 1: Install firewall config; Req 2: Do not use vendor defaults |
| 2. Protect Account Data | Req 3: Protect stored data; Req 4: Encrypt transmission |
| 3. Maintain a Vulnerability Management Program | Req 5: Use antivirus; Req 6: Develop secure systems |
| 4. Implement Strong Access Control Measures | Req 7: Restrict access; Req 8: Identify and authenticate; Req 9: Restrict physical access |
| 5. Regularly Monitor and Test Networks | Req 10: Track and monitor; Req 11: Test security systems |
| 6. Maintain an Information Security Policy | Req 12: Maintain a policy |

**Significance of PCI DSS v4.0** (3 marks):

Released in March 2022 and fully effective by March 2025, PCI DSS v4.0 introduces major enhancements:

- **Customized Approach**: Allows organizations to achieve security objectives using alternative controls, not just the defined approach. This is beneficial for modern cloud and DevOps environments.
- **Enhanced Authentication**: Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) is now required for **all access** into the Cardholder Data Environment (CDE), not just administrative access.
- **Targeted Risk Analysis**: Entities must perform and document a formal risk analysis for each requirement that allows flexibility.
- **Automated Log Review Mechanisms**: v4.0 mandates automated mechanisms for performing audit log reviews, replacing the manual processes of v3.2.1.
- **Broader Encryption Flexibility**: Updated guidance on encryption, including support for stronger cryptographic protocols (e.g., TLS 1.3) and clarification on the use of trusted keys/certificates.

---

### Question B: [14 Marks] — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the GDPR regulation. Discuss its key principles and the requirements for breach notification.**

**Model Answer:**

The **General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR)** (1 mark) is a comprehensive data protection and privacy regulation enacted by the European Union, effective from **25 May 2018**. It supersedes the Data Protection Directive 95/46/EC and applies to all organizations (controllers and processors) that process the personal data of individuals residing in the EU, regardless of the organization's geographical location.

**Key Principles of GDPR (Article 5)** (4 marks):

1. **Lawfulness, Fairness, and Transparency**: Processing must be lawful, fair to the data subject, and transparent about purpose.
2. **Purpose Limitation**: Data must be collected for specified, explicit, and legitimate purposes.
3. **Data Minimization**: Data collected must be adequate, relevant, and limited to what is necessary.
4. **Accuracy**: Personal data must be accurate and kept up to date.
5. **Storage Limitation**: Data must be kept only as long as necessary for the purposes for which it is processed.
6. **Integrity and Confidentiality**: Data must be processed securely (technical and organizational measures).
7. **Accountability**: The controller is responsible for demonstrating compliance with all principles.

**Breach Notification Requirements (Article 33 & 34)** (2 marks):

- **Notification to Supervisory Authority**: In the event of a personal data breach, the controller must notify the competent supervisory authority **without undue delay and, where feasible, not later than 72 hours** after becoming aware of the breach. If notification occurs after 72 hours, it must be accompanied by reasons for the delay.
- **Notification to Data Subjects**: When a breach is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals, the controller must communicate the breach to the affected data subjects **without undue delay**.
- **Documentation**: All breaches must be documented, including facts, effects, and remedial measures.

**(b) [7 Marks] Compare and contrast ISO/IEC 27001 and NIST SP 800-53. Discuss their role in application security compliance.**

**Model Answer:**

**ISO/IEC 27001** (2 marks):

- **Origin**: Published by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) and the International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC).
- **Scope**: Specifies the requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving an **Information Security Management System (ISMS)**.
- **Structure**: Follows the Annex SL high-level structure with 10 clauses (4-10) and an Annex A containing 93 control objectives.
- **Certification**: Organizations can obtain **third-party certification** valid for 3 years with annual surveillance audits.
- **Approach**: **Risk-based** and management-system focused (PDCA cycle: Plan-Do-Check-Act).
- **Application to Apps**: Annex A controls such as A.14 (System acquisition, development, and maintenance) directly address application security.

**NIST SP 800-53** (2 marks):

- **Origin**: Published by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)**, a US federal agency.
- **Scope**: A comprehensive catalog of **security and privacy controls** for federal information systems and organizations (Revision 5 is the latest).
- **Structure**: Organized into 20 control families (e.g., Access Control, Configuration Management, Incident Response) with over 1000 controls.
- **Certification**: Not a certification per se; it is used as a baseline for compliance (e.g., **FedRAMP**, **FISMA**).
- **Approach**: Control-based, aligned with risk management framework (RMF): Categorize, Select, Implement, Assess, Authorize, Monitor.
- **Application to Apps**: Control families like SI (System and Information Integrity), SA (System and Services Acquisition), and SC (System and Communications Protection) provide granular application security guidance.

**Comparison** (2 marks):

| Parameter | ISO/IEC 27001 | NIST SP 800-53 |
|---|---|---|
| Geographic Origin | International | United States |
| Certification | Yes (third-party audit) | No (used as baseline) |
| Approach | ISMS, process-oriented | Control catalog, technology-oriented |
| Number of Controls | 93 (Annex A) | 1000+ (across 20 families) |
| Update Cycle | Periodic | Continuous (Rev 5 published 2020) |
| Primary Use | Global commercial adoption | US government & federal contractors |

**Role in Application Security Compliance** (1 mark):

Both standards complement each other. ISO 27001 is preferred by global enterprises for certification and business reputation, while NIST 800-53 is the de facto standard for US government and defense applications. Organizations often map ISO 27001 controls to NIST 800-53 to satisfy multiple regulatory regimes simultaneously.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing OWASP Top 10 (a risk awareness document) with OWASP ASVS (a verification standard)**. Examiners deduct marks if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Omitting the year** of standards (e.g., "OWASP Top 10 2021" vs "OWASP Top 10 2017") — always cite the version, as the list changes.
> 3. **Misstating GDPR's 72-hour breach notification** as a deadline for public disclosure (it is for the supervisory authority; public disclosure has different rules).
> 4. **Forgetting the difference between ISO 27001 (certifiable) and ISO 27002 (guidance only)**. This is a frequent trick question.
> 5. **Listing PCI DSS as a "law"** — it is an **industry contractual standard**, not a government regulation. This distinction is critical.
> 6. **Mixing up HIPAA's Privacy Rule and Security Rule** — Privacy Rule governs use/disclosure of PHI; Security Rule governs technical/physical safeguards.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**
> - OWASP Top 10 (2021) categories must be memorized in **order** (A01 to A10).
> - **PCI DSS v4.0** is the current version; know the 6 goals and 12 requirements.
> - **GDPR's 72-hour breach notification** rule under Article 33 is a high-yield fact.
> - **ISO 27001** is certifiable; **ISO 27002** is guidance.
> - **HIPAA** applies to PHI in the US; **GDPR** applies to personal data of EU residents.
> - The **CIA Triad** underpins all security standards.
> - Always map application vulnerabilities to a **standard** (OWASP/NIST/ISO) for CTI reporting.
> - Know the difference between **SAST (static)** and **DAST (dynamic)** application security testing.
> - **SOC 2** is an audit, not a certification, and is based on **Trust Service Criteria** (Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, Privacy).
> - **NIST SSDF (SP 800-218)** is the modern standard for secure software development lifecycle practices.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DevSecOps Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT755 - CYBER THREAT INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** DevSecOps Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**DevSecOps** (Development, Security, and Operations) is a software engineering culture, practice, and methodology that integrates security considerations and practices as a shared, continuous responsibility throughout the entire **Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)** — from initial design and coding to integration, testing, deployment, delivery, and ongoing operations. It is a security-first extension of the DevOps philosophy that aims to deliver secure, high-quality software at high velocity without sacrificing one for the other.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECCT755 Module 3):** DevSecOps is the practice of embedding security into every phase of the DevOps pipeline, transforming security from a gatekeeping bottleneck into an automated, collaborative, and continuous function shared by development, security, and operations teams.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Surgery" Analogy

Imagine a hospital's surgical process:

- **Traditional Approach (DevOps without security):** Doctors perform surgery → patient recovers → THEN a quality inspector checks for infections and complications.
- **DevSecOps Approach:** Sterilization, hygiene checks, vital-sign monitoring, and anesthesiologist presence are integrated **from the moment the patient enters the operating theatre**, throughout surgery, and into post-op recovery.

In the software world:
- **DevOps** = Streamlined code → build → test → release.
- **DevSecOps** = Security scans, threat modeling, secret detection, vulnerability assessments, and compliance checks happen **at every step** — not just before release.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea in One Line:** DevSecOps = DevOps + Security baked in, not bolted on.

## 1.3 The "Three Pillars" of DevSecOps

| Pillar | Acronym Expansion | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Dev** | Development | Plan → Code → Build → Test |
| **Sec** | Security | Threat modeling, scanning, compliance |
| **Ops** | Operations | Deploy → Monitor → Operate → Feedback |

These three pillars are **equally weighted**; security is **not subordinate** to development or operations.

## 1.4 Why DevSecOps Exists — The Problem It Solves

Traditional SDLC models (Waterfall, even older Agile) treat security as a **final-stage gate**:

- A separate security team reviews code **after** it is fully developed.
- Findings cause costly rework and delayed releases.
- Attackers exploit vulnerabilities **before** the security team even gets a chance.

DevSecOps solves this by:

1. **Shifting security left** (earlier in the pipeline).
2. **Automating security checks** at every stage.
3. **Empowering developers** with self-service security tooling.
4. **Reducing Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** and **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)** to threats.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric Highlight:** Modern DevSecOps targets an **MTTD ≤ 24 hours** and **MTTR ≤ 1 hour** for critical production vulnerabilities, compared to weeks or months in traditional models.

---

## 1.5 Key Terminology Primer

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **CI/CD** | Continuous Integration / Continuous Delivery or Deployment — automated build-test-release pipeline. |
| **Shift-Left** | Moving security testing earlier (to the left) in the development timeline. |
| **SAST** | Static Application Security Testing — analyzes source code for vulnerabilities. |
| **DAST** | Dynamic Application Security Testing — black-box testing of running application. |
| **SCA** | Software Composition Analysis — scans third-party/open-source dependencies. |
| **IaC** | Infrastructure as Code — managing infrastructure through machine-readable files. |
| **Policy as Code** | Expressing security/compliance rules as programmable policies. |
| **SBOM** | Software Bill of Materials — inventory of all components in a build. |
| **Runtime Protection (RASP)** | Runtime Application Self-Protection — defends the app from inside at runtime. |
| **Container Security** | Securing containerized workloads (Docker, Kubernetes). |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Foundational Principles of DevSecOps

### Principle 1 — **People: Shared Responsibility Culture**

> "Security is everyone's job, not just the security team's."

- **Cross-functional teams:** Developers, security engineers, and operations engineers collaborate from day one.
- **Security champions** are embedded inside development teams.
- Security is reframed from a blocker to an **enabler** of safe, rapid delivery.
- **Training:** Every developer is upskilled in secure coding (OWASP Top 10 awareness).

### Principle 2 — **Process: Continuous Security Integration**

Security activities are inserted at each SDLC phase rather than concentrated at the end:

| SDLC Phase | DevOps Activity | DevSecOps Security Insertion |
|---|---|---|
| **Plan** | User stories, architecture | Threat modeling, security requirements |
| **Code** | Source code, IDE | Pre-commit hooks, secret scanning, IDE plugins (e.g., Snyk) |
| **Build** | Compile, package | SAST, dependency/SCA scan, image scanning |
| **Test** | Unit/Integration tests | DAST, IAST, fuzzing, compliance checks |
| **Release** | Staging, approval | Sign artifacts, SBOM generation, policy-as-code gates |
| **Deploy** | Production rollout | Admission controllers, signed images, IaC scanning |
| **Operate** | Monitor, log | RASP, runtime anomaly detection, SIEM feed |
| **Monitor** | Feedback | Vulnerability dashboards, continuous threat intel |

### Principle 3 — **Technology: Automation and Security Tooling**

- Every manual security review is a candidate for automation.
- Security gates are enforced by **policy engines** (e.g., Open Policy Agent).
- Tools are integrated into **CI/CD pipelines** as native steps.

## 2.2 The CALMS Framework (Adapted for DevSecOps)

The **CALMS** acronym (Culture, Automation, Lean, Measurement, Sharing) from DevOps is extended to DevSecOps:

| Letter | Concept | DevSecOps Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **C** | Culture | Security-first mindset across all teams |
| **A** | Automation | Automated scanning, testing, remediation suggestions |
| **L** | Lean | Eliminate redundant, slow, manual security reviews |
| **M** | Measurement | Track MTTD, MTTR, vulnerability density, scan coverage |
| **S** | Sharing | Threat intel shared between dev, sec, ops (and external feeds) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Key Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These metrics are **exam-favorite** and frequently appear in KTU module questions.

| Metric | Formula / Definition | Target / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **MTTD** (Mean Time to Detect) | $\text{MTTD} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (T_{\text{detect},i} - T_{\text{introduce},i})}{n}$ | Hours / Days |
| **MTTR** (Mean Time to Respond/Remediate) | $\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (T_{\text{fix},i} - T_{\text{detect},i})}{n}$ | Hours / Days |
| **Vulnerability Density** | $\text{VD} = \frac{N_{\text{vulns}}}{KLOC}$ | Vulnerabilities per 1,000 Lines of Code |
| **Scan Coverage** | $\text{SC} = \frac{N_{\text{scanned artifacts}}}{N_{\text{total artifacts}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%) |
| **Compliance Score** | $\text{CS} = \frac{N_{\text{passed controls}}}{N_{\text{total controls}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%) |
| **Deployment Frequency** | Number of production deployments per unit time | Per day / week |
| **Change Failure Rate** | $\text{CFR} = \frac{N_{\text{failed changes}}}{N_{\text{total changes}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%) |
| **SBOM Completeness** | $\text{SBOM}_{\%} = \frac{N_{\text{inventory components}}}{N_{\text{actual components}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%) |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU questions, students are often asked to *define* these metrics. Memorize the full forms, formulas, and what they measure.

## 2.4 The "Shift-Left" Security Paradigm

**Shift-Left** means performing security activities as **early as possible** in the SDLC.

- **Cost of fixing a vulnerability** rises exponentially the later it is found:

$$C_{\text{fix}} \propto e^{k \cdot t}$$

where $t$ is the time taken to detect the defect (in SDLC phases) and $k$ is a constant multiplier ($\approx 0.5$ to $1.0$).

| Detection Point | Relative Cost to Fix |
|---|---|
| Requirements/Design phase | **$0$ to $1\times$** (cheapest) |
| Implementation (coding) | **$5\times$** |
| Build / CI stage | **$10\times$** |
| Testing (QA) | **$15\times$** |
| Staging / Pre-prod | **$30\times$** |
| Production | **$100\times$** (most expensive) |
| After breach/discovery by attacker | **$1000\times$** (reputational + legal) |

## 2.5 DevSecOps vs. DevSec vs. DevOps vs. SecDevOps

> [!WARNING]
> These terms are often used interchangeably in industry, but KTU examiners **do test the distinction**.

| Term | Focus | Order of Priority |
|---|---|---|
| **DevOps** | Speed of delivery | Dev → Ops (security may be a bolt-on) |
| **SecDevOps** | Security-first, then DevOps | Sec → Dev → Ops |
| **DevSecOps** | Equal weight to all three | Dev ⇄ Sec ⇄ Ops (collaborative) |
| **Rugged DevOps** | Resilient, defensive software at every layer | Dev → Ops with deep hardening |

The most widely accepted industry term is **DevSecOps** — emphasizing that **security is integrated**, not prioritized over delivery.

## 2.6 Threat Intelligence in DevSecOps

DevSecOps pipelines consume **Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI)** at multiple points:

- **Pre-commit:** Developers are alerted to threats targeting their tech stack.
- **Build:** SCA tools ingest CVE feeds to flag vulnerable dependencies.
- **Deploy:** Admission controllers block deployment of images with known malware hashes.
- **Operate:** SIEM/SOAR pipelines correlate runtime anomalies with CTI feeds (MITRE ATT&CK, STIX/TAXII).
- **Feedback:** Newly discovered threats are fed back to update detection rules and training.

This creates a **closed-loop threat-intelligent pipeline**.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

| Industry | DevSecOps Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Banking & FinTech** | Continuous PCI-DSS compliance scanning of payment microservices. |
| **Healthcare** | HIPAA-compliant IaC scanning and signed container deployments. |
| **E-Commerce** | Automated bot/credential-stuffing detection in CI/CD-tested storefronts. |
| **Government / Defense** | SBOM enforcement and supply-chain attestation (SLSA levels). |
| **SaaS / Cloud Providers** | Multi-tenant isolation verified through policy-as-code. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The DevSecOps Pipeline — Exhaustive Stage-by-Stage Walkthrough

This section provides a **complete analytical breakdown** of every stage, with derivation of decisions and security insertion points.

---

### **Stage 1: PLAN (Pre-Development)**

**Activities:**
- Requirements gathering, user story creation, architecture drafting.

**Security Insertion Points (Derived Step-by-Step):**

1. **Identify Assets & Data Flows** → List all sensitive data handled (PII, PHI, payment data).
2. **Threat Modeling** → Use STRIDE or PASTA framework to enumerate threats.
3. **Security Requirements** → Define controls (e.g., "all endpoints must use TLS 1.3").
4. **Risk Register** → Map threats to likelihood × impact.

**Derivation of Threat Score:**

$$\text{Risk} = L \times I$$

where:

- $L$ = Likelihood (1–5)
- $I$ = Impact (1–5)

A risk score $\text{Risk} \geq 16$ is typically treated as **critical** and addressed before coding begins.

**Output:** A signed-off **Security Requirements Document (SRD)** that becomes the contract for the rest of the pipeline.

---

### **Stage 2: CODE (Development)**

**Activities:**
- Developers write source code in their IDEs (VS Code, IntelliJ, etc.).

**Security Insertion Points:**

1. **Pre-commit hooks (client-side):**
   - `git-secrets` → blocks hardcoded credentials.
   - `detect-secrets` → scans for API keys, tokens.
2. **IDE plugins:**
   - Snyk, SonarLint, Semgrep → real-time static analysis feedback.
3. **Peer Code Review:**
   - Mandatory security checklist in PR template.
   - At least **one security-trained reviewer** approves changes touching auth/crypto.

**Derivation of Code-Level Vulnerability Detection Rate:**

$$\text{DR}_{\text{code}} = \frac{N_{\text{vulns caught in IDE}}}{N_{\text{total vulns introduced}}} \times 100\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> Industry studies (e.g., Veracode State of Software Security) suggest $\text{DR}_{\text{code}}$ of 30–50% for well-tooled teams, dramatically reducing the burden on later stages.

---

### **Stage 3: BUILD (Continuous Integration)**

**Activities:**
- Source code is compiled, dependencies are resolved, container images are created.

**Security Insertion Points:**

1. **SAST (Static Application Security Testing):**
   - Tools: SonarQube, Checkmarx, Fortify, Semgrep.
   - Scans raw source for SQL injection, XSS, hardcoded creds, insecure deserialization.
2. **SCA (Software Composition Analysis):**
   - Tools: Snyk, OWASP Dependency-Check, npm audit, Trivy.
   - Cross-references dependencies against **CVE / NVD databases**.
3. **Container Image Scanning:**
   - Tools: Trivy, Clair, Anchore, Grype.
   - Inspects base image layers for OS-level CVEs.

**Derivation of Build-Time Risk Exposure:**

Let $V_i$ = vulnerabilities in dependency $i$, and $C_i$ = CVSS score.

$$\text{BuildRisk} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i \cdot C_i$$

A build fails (gate) when $\text{BuildRisk} > T_{\text{threshold}}$, where $T_{\text{threshold}}$ is policy-defined (commonly set so that no *Critical* CVSS $\geq 9.0$ vulnerabilities pass).

---

### **Stage 4: TEST (Continuous Testing)**

**Activities:**
- Automated unit, integration, regression, performance tests.

**Security Insertion Points:**

1. **DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing):**
   - Tools: OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite, Netsparker.
   - Black-box testing against running app — finds runtime issues SAST misses.
2. **IAST (Interactive Application Security Testing):**
   - Tools: Contrast Security, Hdiv.
   - Instruments app from inside during functional tests.
3. **Fuzz Testing:**
   - Tools: AFL, LibFuzzer, OSS-Fuzz.
   - Inputs malformed data to discover crashes/memory issues.
4. **Compliance-as-Code:**
   - Tools: InSpec, Open Policy Agent.
   - Validates infrastructure/app against PCI-DSS, CIS, NIST benchmarks.

**Derivation of Test Coverage:**

$$\text{Security Coverage} = \frac{N_{\text{security test cases executed}}}{N_{\text{total security test cases}}} \times 100\%$$

---

### **Stage 5: RELEASE (Artifact Preparation)**

**Activities:**
- Tagging, signing, and packaging the validated build.

**Security Insertion Points:**

1. **Artifact Signing:**
   - Cosign, Sigstore, GPG → ensures only trusted code is deployed.
2. **SBOM Generation:**
   - Tools: Syft, CycloneDX, SPDX.
   - Produces a full inventory of components in the build.
3. **Policy-as-Code Gates:**
   - OPA/Gatekeeper, Conftest → enforce deployment rules.

**Derivation of SBOM Completeness:**

$$\text{SBOM}_{\%} = \frac{\text{Declared components}}{\text{Actual components}} \times 100\%$$

A high-quality build requires $\text{SBOM}_{\%} \geq 95\%$.

---

### **Stage 6: DEPLOY (Continuous Delivery/Deployment)**

**Activities:**
- Push to staging or production environment via IaC (Terraform, Helm, Ansible).

**Security Insertion Points:**

1. **IaC Scanning:**
   - Tools: Checkov, tfsec, KICS.
   - Scans Terraform/CloudFormation templates for misconfigurations.
2. **Admission Controllers (Kubernetes):**
   - Tools: OPA Gatekeeper, Kyverno.
   - Block pods that lack signed images or violate policy.
3. **Secrets Management:**
   - HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager → no secrets in plaintext.

---

### **Stage 7: OPERATE (Production Monitoring)**

**Activities:**
- Logs, metrics, traces, incident response.

**Security Insertion Points:**

1. **RASP (Runtime Application Self-Protection):**
   - Tools: Imperva RASP, Contrast Protect.
   - Stops attacks (e.g., SQLi) at runtime from inside the app.
2. **WAF (Web Application Firewall):**
   - Tools: ModSecurity, AWS WAF, Cloudflare.
3. **SIEM / SOAR Integration:**
   - Splunk, ELK, IBM QRadar, Sentinel.
   - Ingests alerts, correlates with CTI feeds (STIX/TAXII).
4. **Continuous Compliance Monitoring:**
   - Tools: Cloud Custodian, AWS Config, Azure Policy.

---

### **Stage 8: MONITOR (Feedback Loop)**

**Activities:**
- Threat intelligence, vulnerability disclosure, incident learning.

**Security Insertion Points:**

1. **Threat Intel Feeds:** MISP, AlienVault OTX, Recorded Future.
2. **Bug Bounty Programs:** HackerOne, Bugcrowd.
3. **Post-Mortems:** Blameless retrospectives feed lessons back into Plan.

This stage **closes the loop** — a defining feature of DevSecOps.

---

## 3.2 DevSecOps Maturity Model (Analytical Derivation)

The maturity of an organization's DevSecOps adoption can be expressed as:

$$\text{Maturity}_{\text{Score}} = w_1 \cdot C + w_2 \cdot A + w_3 \cdot T + w_4 \cdot G + w_5 \cdot V$$

Where:

- $C$ = Culture (0–5)
- $A$ = Automation (0–5)
- $T$ = Tooling integration (0–5)
- $G$ = Governance/Compliance (0–5)
- $V$ = Visibility/Monitoring (0–5)
- $w_i$ = weighted importance ($\sum w_i = 1$)

| Maturity Level | Score Range | Characteristics |
|---|---|---|
| **Level 1: Initial** | 0–1.0 | Manual security, siloed teams, ad-hoc |
| **Level 2: Repeatable** | 1.1–2.0 | Basic CI security scans, some automation |
| **Level 3: Defined** | 2.1–3.0 | Security gates in CI/CD, SBOMs, IaC scanning |
| **Level 4: Managed** | 3.1–4.0 | Policy-as-code, RASP, threat intel integration |
| **Level 5: Optimized** | 4.1–5.0 | Continuous learning, AI-assisted, fully autonomous |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"List the levels of DevSecOps maturity."* Memorize the above 5-level model.

---

## 3.3 Supply-Chain Security in DevSecOps (SLSA Framework)

The **Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts (SLSA)** is a security framework to ensure artifact integrity:

| Level | Description |
|---|---|
| **SLSA 1** | Build provenance — basic documentation of build process |
| **SLSA 2** | Hosted build platform with signed provenance |
| **SLSA 3** | Source verified, hardened build, non-forgeable provenance |
| **SLSA 4** | Two-party review, hermetic, reproducible builds |

**Derivation of Provenance Integrity:**

$$\text{Integrity} = \text{Sign}(\text{Artifact}, \text{Build Metadata})$$

If the signature does not match, the artifact is **rejected** at deployment.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Tools Mapped to Pipeline Stages

| Pipeline Stage | SAST | DAST | SCA | IaC Scan | Image Scan | Secret Scan | Runtime (RASP/WAF) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Code** | ✓ (IDE) | — | ✓ (IDE plugin) | — | — | ✓ (pre-commit) | — |
| **Build** | ✓ | — | ✓ | — | ✓ | ✓ | — |
| **Test** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | — | — | — | — |
| **Release** | — | — | ✓ | — | ✓ | — | — |
| **Deploy** | — | — | — | ✓ | ✓ | — | — |
| **Operate** | — | ✓ | — | — | — | — | ✓ |
| **Monitor** | — | ✓ | — | ✓ | — | — | ✓ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Complete DevSecOps Pipeline (End-to-End)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plan & Design] --> B[Code & IDE]
    B --> C[Build & CI]
    C --> D[Test & QA]
    D --> E[Release & Sign]
    E --> F[Deploy & IaC]
    F --> G[Operate & Monitor]
    G --> H[Feedback & Threat Intel]
    H --> A

    %% Security overlays
    S1[Threat Modeling] -.-> A
    S2[Pre-commit Hooks] -.-> B
    S3[SAST and SCA] -.-> C
    S4[DAST and IAST] -.-> D
    S5[SBOM and Signing] -.-> E
    S6[Policy as Code] -.-> F
    S7[RASP and WAF] -.-> G
    S8[CTI and SIEM] -.-> H

    %% Style for security overlays
    classDef sec fill:#ffe4e1,stroke:#b22222,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6,S7,S8 sec

    classDef stage fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H stage
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The outer ring (blue boxes) is the standard DevOps loop. The **dashed red arrows** represent the **security overlay** — a continuous, parallel thread of security activities feeding into every stage. The **feedback loop** from Monitor → Plan ensures continuous improvement.

---

## 4.2 DevSecOps vs Traditional Security Model (Side-by-Side)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Traditional["TRADITIONAL SECURITY (Siloed)"]
        T1[Development] --> T2[Testing] --> T3[Release] --> T4[Security Audit] --> T5[Operations]
        T4 -. Blocks Release .-> T3
    end

    subgraph DevSecOps["DEVSECOPS (Integrated)"]
        D1[Plan with Threat Model] --> D2[Code with IDE Security] --> D3[Build with SAST and SCA] --> D4[Test with DAST] --> D5[Release with Signing] --> D6[Deploy with IaC Scan] --> D7[Operate with RASP]
        D7 --> D1
    end

    classDef trad fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef dev fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    class T1,T2,T3,T4,T5 trad
    class D1,D2,D3,D4,D5,D6,D7 dev
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the **Traditional** model, security acts as a **gate at the end**, often blocking releases. In the **DevSecOps** model, security is **baked in from day one**, with no single bottleneck.

---

## 4.3 Security Tool Integration in a CI/CD Pipeline (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Dev[Developer Push Code] --> Repo[(Git Repository)]
    Repo --> CI1[CI Triggered]
    CI1 --> Scan1[Secret Scan]
    Scan1 --> Scan2[SAST Scan]
    Scan2 --> Scan3[SCA and Dependency Check]
    Scan3 --> Scan4[Container Image Scan]
    Scan4 --> Build[Build Artifact]
    Build --> Sign[Cosign Artifact Signing]
    Sign --> SBOM[Generate SBOM]
    SBOM --> Policy{Policy Gate<br/>OPA or Kyverno}
    Policy -- Pass --> Deploy[Deploy to Staging]
    Deploy --> DAST[DAST Scan]
    DAST --> Compliance[Compliance Check]
    Compliance --> Prod[Deploy to Production]
    Prod --> Monitor[Runtime Monitoring<br/>RASP, WAF, SIEM]
    Monitor --> Feedback[CTI Feedback Loop]
    Feedback --> Repo

    Policy -- Fail --> Block[Block & Notify]
    Compliance -- Fail --> Block

    classDef gate fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef ops fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    class Scan1,Scan2,Scan3,Scan4,DAST,Compliance tool
    class Policy gate
    class Deploy,Prod,Monitor,Feedback ops
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**
**Define DevSecOps. List any four key principles of DevSecOps.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
DevSecOps is a software engineering culture and methodology that integrates security practices as a shared, continuous responsibility throughout the entire Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC), combining Development, Security, and Operations into a unified, automated pipeline.

**Four Key Principles (1.5 Marks — 0.4 each, four points):**

1. **Shared Security Responsibility:** Security is everyone's job, not just the security team's.
2. **Shift-Left Security:** Security checks are moved to the earliest stages of the SDLC.
3. **Automation of Security Testing:** SAST, DAST, SCA, secret scanning, and compliance checks are automated in CI/CD.
4. **Continuous Monitoring & Feedback:** Production telemetry, threat intelligence, and incident lessons are fed back into development.

*(Optional 5th principle: Security as Code & Policy as Code for enforceable, version-controlled security.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**
**Differentiate between DevOps and DevSecOps. Mention any three differences.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark per difference):**

| Sl. No. | DevOps | DevSecOps |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Focuses on speed of delivery and collaboration between Dev and Ops. | Focuses on speed **with security** integrated at every stage. |
| 2 | Security is often handled by a separate team, sometimes as a final-stage gate. | Security is shared by all teams and embedded from planning to operations. |
| 3 | Vulnerabilities are typically detected late, often after release. | Vulnerabilities are detected early via Shift-Left and automation, reducing MTTD and MTTR. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Choice Pattern:** KTU End-Semester Examinations (ESE) typically allow **internal choice** between two questions (Choice A or Choice B) from the same module. Both choices below are designed to be **mutually exclusive in focus** to mirror real KTU papers.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Focus: Pipeline Architecture, Security Tools, and Threat Intelligence Integration**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / Model Paper, Module 3, Question Choice A]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **DevSecOps pipeline** by describing the security activities performed at each of the following stages: *Plan, Code, Build, Test, Release, Deploy, Operate, and Monitor*.

**Model Answer:**

**Diagram (3 Marks):** Draw the 8-stage pipeline loop with security overlays as shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes. Label each stage and the corresponding security activity.

**Stage-wise Explanation (4 Marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Plan:** Threat modeling using STRIDE/PASTA; security requirements documentation; risk register creation.
2. **Code:** Pre-commit hooks (`git-secrets`), IDE plugins (Snyk, SonarLint), secure coding standards, peer review security checklist.
3. **Build:** SAST (SonarQube, Checkmarx), SCA (Snyk, OWASP Dependency-Check), container image scanning (Trivy, Clair).
4. **Test:** DAST (OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite), IAST (Contrast), fuzzing (AFL), compliance-as-code (InSpec, OPA).
5. **Release:** Artifact signing (Cosign, Sigstore), SBOM generation (CycloneDX, SPDX), provenance attestation (SLSA).
6. **Deploy:** IaC scanning (Checkov, tfsec), Kubernetes admission controllers (OPA Gatekeeper, Kyverno), secrets management (Vault).
7. **Operate:** RASP (Imperva, Contrast Protect), WAF (ModSecurity, Cloudflare), SIEM (Splunk, ELK), runtime anomaly detection.
8. **Monitor:** Threat intelligence feed ingestion (STIX/TAXII, MITRE ATT&CK), bug bounty program, blameless post-mortems fed back to Plan.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain how **Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI)** is integrated into a DevSecOps pipeline. Discuss the role of **STIX/TAXII, MITRE ATT&CK, and SBOM** in this integration.

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
DevSecOps pipelines consume CTI at multiple points to proactively defend against known and emerging threats. CTI transforms reactive security into predictive, intelligence-driven security.

**STIX/TAXII in DevSecOps (2 Marks):**
- **STIX (Structured Threat Information eXpression):** A standardized language for representing threat intel (indicators, TTPs, threat actors).
- **TAXII (Trusted Automated eXchange of Intelligence Information):** A transport protocol for sharing STIX-formatted intel between organizations.
- **Integration:** SIEM/SOAR platforms ingest STIX/TAXII feeds from MISP, AlienVault OTX, Recorded Future, etc., enriching alerts and updating detection rules automatically.
- **Pipeline Impact:** A new CVE in a critical library triggers an automatic SCA re-scan of all artifacts in the registry; production WAF rules are auto-updated.

**MITRE ATT&CK in DevSecOps (2 Marks):**
- A curated knowledge base of adversary Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures (TTPs).
- **Used in:** Threat modeling during Plan, red-team testing during Test, detection engineering during Operate.
- **Example:** Mapping observed anomalies to ATT&CK technique T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application) triggers a CI/CD re-scan for that specific class of flaw.

**SBOM in DevSecOps (2 Marks):**
- **SBOM (Software Bill of Materials):** A formal, machine-readable inventory of all components (libraries, packages, OS dependencies) in a build.
- **Generated at:** Release stage using Syft, CycloneDX, or SPDX tools.
- **Used for:** Rapid vulnerability impact analysis — when a new CVE is disclosed, SBOMs across the organization are queried to find all affected artifacts in seconds.
- **Regulatory driver:** US Executive Order 14028 mandates SBOMs for government software.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Focus: Maturity, Metrics, Shift-Left, and Case Studies**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / Model Paper, Module 3, Question Choice B]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **DevSecOps maturity model** with its five levels. Describe the **Shift-Left security** concept and justify why it is critical in modern software development.

**Model Answer:**

**Maturity Model (4 Marks — 0.8 each level):**

1. **Level 1 — Initial:** Security is manual, reactive, and siloed. No integration with DevOps. Vulnerabilities found post-release.
2. **Level 2 — Repeatable:** Basic CI security scans (e.g., SAST) introduced. Awareness of vulnerabilities in code, but no enforced gates.
3. **Level 3 — Defined:** Security gates enforced in CI/CD. SBOMs, IaC scanning, and policy-as-code become standard. Compliance is codified.
4. **Level 4 — Managed:** Full policy-as-code, RASP, runtime threat intel integration. Continuous compliance with frameworks like NIST, PCI-DSS.
5. **Level 5 — Optimized:** AI/ML-assisted vulnerability detection, autonomous remediation, predictive threat modeling, and zero-touch deployments.

**Shift-Left Security (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Shift-Left Security is the practice of moving security activities (modeling, scanning, testing) from the end of the SDLC to the **earliest possible stages** (planning, design, coding).

**Justification (2 Marks — 0.5 each point):**

1. **Cost Efficiency:** Cost of fixing a vulnerability grows exponentially with delay — design-time fix costs ~$0, production fix costs ~100× more.
2. **Reduced MTTD/MTTR:** Vulnerabilities found in IDE are remediated in minutes vs. weeks after release.
3. **Faster, Safer Releases:** Automated gates don't block; they fail fast and early.
4. **Empowered Developers:** Real-time IDE feedback turns security from a "gotcha" into a daily practice.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **key performance metrics** used to measure the success of a DevSecOps program. For each metric, explain what it measures and why it matters.

**Model Answer:**

**Eight Key Metrics (7 Marks — 0.85 each):**

1. **MTTD (Mean Time to Detect):** Average time between vulnerability introduction and detection. **Matters:** Lower MTTD means smaller attack window.

2. **MTTR (Mean Time to Remediate/Respond):** Average time between detection and fix. **Matters:** Lower MTTR limits blast radius.

3. **Vulnerability Density:** Vulnerabilities per KLOC (1,000 Lines of Code). **Matters:** Tracks code-level security health.

4. **Scan Coverage (%):** Percentage of artifacts scanned by security tools. **Matters:** Un-scanned artifacts = blind spots.

5. **Compliance Score (%):** Percentage of passed controls in compliance frameworks (PCI-DSS, CIS, NIST). **Matters:** Demonstrates regulatory adherence.

6. **Deployment Frequency:** Number of production deployments per day/week. **Matters:** Indicates DevOps maturity; DevSecOps must keep this high while remaining secure.

7. **Change Failure Rate (%):** Percentage of deployments causing production failures. **Matters:** Detects over-aggressive security waivers or under-tested code.

8. **SBOM Completeness (%):** Percentage of components inventoried in the SBOM. **Matters:** Critical for supply-chain risk management and zero-day response.

**Conclusion (built into points):** A mature DevSecOps program tracks **all** of these metrics on a real-time dashboard (e.g., Grafana, ELK) and uses them in SRE/dev team retrospectives.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in DevSecOps Questions:**

1. **Conflating DevOps with DevSecOps.** Examiners award 0 marks if the student writes only about CI/CD without mentioning security. Always explicitly name the security activity at each pipeline stage.
2. **Listing tools without context.** Writing "Snyk, SonarQube, Trivy" alone is worth 0.5 marks. You must state **what each tool does** and **at which stage** it operates.
3. **Skipping the "Shift-Left" justification.** Simply defining Shift-Left is incomplete; you must explain **why** it matters (cost curve, MTTD/MTTR).
4. **Ignoring the Feedback Loop.** DevSecOps is a *closed loop*. Forgetting the Monitor → Plan feedback cycle is a 1-mark deduction.
5. **Mixing up acronyms:** Confusing SAST with DAST, or SCA with IaC scanning, signals weak conceptual clarity — examiners will penalize.
6. **Not using diagrams.** KTU valuations reward **neat block diagrams** with labeled stages. Always draw the pipeline, even if the question doesn't explicitly ask.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bulletins — Pin These to Your Wall!**

- ✅ **DevSecOps = Dev + Sec + Ops** with **equal weight**, not security as an afterthought.
- ✅ **Shift-Left Security** = Move security checks to the **earliest** SDLC stage.
- ✅ **CALMS Framework** = **C**ulture, **A**utomation, **L**ean, **M**easurement, **S**haring.
- ✅ **5 Maturity Levels:** Initial → Repeatable → Defined → Managed → Optimized.
- ✅ **Core Pipeline Stages:** Plan → Code → Build → Test → Release → Deploy → Operate → Monitor → (loop back to Plan).
- ✅ **Security Activity per Stage:** Threat Modeling → Pre-commit Hooks → SAST/SCA/Image Scan → DAST/IAST → Signing/SBOM → IaC Scan/Admission Controllers → RASP/WAF/SIEM → CTI Feedback.
- ✅ **Key Acronyms to Memorize:**
  - **SAST** = Static (source code, white-box)
  - **DAST** = Dynamic (running app, black-box)
  - **SCA** = Software Composition Analysis (third-party libs)
  - **IaC** = Infrastructure as Code (Terraform, Helm)
  - **SBOM** = Software Bill of Materials (CycloneDX, SPDX)
  - **RASP** = Runtime Application Self-Protection
  - **WAF** = Web Application Firewall
  - **CTI** = Cyber Threat Intelligence
- ✅ **Threat Intel Standards:** **STIX** (data format), **TAXII** (transport), **MITRE ATT&CK** (TTP knowledge base).
- ✅ **Key Metrics:** MTTD, MTTR, Vulnerability Density, Scan Coverage, Compliance Score, Deployment Frequency, Change Failure Rate, SBOM Completeness.
- ✅ **Cost of Late Fixes** grows exponentially (≈100× from design to production).
- ✅ **DevSecOps ≠ SecDevOps** — DevSecOps emphasizes **integration**, SecDevOps emphasizes **security-first priority** (rarely tested, but a high-value differentiator).
- ✅ **SLSA Framework** = 4 levels of supply-chain artifact integrity (Levels 1–4).
- ✅ **Always close the loop:** Monitor → Threat Intel → Plan is the defining characteristic of mature DevSecOps.
- ✅ **Mermaid-friendly answer tip:** In KTU answer sheets, draw the 8-stage pipeline as a **circular flow** with security tools written in red/boxed beside each stage.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — DevSecOps Overview**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
