topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT755 - CYBER THREAT INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** SIEM Platforms and Threat Hunting: SIEM Concepts and Benefits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SIEM Platforms and Threat Hunting — SIEM Concepts and Benefits

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Security Information and Event Management (SIEM)** is a cybersecurity solution that combines **Security Information Management (SIM)** — the long-term collection, storage, and analysis of log data — with **Security Event Management (SEM)** — the real-time monitoring, correlation, and alerting of events — to provide a holistic, centralized platform for threat detection, incident response, regulatory compliance, and forensic investigation across an organization's entire IT infrastructure.

In the KTU 2024 PECCT755 (Cyber Threat Intelligence) syllabus, SIEM is positioned as the **operational backbone** of a Security Operations Center (SOC). It is the convergence point where raw telemetry from endpoints, networks, applications, cloud workloads, and identity systems is ingested, normalized, correlated, and transformed into **actionable threat intelligence**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The CCTV Control Room

Imagine a **large shopping mall** with hundreds of shops, each having its own alarm, cash register log, door sensor, and CCTV camera. Individually, each system tells you very little. But imagine a **central control room** where:
- Every alarm, sensor, and camera feeds live into **one giant dashboard** (this is **log aggregation**).
- A trained operator notices that "Door 3 opened at 2:47 AM" + "Cash register 12 was opened at 2:48 AM" + "Camera 7 shows a masked person" — these three events are **not individually suspicious**, but **together they form a clear pattern of theft** (this is **event correlation**).
- The operator immediately dispatches security (this is **alerting and incident response**).

**SIEM is exactly that central control room** — but for an enterprise's entire digital estate.

### 1.3 Why SIEM Matters in Cyber Threat Intelligence

In modern threat landscapes, attackers use **multi-stage, low-and-slow techniques** that evade any single security control. A single firewall log or endpoint alert rarely tells the full story. SIEM provides the **context, correlation, and centralized visibility** required to reconstruct these attack chains (kill chains) from fragmented evidence — making it a **Tier-1 intelligence platform** for any SOC.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Terminology Distinction (Frequently Tested):**  
> - **SIM** = Security Information Management → Historical, long-term log storage & analysis  
> - **SEM** = Security Event Management → Real-time monitoring & alerting  
> - **SIEM** = SIM + SEM (combined)
> - **SOAR** (Security Orchestration, Automation & Response) → Works *on top of* SIEM to automate response playbooks  
> - **XDR** (Extended Detection & Response) → A more modern, native-sensor-driven evolution that integrates EDR + NDR + Cloud into a unified detection fabric

### 1.4 Standard Industry Metrics for SIEM Performance

KTU examiners frequently test the following quantitative benchmarks:

| Metric | Industry Standard | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **MTTD (Mean Time To Detect)** | < **1 hour** for critical threats | How fast the SIEM raises an alert |
| **MTTR (Mean Time To Respond)** | < **4 hours** for critical incidents | How fast the SOC contains the threat |
| **EPS (Events Per Second)** | **10,000 – 100,000+ EPS** | Ingestion throughput capacity |
| **Log Retention** | **90 days hot** + **1–7 years cold** | Compliance (PCI-DSS requires 1 year) |
| **False Positive Rate** | < **5%** | Quality of correlation rules |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SIEM Architectural Components — The Six Pillars

A production-grade SIEM platform is built on six tightly integrated pillars. Each pillar performs a discrete function, but their value emerges only when they operate together.

### Pillar 1 — Data Collection (Ingestion)
- **Function:** Ingests logs and events from heterogeneous sources via **agents, syslog forwarding, APIs, or log shippers**.
- **Sources include:** Firewalls, IDS/IPS, EDR agents, Windows Event Logs, Linux syslog, DNS servers, web proxies, cloud audit logs (AWS CloudTrail, Azure Activity Log), databases, applications, and identity providers (Active Directory, Okta).
- **Why it matters:** Without comprehensive ingestion, the SIEM is blind. Gaps in log coverage are the **#1 reason SIEM deployments fail** in real-world audits.

### Pillar 2 — Normalization & Parsing
- **Function:** Converts vendor-specific log formats (e.g., Cisco ASA, Palo Alto, Check Point) into a **common schema** (typically the **CEF — Common Event Format** or **LEEF — Log Event Extended Format**).
- **Why it matters:** A raw Cisco firewall log and a raw Windows event look completely different. Normalization makes them comparable so the correlation engine can reason about them.

### Pillar 3 — Storage & Indexing
- **Function:** Stores events in a high-performance time-series database (e.g., Elasticsearch, Splunk's proprietary index, QRadar's Ariel DB).
- **Two tiers of storage:**
  - **Hot storage (SSD):** 30–90 days, fast search, expensive
  - **Cold storage (S3, Glacier, tape):** Months to years, slow retrieval, cheap
- **Why it matters:** Compliance frameworks like **PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR, and India's DPDP Act 2023** mandate minimum retention periods.

### Pillar 4 — Correlation Engine
- **Function:** The **brain** of the SIEM. Applies rules, statistical models, and machine-learning algorithms to detect patterns that indicate malicious activity.
- **Correlation techniques:**
  - **Rule-based** — Predefined IF-THEN logic (e.g., "5 failed logins in 60 seconds from same IP → alert")
  - **Statistical** — Anomaly detection against historical baselines
  - **Behavioral (UEBA)** — User & Entity Behavior Analytics using ML
  - **Threat intelligence feeds** — Matches events against known IOCs (Indicators of Compromise)
- **Why it matters:** A single failed login is noise; 500 failed logins from one IP across 10 accounts is **credential stuffing**.

### Pillar 5 — Alerting & Dashboarding
- **Function:** Surfaces detected threats to SOC analysts via dashboards, emails, SMS, or SOAR integrations.
- **Why it matters:** The SIEM's value is realized only when an analyst **acts** on the alert. **Alert fatigue** is a major SIEM design challenge.

### Pillar 6 — Incident Response & Forensics
- **Function:** Enables analysts to pivot from an alert to the **full event timeline** of the affected user/host/IP, supporting root-cause analysis and evidence preservation.
- **Why it matters:** SIEM is the SOC's **system of record** for forensic investigations.

---

## 2.2 SIEM Operational Workflow — The End-to-End Pipeline

The lifecycle of an event inside a SIEM follows a deterministic pipeline:

> **Collection → Normalization → Enrichment → Correlation → Alerting → Investigation → Response → Archival**

This pipeline is **linear and idempotent** — the same event flowing through the same pipeline at different times will produce the same normalized output, enabling reliable historical comparisons.

### 2.2.1 Detailed Phase Breakdown

**Phase 1 — Collection (Log Forwarding)**
Sources push or pull logs to the SIEM. Common mechanisms:
- **Agent-based:** Splunk Universal Forwarder, Winlogbeat, Filebeat, NXLog
- **Agentless:** Syslog (UDP/TCP 514), SNMP traps, REST API polling, S3 bucket polling

**Phase 2 — Parsing & Normalization**
Raw events are mapped to a canonical schema. Example normalization:

$$
\text{Raw Cisco ASA Log:} \quad \texttt{"<166>\%ASA-6-302013: Built outbound TCP connection..."}
$$

$$
\xrightarrow{\text{CEF Normalization}}
$$

$$
\text{CEF:}\ \texttt{CEF:0|Cisco|ASA|9.8|302013|Built Outbound Connection|5|src=10.1.1.5 dst=8.8.8.8 act=Create...}
$$

**Phase 3 — Enrichment**
Each event is augmented with context:
- **Geolocation** of source IP (MaxMind GeoIP)
- **Threat intelligence reputation** (VirusTotal, AlienVault OTX)
- **Asset criticality** (is this a domain controller or a test workstation?)
- **User identity resolution** (translating SID to username)

**Phase 4 — Correlation**
Multiple events from different sources are aggregated and evaluated against correlation rules. Example rule:

> **IF** ≥ 5 failed authentication events from the same source IP within 60 seconds **AND** at least one of those attempts targets an admin account **THEN** trigger alert **"Brute-Force Attack Suspected"** with **Severity: HIGH**

**Phase 5 — Alerting**
The correlation engine generates an alert, attaches evidence (the triggering events), assigns a severity, and routes it to the appropriate SOC queue (Tier 1 / Tier 2 / Tier 3).

**Phase 6 — Investigation**
The analyst opens the alert, drills down into the supporting events, queries related logs, builds a timeline, and determines the scope of the incident.

**Phase 7 — Response (often via SOAR integration)**
The analyst either:
- Manually contains the threat (disable account, block IP, isolate host)
- Triggers an automated SOAR playbook (e.g., "Auto-quarantine endpoint on malware detection")

**Phase 8 — Archival**
Events and alerts are written to long-term storage for compliance, audit, and future threat-hunting queries.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]  
> The following table consolidates the **quantitative relationships** and **architectural metrics** most frequently tested in KTU PECCT755 examinations. Memorize these before attempting numerical or descriptive questions.

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Events Per Second (EPS)** | $\text{EPS} = \dfrac{\text{Total Events}}{\text{Time Window (seconds)}}$ | $10^4$ – $10^5\ \text{EPS}$ | SIEM sizing & licensing |
| 2 | **Mean Time To Detect (MTTD)** | $\text{MTTD} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (T_{\text{detect},i} - T_{\text{occur},i})}{n}$ | $< 1\ \text{hr}$ for critical | SOC efficiency KPI |
| 3 | **Mean Time To Respond (MTTR)** | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (T_{\text{resolve},i} - T_{\text{detect},i})}{n}$ | $< 4\ \text{hrs}$ for critical | IR effectiveness KPI |
| 4 | **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{\text{FP}}{\text{FP} + \text{TN}} \times 100\%$ | $< 5\%$ | Correlation rule quality |
| 5 | **True Positive Rate / Recall** | $\text{TPR} = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}} \times 100\%$ | $> 95\%$ target | Detection coverage |
| 6 | **Log Retention Compliance** | $R_{\text{required}} \geq R_{\text{regulatory}}$ | PCI-DSS: 1 yr; HIPAA: 6 yr | Audit & legal defense |
| 7 | **Storage Cost Estimation** | $C_{\text{storage}} = (\text{EPS} \times \text{Avg Size} \times \text{Retention}) \times \text{Cost/GB}$ | USD/GB/month | SIEM TCO calculation |
| 8 | **Correlation Window** | $W_c \in [t_{\text{start}},\ t_{\text{start}} + \Delta t]$ | $\Delta t = 1$ – $60\ \text{min}$ | Rule tuning parameter |
| 9 | **Detection Latency** | $L_d = T_{\text{alert}} - T_{\text{event}}$ | Seconds to minutes | Real-time capability proof |
| 10 | **Threat Intelligence Match Rate** | $\text{Match\%} = \dfrac{\text{IOC Hits}}{\text{Total IOCs Loaded}} \times 100\%$ | $> 60\%$ for relevant feeds | Feed effectiveness metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "size a SIEM for an organization", always compute **EPS first** → then **daily log volume** → then **storage cost**. This three-step derivation is a guaranteed 14-mark question pattern.

---

## 2.4 SIEM Benefits — The Strategic Value Proposition

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to articulate **at least 6 distinct benefits** with engineering justification. The benefits fall into four strategic categories:

### Category A — Security Benefits
1. **Centralized Visibility:** Single pane of glass across on-prem, cloud, and hybrid environments.
2. **Real-Time Threat Detection:** Correlation of multi-source events reveals attacks invisible to siloed tools.
3. **Reduced Dwell Time:** Lower MTTD shrinks the window attackers have inside the network.
4. **Advanced Threat Detection via UEBA:** ML-driven baselining catches insider threats and credential abuse.

### Category B — Operational Benefits
5. **Automation & Orchestration:** Native or SOAR-integrated response playbooks reduce manual toil.
6. **Improved SOC Efficiency:** Dashboards, KPI tracking, and case management streamline analyst workflow.
7. **Scalability:** Modern cloud-native SIEMs (e.g., Microsoft Sentinel, Chronicle) scale elastically.

### Category C — Compliance Benefits
8. **Regulatory Reporting:** Pre-built templates for PCI-DSS, HIPAA, SOX, GDPR, DPDP Act 2023.
9. **Audit Trail:** Immutable log archive satisfies forensic & legal requirements.
10. **Continuous Compliance Monitoring:** Real-time policy violation alerts.

### Category D — Intelligence Benefits
11. **Threat Intelligence Integration:** Ingests STIX/TAXII feeds for IOC matching.
12. **Hypothesis-Driven Threat Hunting:** Analysts query historical data using MITRE ATT&CK-aligned searches.
13. **Foundation for CTI Programs:** SIEM data feeds TIPs (Threat Intelligence Platforms) to enrich adversary profiles.

---

## 2.5 Comparison of Leading SIEM Platforms (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Platform | Vendor | Deployment | Key Strength | KTU Exam Hook |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Splunk Enterprise Security** | Cisco (Splunk) | On-prem / Cloud | Industry gold standard, SPL query language | Most tested in KTU |
| **IBM QRadar** | IBM | On-prem / SaaS | Strong offense-oriented correlation | Often asked in PSU exams |
| **Microsoft Sentinel** | Microsoft | Cloud-native (Azure) | Native Azure integration, KQL | High relevance for Azure students |
| **Elastic Security (ELK)** | Elastic | Self-hosted / Cloud | Open-source, low cost | Mentioned for SME use cases |
| **ArcSight ESM** | OpenText | On-prem | Mature, high-throughput correlation | Legacy enterprise context |
| **Google Chronicle** | Google | Cloud-native (SaaS) | Petabyte-scale, unlimited ingestion | Emerging exam topic |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Correlation Logic Derivation

## 3.1 Canonical Example — Detecting a Brute-Force Attack Using SIEM Correlation Logic

This section walks through a **complete, end-to-end analytical derivation** of how a SIEM detects a credential brute-force attack. This is the **most frequently asked descriptive problem** in KTU PECCT755 examinations.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

> An attacker at IP $\mathbf{203.0.113.45}$ launches a brute-force attack against an organization's Active Directory. The attacker attempts **500 username-password combinations** against 8 different user accounts within a **3-minute window**. The SIEM ingests authentication logs from the domain controller.

We are required to:
1. Show how the SIEM ingests and normalizes the events.
2. Demonstrate the correlation rule that triggers an alert.
3. Calculate the EPS the SIEM must handle.
4. Compute the MTTD if the alert fires at the **300th failed login**.

---

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Log Ingestion & Normalization

Each failed authentication on the domain controller generates a **Windows Security Event ID 4625**. Raw events arrive at the SIEM via the **Splunk Universal Forwarder** running on the DC.

The raw event:

```
An account failed to log on.
Subject: Security ID: NULL SID
Account For Which Logon Failed:
  Security ID: NULL SID
  Account Name: administrator
Failure Information:
  Failure Reason: Unknown user name or bad password.
  Status: 0xC000006D
Network Information:
  Workstation Name: WIN-ATTACKER
  Source Network Address: 203.0.113.45
```

After normalization into **CEF format**, the SIEM parses the event into structured fields:

$$
\text{Normalized Event Schema:}
$$

$$
\text{event\_type} = \text{"authentication\_failure"}
$$

$$
\text{src\_ip} = 203.0.113.45
$$

$$
\text{dst\_user} = \text{"administrator"}
$$

$$
\text{dst\_host} = \text{"DC01.example.com"}
$$

$$
\text{event\_id} = 4625
$$

$$
\text{timestamp} = T_i
$$

$$
\text{severity} = 3
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The SIEM parser uses **regex-based field extraction** to map the unstructured raw log text into the structured schema above. This is a one-to-one deterministic mapping — every raw log produces exactly one normalized event.

---

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Correlation Rule Definition

The SIEM admin defines the following correlation rule in **pseudo-SPL (Splunk-like)** syntax:

$$
\text{RULE:}\ \text{BruteForceDetection}
$$

$$
\textbf{IF}\ \text{COUNT}(\text{event\_type} = \text{"authentication\_failure"},\ \text{src\_ip} = \text{"203.0.113.45"})
$$

$$
\geq 5\ \text{within window} = 60\ \text{seconds}
$$

$$
\textbf{AND}\ \text{DISTINCT}(\text{dst\_user}) \geq 3
$$

$$
\textbf{AND}\ \text{THREAT\_INTEL\_REPUTATION}(\text{src\_ip}) = \text{"MALICIOUS"}
$$

$$
\textbf{THEN}\ \text{trigger alert}\ \text{"Brute Force Attack"}\ \text{with severity} = 9
$$

$$
\textbf{AND}\ \text{enrich with GeoIP}(\text{src\_ip}) = \text{"Unknown Hosting Provider, RU"}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The correlation engine groups incoming normalized events by the field `src_ip`, counts them over a **sliding 60-second window**, applies the secondary condition of ≥3 distinct target users, enriches the source IP with threat intel, and finally evaluates the Boolean expression. If true, an alert object is instantiated and pushed to the alert queue.

---

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — EPS Calculation

Assume each failed login generates one authentication event, plus 500 DNS lookups, plus 500 TCP connection events. Total events generated:

$$
E_{\text{total}} = E_{\text{auth}} + E_{\text{DNS}} + E_{\text{TCP}}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
E_{\text{total}} = 500 + 500 + 500 = 1500\ \text{events}
$$

The attack lasted 3 minutes = **180 seconds**. Therefore the average EPS during the attack window is:

$$
\text{EPS} = \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{T_{\text{window}}} = \dfrac{1500}{180} = 8.33\ \text{EPS}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** EPS is the rate, not the absolute count. We divide total events by the duration in seconds to get the per-second ingestion rate. A production SIEM should comfortably handle this — but a **distributed botnet** could generate $10^4$–$10^5$ EPS, which is why **horizontal scaling** of the SIEM is essential.

---

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — MTTD Calculation

The first failed login occurs at:

$$
T_{\text{occur},1} = T_0
$$

The alert fires when the **5th failed login** occurs within 60 seconds (per the rule). Assuming the attacker attempts logins at a uniform rate:

$$
T_{\text{detect}} = T_0 + \dfrac{5\ \text{logins} \times 60\ \text{s}}{500\ \text{logins}} = T_0 + 0.6\ \text{seconds}
$$

Therefore the detection latency is:

$$
L_d = T_{\text{detect}} - T_{\text{occur},1} = 0.6\ \text{seconds}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The attacker is trying 500 logins in 180 seconds, which means the inter-attempt gap is $180/500 = 0.36\ \text{s}$. The 5th attempt therefore occurs at $T_0 + 5 \times 0.36 \approx 1.8\ \text{s}$ — but the SIEM fires as soon as the 5th event lands in the correlation window, giving an MTTD of roughly **1.8 seconds**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Practical Insight:** This example shows that a well-tuned SIEM detects a brute-force attack **within seconds** — far faster than any human analyst reviewing logs. This is the engineering justification for SIEM adoption in any medium-to-large enterprise.

---

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Alert Enrichment & SOC Handoff

Once the alert fires, the SIEM automatically attaches:
- The **5 triggering events** as evidence.
- The **GeoIP context** of the source IP.
- The **threat-intel reputation** (MALICIOUS).
- A **link to a pre-built investigation dashboard**.

A **SOAR playbook** (e.g., Splunk SOAR / Phantom) can then automatically:
1. **Block** the source IP at the perimeter firewall.
2. **Disable** any accounts that were successfully compromised.
3. **Open a ticket** in ServiceNow with full evidence attached.
4. **Notify** the SOC manager via PagerDuty.

This end-to-end automation reduces MTTR from hours to **minutes**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — SIEM vs Competing Platforms

This matrix is a high-yield KTU comparison question (often asked for 7 marks).

| Capability | SIEM | EDR | NDR | SOAR | XDR |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Data Source** | Logs from all sources | Endpoint telemetry | Network traffic | Alert queue | Unified (endpoint + network + cloud) |
| **Detection Method** | Correlation + rules + ML | Signature + behavioral | Flow + packet analysis | N/A (orchestrator) | ML + cross-domain correlation |
| **Response Capability** | Manual or via SOAR | Local containment | None natively | Automated playbooks | Native + automated |
| **Log Retention** | Months to years | Days to weeks | Days to weeks | N/A | Varies |
| **Best For** | Compliance + broad visibility | Endpoint threats | Lateral movement | Response automation | Modern unified SOC |
| **Cost** | High | Medium | Medium | Low–Medium | High |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Conceptual takeaway:** SIEM is the **broadest** platform but often the **noisiest** (high false positives). XDR is the **newest evolution** that natively integrates the three telemetry domains to reduce noise and improve MTTD.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SIEM High-Level Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **end-to-end architectural flow** of a SIEM, showing all six pillars and their interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[Firewalls and IDPS] --> ingest
    src2[Endpoint Agents EDR] --> ingest
    src3[Servers and Apps] --> ingest
    src4[Cloud Audit Logs] --> ingest
    src5[Identity Providers AD OKTA] --> ingest

    ingest[Data Collectors and Forwarders] --> parse[Normalization and Parsing Engine]

    parse --> enrich[Enrichment Layer GeoIP ThreatIntel AssetDB]

    enrich --> store[Storage Tier Hot SSD and Cold S3]

    enrich --> corr[Correlation Engine Rules ML UEBA]

    corr --> alert[Alert Manager Severity Scoring]

    alert --> soc[SOC Analyst Dashboard]

    soc --> soar[SOAR Playbook Engine]

    soar --> act1[Block IP at Firewall]
    soar --> act2[Disable Compromised Account]
    soar --> act3[Isolate Affected Host]

    corr --> hunt[Threat Hunting Workbench]
    hunt --> soc

    store --> report[Compliance Reports PCI HIPAA GDPR]
    report --> auditor[External Auditor]

    classDef pillar fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef action fill:#7a1f1f,stroke:#3b0a0a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef consumer fill:#1f6f3a,stroke:#0a3d1f,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px

    class ingest,parse,enrich,store,corr,alert pillar
    class act1,act2,act3 action
    class soc,soar,hunt,report,auditor consumer
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Blue nodes** = The six architectural pillars of the SIEM.
- **Red nodes** = Automated response actions orchestrated via SOAR.
- **Green nodes** = Consumers (human analysts, external auditors, threat hunters).

---

## 4.2 SIEM Event Processing Pipeline — Detailed Sequence Flow

This sequence diagram illustrates the **temporal interaction** between a log source, the SIEM, the SOC, and the SOAR platform during a brute-force detection.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant DC as Domain Controller
    participant FW as Universal Forwarder
    participant SIEM as SIEM Correlation Engine
    participant TI as Threat Intel Feed
    participant SOC as SOC Analyst
    participant SOAR as SOAR Engine

    DC->>FW: Generate Windows Event ID 4625
    FW->>SIEM: Forward raw syslog / API push
    SIEM->>SIEM: Parse and normalize to CEF schema
    SIEM->>TI: Query reputation of source IP 203.0.113.45
    TI-->>SIEM: Return MALICIOUS reputation score
    SIEM->>SIEM: Enrich event with GeoIP and asset context
    SIEM->>SIEM: Apply correlation rule BruteForceDetection

    Note over SIEM: 5th failed login in 60 second window detected

    SIEM->>SOC: Generate HIGH severity alert with evidence
    SOC->>SIEM: Open case and review alert details
    SOC->>SOAR: Trigger Playbook AutoContainBruteForce
    SOAR->>DC: Disable compromised user accounts
    SOAR->>FW: Push block rule for source IP
    SOAR->>SOC: Notify containment complete with audit log
    SOC->>SIEM: Mark alert resolved with incident notes
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each arrow is a **message exchange** in the SIEM pipeline.
- The **TI lookup** is a synchronous enrichment call.
- The **SOAR playbook** executes three parallel containment actions.
- The final write back to the SIEM closes the audit loop.

---

## 4.3 SIEM vs SOAR vs XDR — Functional Overlap Map

This diagram clarifies how SIEM, SOAR, and XDR relate to each other in a modern SOC stack.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sim[SIM Historical Log Storage]
    sem[SEM Real Time Monitoring]
    siem[SIEM SIM plus SEM]
    edr[EDR Endpoint Detection]
    ndr[NDR Network Detection]
    soar[SOAR Orchestration and Response]
    xdr[XDR Unified EDR plus NDR plus Cloud]

    sim --> siem
    sem --> siem

    edr --> xdr
    ndr --> xdr

    siem --> soar
    xdr --> soar

    classDef core fill:#2c5f8d,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef modern fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#3a0a5e,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef support fill:#5d4037,stroke:#2b1d17,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px

    class siem core
    class xdr modern
    class sim,sem,edr,ndr,soar support
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **SIEM = SIM + SEM** (core foundation).
- **XDR = EDR + NDR + Cloud** (modern unified detection).
- **SOAR sits on top** of either SIEM or XDR to drive automated response.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define SIEM. List any four benefits of a SIEM platform. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) is a cybersecurity solution that combines the historical log management capabilities of SIM with the real-time event monitoring capabilities of SEM to provide centralized threat detection, correlation, alerting, incident response, and compliance reporting.

**Benefits (any four, 0.5 mark each = 2 marks):**
1. Centralized visibility across the entire IT infrastructure.
2. Real-time correlation of multi-source events to detect complex attacks.
3. Reduced MTTD and MTTR through automated alerting and SOAR integration.
4. Regulatory compliance reporting for PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR, and DPDP Act 2023.
5. Forensic investigation support via long-term log retention.
6. Foundation for threat hunting and CTI programs.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between SIM, SEM, and SIEM in two points each. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | SIM | SEM | SIEM |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Focus** | Historical storage and analysis of logs (1 mark) | Real-time monitoring and alerting of live events (1 mark) | Combines both SIM and SEM into one unified platform (1 mark) |
| **Use Case** | Compliance reporting, forensic investigation | Live threat detection, SOC dashboards | End-to-end SOC operations including compliance + detection + response |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **six architectural pillars of a SIEM platform** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **correlation engine** in detecting multi-stage attacks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mid-sized enterprise generates **20,000 events per second** during peak hours and needs to retain logs for **1 year** for PCI-DSS compliance. The average log size is **512 bytes**. Compute the **daily log volume**, the **annual storage requirement**, and the **minimum EPS capacity** the SIEM must support. Assume hot storage costs **USD 0.10/GB/month** and cold storage costs **USD 0.004/GB/month** with a hot:cold ratio of 90 days : 275 days. Estimate the **monthly storage cost**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Six Pillars + Correlation Engine (7 Marks)

**The Six Pillars (1 mark each = 6 marks):**

1. **Data Collection Layer:** Ingests logs from firewalls, IDS/IPS, EDR, servers, applications, cloud workloads, and identity providers via agents, syslog, or APIs.
2. **Normalization & Parsing Layer:** Converts vendor-specific formats into a common schema (CEF / LEEF) so that logs from different sources are comparable.
3. **Storage & Indexing Layer:** Two-tier storage — hot SSD for fast recent queries, cold S3/tape for long-term retention.
4. **Correlation Engine:** The analytical brain that applies rules, statistical models, UEBA, and threat-intel matches to detect multi-stage attacks.
5. **Alerting & Dashboarding Layer:** Generates severity-scored alerts and presents them on SOC dashboards (Tier 1/2/3 queues).
6. **Incident Response & Forensics Layer:** Enables timeline reconstruction, evidence preservation, and integration with SOAR for automated response.

**Role of the Correlation Engine (1 mark):**

The correlation engine is the **SIEM's analytical core**. It aggregates events from multiple sources, applies rule-based logic (e.g., "≥5 failed logins in 60 seconds"), statistical baselining (anomaly detection), and UEBA (behavioral ML) to identify patterns that indicate a **multi-stage attack chain** — such as reconnaissance → initial access → privilege escalation → lateral movement → exfiltration. Without correlation, each individual event would appear benign; correlation reveals the **kill-chain narrative** hidden in fragmented telemetry.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Daily Log Volume (2 marks):**

$$
V_{\text{day}} = \text{EPS} \times 86400 \times \text{Avg Size}
$$

$$
V_{\text{day}} = 20000 \times 86400 \times 512\ \text{bytes}
$$

Converting to GB (1 GB = $10^9$ bytes):

$$
V_{\text{day}} = \dfrac{20000 \times 86400 \times 512}{10^9} = \dfrac{8.847 \times 10^{11}}{10^9} \approx 884.7\ \text{GB/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual Storage Requirement (2 marks):**

$$
V_{\text{year}} = V_{\text{day}} \times 365 = 884.7 \times 365 \approx 322{,}915\ \text{GB} \approx 323\ \text{TB/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Minimum EPS Capacity (1 mark):**

The SIEM must handle at least the **peak EPS**, so:

$$
\text{Min EPS} = 20{,}000\ \text{EPS}
$$

A safety margin of **30%** is recommended for burst absorption:

$$
\text{Recommended EPS} = 20000 \times 1.3 = 26{,}000\ \text{EPS}
$$

**Step 4 — Monthly Storage Cost (2 marks):**

Hot storage volume for 90 days:

$$
V_{\text{hot}} = 884.7 \times 90 = 79{,}623\ \text{GB}
$$

Cold storage volume for 275 days:

$$
V_{\text{cold}} = 884.7 \times 275 = 243{,}292.5\ \text{GB}
$$

Hot cost:

$$
C_{\text{hot}} = 79623 \times 0.10 = \text{USD } 7962.3/\text{month}
$$

Cold cost:

$$
C_{\text{cold}} = 243292.5 \times 0.004 = \text{USD } 973.17/\text{month}
$$

Total monthly cost:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{hot}} + C_{\text{cold}} = 7962.3 + 973.17 \approx \textbf{USD 8935.47/month}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare **SIEM, SOAR, EDR, and XDR** in terms of primary data source, detection method, response capability, and best-fit use case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a real-world example how SIEM correlates events to detect a **brute-force authentication attack**. Include the correlation rule logic, the events involved, and the automated response steps. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Comparison Matrix (7 Marks)

| Capability | SIEM | SOAR | EDR | XDR |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Data Source** | Logs from all sources (firewall, apps, cloud) | Alert queue from SIEM/EDR | Endpoint telemetry (process, file, registry) | Unified across endpoint, network, cloud, identity |
| **Detection Method** | Rule-based + statistical + ML correlation | No native detection; executes playbooks | Signature + behavioral analytics on endpoints | ML-driven cross-domain correlation |
| **Response Capability** | Manual or via SOAR integration | Automated playbooks (block, isolate, notify) | Local endpoint containment | Native + automated cross-domain response |
| **Best-Fit Use Case** | Compliance + broad SOC visibility | Reducing analyst toil via automation | Endpoint-focused threat hunting | Modern SOC seeking unified detection & response |

*(2 marks for correct identification of the four platforms, 4 marks for filling the comparison table with accurate technical details, 1 mark for concluding with a real-world use case example.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Brute-Force Correlation Example (7 Marks)

**Scenario (2 marks):**
An attacker at IP `203.0.113.45` sends **500 failed login attempts** against 8 user accounts on a Windows AD domain controller within **3 minutes**. Each attempt generates a **Windows Security Event ID 4625** on the DC, which is forwarded to the SIEM via a Splunk Universal Forwarder.

**Correlation Rule Logic (2 marks):**

$$
\textbf{IF}\ \text{COUNT}(\text{EventCode} = 4625,\ \text{src\_ip} = \text{"203.0.113.45"},\ \text{window} = 60\ \text{seconds}) \geq 5
$$

$$
\textbf{AND}\ \text{DISTINCT}(\text{target\_user}) \geq 3
$$

$$
\textbf{THEN}\ \text{trigger alert}\ \text{"Brute Force Suspected"}\ \text{severity} = \text{HIGH}
$$

**Automated Response Steps (3 marks):**

1. SIEM enriches the source IP with GeoIP and threat-intel reputation (MALICIOUS).
2. SOAR playbook **"AutoContainBruteForce"** is triggered automatically.
3. Playbook actions:
   - Push **firewall block rule** for `203.0.113.45`.
   - **Disable any accounts** that may have been compromised (check for successful Event ID 4624 from same IP).
   - **Open ServiceNow incident** with all evidence attached.
   - **Notify SOC manager** via PagerDuty / email.
4. Analyst reviews the auto-contained alert, marks it as a true positive, and updates IOC list in TIP for future prevention.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in SIEM Questions (PECCT755):**  
> 1. **Confusing SIM with SIEM** — SIM is only the historical storage component; the full platform is SIEM. Examiners deduct 1 mark if this distinction is blurred.  
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** — EPS must be in *per second*, storage in *GB or TB*, cost in *USD/month*. Always write the unit explicitly.  
> 3. **Writing "SIEM and SOAR are the same"** — This is a frequent misconception. SIEM *detects*, SOAR *responds*. They are complementary, not equivalent.  
> 4. **Skipping the block diagram** — For 7-mark architecture questions, a neat labelled diagram is **mandatory** (1–2 marks are reserved for it).  
> 5. **Not mentioning compliance frameworks** — When listing SIEM benefits, always cite at least one regulation (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR, DPDP Act 2023) for full marks.  
> 6. **Using vague terms like "it improves security"** — Examiners expect *quantified* benefits (e.g., "reduces MTTD from 24 hours to under 1 hour").

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — SIEM Concepts & Benefits**

- **SIEM = SIM + SEM** (Security Information Management + Security Event Management).
- **Six pillars:** Collection → Normalization → Storage → Correlation → Alerting → Response/Forensics.
- **Three primary functions:** (1) Log aggregation, (2) Correlation, (3) Alerting + Reporting.
- **Key metrics:** EPS, MTTD (< 1 hr critical), MTTR (< 4 hrs critical), FPR (< 5%), TPR (> 95%).
- **Correlation techniques:** Rule-based, Statistical, UEBA (ML), Threat-Intel matching.
- **Two-tier storage:** Hot SSD (90 days) + Cold S3/tape (years).
- **Compliance frameworks requiring SIEM:** PCI-DSS, HIPAA, SOX, GDPR, India's DPDP Act 2023.
- **Leading platforms:** Splunk ES, IBM QRadar, Microsoft Sentinel, Elastic Security, Chronicle.
- **Differentiators:** SIEM ≠ SOAR (response), SIEM ≠ EDR (endpoint), SIEM ≠ XDR (unified modern stack).
- **Threat hunting on SIEM:** Query historical data using MITRE ATT&CK-aligned searches (e.g., T1110 — Brute Force).
- **SOAR integration:** Auto-containment playbooks reduce MTTR from hours to minutes.
- **Cost driver:** Storage dominates SIEM TCO; cloud-native SIEMs (Sentinel, Chronicle) reduce this burden.
- **Failure mode:** Poor log coverage is the **#1 reason SIEM deployments fail** — always ingest ≥ 90% of critical asset logs.
- **Future trend:** Cloud-native SIEM + XDR convergence; ML-based UEBA replacing pure rule correlation.
- **KTU one-liner to memorize:** *"SIEM is the SOC's central nervous system — it sees, thinks, alerts, but does not act (without SOAR)."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SIEM Deployment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT755 - CYBER THREAT INTELLIGENCE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** SIEM Deployment # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) Deployment** is the systematic, phased process of architecting, installing, configuring, integrating, tuning, and operationalizing a Security Information and Event Management platform within an enterprise IT environment. It transforms raw, distributed log data from heterogeneous sources into actionable security intelligence, real-time threat detection, and compliance-ready evidence. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECCT755)** syllabus, SIEM deployment encompasses the complete lifecycle from **planning & architecture design → log source onboarding → correlation rule engineering → use-case development → tuning & baselining → 24×7 SOC operations**. The deployment maturity directly determines the Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) and Mean Time to Respond (MTTR) capabilities of the security operations center. > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition - Deployment vs. Implementation** > Students must distinguish between **Implementation** (the technical installation of SIEM software) and **Deployment** (the end-to-end process including people, process, technology, and continuous improvement). KTU examiners expect the broader "deployment" definition. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Hospital ICU Monitor" Analogy for SIEM Deployment** Imagine a massive hospital with thousands of patients (your **endpoints, servers, firewalls, applications**). Each patient is wired to a bedside monitor that records heart rate, blood pressure, oxygen levels, and body temperature (your **logs and events**). Now imagine: - The **Central Monitoring Station (SIEM)** sits in the nurses' hub, aggregating data streams from every monitor in real-time. - **Correlation Rules** are like the trained cardiologists who look at the combined vitals — a single high heart rate might mean nothing, but combined with low oxygen and dropping blood pressure, it signals a critical cardiac event (**multi-stage attack**). - **SOC Analysts** are the on-duty nurses watching the screens, triaging alerts, and calling doctors when needed. - **Tuning & Baselining** is the process of learning each patient's "normal" vitals so that false alarms are minimized. - **SOAR Integration** is calling the rapid response team automatically when an alert fires. **Deploying a SIEM** is therefore like setting up this entire ICU infrastructure — from choosing the right monitors (log collectors), installing cabling (log forwarders), training the watchers (analysts), writing the diagnostic playbooks (correlation rules), and continuously calibrating the thresholds (tuning). Without proper deployment, you have screens showing data but no one able to interpret it. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following industry-standard metrics govern SIEM deployment success and must be memorized: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key SIEM Deployment Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis)** > > - **EPS (Events Per Second)** — The ingestion throughput benchmark; typical enterprise deployments range from **5,000 to 100,000+ EPS** > - **MTTD (Mean Time to Detect)** — Industry benchmark: **< 1 hour** for critical threats > - **MTTR (Mean Time to Respond/Remediate)** — Industry benchmark: **< 4 hours** > - **FPR (False Positive Rate)** — Acceptable target: **< 5%** post-tuning > - **Log Retention Period** — Compliance-mandated: typically **90 days hot, 1 year cold**, or per regulatory requirements > - **Use-Case Coverage** — Percentage of MITRE ATT&CK techniques covered by active correlation rules --- ## 1.4 KTU Syllabus Mapping > [!NOTE] > This topic directly maps to **Course Outcome CO4** of PECCT755: *"Deploy and operationalize SIEM solutions for enterprise threat detection and compliance monitoring."* It also reinforces **CO5** relating to incident response orchestration. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The SIEM Deployment Lifecycle — Structured Logic SIEM deployment is not a single event but a **multi-phase, iterative lifecycle**. Each phase builds upon the outputs of the previous one. ### Phase 1: Requirements Gathering & Planning - **Identify stakeholders**: CISO, SOC Manager, Compliance Officer, IT Operations, Legal. - **Define use cases** aligned to business risks (e.g., insider threat, data exfiltration, ransomware). - **Determine scope**: Number of log sources, geographic distribution, cloud vs. on-premises. - **Compliance drivers**: PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR, RBI Cyber Security Framework — each mandates specific log retention and monitoring requirements. ### Phase 2: Architecture Design - **Topology choice**: Centralized, Distributed (tiered), or Hybrid. - **Sizing calculation**: EPS estimation per log source × number of sources × peak multiplier (typically **2×–3×** average). - **High Availability (HA)**: Active-Active or Active-Passive clustering. - **Network placement**: Tap/SPAN ports, log forwarders (Syslog-ng, NXLog, Winlogbeat), dedicated management network. ### Phase 3: Infrastructure Provisioning - Hardware sizing (CPU, RAM, storage IOPS) or cloud instance selection (AWS, Azure, GCP). - Storage tiering: **Hot** (SSD, fast search), **Warm** (SAS, moderate search), **Cold** (object storage, archival). - Database selection: Relational for alerts/cases, NoSQL/columnar for raw event storage. ### Phase 4: Log Source Onboarding - **Agent-based** (e.g., Splunk Universal Forwarder, Wazuh agent): Installed on the endpoint. - **Agentless** (Syslog, SNMP traps, API polling, WMI): Pulls or receives data remotely. - **Parser development**: Mapping proprietary log formats into a normalized Common Information Model (CIM) or Elastic Common Schema (ECS). ### Phase 5: Correlation Rule Engineering - **Atomic rules**: Single-event detection (e.g., 5 failed logins). - **Sequential/correlation rules**: Multi-event patterns (e.g., failed logins → successful login → privilege escalation). - **Threshold/aggregation rules**: Statistical anomaly detection. - **Threat intelligence integration**: Enrichment with IOC feeds (STIX/TAXII). ### Phase 6: Use-Case Development & Content Creation - Build dashboards tailored to stakeholder personas (Executive, SOC Analyst, Compliance). - Develop runbooks/SOPs for each alert category. - Map every rule to MITRE ATT&CK and compliance controls. ### Phase 7: Tuning & Baselining - **Suppression lists**: Known-good repetitive events (e.g., vulnerability scanner traffic). - **Threshold adjustment**: Based on observed behavioral baselines. - **Whitelisting**: Trusted internal sources for low-risk events. ### Phase 8: Operations & Continuous Improvement - 24×7 SOC monitoring (in-house, MSSP, or hybrid). - Purple team exercises to validate detection coverage. - Quarterly content review and threat intel refresh. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates every formula, sizing rule, and metric a student must know for KTU examinations on this topic. **No `vert` characters used** to preserve table integrity. | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility | |---|---------|---------------|-------------------|---------------------| | 1 | EPS Sizing | $$EPS_{peak} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} EPS_i \times 1.5$$ | $EPS_i$ = avg EPS per source, $n$ = source count, **1.5** = peak safety factor | Hardware sizing & licensing | | 2 | Daily Event Volume | $$V_{day} = EPS_{avg} \times 86400$$ | $V_{day}$ in events/day, $EPS_{avg}$ in events/sec | Storage capacity planning | | 3 | Storage Sizing | $$S = V_{day} \times R \times C_{avg}$$ | $S$ in GB, $R$ = retention days, $C_{avg}$ = avg event size (~500 B) | Disk procurement | | 4 | Compression Ratio | $$CR = \frac{S_{raw}}{S_{compressed}}$$ | Typically **5:1 to 10:1** for SIEM | Reduces storage cost | | 5 | MTTD | $$MTTD = \frac{\sum (T_{detected} - T_{occurred})}{N_{incidents}}$$ | Hours, target **< 1 h** | Detection efficiency KPI | | 6 | MTTR | $$MTTR = \frac{\sum (T_{resolved} - T_{detected})}{N_{incidents}}$$ | Hours, target **< 4 h** | Response efficiency KPI | | 7 | False Positive Rate | $$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$$ | Expressed as percentage | Tuning effectiveness | | 8 | True Positive Rate (Recall) | $$TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$ | Detection coverage | SOC effectiveness | | 9 | Detection Coverage | $$Coverage = \frac{Rules_{mapped}}{ATT\&CK_{techniques}}$$ | Expressed as % of **~200 ATT&CK techniques** | Threat visibility | | 10 | HA Uptime SLA | $$Uptime\% = \frac{T_{total} - T_{down}}{T_{total}} \times 100$$ | Target **99.99%** (four nines) | Business continuity | | 11 | Rule Execution Cost | $Cost = \frac{Events\_Scanned}{EPS_{engine}}$ | Events/sec per correlation engine | Performance tuning | | 12 | Mean Events per Incident | $MEI = \frac{Total\_Events}{Incidents}$ | Used to size alert storage | SOC workload estimation | --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility SIEM deployment is the **operational backbone of any modern Security Operations Center (SOC)**. In banking, deployed SIEMs feed regulatory reports to RBI; in healthcare, they enforce HIPAA audit controls; in e-commerce, they detect account takeover (ATO) and card-not-present (CNP) fraud. A poorly deployed SIEM generates alert fatigue, causing analysts to miss genuine threats — a phenomenon known as **"SIEM Death by Alert Fatigue"**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — EPS Sizing for an Enterprise SIEM > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement (KTU-Style)**: An enterprise has the following log sources: 200 Windows Servers (avg 50 EPS each), 50 Linux Servers (avg 30 EPS each), 10 Firewalls (avg 2000 EPS each), 5 Database Servers (avg 500 EPS each), and 1 Active Directory Domain Controller (avg 3000 EPS). Compute: (a) Total Average EPS, (b) Peak EPS using a safety factor of 1.5, (c) Daily event volume, (d) Storage required for 90 days retention at 500 Bytes/event with 7:1 compression. ### Step 1: Compute Average EPS per Source Category $$ \begin{aligned} EPS_{windows} &= 200 \times 50 = 10{,}000 \text{ EPS} \\ EPS_{linux} &= 50 \times 30 = 1{,}500 \text{ EPS} \\ EPS_{firewalls} &= 10 \times 2000 = 20{,}000 \text{ EPS} \\ EPS_{databases} &= 5 \times 500 = 2{,}500 \text{ EPS} \\ EPS_{AD} &= 1 \times 3000 = 3{,}000 \text{ EPS} \end{aligned} $$ **Explanation:** EPS per category = number of devices × EPS emitted per device. This is straightforward aggregation across homogeneous device classes. ### Step 2: Sum Total Average EPS $$ \begin{aligned} EPS_{avg} &= 10{,}000 + 1{,}500 + 20{,}000 + 2{,}500 + 3{,}000 \\ &= 37{,}000 \text{ events per second} \end{aligned} $$ **Explanation:** Direct summation across categories. The firewall tier dominates because perimeter devices generate the highest log volume. ### Step 3: Compute Peak EPS $$ \begin{aligned} EPS_{peak} &= EPS_{avg} \times 1.5 \\ &= 37{,}000 \times 1.5 \\ &= 55{,}500 \text{ EPS} \end{aligned} $$ **Explanation:** The **1.5× safety factor** absorbs burst events during business hours, logon storms, or attack-driven spikes. Production SIEMs must sustain peak EPS without dropping events. ### Step 4: Compute Daily Event Volume $$ \begin{aligned} V_{day} &= EPS_{avg} \times 86{,}400 \\ &= 37{,}000 \times 86{,}400 \\ &= 3{,}196{,}800{,}000 \text{ events/day} \\ &\approx 3.197 \text{ billion events/day} \end{aligned} $$ **Explanation:** 1 day = 86,400 seconds. This volume is used to size storage and to verify licensing tiers in commercial SIEMs. ### Step 5: Compute Raw Storage for 90 Days $$ \begin{aligned} S_{raw} &= V_{day} \times R \times C_{avg} \\ &= 3.197 \times 10^9 \times 90 \times 500 \text{ bytes} \\ &= 1.4386 \times 10^{14} \text{ bytes} \\ &= 143.86 \text{ TB (raw)} \end{aligned} $$ **Explanation:** 500 Bytes/event is the standard average event size (it accounts for metadata, source IP, user, action). 90 days = typical PCI-DSS-style retention. ### Step 6: Apply 7:1 Compression $$ \begin{aligned} S_{compressed} &= \frac{S_{raw}}{CR} = \frac{143.86 \text{ TB}}{7} \\ &\approx 20.55 \text{ TB} \end{aligned} $$ **Explanation:** SIEM compression algorithms (zstd, LZ4) typically achieve 5:1 to 10:1 ratios for structured logs. ### **Final Answer** > **Average EPS = 37,000**, **Peak EPS = 55,500**, **Daily Volume ≈ 3.197 B events**, **Compressed Storage ≈ 20.55 TB**. --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — MTTD/MTTR & SOC Efficiency > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement**: Over a quarter, a SOC records the following incident timelines (in minutes from compromise): Incident 1 detected at +45, resolved at +180. Incident 2 detected at +120, resolved at +300. Incident 3 detected at +30, resolved at +90. Compute MTTD, MTTR, and identify whether the SOC meets industry benchmarks. ### Step 1: Calculate MTTD $$ \begin{aligned} MTTD &= \frac{(45 + 120 + 30)}{3} \\ &= \frac{195}{3} \\ &= 65 \text{ minutes} \approx 1.08 \text{ hours} \end{aligned} $$ **Explanation:** The average detection latency across all incidents. 65 minutes is **slightly above** the industry benchmark of < 1 hour, indicating a need for better real-time correlation. ### Step 2: Calculate MTTR $$ \begin{aligned} MTTR &= \frac{(180-45) + (300-120) + (90-30)}{3} \\ &= \frac{135 + 180 + 60}{3} \\ &= \frac{375}{3} \\ &= 125 \text{ minutes} \approx 2.08 \text{ hours} \end{aligned} $$ **Explanation:** Response time = resolution time minus detection time, averaged per incident. **125 minutes < 4-hour benchmark**, so response is compliant. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — SIEM Deployment Architectural Options | Criteria | Centralized SIEM | Distributed (Tiered) SIEM | Cloud-Native SIEM | Hybrid SIEM | |----------|------------------|---------------------------|-------------------|-------------| | **Deployment Complexity** | Low | High | Medium | High | | **EPS Scalability** | Limited by single site | Very High | Elastic (auto-scale) | High | | **Latency** | Low (local) | Medium (forwarding delay) | Variable | Medium | | **Cost (CapEx)** | High (hardware) | Very High | Low (OpEx) | Medium-High | | **Maintenance** | Simple | Complex | Vendor-managed | Complex | | **Data Sovereignty** | Full control | Full control | Depends on region | Configurable | | **Best For** | SMB / Single-region | Global MNCs | Cloud-first orgs | Regulated + cloud | | **Examples** | Splunk Enterprise on-prem | QRadar Distributed | Microsoft Sentinel | Splunk Cloud + on-prem | --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Log Collection Methods | Method | Protocol | Pros | Cons | Use Case | |--------|----------|------|------|----------| | **Agent-based** | Proprietary (Forwarder) | Reliable, filtering at source, encrypted | Endpoint overhead | Servers, workstations | | **Syslog** | UDP/TCP 514 | Standard, agentless | UDP loss, no filtering | Network devices, Linux | | **WMI / WinRM** | Microsoft | Native Windows integration | Slow polling, complex | Windows endpoints | | **API / SDK** | HTTPS/REST | Real-time, structured | Rate-limited | Cloud apps (M365, AWS) | | **SNMP Traps** | UDP 162 | Device health | Limited data | UPS, hardware sensors | | **File/DB Tail** | JDBC, file monitor | Legacy support | Latency | Mainframes, legacy DBs | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 SIEM Deployment High-Level Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SRC[Log Source Layer] SRV[Servers
Windows and Linux] FW[Firewalls
Perimeter] NW[Network Devices
Switches and Routers] APP[Applications
Web and DB] CLD[Cloud Tenants
AWS and Azure] EP[Endpoints
User Workstations] end subgraph COL[Collection Layer] AG[Agents
Forwarders] SL[Syslog
Receiver] AP[API
Connectors] DB[DB
Adapters] end subgraph ING[Ingestion and Normalization Layer] NP[Parsers
Log Normalization] CIM[CIM or ECS
Schema Mapper] ENR[Enrichment
GeoIP and Threat Intel] end subgraph COR[Correlation and Analysis Layer] CR[Correlation Rules
Atomic and Sequential] ML[Behavioral Analytics
UEBA Engine] TI[Threat Intel
STIX TAXII Feeds] end subgraph STO[Storage Layer] HT[Hot Tier
SSD Index] WT[Warm Tier
Compressed] CT[Cold Tier
Object Archive] end subgraph OPS[Operations Layer] SOC[SOC Analysts
Tier 1 to 3] DR[Dashboards
KPI Views] RP[Reporting
Compliance] SOAR[SOAR
Automated Response] end SRV --> AG EP --> AG FW --> SL NW --> SL APP --> AP CLD --> AP DB --> DB AG --> NP SL --> NP AP --> NP DB --> NP NP --> CIM CIM --> ENR ENR --> CR ENR --> ML TI --> CR TI --> ML CR --> HT ML --> HT HT --> WT WT --> CT CR --> SOC ML --> SOC SOC --> DR SOC --> RP SOC --> SOAR SOAR --> FW ``` --- ## 4.2 SIEM Deployment Lifecycle State Diagram ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Plan: Phase 1 Requirements Plan --> Design: Phase 2 Architecture Design --> Provision: Phase 3 Infra Setup Provision --> Onboard: Phase 4 Log Sources Onboard --> Correlate: Phase 5 Rule Engineering Correlate --> Usecase: Phase 6 Content Creation Usecase --> Tune: Phase 7 Baselining Tune --> Operate: Phase 8 SOC Go-Live Operate --> Tune: Continuous Tuning Operate --> Audit: Quarterly Review Audit --> Operate Operate --> [*]: Decommission or Upgrade ``` --- ## 4.3 Decision Flow — Choosing Deployment Topology ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: SIEM Deployment Need] --> B{Cloud or On-Prem?} B -->|Fully Cloud| C[Cloud-Native SIEM
Ex: Sentinel, Chronicle] B -->|On-Premises| D{Single or Multi-Site?} B -->|Hybrid| E[Hybrid SIEM
Ex: Splunk Cloud + on-prem indexers] D -->|Single| F[Centralized SIEM] D -->|Multiple| G[Distributed SIEM] C --> H[Validate Compliance
Data Residency] F --> I[Sizing Calculation] G --> I E --> I H --> I I --> J[Sizing Validated] J --> K[Proceed to Provisioning] ``` --- ## 4.4 Use-Case Development Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Threat Scenario] --> B[Map to MITRE ATT&CK] B --> C[Define Data Sources] C --> D[Build Correlation Logic] D --> E[Test in Lab] E --> F{False Positive Rate Acceptable?} F -->|No| G[Refine Threshold] G --> D F -->|Yes| H[Promote to Production] H --> I[Document Runbook] I --> J[Train SOC Analysts] J --> K[Monitor Effectiveness] K --> L[Quarterly Review] L --> A ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define SIEM Deployment. List any four phases of the SIEM deployment lifecycle.** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECCT755, Module 4]** **Model Answer:** > **SIEM Deployment** is the end-to-end process of architecting, installing, configuring, integrating, tuning, and operationalizing a Security Information and Event Management platform to deliver real-time threat detection, log analytics, and compliance reporting. **Four phases of the deployment lifecycle:** 1. **Requirements Gathering & Planning** — stakeholder analysis, use-case definition, compliance mapping. 2. **Architecture Design** — topology, sizing, HA, network placement. 3. **Log Source Onboarding** — agent/agentless collection, parser development, normalization. 4. **Tuning & Continuous Improvement** — baselining, FPR reduction, content refresh. *(Any four from the eight documented phases are accepted.)* --- ### **Q2. Differentiate between agent-based and agentless log collection methods in SIEM deployment.** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECCT755, Module 4]** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Agent-Based | Agentless | |-----------|-------------|-----------| | **Installation** | Software installed on endpoint | Remote polling (Syslog, WMI, API) | | **Reliability** | High — survives network outages | Lower — depends on network availability | | **Filtering** | At source (reduces EPS) | No pre-filtering | | **Encryption** | Built-in TLS | Application-dependent | | **Maintenance** | Agent updates required | None | | **Best for** | Servers, workstations | Network devices, legacy systems | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**: Students frequently lose marks by: (1) confusing *Implementation* with *Deployment*, (2) skipping the sizing math, (3) omitting use-case examples, (4) failing to map answers to MITRE ATT&CK or compliance frameworks, and (5) ignoring the **people-process-technology** triad. Always end with a real-world example. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### **(a) Explain the complete SIEM deployment lifecycle with a detailed block diagram. Highlight the role of correlation rules. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The SIEM deployment lifecycle consists of **eight sequential phases**: 1. **Planning**: Identify stakeholders (CISO, SOC, Compliance, IT Ops), define business-aligned use cases (insider threat, ransomware, data exfiltration), and map regulatory drivers (PCI-DSS, GDPR, RBI). 2. **Architecture Design**: Choose topology (Centralized / Distributed / Hybrid). Compute **EPS** using the formula: $$ EPS_{peak} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} EPS_i \times 1.5 $$ Design HA clustering for **99.99% uptime**. 3. **Infrastructure Provisioning**: Size CPU, RAM, and tiered storage (Hot SSD → Warm SAS → Cold object store). 4. **Log Source Onboarding**: Deploy agents (Universal Forwarder, Wazuh) and configure agentless collectors (Syslog 514, WMI, REST APIs). Develop **parsers** to normalize proprietary formats into CIM/ECS schema. 5. **Correlation Rule Engineering**: Build three rule categories: - **Atomic**: Single-event detection (5 failed logins in 5 minutes). - **Sequential**: Multi-stage pattern (failed logins → successful login → privilege escalation → outbound traffic to suspicious IP). - **Threshold/aggregation**: Statistical anomaly. 6. **Use-Case Development**: Map each rule to **MITRE ATT&CK** techniques (T1110 Brute Force, T1078 Valid Accounts). Build dashboards and runbooks. 7. **Tuning & Baselining**: Apply suppression lists for scanner traffic; adjust thresholds based on observed user/entity behavior baselines (**UEBA**). 8. **Operations & Continuous Improvement**: 24×7 SOC monitoring, purple team exercises, quarterly content refresh. **Role of Correlation Rules**: Correlation is the analytical heart of SIEM. A single failed login is noise; ten failed logins from one IP followed by a successful login and immediate access to a sensitive file is **signal**. Correlation rules apply Boolean logic, time-window aggregation, and threat-intel enrichment to convert low-fidelity events into high-confidence alerts. --- #### **(b) A mid-size enterprise plans to deploy a SIEM with the following log sources: 500 endpoints (20 EPS each), 20 servers (100 EPS each), 5 firewalls (3000 EPS each). Compute: (i) Total Average EPS, (ii) Peak EPS (safety factor 1.5), (iii) Daily event volume, (iv) Storage required for 60-day retention at 500 Bytes/event with 6:1 compression. Comment on the hardware sizing implications. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Total Average EPS:** $$ \begin{aligned} EPS_{endpoints} &= 500 \times 20 = 10{,}000 \\ EPS_{servers} &= 20 \times 100 = 2{,}000 \\ EPS_{firewalls} &= 5 \times 3000 = 15{,}000 \\ EPS_{avg} &= 10{,}000 + 2{,}000 + 15{,}000 = 27{,}000 \text{ EPS} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Peak EPS:** $$ EPS_{peak} = 27{,}000 \times 1.5 = 40{,}500 \text{ EPS} $$ **(iii) Daily Event Volume:** $$ V_{day} = 27{,}000 \times 86{,}400 = 2.3328 \times 10^9 \approx 2.33 \text{ billion events/day} $$ **(iv) Storage Sizing:** $$ \begin{aligned} S_{raw} &= 2.33 \times 10^9 \times 60 \times 500 \text{ bytes} \\ &= 6.99 \times 10^{13} \text{ bytes} \\ &\approx 69.9 \text{ TB raw} \\ S_{compressed} &= \frac{69.9}{6} \approx 11.65 \text{ TB} \end{aligned} $$ **Hardware Sizing Implications:** - A mid-range SIEM appliance (e.g., 2× indexer cluster, 64 vCPU, 256 GB RAM, 20 TB SSD) can sustain ~40K EPS at peak. - For cloud-native SIEM (Microsoft Sentinel), the licensing is per-GB ingested — ~70 GB/day raw ≈ 12 TB compressed across 60 days falls well within standard enterprise tiers. - Recommend **Active-Active HA** with load balancer in front of indexers to avoid single-point-of-failure during peak bursts. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice #### **(a) Discuss the various SIEM deployment topologies (Centralized, Distributed, Cloud-Native, Hybrid) with their architectural diagrams. Mention two industry-leading SIEM products for each. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **1. Centralized Topology:** All log sources send data to a single SIEM cluster located at the headquarters. Pros: simple, low operational overhead. Cons: WAN latency, single point of failure. - *Products*: Splunk Enterprise, IBM QRadar. **2. Distributed (Tiered) Topology:** Regional SIEM collectors forward aggregated/filtered logs to a central correlation tier. Suitable for multi-national enterprises. Pros: scales to millions of EPS; reduces WAN traffic via filtering. Cons: complex, high CapEx. - *Products*: QRadar Distributed, ArcSight ESM. **3. Cloud-Native SIEM:** Software-as-a-Service model hosted by the vendor. Auto-scaling, no infrastructure management. Pros: elastic scaling, low OpEx. Cons: data residency concerns, recurring cost. - *Products*: Microsoft Sentinel, Google Chronicle. **4. Hybrid SIEM:** Combines on-premises collection tier (for sensitive data) with cloud analytics tier. Pros: data sovereignty + cloud scalability. Cons: integration complexity. - *Products*: Splunk Cloud with on-prem forwarders, Elastic SIEM hybrid. Each topology's block diagram should illustrate the source → collector → indexer → search-head/data lake flow, with HA boundaries explicitly shown. --- #### **(b) Explain the concept of SIEM tuning and baselining. Why is it critical in the deployment lifecycle? List any four tuning techniques with examples. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Concept:** - **Baselining**: Establishing a statistical profile of "normal" activity for users, hosts, and network flows over a defined observation window (typically 2–4 weeks). Metrics include login frequency, data transfer volumes, service access patterns. - **Tuning**: The iterative adjustment of correlation rules, thresholds, suppression lists, and enrichment logic to reduce noise and increase detection fidelity. **Why Critical:** A freshly deployed SIEM without tuning generates **alert fatigue** — SOC analysts drown in false positives, leading to missed genuine threats. Tuning reduces the False Positive Rate (FPR) to **< 5%**, ensuring that alerts are actionable and that analysts can focus on true positives. **Four Tuning Techniques:** 1. **Suppression Lists**: Suppress known-good repetitive events. *Example*: Exclude vulnerability scanner IPs from authentication failure alerts. 2. **Threshold Adjustment**: Calibrate thresholds based on observed baselines. *Example*: Change "10 failed logins" to "50 failed logins" if normal user error rate is high. 3. **Whitelisting Trusted Sources**: Bypass low-risk internal automation. *Example*: Exclude backup service accounts from "privilege escalation" alerts. 4. **Enrichment Refinement**: Add context to reduce ambiguity. *Example*: Enrich with asset criticality so alerts on production servers are prioritized over dev/test. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for SIEM Deployment (PECCT755 Module 4)** - ✅ SIEM deployment is an **8-phase lifecycle**: Plan → Design → Provision → Onboard → Correlate → Use-Case → Tune → Operate. - ✅ Always distinguish **Deployment** (end-to-end lifecycle) from **Implementation** (technical install). - ✅ Memorize the **EPS sizing formula**: $EPS_{peak} = \sum EPS_i \times 1.5$. - ✅ **Storage formula**: $S = V_{day} \times R \times C_{avg}$, then divide by compression ratio. - ✅ Standard compression ratio for SIEM logs: **5:1 to 10:1**. - ✅ Industry benchmarks: **MTTD < 1 hour**, **MTTR < 4 hours**, **FPR < 5%**, **HA 99.99%**. - ✅ Four deployment topologies: **Centralized, Distributed, Cloud-Native, Hybrid** — know the trade-offs. - ✅ Three rule types: **Atomic, Sequential, Threshold/Aggregation**. - ✅ Three log collection methods: **Agent-based, Agentless (Syslog/WMI), API**. - ✅ Always map detection rules to **MITRE ATT&CK** techniques and compliance controls (PCI-DSS, GDPR, HIPAA, RBI). - ✅ Tuning is **continuous**, not a one-time activity — perform quarterly reviews. - ✅ SIEM feeds **SOAR** for automated response; integration is a KTU high-yield concept. - ✅ Storage tiering: **Hot (SSD)**, **Warm (compressed)**, **Cold (object archive)**. - ✅ Remember the **people-process-technology** triad — KTU examiners expect mention of SOC analyst training and runbook development. - ✅ For KTU numerical problems, always show **step-by-step EPS → Daily Volume → Storage** calculations with units.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT755 - CYBER THREAT INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** SIEM Solutions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SIEM Solutions — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

**SIEM (Security Information and Event Management)** is a comprehensive, integrated cybersecurity solution that combines two previously distinct disciplines — **Security Information Management (SIM)** (long-term log archival, compliance reporting, and forensic analysis) and **Security Event Management (SEM)** (real-time monitoring, event correlation, and incident response) — into a single unified platform.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECCT755 — Module 4):**
> *A SIEM solution is a centralized security operations platform that ingests, normalizes, indexes, correlates, and stores security-relevant event data (logs, flows, alerts) generated across an organization's entire IT infrastructure (endpoints, networks, applications, cloud workloads) in order to enable real-time threat detection, regulatory compliance, forensic investigation, and incident response.*

SIEM is the technological **backbone of a Security Operations Center (SOC)**. In the modern Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) lifecycle, SIEM acts as the **central nervous system** that consumes raw telemetry, applies intelligence (threat feeds, IOCs, TTPs), and produces actionable alerts.

## Intuitive Overview — The Airport Security Analogy

Imagine a large international airport with thousands of passengers entering daily. Plain log files (raw data) are like a flood of individual ticket scans happening at dozens of gates. By themselves, a single ticket scan tells you almost nothing useful. But if you collect **all scans in one place**, **normalize them** into a common format (boarding pass + passport + boarding time), **correlate** them (this passenger scanned at Gate 1 ten minutes after scanning at Gate 5 — impossible!), and **alert** when anomalies appear, you have a **SIEM**.

- The **airport** = your enterprise IT environment.
- The **ticket scanners** = log sources (firewalls, servers, endpoints, IDS, cloud).
- The **control room** = SIEM correlation engine.
- The **analyst watching screens** = SOC analyst using SIEM dashboards.
- The **threat intel briefing the analyst received this morning** = Threat Intelligence Feeds feeding into the SIEM.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** A SIEM is NOT a single tool that *blocks* attacks (that is the job of firewalls, EDR, IPS). A SIEM's primary purpose is **visibility, correlation, and detection** — it is the platform that lets analysts *see* the attack unfold across silos.

## Standard Metrics & Industry Terminology (Bold per Protocol)

The following standard metrics are universally used to evaluate a SIEM:

- **EPS (Events Per Second)** — the rate at which the SIEM ingests events. Enterprise SIEMs handle **10,000 – 500,000+ EPS**.
- **FPM (Events Per Minute)** — used in smaller deployments; **FPM = EPS × 60**.
- **MTTD (Mean Time To Detect)** — average time between attack occurrence and SIEM alert. **Industry benchmark: < 24 hours**, mature SOCs target **< 1 hour**.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Respond)** — average time from alert to remediation. **Industry benchmark: < 72 hours**, mature SOCs target **< 30 minutes**.
- **Log Retention Period** — typical values are **90 days (hot storage)**, **1 year (warm)**, **7 years (cold/archival)**.
- **FAR (False Alert Rate) / FPR (False Positive Rate)** — percentage of alerts that are not real incidents. Mature SIEM tuning aims for **< 5%**.

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Functional Pillars of a SIEM

A modern SIEM is built on **six functional pillars**. KTU questions frequently ask students to enumerate and explain these.

### Pillar 1 — Data Collection (Log Acquisition)
SIEMs collect data using:
- **Agent-based collection** — a lightweight software agent installed on the source host (e.g., Splunk Universal Forwarder, Wazuh agent, OSSEC agent). Captures OS-level events, registry changes, file integrity events.
- **Agentless collection** — uses protocols like **Syslog (UDP/TCP 514)**, **SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol)**, **NetFlow (v5/v9)**, **sFlow**, **Windows Event Forwarding (WEF)**, **API polling** (e.g., pulling logs from cloud SaaS like Microsoft 365, AWS CloudTrail).
- **TAP/SPAN ports** — for full packet capture (PCAP) ingestion for network forensic analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Log Source Coverage Rule of Thumb:** A mature SIEM integration should cover at minimum: **firewalls, IDS/IPS, EDR/antivirus, authentication systems (AD/LDAP), web proxies, DNS servers, DHCP servers, mail gateways, database servers, cloud control plane, application logs, and physical security systems**.

### Pillar 2 — Log Normalization & Parsing
Raw logs come in vendor-specific formats (Cisco ASA, Palo Alto, Windows Event Log, Linux syslog, JSON, CEF, LEEF). The SIEM uses **parsers** to extract key fields into a **common schema** (typically: `timestamp`, `source_ip`, `destination_ip`, `username`, `event_id`, `action`, `severity`, `raw_event`).

> Common normalization standards: **CEF (Common Event Format)** by ArcSight, **LEEF (Log Event Extended Format)** by IBM QRadar, **ODBC normalization** in Splunk, **ECS (Elastic Common Schema)** in Elastic SIEM.

### Pillar 3 — Log Enrichment
Enrichment augments raw events with **contextual intelligence**:
- **GeoIP enrichment** — maps IPs to geographic location.
- **Threat intelligence enrichment** — matches IOCs (IPs, domains, file hashes) against feeds (AlienVault OTX, MISP, Recorded Future, STIX/TAXII).
- **Asset context** — attaches criticality, owner, business unit, network zone to source/destination IPs.
- **User context** — attaches department, role, privileged status to usernames.
- **Vulnerability context** — cross-references destination IPs against vulnerability scanner (Nessus, Qualys) output.

### Pillar 4 — Correlation Engine
The **brain of the SIEM**. Correlation rules analyze events across multiple sources and time windows to identify multi-step attacks that would be invisible in any single log source.

**Correlation Rule Types:**

| Rule Type | Description | Example |
|-----------|-------------|---------|
| **Threshold-based** | Fires when N events occur within T seconds | > 5 failed logins in 60 sec |
| **Statistical / Behavioral** | Fires on deviation from baseline | User downloads 50 GB at 3 AM (baseline: 100 MB) |
| **Pattern / Signature** | Fires on known attack pattern | "WannaCry" SMB exploit signature detected |
| **Contextual / Cross-source** | Correlates across multiple log types | VPN login from country X + immediate privileged AD change |
| **Sequential / Chain** | Event A followed by Event B followed by Event C | Phishing email → user click → malware download → C2 beacon |
| **Risk-based (UEBA)** | Aggregates low-risk events to a critical risk score | 10 small anomalies on a user account sum to a high-risk alert |

### Pillar 5 — Alerting, Dashboards & Reporting
The SIEM generates **alerts**, **incidents**, and **cases**. Analysts view these through:
- **Real-time dashboards** (KPI tiles, geo-maps, heatmaps).
- **Incident queues** (prioritized by severity/criticality).
- **Compliance reports** (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR, ISO 27001, SOX, NIST 800-53) — auto-generated.

### Pillar 6 — Long-term Storage & Forensics
SIEMs tier storage for cost and performance:
- **Hot storage** (SSD, < 30 days) — fast search, real-time correlation.
- **Warm storage** (HDD, 30–365 days) — slower but searchable.
- **Cold storage** (object storage like AWS S3, 1–7+ years) — archival, restored on-demand for forensic investigations.

## SIEM vs SOAR vs EDR vs XDR — Critical Distinction

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners **love** asking comparison questions. Memorize the differences below.

| Capability | **SIEM** | **SOAR** | **EDR** | **XDR** |
|------------|----------|----------|---------|---------|
| Primary Function | Log aggregation, correlation, detection | Orchestration, automation, response | Endpoint threat detection/response | Cross-domain detection/response (endpoint + network + cloud + identity) |
| Data Source | Logs from everywhere | SIEM alerts + ITSM | Endpoints only | Endpoints, network, cloud, identity (correlated natively) |
| Response Action | Manual + ticket creation | **Automated** (playbooks) | Containment on endpoint | Native response across domains |
| Key Vendors | Splunk, QRadar, ArcSight, Sentinel, Elastic | Cortex XSOAR, Splunk SOAR, Swimlane | CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Defender for Endpoint | CrowdXDR, Microsoft Defender XDR, Palo Alto Cortex XDR |
| Replaces SIEM? | — | No, complements it | No, complements it | Partially, can reduce SIEM dependency |

## SIEM Architecture — Core Components

```
[Log Sources] → [Collectors/Fwd] → [Parsers/Normalizers] → [Enrichment] → [Correlation Engine] → [Indexing/Storage] → [Dashboards/Alerts] → [SOC Analyst]
```

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric / Formula | Equation | Typical Range / Unit |
|------------------|----------|----------------------|
| Event Volume (daily) | $E_{daily} = EPS \times 86400$ | Enterprise: $\approx 8.64 \times 10^{9}$ events/day at 100k EPS |
| Storage per day (uncompressed) | $S_{day} = E_{daily} \times \bar{L}$ | Where $\bar{L}$ = avg log size in bytes (~500 B) |
| Annual Storage | $S_{year} = S_{day} \times 365 \times c_{comp}$ | Compression $c_{comp} \approx 0.1$–$0.3$ |
| True Positive Rate | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Target $> 0.90$ |
| False Positive Rate | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Target $< 0.05$ |
| F1 Score (detection quality) | $F_1 = 2 \times \dfrac{precision \times recall}{precision + recall}$ | Range 0–1, higher is better |
| MTTD Reduction Goal | $MTTD_{goal} \le 24 \text{ hours}$ | Mature SOC: $< 1$ hour |
| MTTR Reduction Goal | $MTTR_{goal} \le 72 \text{ hours}$ | Mature SOC: $< 30$ min |
| Risk Score (UEBA) | $R = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i$ | Where $w_i$ = weight, $s_i$ = anomaly score |

> **Note on units:** EPS is dimensionless count per second; storage in bytes (B), kilobytes (KB), megabytes (MB), gigabytes (GB), terabytes (TB), or petabytes (PB).

## Real-World Engineering Utility

In a live SOC, SIEM is the **single pane of glass** that lets a Tier-1 analyst see — within seconds — that "User *raj.k* failed to log in 47 times from 14 different countries in 10 minutes, which triggered a UEBA anomaly, which crossed our correlation threshold for **Impossible Travel**, which we have associated with **T1078 (Valid Accounts)** in the MITRE ATT&CK framework." Without SIEM, the analyst would be drowning in 50+ disconnected log sources.
