# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Worked Example 1 — Event Volume and Storage Calculation

**Problem:** A mid-sized enterprise SIEM ingests logs at an average rate of **35,000 EPS**. Each event, after normalization and enrichment, consumes approximately **600 bytes** of storage. Calculate:
(a) Total events per day.
(b) Daily storage requirement in GB.
(c) Annual storage requirement in TB (assume compression ratio of 0.25).

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**(a) Events per day:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{daily} &= EPS \times 86{,}400 \text{ seconds/day} \\
E_{daily} &= 35{,}000 \times 86{,}400 \\
E_{daily} &= 3{,}024{,}000{,}000 \text{ events/day} \\
E_{daily} &= 3.024 \times 10^{9} \text{ events/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

Conversion logic: There are 24 hours × 60 minutes × 60 seconds = 86,400 seconds in one day. Multiplying the per-second ingestion rate by the number of seconds in a day gives the daily event volume.

**(b) Daily storage in GB:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{day,\text{bytes}} &= E_{daily} \times \bar{L} \\
S_{day,\text{bytes}} &= 3.024 \times 10^{9} \times 600 \\
S_{day,\text{bytes}} &= 1.8144 \times 10^{12} \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

Conversion logic: Total bytes = event count × average bytes per event.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{day,\text{GB}} &= \frac{S_{day,\text{bytes}}}{1024^3} \\
S_{day,\text{GB}} &= \frac{1.8144 \times 10^{12}}{1.07374 \times 10^{9}} \\
S_{day,\text{GB}} &\approx 1{,}689.8 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

Conversion logic: 1 GB = 1024³ bytes (binary). Dividing yields approximately **1.69 TB/day** of raw storage.

**(c) Annual storage in TB (with compression 0.25):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{year,\text{GB}} &= S_{day,\text{GB}} \times 365 \times c_{comp} \\
S_{year,\text{GB}} &= 1{,}689.8 \times 365 \times 0.25 \\
S_{year,\text{GB}} &= 154{,}194.85 \text{ GB} \\
S_{year,\text{TB}} &= \frac{154{,}194.85}{1024} \approx 150.58 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The enterprise requires approximately **150.6 TB of compressed annual SIEM storage**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in real SOC budgeting:** Storage costs are the single largest line item in a SIEM budget. An engineer sizing an enterprise SIEM *must* perform this calculation to justify hot/warm/cold tiered storage architecture.

## Worked Example 2 — Detection Quality (Precision, Recall, F1)

**Problem:** A SIEM correlation rule produced the following results over a one-month evaluation period:
- True Positives (TP) = 142 (real attacks correctly flagged)
- False Positives (FP) = 38 (benign events flagged as attacks)
- False Negatives (FN) = 11 (real attacks missed)
- True Negatives (TN) = 9,82,809 (benign events correctly ignored)

Calculate: Precision, Recall (TPR), FPR, and F1 Score.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Precision (Positive Predictive Value):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \\
P &= \frac{142}{142 + 38} = \frac{142}{180} \\
P &= 0.7889 \approx 78.89\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Conversion logic: Precision measures *of all alerts fired, how many were real attacks*. Total alerts = TP + FP = 180.

**Recall / True Positive Rate (TPR):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \\
R &= \frac{142}{142 + 11} = \frac{142}{153} \\
R &= 0.9281 \approx 92.81\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Conversion logic: Recall measures *of all real attacks, how many did we catch*. Total real attacks = TP + FN = 153.

**False Positive Rate (FPR):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FPR &= \frac{FP}{FP + TN} \\
FPR &= \frac{38}{38 + 982{,}809} = \frac{38}{982{,}847} \\
FPR &= 3.866 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0.00387\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Conversion logic: FPR measures *of all benign events, how many were incorrectly flagged*. This is a very small FPR — the rule is well-tuned.

**F1 Score (harmonic mean of precision and recall):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= 2 \times \frac{P \times R}{P + R} \\
F_1 &= 2 \times \frac{0.7889 \times 0.9281}{0.7889 + 0.9281} \\
F_1 &= 2 \times \frac{0.7323}{1.7170} \\
F_1 &= 2 \times 0.4265 \\
F_1 &= 0.853 \approx 85.3\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A strong F1 of **0.853** indicates the correlation rule is well-balanced — high recall catches most real attacks while acceptable precision keeps analyst alert fatigue manageable.

## Worked Example 3 — UEBA Risk Score Aggregation

**Problem:** A user *amrita.s* triggers the following anomalies in a 1-hour window. Each anomaly has a score (0–10) and a weight (0–1) defined by the SOC's risk model:

| Anomaly | Score $s_i$ | Weight $w_i$ |
|---------|------------|--------------|
| Unusual login time | 4 | 0.3 |
| Geo-impossible travel | 9 | 0.7 |
| Mass file download | 6 | 0.5 |
| Privileged group addition | 8 | 0.9 |

Compute the aggregated risk score $R = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i$.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= (0.3 \times 4) + (0.7 \times 9) + (0.5 \times 6) + (0.9 \times 8) \\
R &= 1.2 + 6.3 + 3.0 + 7.2 \\
R &= 17.7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** With a SOC threshold of **15.0** for "Critical" alerts, *amrita.s* triggers a **Critical** incident. The system will page the on-call analyst and auto-create a SOAR playbook to disable the account pending review.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold tuning principle:** Thresholds are set based on *baseline user behavior over 30+ days*. They are NOT static — they are continuously refined using the FP/FN feedback loop from analyst triage.

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define SIEM. List any FOUR core functional components of a SIEM solution.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**SIEM (Security Information and Event Management)** is an integrated cybersecurity platform that centralizes the collection, normalization, correlation, and analysis of security log data from across an organization's IT infrastructure to enable real-time threat detection, compliance reporting, and incident response. **(1 mark)**

**Four core functional components:**
1. **Log Collection / Data Acquisition** — agents, syslog forwarders, API connectors that ingest events from heterogeneous sources. **(0.5 mark)**
2. **Log Normalization and Parsing** — converts vendor-specific formats into a common schema (CEF, LEEF, ECS). **(0.5 mark)**
3. **Correlation Engine** — applies rules, ML/UEBA, and behavioral analytics to detect multi-stage attacks. **(0.5 mark)**
4. **Alerting, Dashboarding & Reporting** — produces real-time alerts, SOC dashboards, and compliance reports. **(0.5 mark)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | **SIEM** | **SOAR** | Marks |
|-----------|----------|----------|-------|
| (i) Primary Purpose | Real-time **detection, correlation, log management** | **Orchestration, automation, response** | 1.0 |
| (ii) Data Source | Logs from all sources (firewall, server, cloud, EDR) | Alerts from SIEM + ITSM tickets | 1.0 |
| (iii) Automation | Limited (alert creation, ticket generation) | **Extensive (playbooks, auto-containment, auto-remediation)** | 1.0 |

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern:** In the End Semester Examination (ESE), Module 4 contains one 14-mark question with internal choice (either-or). Each question has two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q3A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **six functional pillars of a SIEM architecture**, with suitable examples for each pillar. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **end-to-end flow of a log event** in a SIEM — from its generation at the source host to its appearance as an alert on the SOC analyst's dashboard. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Six Functional Pillars (7 marks):**

1. **Data Collection (1.5 marks):**
   - *Agent-based* — Splunk Universal Forwarder on a Windows server collects Event Logs and ships them to the indexer.
   - *Agentless* — Syslog (UDP 514) from Cisco ASA firewall; API polling of AWS CloudTrail.
   - *Network* — NetFlow v9 from router, PCAP from SPAN port.

2. **Log Normalization & Parsing (1 mark):**
   - Example: Raw Cisco ASA log "`<166>ASA-6-302013: Built outbound TCP connection 12345 for outside:8.8.8.8/443 to inside:192.168.1.10/50123`" is parsed into fields: `timestamp`, `action=Built`, `protocol=TCP`, `src_ip=192.168.1.10`, `dst_ip=8.8.8.8`, `dst_port=443`.

3. **Enrichment (1 mark):**
   - GeoIP lookup on `8.8.8.8` → `Mountain View, USA`.
   - Asset DB lookup on `192.168.1.10` → `Asset: Finance-DB-Server, Criticality: High, Owner: Finance Team`.
   - Threat-intel lookup on `8.8.8.8` against AlienVault OTX → `No match`.

4. **Correlation Engine (1.5 marks):**
   - Example: A *threshold-based rule* "≥ 5 failed SSH logins on a single host within 60 seconds, originating from the same source IP, followed by a successful login" fires a *Brute Force Success* alert.

5. **Alerting, Dashboards & Reporting (1 mark):**
   - Real-time alert in incident queue with severity = High.
   - Compliance dashboard shows PCI-DSS Requirement 10 (logging) green.

6. **Long-term Storage & Forensics (1 mark):**
   - 0–30 days: Hot SSD index — full search, sub-second response.
   - 30–365 days: Warm HDD — slightly slower.
   - 1–7 years: Cold S3 archive — restored on-demand for forensic case.

**(b) End-to-End Log Event Flow (7 marks):**

Refer to **Diagram 1 (Section 4)** of these notes. Step-by-step written answer:

1. **Event Generation (1 mark)** — A user `raj.k` types wrong password 6 times. Windows Security Log records Event ID `4625 (Logon Failure)` on Domain Controller DC01.

2. **Collection (1 mark)** — The Splunk Universal Forwarder (UF) installed on DC01 reads the Security Log in real time and forwards it via TCP/9997 to the Splunk Indexer.

3. **Parsing & Normalization (1 mark)** — Splunk's `props.conf` and `transforms.conf` parse the Windows Event XML and extract `EventCode=4625`, `user=raj.k`, `src_ip=10.1.5.22`, `timestamp=2024-09-15T10:23:11Z`.

4. **Indexing & Storage (1 mark)** — The normalized event is written to a Splunk bucket (hot/warm/cold) and indexed for search.

5. **Enrichment (1 mark)** — The Splunk Search Head enriches with GeoIP (10.1.5.22 → Kerala, India), AD user details (raj.k → Intern, dept=Marketing), and asset context (10.1.5.22 → intern-laptop, low criticality).

6. **Correlation (1 mark)** — The saved search `Brute Force Detection` runs every 1 minute. The 6th failure in 60 sec triggers the rule. Risk score: 75 (High). Alert fires.

7. **Alert & Response (1 mark)** — Alert appears in SOC queue. SOAR (Splunk SOAR) auto-creates a ticket in ServiceNow, notifies Tier-1 analyst on Slack, and runs playbook "Notify User of Suspicious Login" (sends email to raj.k).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Q3B. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **five major SIEM solutions** (any five: Splunk, QRadar, Microsoft Sentinel, Elastic SIEM, LogRhythm, ArcSight) on the basis of deployment model, query language, AI/ML capability, pricing, and best-suited use case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A SOC team is evaluating whether to use an **on-premise SIEM** versus a **cloud-native SIEM**. List the key factors the team must consider, and provide a justified recommendation for a startup with 150 employees and 80% cloud workload. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison of Five SIEM Solutions (7 marks):**

| SIEM | Deployment | Query Lang | AI/ML | Pricing | Best For | Marks |
|------|------------|------------|-------|---------|----------|-------|
| **Splunk** | On-prem / Cloud | SPL | MLTK, UBA, AI Assistant | Per-GB ($$$$) | Large enterprises, flexible analytics | 1.5 |
| **IBM QRadar** | On-prem / Cloud | AQL | QRadar Advisor (Watson) | Per-EPS ($$$$) | Regulated banks, finance | 1.5 |
| **Microsoft Sentinel** | Cloud-native (Azure) | KQL | Defender Copilot, Fusion ML | Pay-per-GB ($$) | Microsoft-heavy / Azure orgs | 1.0 |
| **Elastic SIEM** | Self-hosted / Cloud | Lucene / EQL | Elastic ML, Anomaly Detection | Free / Per-GB ($) | DevOps, cost-sensitive, open-source fans | 1.5 |
| **LogRhythm** | On-prem / Cloud | Basic | UEBA | Per-sensor ($$$) | Mid-market, fast deployment | 1.5 |

**(b) On-Premise vs Cloud-Native SIEM — Justified Recommendation (7 marks):**

**Key Decision Factors (4 marks):**

1. **Capital vs Operating Expenditure (1 mark)** — On-prem SIEMs (Splunk Enterprise, QRadar) require huge upfront CapEx (servers, licenses, storage). Cloud-native SIEMs (Microsoft Sentinel, Elastic Cloud, Sumo Logic) are pure OpEx subscription.

2. **Scalability and Elasticity (1 mark)** — Cloud-native SIEMs scale EPS on-demand; on-prem requires hardware procurement cycles of weeks/months.

3. **Compliance and Data Residency (0.5 mark)** — RBI, DPDP Act 2023 (India), or sectoral regulators may mandate data to remain within Indian borders — this favors specific cloud regions or on-prem.

4. **In-House Expertise (0.5 mark)** — On-prem SIEMs need dedicated SIEM engineers; cloud-native reduces operational burden.

5. **Time-to-Value (0.5 mark)** — Cloud-native SIEMs are operational in hours/days; on-prem takes months.

6. **Integration with Existing Stack (0.5 mark)** — Azure-native orgs benefit massively from Sentinel's auto-connectors to Azure AD, M365, Defender.

**Recommendation (3 marks):**

For a **150-employee startup with 80% cloud workload (AWS/Azure)**, the justified recommendation is a **cloud-native SIEM**, specifically **Microsoft Sentinel** (if Azure) or **Elastic SIEM Cloud** (if AWS/multi-cloud). Justification:
- The startup lacks the CapEx and 24×7 SIEM engineering staff required for an on-prem deployment. **(1 mark)**
- 80% cloud workload means logs are already in the cloud — moving them to an on-prem SIEM adds egress cost, latency, and complexity. **(1 mark)**
- Cloud-native SIEM scales with the startup's growth from 150 to 1,500 employees without hardware procurement. Pay-per-GB aligns cost with usage. **(1 mark)**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in SIEM Questions:**
> 1. **Writing "SIEM prevents attacks."** ❌ — SIEM primarily *detects* and *alerts*. Prevention is the job of firewalls, EDR, IPS. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 2. **Confusing SIEM with SOAR.** Examiners *will* deduct marks if you call SOAR a "log collector" or call SIEM an "automation tool."
> 3. **Omitting normalization.** A question that says "explain SIEM architecture" without mentioning *parsing/normalization* loses 1–2 marks — it is one of the **six pillars** for a reason.
> 4. **Not mentioning compliance reporting** when asked about SIEM benefits. PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR, SOX, ISO 27001 compliance is a *major* SIEM use case in KTU answer key expectations.
> 5. **Skipping the log retention tiering (hot/warm/cold)** in a "storage" question — full 2-mark deduction.
> 6. **Forgetting units** in numerical problems (EPS, GB, TB, seconds). Always write the unit.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **SIEM = SIM (info/archival) + SEM (real-time events)**. It is the **central nervous system of the SOC**.
- **Six pillars:** Collect → Normalize → Enrich → Correlate → Alert/Report → Store. Memorize this order.
- **Three collection methods:** Agent-based, Agentless (Syslog/SNMP/API), Network (NetFlow/PCAP).
- **Three normalization standards:** CEF (ArcSight), LEEF (QRadar), ECS (Elastic).
- **Six correlation rule types:** Threshold, Statistical/Behavioral, Pattern/Signature, Contextual/Cross-source, Sequential/Chain, Risk-based (UEBA).
- **SIEM ≠ SOAR ≠ EDR ≠ XDR.** SIEM detects, SOAR automates, EDR protects endpoints, XDR correlates natively across domains.
- **Key metrics:** EPS, MTTD (< 24h), MTTR (< 72h), FPR (< 5%), Precision/Recall/F1.
- **Storage tiers:** Hot (0–30 d, SSD) → Warm (30–365 d, HDD) → Cold (1–7+ y, S3).
- **Top SIEMs to remember:** Splunk (SPL), QRadar (AQL), Microsoft Sentinel (KQL, cloud-native), Elastic SIEM (Lucene/EQL, open-source), LogRhythm, ArcSight.
- **Trend (2024–2026):** SIEM + SOAR + AI (LLM assistants like Microsoft Security Copilot, Splunk AI Assistant) → autonomous SOC.
- **Cloud-native SIEMs** (Sentinel, Sumo Logic, Elastic Cloud) are **OpEx/subscription**; on-prem SIEMs are **CapEx + heavy maintenance**.
- **Formula essentials:** $E_{daily} = EPS \times 86{,}400$; $F_1 = 2PR/(P+R)$; $R = \sum w_i s_i$.
- **Compliance is a *first-class* SIEM use case:** PCI-DSS Req 10, HIPAA §164.312(b), ISO 27001 A.12.4, GDPR Art. 32, NIST SP 800-53 AU family.
- **Modern SIEMs are threat-intel-aware:** they consume STIX/TAXII feeds and IOCs to enrich and prioritize alerts.
- **MITRE ATT&CK mapping** is now a *standard SIEM feature* — every alert should ideally be tagged with a TTP (e.g., T1078, T1110).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vendors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT755 - CYBER THREAT INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vendors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of CTI Vendors

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI), a **Threat Intelligence Vendor** is a commercial, governmental, or community-driven organization that systematically collects, processes, analyzes, and disseminates actionable threat data, indicators of compromise (IoCs), tactical/operational/strategic intelligence, and contextualized threat reports to subscribing consumer organizations to enhance their defensive posture, decision-making, and risk management capabilities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:**
> A CTI vendor is an external entity — commercial (e.g., Mandiant, CrowdStrike, Recorded Future), open-source (e.g., MISP Project, OTX by AlienVault), governmental (e.g., NCIIPC, CISA, NCSC), or community-driven (e.g., ISACs, FS-ISAC) — that delivers curated, timely, and relevant threat intelligence to client organizations through feeds, APIs, platforms, or advisory services.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **city's fire department**. The fire department doesn't wait for fires to happen — they study past fires, monitor weather conditions, identify risky buildings, share warnings with businesses, and even sell specialized fire-prevention services.

A **CTI vendor operates exactly like that for the cyber world**:

- Instead of fire, they study **cyber attacks**.
- Instead of weather, they monitor the **dark web, hacker forums, malware samples, and global breach data**.
- Instead of risky buildings, they identify **vulnerable systems, exposed credentials, and trending attacker tactics**.
- Instead of fire alarms, they deliver **threat alerts, IoC feeds, and finished intelligence reports**.

You can either **build your own fire-monitoring team (in-house CTI)** or **subscribe to a professional service (CTI vendor)**. Most companies use a mix of both.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Vendors Exist:** Not every organization has the budget, talent, language skills, dark-web access, malware reverse-engineering labs, and global telemetry required to produce world-class threat intelligence. Vendors amortize these costs across thousands of subscribers.

## 1.3 Primary Categories of CTI Vendors

| # | Vendor Type | Description | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Commercial / Enterprise Vendors** | Paid subscription services with proprietary telemetry, analysts, and platforms. | Mandiant (Google Cloud), CrowdStrike, Recorded Future, Palo Alto Unit 42, FireEye |
| 2 | **Open-Source / Free Vendors** | Free platforms and feeds maintained by companies or the community. | AlienVault OTX, Abuse.ch, VirusTotal, MISP Project, MITRE ATT&CK |
| 3 | **Governmental / National CERTs** | State-sponsored intelligence bodies protecting critical infrastructure. | CISA (USA), NCSC (UK), CERT-In (India), BSI (Germany) |
| 4 | **Industry ISACs / Information Sharing Communities** | Sector-specific non-profit intelligence sharing groups. | FS-ISAC (Finance), H-ISAC (Healthcare), Auto-ISAC, E-ISAC (Energy) |
| 5 | **Vendor-Agnostic Aggregators** | Platforms that normalize and consolidate feeds from multiple vendors. | ThreatConnect, Anomali, ThreatQuotient, EclecticIQ |
| 6 | **Specialized / Boutique Vendors** | Niche providers focused on a specific domain (e.g., fraud, brand protection, dark web). | Recorded Future (Insikt Group), Flashpoint, Intel 471, KELA |

## 1.4 What Vendors Actually Sell — The Intelligence Pyramid

Vendors deliver intelligence at different levels of the **Intelligence Pyramid (also called the "Pyramid of Pain" reversed, or "Intelligence Readiness")**:

$$
\text{Raw Data} \rightarrow \text{Information} \rightarrow \text{Indicators (IoCs)} \rightarrow \text{Tactical Intel} \rightarrow \text{Operational Intel} \rightarrow \text{Strategic Intel}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Vendors typically differentiate themselves by the **depth of the pyramid** they serve. SIEM/EDR vendors (e.g., CrowdStrike) often deliver **tactical** intelligence, while boutique firms (e.g., Mandiant, Insikt Group) deliver **strategic** intelligence about nation-state actors.

## 1.5 Common Terminology a KTU Student Must Know

- **IoC (Indicator of Compromise):** Atomic artifacts like IPs, domains, file hashes, mutexes.
- **IoA (Indicator of Attack):** Behavioral patterns showing attacker intent.
- **TTP (Tactics, Techniques, Procedures):** Higher-order behavioral signatures mapped to MITRE ATT&CK.
- **STIX / TAXII:** Standardized formats (Structured Threat Information eXpression / Trusted Automated eXchange of Intelligence Information) used by vendors to deliver data.
- **MISP:** Malware Information Sharing Platform — an open-source format used by many vendors.
- **Feed:** A continuous stream of threat data delivered via API, email, or platform.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Vendor Ecosystem — Operational Layers

A modern CTI vendor ecosystem operates across **four distinct operational layers**. Understanding these layers is critical for the KTU examination.

### Layer 1 — Data Collection (The "Where")
Vendors gather raw telemetry from heterogeneous sources:

- **Honeypots & Sandboxes** (e.g., catching live malware execution)
- **Dark Web & Deep Web Crawlers** (forums, marketplaces, Telegram channels)
- **Open-Source Scraping** (paste sites, GitHub leaks, social media)
- **Commercial Partnerships** (ISPs, CDN providers, cloud logs)
- **Customer Telemetry** (opt-in data from EDR/XDR deployments like CrowdStrike's Falcon)
- **HUMINT** (human intelligence — embedded researchers with linguistic skills)

### Layer 2 — Processing & Normalization
Raw data is normalized into standardized schemas:

- **STIX 2.1** (industry standard)
- **MISP Core Format**
- **OpenIOC** (legacy Mandiant format)
- **YARA rules** (for malware pattern matching)

### Layer 3 — Analysis & Enrichment
Analysts (often with nation-state or military backgrounds) contextualize data:

- Attributing attacks to **threat actors** (APT groups, cybercrime syndicates, hacktivists).
- Mapping behaviors to **MITRE ATT&CK** techniques.
- Producing **finished intelligence reports** for executives and SOC teams.

### Layer 4 — Delivery & Integration
Vendors deliver intelligence through:

- **APIs** (REST, STIX/TAXII servers)
- **Platform UIs** (web dashboards, threat intelligence platforms — TIPs)
- **SIEM/SOAR Integrations** (Splunk, QRadar, Sentinel, ServiceNow)
- **Email Digests & Advisories** (for executives and IT teams)
- **Mobile Apps & Slack/Teams bots** (for real-time alerting)

## 2.2 Vendor Selection Criteria — The KTU Evaluation Matrix

When an organization evaluates a CTI vendor, it applies the following **eight-criterion model**:

| # | Criterion | What to Evaluate | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Coverage** | Threat actor coverage, geographic visibility, sector focus | Determines blind spots |
| 2 | **Timeliness (Latency)** | Time-to-detect (TTD) and time-to-deliver (TTD-deliver) | Stale IoCs are useless |
| 3 | **Relevance** | Industry-specific (finance vs healthcare) vs generic | Generic noise = alert fatigue |
| 4 | **Accuracy / False Positive Rate (FPR)** | Percentage of IoCs that are valid | High FPR = wasted analyst hours |
| 5 | **Actionability** | Can IoCs be directly ingested into firewalls/EDR? | Strategic reports are read by CISOs; IoCs are used by SOC |
| 6 | **Format & Interoperability** | STIX/TAXII, MISP, YARA, CSV, JSON | Poor formats = integration cost |
| 7 | **Analyst Quality & Trust** | Source pedigree, attribution confidence, track record | Garbage in = garbage out |
| 8 | **Cost & Licensing Model** | Per-seat, per-feed, enterprise, MSSP resale | Budget alignment |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Table — Vendor Comparison

| Parameter | Commercial Vendor | Open-Source Vendor | Government CERT | ISAC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | High ($$$) | Free | Free | Membership fee |
| **Timeliness** | Near real-time | Variable | Often delayed | Sector-real-time |
| **Sector Specificity** | Varies | Generic | Critical infra | Sector-specific |
| **Analyst Support** | 24/7 SOC support | Community forums | Advisory only | Member-only calls |
| **Customization** | High | Low | None | Moderate |
| **Trust / Provenance** | Commercial NDA | Public | Government | Trusted peers |
| **Use Case** | Enterprise SOC | Academia, SMBs | Critical infra | Cross-industry sharing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Hook — "CT-VATS":** The six vendor categories are **C**ommercial, **T**actical-free (open-source), **V**erified (government), **A**ssociated (ISACs), **T**hird-party aggregators, **S**pecialized boutique.

## 2.4 Vendor Integration Models in the SOC

Vendors deliver intelligence that is consumed in **three primary integration patterns**:

### Pattern A — Direct SIEM/EDR Ingestion
IoCs are pushed directly into firewalls, EDRs, and SIEMs for automated blocking and detection.

### Pattern B — Threat Intelligence Platform (TIP) Aggregation
A TIP (e.g., ThreatConnect, MISP, Anomali) acts as a **middleware broker**, normalizing, deduplicating, scoring, and routing vendor feeds to downstream tools.

$$
\text{Vendor Feeds} \rightarrow \text{TIP (Normalize + Score)} \rightarrow \text{SIEM/SOAR/EDR}
$$

### Pattern C — Analyst-Driven Workflow
Finished reports (PDF, HTML, briefings) are read by SOC analysts and CTI teams, who manually translate insights into detection rules and incident response playbooks.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

CTI vendors underpin virtually every modern **Security Operations Center (SOC)**:

- A **financial bank's SOC** subscribes to FS-ISAC + Recorded Future + Mandiant to track banking trojans and APT groups targeting SWIFT.
- A **hospital** subscribes to H-ISAC + CrowdStrike to defend against ransomware groups like Conti, LockBit, BlackCat.
- A **national telecom** partners with NCIIPC (India's national CTI coordinator) for sovereign threat data.

## 2.6 Risks and Limitations of Vendor Reliance

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — Vendor Over-Reliance:** No single vendor has global visibility. Studies show that **commercial CTI feeds overlap by only ~15-30%** — meaning each vendor sees a different slice of threat activity. Best practice is **multi-vendor strategy** + **in-house validation**.

Other risks include:

- **Single Point of Failure (SPOF):** If the vendor goes down, your detection gap opens.
- **False Sense of Security:** Subscribing to a vendor ≠ being secure. Intelligence must be **operationalized**.
- **Data Quality Variance:** Some vendors repackage open-source data as proprietary — always verify the **provenance** of intelligence.
- **Adversarial Awareness:** Sophisticated attackers specifically test whether their IoCs appear in commercial feeds before launching an attack.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Analytical Framework — Vendor Selection and Consumption Lifecycle

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a non-mathematical module, the "derivations" here take the form of a **step-by-step analytical framework** showing how an organization selects, integrates, and operationalizes a CTI vendor. This is the KTU-expected analytical depth.

## 3.1 The Five-Phase Vendor Lifecycle (Step-by-Step)

$$
\text{Vendor Lifecycle} = \{\text{Phase 1: Needs Assessment},\ \text{Phase 2: Scouting},\ \text{Phase 3: Piloting},\ \text{Phase 4: Integration},\ \text{Phase 5: Continuous Evaluation}\}
$$

### **Phase 1 — Intelligence Requirements Definition**

Before choosing a vendor, the organization defines its **Intelligence Requirements (IRs)**:

- **Priority Intelligence Requirements (PIRs):** "Who is targeting our industry?"
- **Specific Intelligence Requirements (SIRs):** "What IoCs are associated with APT-29 this quarter?"
- **Operational/Strategic questions:** "What ransomware affiliates are active in our region?"

$$
\text{IR} = \{\text{Decision-maker need}\} \rightarrow \{\text{Question}\} \rightarrow \{\text{Required intelligence type}\}
$$

> **Logic Step:** If the IR is *"tactical IoC blocking"*, you need a vendor with **API-first, STIX/TAXII, low-latency feeds** (e.g., CrowdStrike, Anomali). If the IR is *"strategic board-level risk reporting"*, you need a boutique vendor with deep analyst reports (e.g., Mandiant, Recorded Future Insikt Group).

### **Phase 2 — Vendor Scouting & Shortlisting**

Build a **scoring matrix** of candidate vendors against the eight criteria from Section 2.2.

$$
\text{Vendor Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{8} w_i \cdot s_i
$$

Where:
- $w_i$ = weight assigned to criterion $i$ (sum of weights = 1).
- $s_i$ = score of the vendor on criterion $i$ (typically 1–10).

> **Logic Step:** Weights are determined by organizational priorities. A **banking SOC** might weight *Relevance* and *Accuracy* at 0.25 each, while a **national CERT** might weight *Coverage* and *Analyst Quality* at 0.30 each.

#### Worked Example: Vendor Scoring

Suppose an organization evaluates three vendors — **Vendor A, B, C** — against four weighted criteria (a simplified version):

| Criterion | Weight $w_i$ | Vendor A $s_i$ | Vendor B $s_i$ | Vendor C $s_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Coverage | 0.30 | 8 | 6 | 9 |
| Timeliness | 0.25 | 9 | 7 | 6 |
| Accuracy | 0.25 | 7 | 8 | 7 |
| Cost | 0.20 | 5 | 8 | 4 |
| **Total Score** | **1.00** | **7.50** | **7.15** | **6.85** |

The mathematical evaluation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_A &= (0.30 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 5) \\
&= 2.40 + 2.25 + 1.75 + 1.00 \\
&= 7.40
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_B &= (0.30 \times 6) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 8) \\
&= 1.80 + 1.75 + 2.00 + 1.60 \\
&= 7.15
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_C &= (0.30 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 6) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 4) \\
&= 2.70 + 1.50 + 1.75 + 0.80 \\
&= 6.75
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** Vendor A wins with 7.40, despite Vendor C having the best *Coverage*. This shows that **multi-criteria decision-making** is essential — no single dimension should dominate.

### **Phase 3 — Pilot / Proof of Concept (PoC)**

- Run a **30–90 day pilot** with the shortlisted vendor.
- Inject known **ground-truth IoCs** (from internal incidents) and measure if the vendor flagged them.
- Compute the **Detection Rate (Recall)** and **False Positive Rate**:

$$
\text{Detection Rate} = \frac{\text{True Positives}}{\text{True Positives + False Negatives}}
$$

$$
\text{False Positive Rate} = \frac{\text{False Positives}}{\text{False Positives + True Negatives}}
$$

> **Logic Step:** A vendor with 95% detection rate but 80% FPR is operationally **worse** than a vendor with 80% detection rate and 5% FPR, because SOC analysts drown in false alerts.

### **Phase 4 — Integration and Operationalization**

The vendor's feed is integrated into the **detection stack**:

$$
\text{Vendor API} \rightarrow \text{TIP} \rightarrow \text{SIEM Correlation Engine} \rightarrow \text{SOAR Playbooks} \rightarrow \text{EDR/IPS Auto-Block}
$$

> **Logic Step:** Integration must follow the **CIA-Q principle** — **C**onfidence, **I**ntegrity, **A**ctionability, **Q**uality. IoCs are tagged with confidence scores (low/medium/high) so the SOAR can auto-block high-confidence IoCs and queue low-confidence ones for analyst review.

### **Phase 5 — Continuous Evaluation & Vendor Drift**

Vendors are not static. Their quality can drift due to:

- Loss of key analysts.
- Shift in business focus (e.g., a vendor pivoting from CTI to XDR).
- Acquisition by a larger company (e.g., FireEye → Mandiant → Google Cloud).
- Adversaries adapting tactics to evade the vendor's visibility.

> **Logic Step:** Quarterly **vendor scorecard reviews** should re-evaluate timeliness, accuracy, and relevance, with the ability to swap vendors if KPIs degrade.

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Vendor Type

| Organizational Need | Recommended Vendor Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Real-time IoC blocking in SIEM/EDR | Commercial EDR/XDR vendor (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne) | Direct integration, low latency |
| Sector-specific threat actor monitoring | ISAC (FS-ISAC, H-ISAC) | Peer-shared, validated intelligence |
| Nation-state APT attribution | Boutique / Mandiant / Insikt Group | Deep HUMINT and forensic expertise |
| Budget-constrained SMB | Open-source (Abuse.ch, MISP, OTX) | Free, decent quality for commodity threats |
| Critical infrastructure protection | Government CERT (CERT-In, CISA) | Sovereign, classified-level data |
| Multi-vendor feed aggregation | Aggregator TIP (Anomali, ThreatConnect) | Reduces integration overhead |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The CTI Vendor Ecosystem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sourcesA[Dark Web Forums] --> vendorCore
    sourcesB[Honeypots and Sandboxes] --> vendorCore
    sourcesC[Customer EDR Telemetry] --> vendorCore
    sourcesD[Open Source Scraping] --> vendorCore
    sourcesE[Human Intelligence HUMINT] --> vendorCore
    sourcesF[ISAC Member Reports] --> vendorCore

    vendorCore[CTI Vendor Core Processing and Analysis Engine]

    vendorCore --> normalize[Normalization Layer STIX 2.1, MISP, YARA]
    normalize --> scoring[Confidence and Relevance Scoring]
    scoring --> tierA[Tactical Feeds IoCs and IoAs]
    scoring --> tierB[Operational Intel Threat Actor Profiles]
    scoring --> tierC[Strategic Intel Executive Reports]

    tierA --> deliveryAPI[API and TAXII Server]
    tierA --> deliveryTIP[TIP Aggregator]
    tierB --> deliveryPlatform[Web Platform UI]
    tierC --> deliveryBriefing[PDF Briefing and Email Advisory]

    deliveryAPI --> consumerSIEM[Consumer SIEM]
    deliveryAPI --> consumerEDR[Consumer EDR]
    deliveryTIP --> consumerSOAR[Consumer SOAR]
    deliveryPlatform --> consumerSOC[Consumer SOC Analyst]
    deliveryBriefing --> consumerCISO[Consumer CISO and Executives]
```

## 4.2 Vendor Selection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start([Organization CTI Need Identified]) --> phase1[Phase 1: Define Intelligence Requirements IRs]
    phase1 --> phase2[Phase 2: Market Scan of Vendors]
    phase2 --> phase3[Phase 3: Build Scoring Matrix]
    phase3 --> phase4[Phase 4: Conduct PoC Pilot 30 to 90 days]
    phase4 --> decision{Quality Acceptable?}
    decision -- Yes --> phase5[Phase 5: Contract and Onboard Vendor]
    decision -- No --> phase2
    phase5 --> phase6[Phase 6: Integration into SOC Stack]
    phase6 --> phase7[Phase 7: Continuous Quarterly Review]
    phase7 --> phase7
```

## 4.3 Multi-Vendor Layered Consumption Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer1[Layer 1: Government CERTs NCIIPC, CISA, CERT-In] --> tipCenter
    layer2[Layer 2: Commercial Vendors Mandiant, Recorded Future] --> tipCenter
    layer3[Layer 3: Open Source Feeds Abuse.ch, OTX, MISP] --> tipCenter
    layer4[Layer 4: ISAC Feeds FS-ISAC, H-ISAC] --> tipCenter

    tipCenter[Central Threat Intelligence Platform TIP]

    tipCenter --> dedup[Deduplication Engine]
    dedup --> confScore[Confidence Scoring]
    confScore --> priority[Priority Routing by Sector and Asset]

    priority --> socQueue[SOC Analyst Queue]
    priority --> soarAuto[SOAR Automated Response]
    priority --> execBrief[CISO Executive Brief]
```

## 4.4 Vendor Maturity Model (How Sophisticated Your Vendor Use Is)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    level1[Level 1: Ad Hoc - Free OSINT Feeds Only] --> level2[Level 2: Reactive - One Commercial Vendor Subscribed]
    level2 --> level3[Level 3: Proactive - Multi Vendor TIP Aggregated]
    level3 --> level4[Level 4: Operationalized - SOAR Integrated Auto-Response]
    level4 --> level5[Level 5: Intelligence Driven - In House CTI Team With Vendor Partnerships]

    level1 -.gap.-> level5
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a CTI vendor. List the four main categories of CTI vendors with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) vendor** is an external organization that systematically collects, analyzes, and disseminates threat intelligence — including IoCs, threat actor profiles, and strategic reports — to subscribing consumer organizations to enhance their cybersecurity posture.

**Four main categories:**

1. **Commercial / Enterprise Vendors** — e.g., Mandiant, CrowdStrike, Recorded Future.
2. **Open-Source / Free Vendors** — e.g., AlienVault OTX, Abuse.ch, MISP Project.
3. **Governmental / National CERTs** — e.g., CERT-In, CISA, NCSC.
4. **Industry ISACs** — e.g., FS-ISAC (Finance), H-ISAC (Healthcare).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the difference between a Commercial CTI vendor and an Open-Source CTI vendor. Give two pros and two cons of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Commercial Vendor | Open-Source Vendor |
|---|---|---|
| **Pros** | (1) 24/7 analyst support, (2) Proprietary dark-web visibility, (3) Customized feeds, (4) Low latency | (1) Free, (2) Community-validated, (3) Transparent provenance, (4) No vendor lock-in |
| **Cons** | (1) Expensive, (2) Vendor lock-in, (3) May repackage open-source data as proprietary, (4) Opaque methodologies | (1) No dedicated support, (2) Quality varies, (3) No SLAs, (4) Integration effort is on the consumer |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **operational layers of a CTI vendor ecosystem**. Explain how intelligence flows from raw collection to delivery at the consumer end.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **eight-criterion vendor evaluation matrix** used by enterprises to select CTI vendors. Why is single-vendor reliance considered risky?

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

The CTI vendor ecosystem operates in **four operational layers**:

**Layer 1 — Data Collection:**
Vendors ingest raw threat data from:
- **Honeypots & Sandboxes** — capture live malware.
- **Dark Web Crawlers** — scrape hacker forums and marketplaces.
- **Customer Telemetry** — opt-in EDR/XDR data.
- **Open-Source Scraping** — paste sites, GitHub leaks, social media.
- **HUMINT** — embedded human researchers.

**Layer 2 — Processing and Normalization:**
Raw data is converted into standardized schemas such as **STIX 2.1**, **MISP**, **YARA**, or **OpenIOC** to ensure machine-readable interoperability.

**Layer 3 — Analysis and Enrichment:**
Analysts perform:
- **Attribution** to threat actors (APTs, cybercrime groups, hacktivists).
- **TTP mapping** to MITRE ATT&CK.
- **Contextualization** with geopolitical and sector data.
- **Confidence scoring** to filter noise.

**Layer 4 — Delivery and Integration:**
Finished intelligence is delivered via:
- **APIs / TAXII servers** (for SIEM/EDR ingestion).
- **Threat Intelligence Platforms (TIPs)** (for aggregation).
- **Web dashboards** (for SOC analysts).
- **PDF briefings & email advisories** (for executives).

**Flow Summary:**

$$
\text{Raw Sources} \rightarrow \text{Normalization} \rightarrow \text{Analysis} \rightarrow \text{Scoring} \rightarrow \text{Consumer SOC/CISO}
$$

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

The **eight-criterion vendor evaluation matrix** includes:

1. **Coverage** — geographic and actor visibility.
2. **Timeliness (Latency)** — time-to-deliver IoCs.
3. **Relevance** — sector-specific vs generic.
4. **Accuracy / FPR** — false positive rate.
5. **Actionability** — direct SIEM/EDR ingestibility.
6. **Format & Interoperability** — STIX/TAXII/MISP support.
7. **Analyst Quality & Trust** — source pedigree, attribution confidence.
8. **Cost & Licensing** — fit with budget.

**Why Single-Vendor Reliance Is Risky:**

- **Coverage Gap:** Studies show commercial CTI feeds overlap by only **15–30%**. No single vendor has global visibility.
- **Single Point of Failure:** Vendor outage, acquisition, or pivot to another product line can blind the SOC.
- **Adversary Evasion:** Sophisticated attackers test their IoCs against major commercial feeds before attacking.
- **False Sense of Security:** Subscribing ≠ being secure; intelligence must be operationalized.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often **forget to name the eight criteria explicitly** and write vague statements like *"vendor should be good"*. You will lose up to **3 marks** if the criteria are not enumerated. Also, in 14-mark answers, **always show the intelligence flow as a diagram or equation** — it earns easy marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **Commercial CTI vendors, Open-Source vendors, and Government CERTs**. Provide a recommendation framework for choosing between them.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **vendor maturity model** for CTI consumption. Describe what an organization at Level 3 (Proactive) versus Level 5 (Intelligence-Driven) looks like in practice.

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

| Parameter | Commercial Vendors | Open-Source Vendors | Government CERTs |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | High (subscription) | Free | Free |
| **Timeliness** | Real-time / near real-time | Variable (community-dependent) | Often delayed (advisory-style) |
| **Sector Specificity** | Industry-vertical options | Generic | Critical Infrastructure focus |
| **Analyst Support** | 24/7 SOC, dedicated CSM | Community forums | Advisory only |
| **Trust / Provenance** | NDA-bound, opaque | Public, transparent | Sovereign, classified-grade |
| **Customization** | High (tailored feeds) | Low (one-size-fits-all) | None |
| **Examples** | Mandiant, CrowdStrike, Recorded Future | MISP, OTX, Abuse.ch | CERT-In, CISA, NCSC |

**Recommendation Framework:**

- If the organization is a **regulated critical infrastructure entity** → use **Government CERTs** + one **Commercial vendor**.
- If the organization is an **enterprise with a mature SOC** → use **Commercial + ISAC + Open-Source** in a multi-vendor model.
- If the organization is an **SMB or academic institution** → start with **Open-Source** + one **free commercial trial**.

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

The **CTI Vendor Maturity Model** has **five levels**:

| Level | Description | Practical Characteristics |
|---|---|---|
| **Level 1 — Ad Hoc** | Free OSINT only | No dedicated TIP, manual lookup, no SLAs |
| **Level 2 — Reactive** | One commercial subscription | Basic SIEM ingestion, no automation |
| **Level 3 — Proactive** | Multi-vendor with TIP aggregation | Dedup engine, confidence scoring, sector-tuned feeds, SOAR integration begins |
| **Level 4 — Operationalized** | SOAR-integrated automated response | High-confidence IoCs auto-blocked; low-confidence queued for review |
| **Level 5 — Intelligence-Driven** | In-house CTI team + vendor partnerships | In-house analysts produce original intelligence; vendors supplement, not replace |

**Level 3 (Proactive) vs Level 5 (Intelligence-Driven):**

- A **Level 3** organization **consumes** multi-vendor feeds through a TIP, with analysts triaging and tuning detection rules. The org **depends** on vendors for original intelligence.
- A **Level 5** organization **produces** its own original intelligence (e.g., by reverse-engineering malware, monitoring dark-web in regional languages), **publishes** threat reports, and **partners** with vendors as a peer contributor.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in Maturity Model Answers:** Students often write the five levels as a "wish list" without explaining the **operational differences**. For full marks, you must describe **what the SOC team actually does differently** at each level.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Bullets — Read This Twice Before the Exam**

- A **CTI vendor** is an external provider of curated threat intelligence — covering IoCs, IoAs, TTPs, actor profiles, and strategic reports.
- **Six vendor types** to memorize: **C**ommercial, **O**pen-source, **G**overnment, **I**SAC, **A**ggregator, **S**pecialized (COGIAS).
- The **intelligence pyramid**: Raw Data → Information → IoCs → Tactical → Operational → Strategic.
- **Eight evaluation criteria**: Coverage, Timeliness, Relevance, Accuracy, Actionability, Format, Trust, Cost.
- **Vendor maturity model** has **5 levels**: Ad Hoc → Reactive → Proactive → Operationalized → Intelligence-Driven.
- **STIX/TAXII** is the dominant delivery standard; **MISP** is a popular open-source alternative; **YARA** is for malware pattern matching.
- **No single vendor** gives complete coverage — commercial feeds overlap by only 15–30%. Use **multi-vendor** strategy.
- **Operationalization** is the goal: a vendor that delivers only PDF reports without API feeds is of limited value to a SOC.
- **Intelligence Requirements (IRs)** must be defined **before** vendor selection — otherwise, you buy a vendor and then hunt for a use case.
- **Vendor drift** is real — quality can degrade due to analyst attrition, acquisitions, or pivot in product focus. Quarterly review is essential.
- **Confidence scoring** (low/medium/high) on IoCs enables SOAR-based automated response.
- **Risks of vendor reliance**: SPOF, false sense of security, opaque provenance, adversary evasion.
- For KTU exams, **always enumerate criteria**, **show the intelligence flow** (as a diagram or equation), and **end with a recommendation framework**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: User Behaviour Analytics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT755 - CYBER THREAT INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** User Behaviour Analytics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**User Behaviour Analytics (UBA)**, often extended to **User and Entity Behaviour Analytics (UEBA)**, is a class of cybersecurity analytics that establishes a **mathematical behavioural baseline** for every user (and machine identity) within an enterprise information system, then continuously monitors live activity streams to detect **statistical deviations** that may indicate compromised credentials, insider threats, lateral movement, or data exfiltration.

In the context of KTU's PECCT755 (Cyber Threat Intelligence) syllabus, UBA is positioned as a **threat-detection layer** that sits *above* traditional signature-based systems (SIEM, AV, IDS) and *below* human-led threat hunting, leveraging telemetry from logs, network flows, EDR agents, and identity providers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> "User Behaviour Analytics is the process of collecting, aggregating, analysing, and reporting on user activity data to detect anomalous behaviour indicative of malicious intent, where 'entities' may be users, hosts, applications, or service accounts."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "School Attendance Warden"

Imagine a school where a warden has watched **Riya** walk in at 8:50 a.m. every day for 5 years, take the same staircase, and eat lunch at 12:30 p.m. One Tuesday, Riya:

- Logs in at **3:14 a.m.**
- Uses a **different staircase**
- Prints **47 pages** in 6 minutes
- Accesses the **staff records** (a folder she has never opened)

The warden does not need to know *why* the behaviour is wrong — the **statistical deviation** itself is the alert. UBA is exactly this warden, except it operates on petabytes of logs across millions of users, using **probabilistic models** instead of memory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for the Board Exam:**
> - **SIEM** → *What happened* (correlates known events)
> - **UBA/UEBA** → *What is unusual* (detects behavioural anomalies)
> - **Threat Intelligence** → *Why it matters* (adds adversary context)

## 1.3 Core Entities, Metrics & Vocabulary

| Term | Definition | Typical Unit |
|------|------------|--------------|
| **User Entity** | Human, service account, or machine identity | Unique ID (SID, UPN) |
| **Behavioural Baseline** | Aggregated historical pattern of an entity | Days/Weeks window |
| **Risk Score** | Quantitative deviation from baseline | **0 – 100** (industry standard) |
| **Anomaly** | Observation outside the acceptable range | σ (sigma) or percentile |
| **Peer Group** | Cohort of users with similar job functions | Cluster label |
| **TTPs** | Tactics, Techniques & Procedures (MITRE ATT&CK) | ATT&CK ID (e.g., T1078) |
| **MTTD** | Mean Time to Detect | Hours / Days |
| **MTTR** | Mean Time to Respond | Hours / Days |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry-Standard Risk Score Threshold:** A risk score of **≥ 75** (out of 100) is conventionally considered *high severity*, **50 – 74** as *medium*, and **< 50** as *low/informational* in most commercial UBA platforms (e.g., Microsoft Defender for Identity, Exabeam, Splunk UBA).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Operational Phases of UBA

The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to articulate UBA as a **five-stage pipeline**. Each stage answers a distinct question:

1. **Data Collection (Telemetry Ingestion)** — *What raw signals are we gathering?*
   - Sources: Active Directory logs, VPN concentrators, DNS logs, EDR telemetry, Cloud audit logs (AWS CloudTrail, Azure AD), DLP alerts, email gateways, HR systems (joiner/leaver feeds).

2. **Data Normalisation & Enrichment** — *Can we make sense of heterogeneous data?*
   - Parsing, timestamping, geolocation tagging, asset criticality weighting, threat-intel enrichment (IP reputation, hash reputation).

3. **Behavioural Modelling** — *What is "normal" for this entity?*
   - Builds a **per-user profile** using statistical or machine-learning methods (see §2.3).

4. **Anomaly Detection & Risk Scoring** — *Does the current activity deviate from the baseline?*
   - Assigns a risk score; if **score ≥ threshold**, an alert is raised.

5. **Response & Feedback Loop** — *What do we do, and how do we improve?*
   - SOAR playbook execution, analyst triage, analyst feedback (true/false positive) that re-trains the model.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage

| Stage | Why It Exists | Failure Mode If Skipped |
|-------|---------------|--------------------------|
| Collection | No data = no model | Blind spots in monitoring |
| Normalisation | Logs use different schemas | Garbage-in/garbage-out |
| Modelling | "Normal" is contextual | High false-positive rate |
| Scoring | Analysts need prioritisation | Alert fatigue |
| Response | Detection without action = theatre | Unmitigated dwell time |

## 2.3 The Three Modelling Paradigms (High-Yield for KTU)

### A. Statistical / Rule-Based Models
- **Z-score, percentiles, moving averages**
- Pros: Interpretable, fast
- Cons: Cannot capture complex non-linear behaviour

### B. Machine-Learning Models
- **Supervised:** Random Forest, XGBoost (needs labelled attack data)
- **Unsupervised:** Isolation Forest, K-Means, DBSCAN (no labels needed)
- **Deep Learning:** LSTM / Autoencoders for sequential log analysis

### C. Graph-Based Models
- Represent users, hosts, files, IPs as nodes; relationships as edges
- Detect **lateral movement paths** and **impossible travel**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are commonly tested either as direct 3-mark derivations or as components of 14-mark Part B analytical questions.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables | Application |
|---|---------|----------------------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Z-Score (Standard Score)** | $Z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | $x$ = observed, $\mu$ = mean, $\sigma$ = std dev | Detect outlier events |
| 2 | **Anomaly Indicator** | $A = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \vert Z \vert > k \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | $k$ = threshold (typically **2.5 or 3**) | Binary anomaly flag |
| 3 | **Risk Score (Weighted)** | $R = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i$ | $w_i$ = weight, $s_i$ = sub-score, $\sum w_i = 1$ | Composite risk |
| 4 | **Log-Likelihood Anomaly** | $L(x) = -\log P(x \mid \theta)$ | $P$ = learned prob. distribution | Autoencoder loss |
| 5 | **Detection Rate (Recall)** | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | True/False Positives/Negatives | Model performance |
| 6 | **False Positive Rate** | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Same as above | Precision proxy |
| 7 | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{PR \cdot TPR}{PR + TPR}$ | $PR$ = precision | Imbalanced datasets |
| 8 | **Information Entropy (Shannon)** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $p_i$ = probability of event $i$ | Behavioural diversity |
| 9 | **Cosine Similarity (Peer Group)** | $\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}}{\vert \vec{u} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{v} \vert}$ | $\vec{u}, \vec{v}$ = behaviour vectors | Peer comparison |
| 10 | **Isolation Forest Score** | $s(x, n) = 2^{-\dfrac{E(h(x))}{c(n)}}$ | $E(h(x))$ = avg path length | Anomaly score |
| 11 | **Shannon Entropy (Password/Host)** | $H = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Higher $H$ → more random | DGA detection, password strength |
| 12 | **Bayes' Theorem (Threat Prob.)** | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$ | $A$ = threat, $B$ = evidence | Risk inference |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Convention:** In commercial UBA, the alert threshold for Z-score is **|Z| > 3** (i.e., outside 99.7% of a normal distribution). For exam answers, always state this threshold explicitly.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

UBA is deployed across:
- **Banking & Financial Services** — detect fraudulent wire transfers and credential abuse
- **Healthcare (HIPAA)** — flag abnormal EHR access (e.g., celebrity patient snooping)
- **Defence & Government (NIC/CERT-In)** — detect insider leaks and lateral movement
- **Cloud-native SOCs** — protect SaaS tenants (M365, Google Workspace)
- **OT/ICS** — detect anomalous engineering-station behaviour

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per the engine mandate, **every algebraic step is explicitly shown**. No step is skipped, summarised, or abbreviated.

## 3.1 Derivation: Z-Score Based Anomaly Score (Worked Example)

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A user's daily login count over 30 days is observed to have a mean $\mu = 8$ logins/day and standard deviation $\sigma = 2$ logins/day. On Day 31, the user logs in **17** times. Using a threshold of $|Z| > 3$, determine whether the event should be flagged as an anomaly and compute the equivalent risk score on a 0–100 scale.

### Step 1 — Identify Known Quantities

$$\mu = 8, \quad \sigma = 2, \quad x = 17, \quad k = 3$$

The observed value is $x = 17$ logins on Day 31.

### Step 2 — Apply the Z-Score Formula

$$Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Substitute the values:

$$Z = \frac{17 - 8}{2}$$

$$Z = \frac{9}{2}$$

$$Z = 4.5$$

### Step 3 — Compare Against the Threshold

The anomaly rule is:

$$A = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \vert Z \vert > 3 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

$$\vert 4.5 \vert = 4.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 4.5 > 3$$

Therefore, the event **IS** an anomaly:

$$A = 1$$

### Step 4 — Convert Z-Score to 0–100 Risk Score

A common linear mapping used in UBA platforms:

$$R = \min\left(100, \frac{\vert Z \vert}{6} \times 100\right)$$

The constant **6** corresponds to ~6σ (near-zero probability in a Gaussian).

$$R = \min\left(100, \frac{4.5}{6} \times 100\right)$$

$$R = \min(100, 75)$$

$$\boxed{R = 75 \;\; (\text{High Severity})}$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation

> A risk score of **75** falls into the **High Severity** band (75–100), and the SOC analyst should triage the alert within **1 hour** per most UBA SLAs. The 17 logins may indicate credential stuffing, a brute-force attempt, or a compromised session running automated tasks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Bayesian Risk Inference (Worked Example)

**Problem Statement:**
> A UBA system flags 1% of all sessions as anomalous (i.e., $P(\text{Anomaly}) = 0.01$). Historical data shows that when a session is truly malicious, the UBA flag is raised 95% of the time ($P(\text{Flag} \mid \text{Malicious}) = 0.95$). The false-positive rate of the flag is 4% ($P(\text{Flag} \mid \text{Benign}) = 0.04$). Given that a session has been flagged, find the probability that it is genuinely malicious.

### Step 1 — Bayes' Theorem

$$P(M \mid F) = \frac{P(F \mid M) \cdot P(M)}{P(F)}$$

Where:
- $M$ = session is malicious
- $F$ = session is flagged

### Step 2 — Identify the Given Probabilities

$$P(M) = 0.01, \quad P(F \mid M) = 0.95, \quad P(F \mid \neg M) = 0.04$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Marginal $P(F)$ Using the Law of Total Probability

$$P(F) = P(F \mid M) \cdot P(M) + P(F \mid \neg M) \cdot P(\neg M)$$

$$P(F) = (0.95 \times 0.01) + (0.04 \times 0.99)$$

$$P(F) = 0.0095 + 0.0396$$

$$P(F) = 0.0491$$

### Step 4 — Substitute into Bayes' Theorem

$$P(M \mid F) = \frac{0.95 \times 0.01}{0.0491}$$

$$P(M \mid F) = \frac{0.0095}{0.0491}$$

$$P(M \mid F) \approx 0.1935$$

$$\boxed{P(M \mid F) \approx 19.35\%}$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> Despite a 95% true-positive rate, only **~19%** of flagged sessions are actually malicious. This is the **Base-Rate Fallacy** — a classic UBA pitfall. To boost posterior probability, UBA must **stack multiple weak signals** (geolocation + time + asset sensitivity + peer deviation) so that $P(M)$ effectively increases per additional corroborating signal.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping ATT&CK Techniques to UBA Signals

| MITRE ATT&CK Technique | UBA Telemetry Source | Statistical Indicator |
|------------------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| T1078 — Valid Accounts | Auth logs, VPN logs | Login volume, geo-velocity |
| T1110 — Brute Force | Auth failure logs | Failure-to-success ratio |
| T1020 — Automated Exfiltration | DLP, NetFlow | Outbound bytes, file count |
| T1087 — Account Discovery | AD enumeration events | Unusual LDAP queries |
| T1059 — Command Execution | EDR process telemetry | Parent-child process tree deviation |
| T1567 — Exfil to Web Service | Proxy logs | Unusual destination domain, byte volume |
| T1530 — Cloud Storage Exfil | Cloud audit logs | Bucket access from unusual IP |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 UBA End-to-End Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Telemetry Sources] --> B[Data Lake]
    B --> C[Normalisation and Parsing Engine]
    C --> D[Behavioural Modelling Engine]
    D --> E[Anomaly Detection Engine]
    E --> F[Risk Scoring Module]
    F --> G{Score Threshold}
    G -- Above 75 --> H[High Severity Alert]
    G -- 50 to 75 --> I[Medium Severity Alert]
    G -- Below 50 --> J[Log Only]
    H --> K[SOAR Playbook Trigger]
    I --> L[Analyst Triage Queue]
    J --> M[Long Term Storage]
    K --> N[Containment Actions]
    L --> O[Analyst Feedback]
    O --> P[Model Retraining Pipeline]
    P --> D
```

## 4.2 UBA Peer-Group Comparison Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Target User Behaviour Vector] --> C[Cosine Similarity Engine]
    B[Peer Group Baseline Vector] --> C
    C --> D{Similarity Score}
    D -- Above 0.8 --> E[Behaviour Consistent]
    D -- 0.5 to 0.8 --> F[Minor Deviation]
    D -- Below 0.5 --> G[Major Anomaly Flagged]
    G --> H[Risk Score Increment]
```

## 4.3 UBA Feedback Loop & Model Improvement Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Deployed UBA Model] --> B[Generate Alerts]
    B --> C[SOC Analyst Reviews]
    C --> D{True or False Positive}
    D -- True Positive --> E[Threat Intel Enrichment]
    D -- False Positive --> F[Whitelist and Tune Weights]
    E --> G[Labelled Dataset Updated]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Retrain Model Pipeline]
    H --> A
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1.** Define **User Behaviour Analytics (UBA)**. How does it differ from a traditional **SIEM** in terms of detection philosophy?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
User Behaviour Analytics (UBA) is a cybersecurity process that builds a behavioural baseline for every user/entity by analysing historical activity, then detects statistical deviations that may indicate threats (1 mark). It is **anomaly-based** (detects *what is unusual*) (1 mark), whereas SIEM is **correlation-based** (detects *what is known-bad* across rules) (1 mark). UBA complements SIEM by catching novel, unknown, and insider threats that lack signatures.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q2.** List any **three** telemetry sources that feed a UBA platform and state one anomalous indicator each source can surface.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Active Directory / Authentication Logs** → indicator: impossible travel / unusual login time (1 mark)
2. **Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR)** → indicator: anomalous parent-child process tree (1 mark)
3. **DLP / NetFlow** → indicator: sudden spike in outbound data volume (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End-Semester Exam – Module 4, Model Paper]**

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **five operational phases** of a User Behaviour Analytics pipeline. For each phase, identify **one failure mode** that would degrade detection effectiveness.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A user historically logs in $\mu = 4$ times/day with $\sigma = 1.5$. On a given day, the user logs in **11 times** and downloads **2.1 GB** of data (baseline mean = 50 MB, std dev = 20 MB). Using a Z-score threshold of **|Z| > 3**, evaluate whether either activity should be flagged. Compute the **composite risk score** using equal weights on both sub-scores and recommend a SOC action.

#### Model Solution — Q1(a) [7 Marks]

The five phases are:

1. **Data Collection (1 mark):** Gathers raw logs from AD, VPN, EDR, Cloud, DLP, etc. *Failure mode:* blind spots if critical source (e.g., VPN) is not integrated.

2. **Data Normalisation & Enrichment (1 mark):** Parses, timestamps, geo-tags, and enriches with threat intel. *Failure mode:* schema mismatch causes silent drop of events.

3. **Behavioural Modelling (1 mark):** Builds per-user statistical / ML profile. *Failure mode:* insufficient training window causes a poor baseline.

4. **Anomaly Detection & Risk Scoring (2 marks):** Scores deviations; threshold-based alerting. *Failure mode:* threshold too low → alert fatigue; too high → missed detections.

5. **Response & Feedback (2 marks):** SOAR execution, analyst triage, model retraining. *Failure mode:* no feedback loop → model drift over time.

**Total: 7 marks**

---

#### Model Solution — Q1(b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Z-score for login count** (1 mark)
$$Z_1 = \frac{11 - 4}{1.5} = \frac{7}{1.5} \approx 4.67$$

Since $4.67 > 3$ → **flagged anomaly**. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Z-score for data download** (1 mark)
$$Z_2 = \frac{2100 - 50}{20} = \frac{2050}{20} = 102.5$$

Since $102.5 > 3$ → **flagged anomaly**. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Convert to 0–100 sub-scores** (1 mark)
$$R_1 = \min\left(100, \frac{4.67}{6} \times 100\right) \approx 77.8$$
$$R_2 = \min\left(100, \frac{102.5}{6} \times 100\right) = 100$$

**Step 4 — Composite risk score (equal weights, $w_1 = w_2 = 0.5$)** (1 mark)
$$R = 0.5 \times 77.8 + 0.5 \times 100 = 88.9$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 89 \;\; (\text{Critical})}$$

**Step 5 — Recommended SOC Action** (1 mark)
> **Immediate containment:** Disable the user account, force password reset, isolate the endpoint, and escalate to L2 incident response. Initiate forensic review of the 2.1 GB download destination. Possible insider exfiltration or compromised account.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Alternative*

**(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the role of **machine learning** in UBA. Compare **supervised, unsupervised, and graph-based** approaches with one example algorithm and one limitation each.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A UBA system raises flags on 2% of sessions. Of those flagged, 90% are true malicious ($P(M \mid F) = 0.90$) and 10% are false. Using **Bayes' theorem**, derive the **base rate of malicious sessions** $P(M)$ given that $P(F \mid \neg M) = 0.005$. Comment on how the UBA should combine multiple weak signals to overcome the base-rate fallacy.

#### Model Solution — Q2(a) [7 Marks]

| Approach | Algorithm Example | Use Case | Limitation |
|----------|-------------------|----------|------------|
| **Supervised** (2 marks) | Random Forest / XGBoost | Classify known attack types | Needs **labelled data**; poor on novel TTPs |
| **Unsupervised** (2 marks) | Isolation Forest / K-Means | Detect *unknown* anomalies | High false-positive rate |
| **Graph-based** (2 marks) | PageRank / Louvain community detection | Map lateral movement | Computationally expensive on large graphs |
| **Why ML in UBA** (1 mark) | — | Scales to petabytes; finds patterns humans cannot | — |

**Total: 7 marks**

---

#### Model Solution — Q2(b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
$$P(F) = 0.02, \quad P(M \mid F) = 0.90, \quad P(F \mid \neg M) = 0.005$$

**Step 1 — Write Bayes' theorem** (1 mark)
$$P(M \mid F) = \frac{P(F \mid M) \cdot P(M)}{P(F)}$$

**Step 2 — Expand $P(F)$ using the law of total probability** (1 mark)
$$P(F) = P(F \mid M) P(M) + P(F \mid \neg M) P(\neg M)$$

Let $p = P(M)$ and $1 - p = P(\neg M)$. Then:
$$0.02 = P(F \mid M) \cdot p + 0.005 \cdot (1 - p)$$

**Step 3 — Use the posterior relationship** (1 mark)
From Bayes: $P(F \mid M) \cdot p = P(M \mid F) \cdot P(F) = 0.90 \times 0.02 = 0.018$

Substitute:
$$0.02 = 0.018 + 0.005 (1 - p)$$

$$0.02 - 0.018 = 0.005 (1 - p)$$

$$0.002 = 0.005 (1 - p)$$

$$1 - p = \frac{0.002}{0.005} = 0.4$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $p$** (1 mark)
$$p = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6$$

$$\boxed{P(M) = 0.6 \;\; (60\% \text{ base rate of malicious sessions})}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation: Base-Rate Fallacy** (1 mark)
> A 60% base rate is **artificially high** (real enterprise base rates are often < 1%). This problem demonstrates how *prior probabilities dominate posteriors*. UBA overcomes this by **stacking weak signals** — each additional corroborating indicator (geo + time + asset + peer) raises the effective prior, dramatically sharpening the posterior.

**Step 6 — Why Multi-Signal Stacking Works** (1 mark)
> Combining *n* independent weak signals each with $P(\text{flag} \mid M) = 0.7$ yields a joint detection probability of $1 - 0.3^n$, which quickly approaches 1.0 — a Bayesian reason why modern UEBA fuses **identity, network, endpoint, and cloud** telemetry.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points (Read Carefully!):**
> 1. **Forgetting units / threshold statements.** Always state $|Z| > 3$ explicitly when grading anomalies.
> 2. **Mixing UBA and SIEM definitions.** UBA is *anomaly-based*; SIEM is *rule/correlation-based*. Examiners award **1 mark** for this distinction.
> 3. **Skipping the "feedback loop"** in the 5-phase pipeline. It is the 5th phase and is *commonly* forgotten — losing **2 marks** on a 14-mark question.
> 4. **Not converting Z-scores to 0–100 risk scores** in numerical questions. Most questions explicitly ask for the risk score; stating only the Z-score loses **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Confusing UBA with UEBA.** UEBA = UBA + Entity (hosts, service accounts). Examiners expect students to know the distinction.
> 6. **Skipping the 0–100 mapping formula.** Memorise $R = \min(100, \frac{|Z|}{6} \times 100)$.
> 7. **Bayes' theorem: forgetting the law of total probability** when computing $P(F)$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorise:**

- ☐ **UBA = anomaly-based detection** of user behaviour using statistical / ML baselines
- ☐ **UEBA = UBA + machine entities** (hosts, service accounts, applications)
- ☐ **Five operational phases:** Collection → Normalisation → Modelling → Scoring → Response
- ☐ **Z-score formula:** $Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ — threshold is **|Z| > 3**
- ☐ **Risk score mapping:** $R = \min\left(100, \frac{|Z|}{6} \times 100\right)$
- ☐ **Severity bands:** Low < 50, Medium 50–74, **High ≥ 75**
- ☐ **Bayes' theorem** is the mathematical backbone of risk inference
- ☐ **Base-rate fallacy** — stacking multiple weak signals is essential
- ☐ **Telemetry sources:** AD, VPN, EDR, Cloud, DLP, Proxy, DNS, Email
- ☐ **Peer-group analysis** uses **cosine similarity** between behaviour vectors
- ☐ **MITRE ATT&CK** mapping is the modern standard for UBA alert enrichment
- ☐ **UBA complements SIEM** — they are *not* substitutes
- ☐ **Feedback loop** (analyst → model retraining) is the 5th phase — **don't forget it**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AI and SIEM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT755 - CYBER THREAT INTELLIGENCE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** AI and SIEM ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Security Information and Event Management (SIEM)** is a comprehensive cybersecurity solution that aggregates and analyzes activity from multiple sources across an organization's IT infrastructure — including networks, servers, applications, endpoints, and security devices — to provide real-time detection, correlation, and response capabilities against cyber threats. **Artificial Intelligence (AI) in SIEM** refers to the integration of machine learning (ML), deep learning (DL), natural language processing (NLP), and statistical modeling techniques into traditional SIEM platforms to automate threat detection, reduce false positives, predict emerging threats, and accelerate incident response beyond rule-based correlation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECCT755 – Module 4):** > "AI in SIEM applies machine learning algorithms and statistical models to log data and security events to identify anomalous behavior, correlate threats, and provide predictive analytics — transforming SIEM from a reactive log-collector into a proactive threat-intelligence engine." --- ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: "The AI-Powered Security Control Room"** Imagine a **massive airport** with thousands of passengers passing through every hour. - A **traditional SIEM** is like a **security control room** with human operators watching dozens of CCTV screens. They follow a checklist ("if you see someone wearing a red cap at gate B, alert the police"). It works — but humans get tired, miss things, and can only check the screens one by one. - An **AI-powered SIEM** is like giving those operators an **AI assistant** that: 1. **Watches ALL screens simultaneously** (24/7 without fatigue). 2. **Learns what "normal" passenger behavior looks like** (a person walking is normal, a person running with a bag is suspicious). 3. **Correlates events** (the same suspicious person appeared at gate A, then the café, then gate B — likely a threat). 4. **Predicts future threats** based on global airport incident data. 5. **Reduces false alarms** by understanding context (a "red cap" at a sports event gate is normal, not a threat). In short, **AI turns SIEM from a reactive log-aggregator into a proactive, intelligent threat-hunting platform**. --- ### 1.3 Core Components & Standard Metrics | Component | Standard Metric | Value/Range | |---|---|---| | **SIEM Log Ingestion Rate** | EPS (Events Per Second) | **1,000 – 500,000 EPS** for enterprise | | **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | FP / (FP + TN) | **> 80%** in traditional SIEMs; **< 5%** with AI | | **Detection Latency** | Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) | **Days (traditional) → Minutes (AI-driven)** | | **Alert Noise Reduction** | Alert-to-Incident Ratio | **1000:1 (traditional) → 10:1 (AI)** | | **Log Retention** | Compliance duration | **90 days hot, 1–7 years cold** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Industry Benchmarks (NIST/Gartner):** Modern AI-augmented SIEMs report a **detection accuracy of 95–99%** and a **false positive reduction of up to 90%** compared to legacy rule-based systems. --- ### 1.4 Why AI + SIEM? The Motivation 1. **Log Volume Explosion**: Modern enterprises generate **terabytes of logs daily**. Humans cannot process this manually. 2. **Sophisticated Threats**: Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs) evade static rules. 3. **Alert Fatigue**: SOC analysts ignore alerts when 95% are false positives. 4. **Speed Requirement**: Cyberattacks happen in **milliseconds**; response must be faster. 5. **Skill Shortage**: A global shortage of ~**3.5 million cybersecurity professionals** (ISC² 2023 report) demands automation. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Evolution of SIEM — From Logs to Intelligence | Era | Technology | Capability | Limitation | |---|---|---|---| | **1.0 — Log Management (1990s)** | Syslog, flat files | Centralized storage | No analysis | | **2.0 — SIM/SEM (2000s)** | Correlation engines, rules | Event correlation | Static rules, high FPs | | **3.0 — Modern SIEM (2010s)** | Big data, dashboards | Real-time dashboards | Still rule-heavy | | **4.0 — AI-Driven SIEM (2020s+)** | ML, DL, NLP, UEBA | Predictive, autonomous | Data quality dependent | > [!NOTE] > KTU examiners frequently frame questions around "**how AI transforms SIEM from reactive to proactive**" — the four pillars of the 4.0 era are: **Detect, Predict, Respond, Adapt**. --- ### 2.2 Theoretical Foundation: How AI Works Inside a SIEM #### Step 1: Data Ingestion - Logs, flows, packets, endpoint telemetry, threat intel feeds are normalized into a **Common Information Model (CIM)**. #### Step 2: Feature Engineering - Raw events are converted into **numerical vectors** (features) such as: - Login frequency per user - Bytes transferred per session - Geographic anomaly score - Time-of-day deviation #### Step 3: Model Training - **Supervised learning**: Trained on labeled attack/normal data (e.g., KDD Cup 99, NSL-KDD datasets). - **Unsupervised learning**: Discovers unknown patterns (e.g., K-Means, Isolation Forest). - **Semi-supervised learning**: Combines a small set of labels with large unlabeled data. #### Step 4: Inference & Scoring - New events receive a **threat score** between **0.0 and 1.0**. - Scores above a threshold (commonly **0.7**) trigger alerts. #### Step 5: Feedback Loop - Analyst verdicts (True/False Positive) are fed back to **retrain the model** — this is the **closed-loop MLOps cycle** for SIEM. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following equations are **mandatory** for KTU board exam derivations in this topic. | # | Concept | Formula | Description | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$$ | Fraction of legitimate events falsely flagged | | 2 | **True Positive Rate (Recall / Sensitivity)** | $$TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$ | Fraction of real attacks correctly detected | | 3 | **Precision** | $$Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$ | How trustworthy an alert is | | 4 | **F1-Score (harmonic mean)** | $$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision + Recall}$$ | Balanced metric for imbalanced threat data | | 5 | **Anomaly Score (Z-Score)** | $$Z = \frac{\vert x - \mu \vert}{\sigma}$$ | Distance of an event from the mean in standard deviations | | 6 | **Euclidean Distance (KNN Anomaly)** | $$d(x, y) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - y_i)^2}$$ | Used in nearest-neighbor anomaly detection | | 7 | **Shannon Entropy (Information Gain)** | $$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$$ | Measures randomness in network traffic (high entropy → encryption or attack) | | 8 | **Bayes' Theorem (Threat Probability)** | $$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$ | Updates threat probability given new evidence | | 9 | **Log Volume Reduction Ratio** | $$R = \frac{\text{Original Logs}}{\text{Retained Post-Correlation Events}}$$ | SIEM efficiency metric | | 10 | **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** | $$MTTD = \frac{\sum(\text{Detection Time} - \text{Attack Time})}{N}$$ | Average detection latency | --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Banking & Finance**: Detects fraudulent transactions via anomaly detection on user behavior. - **Healthcare**: Protects Electronic Health Records (EHR) from ransomware. - **Government/Defense**: SOCAR, Splunk UBA used by intelligence agencies. - **Cloud (AWS/Azure/GCP)**: AI-SIEMs integrate with cloud-native logs (CloudTrail, Azure Monitor). - **IoT/OT Security**: AI detects anomalous PLC/SCADA commands in industrial control systems. > [!NOTE] > Real platforms implementing these concepts: **Splunk Enterprise Security, Microsoft Sentinel, IBM QRadar, Elastic Security, Exabeam, LogRhythm, Sumo Logic**. --- ### 2.5 Core AI Techniques Applied in SIEM | AI/ML Technique | SIEM Use Case | |---|---| | **Supervised Classification (Random Forest, XGBoost)** | Malware family classification | | **Unsupervised Clustering (K-Means, DBSCAN)** | User behavior grouping | | **Anomaly Detection (Isolation Forest, Autoencoders)** | Insider threat, zero-day attacks | | **Deep Learning (LSTM, CNN, Transformers)** | Network traffic sequence analysis | | **Reinforcement Learning** | Automated incident response (SOAR) | | **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** | Threat intel parsing from reports/CTI feeds | | **Graph Neural Networks (GNN)** | Lateral movement path prediction | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example: Z-Score Anomaly Detection Derivation **Problem:** A SIEM collects login-time data for a user. Over 30 days, the mean login time is **μ = 09:00 (540 minutes from midnight)** with standard deviation **σ = 15 minutes**. On a flagged day, the user logs in at **07:30 (450 minutes)**.
