Compute the Z-score and classify the event. --- ### Step 1 — State the Formula We use the standard Z-score formula from the KTU Formula Sheet: $$ Z = \frac{\vert x - \mu \vert}{\sigma} $$ Where: - $x$ = observed event value - $\mu$ = population mean - $\sigma$ = population standard deviation --- ### Step 2 — Substitute the Values Given: - $x = 450$ minutes (07:30 AM) - $\mu = 540$ minutes (09:00 AM) - $\sigma = 15$ minutes $$ Z = \frac{\vert 450 - 540 \vert}{15} $$ --- ### Step 3 — Compute the Numerator $$ 450 - 540 = -90 $$ Take the absolute value (anomaly magnitude, not direction): $$ \vert -90 \vert = 90 $$ --- ### Step 4 — Final Calculation $$ Z = \frac{90}{15} = 6.0 $$ --- ### Step 5 — Interpretation A Z-score of **6.0** means the event is **6 standard deviations** away from the mean. In a normal distribution, **99.7% of data lies within ±3σ** (the 3-sigma rule). A value of $Z = 6$ is **virtually impossible under normal behavior** — the SIEM must **flag this as a high-confidence anomaly**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Always show the **3-sigma rule** in your answer: $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ covers 99.7% of legitimate events. Any event with $\vert Z \vert > 3$ is a candidate alert. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example: Bayes' Theorem for Threat Correlation **Problem:** A SIEM's intrusion detection system has prior knowledge that **2% of network packets** in the organization are malicious: $P(M) = 0.02$. The AI classifier has the following performance: - **True Positive Rate (Sensitivity):** $P(\text{Alert} \mid M) = 0.95$ - **False Positive Rate:** $P(\text{Alert} \mid \neg M) = 0.05$ A packet is flagged by the AI. Compute the posterior probability $P(M \mid \text{Alert})$ that it is truly malicious. --- ### Step 1 — Bayes' Theorem $$ P(M \mid \text{Alert}) = \frac{P(\text{Alert} \mid M) \cdot P(M)}{P(\text{Alert})} $$ --- ### Step 2 — Total Probability of an Alert $$ P(\text{Alert}) = P(\text{Alert} \mid M) \cdot P(M) + P(\text{Alert} \mid \neg M) \cdot P(\neg M) $$ We know $P(\neg M) = 1 - 0.02 = 0.98$. $$ P(\text{Alert}) = (0.95)(0.02) + (0.05)(0.98) $$ $$ P(\text{Alert}) = 0.019 + 0.049 $$ $$ P(\text{Alert}) = 0.068 $$ --- ### Step 3 — Apply Bayes' Theorem $$ P(M \mid \text{Alert}) = \frac{(0.95)(0.02)}{0.068} $$ $$ P(M \mid \text{Alert}) = \frac{0.019}{0.068} $$ $$ P(M \mid \text{Alert}) \approx 0.2794 $$ --- ### Step 4 — Interpretation Even with a 95% accurate AI, an alert has only a **~28% chance of being a real attack**. This is the **Base Rate Fallacy** — a critical KTU concept. The implication: > [!WARNING] > **AI alerts alone are not enough.** SIEMs must use **multi-signal correlation** (e.g., alert + unusual login + new geo-location) to raise the posterior probability above **0.9** before escalating to analysts. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: AI Technique vs. Threat Type | Threat Type | Best AI Technique | Why | |---|---|---| | **DDoS Attack** | LSTM / Time-Series Anomaly | Detects traffic pattern deviation | | **Insider Threat** | UEBA + Isolation Forest | Learns per-user behavior baseline | | **Phishing Email** | NLP + Transformer (BERT) | Understands email semantics | | **Malware Detection** | Random Forest / CNN on binaries | Static + dynamic feature classification | | **Lateral Movement** | Graph Neural Network | Models privilege escalation paths | | **Zero-Day Exploit** | Autoencoder (reconstruction error) | Flags unknown behavior | | **Brute Force Login** | Z-Score / Statistical Threshold | Detects frequency outliers | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level AI-SIEM Architecture (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Data Sources
Servers Endpoints Network Cloud Apps] --> B[Log Collector
Syslog Agent Beats] B --> C[Normalization Layer
Common Information Model CIM] C --> D[Storage Layer
Hot Warm Cold Tiers] D --> E[AI ML Engine
Supervised Unsupervised Deep Learning] E --> F[Correlation and Scoring
Threat Score 0 to 1] F --> G[Alerting and Triage
SOAR Integration] G --> H[SOC Analyst Dashboard] H --> I[Feedback Loop
Analyst Verdicts Retrain Model] I --> E E --> J[Threat Intelligence Enrichment
CTI Feeds STIX TAXII] J --> F H --> K[Incident Response
Containment Eradication Recovery] ``` --- ### 4.2 AI-SIEM Detection Pipeline (Mermaid Sequence) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Step 1 Ingest Logs] --> P2[Step 2 Parse and Normalize] P2 --> P3[Step 3 Feature Extraction] P3 --> P4[Step 4 ML Model Inference] P4 --> P5{Step 5 Score Threshold} P5 -- Score above 0.7 --> P6[Step 6 Generate Alert] P5 -- Score below 0.7 --> P7[Step 7 Log to Cold Storage] P6 --> P8[Step 8 SOC Triage] P8 --> P9[Step 9 Analyst Verdict] P9 --> P10[Step 10 Retrain Model] P10 --> P4 ``` --- ### 4.3 AI Model Selection Decision Tree (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TB Q1{Is labeled data available?} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is data text based?} Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Use NLP
BERT or LSTM] Q2 -- No --> Q4[Use Supervised Learning
Random Forest XGBoost] Q1 -- No --> Q5{Need to detect known attacks?} Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[Use Signature Based AI
Hybrid Model] Q5 -- No --> Q7[Use Unsupervised Anomaly
Isolation Forest or Autoencoder] Q3 --> R[Output: Threat Score] Q4 --> R Q6 --> R Q7 --> R ``` --- ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture | Block Layer | Component | Function | |---|---|---| | **L1 — Data Sources** | Firewalls, IDS/IPS, EDR, Cloud APIs, Apps | Generate raw security telemetry | | **L2 — Collection** | Log shippers (Beats, Fluentd, Syslog) | Transport logs to SIEM | | **L3 — Normalization** | Parsers, CIM mappers | Convert vendor-specific formats to a unified schema | | **L4 — Storage** | Elasticsearch, Hadoop, Data Lake | Index and persist events | | **L5 — AI/ML Engine** | Python/Spark MLlib, TensorFlow | Train and infer models | | **L6 — Correlation** | Rule + Graph engines | Multi-event attack chain reconstruction | | **L7 — Presentation** | Dashboards, reports, alerts | SOC interface | | **L8 — Response** | SOAR playbooks, EDR actions | Automated containment | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "draw the AI-SIEM architecture," always label **all 8 layers** with at least one example technology. This is a guaranteed 7-mark question in Part B. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q1. Define SIEM. How does AI enhance traditional SIEM capabilities? (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) is a centralized platform that collects, normalizes, correlates, and analyzes security logs from heterogeneous sources to detect threats and support compliance. **AI enhances SIEM by:** 1. **Automating anomaly detection** using ML models instead of static correlation rules. 2. **Reducing false positives** via behavioral baselining (UEBA). 3. **Predicting emerging threats** using historical and global threat intel. --- **Q2. What is UEBA and how does it differ from rule-based correlation in SIEM? (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **UEBA (User and Entity Behavior Analytics)** uses machine learning to build per-user/per-entity behavioral baselines (login times, locations, accessed resources). It flags deviations as insider threats or compromised accounts. **Difference from rule-based correlation:** | Aspect | Rule-Based | UEBA (AI) | |---|---|---| | Logic | Predefined IF-THEN | Statistical + ML models | | Adaptability | Static | Self-learning | | Detection | Known patterns | Unknown anomalies | | False Positives | Very high | Significantly lower | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Paper]` --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1** **Q. (a)** Explain the architecture of an AI-driven SIEM system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each layer in detecting advanced threats. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** An AI-driven SIEM consists of **8 functional layers**, each performing a specialized role: 1. **Data Source Layer:** Collects raw logs from firewalls, IDS/IPS, EDR, cloud platforms, and applications. 2. **Collection Layer:** Log shippers (e.g., Beats, Fluentd) transport events to the central platform in near real-time. 3. **Normalization Layer:** Parsers map vendor-specific formats into a **Common Information Model (CIM)** for uniform processing. 4. **Storage Layer:** Multi-tier storage — hot (SSD, fast queries), warm (HDD), cold (object storage, long-term compliance). 5. **AI/ML Engine:** Runs supervised (Random Forest), unsupervised (K-Means, Isolation Forest), and deep learning (LSTM, Autoencoders) models for anomaly detection, classification, and prediction. 6. **Correlation Layer:** Combines AI scores with rule-based logic and graph analytics to reconstruct multi-stage attack chains (e.g., kill-chain mapping). 7. **Presentation Layer:** Dashboards, alerts, and reports for SOC analysts. 8. **Response Layer:** Integrates with **SOAR** (Security Orchestration, Automation, Response) for automated containment — e.g., isolating an infected endpoint. **Role in Advanced Threat Detection:** AI-driven SIEM detects zero-day attacks, insider threats, and APTs by recognizing subtle deviations invisible to static rules. --- **Q. (b)** With a suitable example, derive the Z-score anomaly detection formula and explain how it is used in a SIEM to flag login anomalies. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Formula:** $$ Z = \frac{\vert x - \mu \vert}{\sigma} $$ **Step 2 — Example Calculation:** A user's average login time over 30 days is $\mu = 09:00$ (540 min), $\sigma = 15$ min. On day 31, login occurs at $x = 07:30$ (450 min). $$ Z = \frac{\vert 450 - 540 \vert}{15} = \frac{90}{15} = 6.0 $$ **Step 3 — Interpretation:** A Z-score of 6.0 is far beyond the **3-sigma rule** ($\mu \pm 3\sigma$ covers 99.7% of legitimate data). The SIEM classifies this as a **high-confidence anomaly** and raises an alert. **Step 4 — SIEM Workflow:** The AI engine computes Z-scores for every user activity metric (login time, file access volume, geo-location shifts) and feeds the score into the **threat scoring pipeline** (0.0–1.0 scale). A composite score above 0.7 triggers a SOAR playbook for investigation. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2** **Q. (a)** Discuss the various Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning techniques used in modern SIEM platforms. Provide one use case for each. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** | AI/ML Technique | SIEM Use Case | |---|---| | **Supervised Learning (Random Forest, SVM)** | Classifying known malware families based on extracted features | | **Unsupervised Learning (K-Means, DBSCAN)** | Grouping users by behavior to detect outliers (insider threats) | | **Deep Learning (LSTM, CNN)** | Detecting time-series anomalies in network traffic (DDoS prediction) | | **Autoencoders** | Identifying zero-day attacks via reconstruction error | | **Natural Language Processing (BERT)** | Parsing threat intelligence reports to extract IoCs automatically | | **Reinforcement Learning** | Optimizing automated incident response playbooks (SOAR) | | **Graph Neural Networks** | Mapping lateral movement paths across the enterprise network | **Conclusion:** Modern SIEMs are no longer single-technique systems; they employ an **ensemble of AI models** tailored to specific threat categories for maximum detection efficacy. --- **Q. (b)** Apply Bayes' Theorem to a SIEM scenario where the AI generates an alert. Given $P(M) = 0.02$, sensitivity = 0.95, and false positive rate = 0.05, compute the posterior probability and explain the significance of the result. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Bayes' Theorem:** $$ P(M \mid \text{Alert}) = \frac{P(\text{Alert} \mid M) \cdot P(M)}{P(\text{Alert})} $$ **Step 2 — Compute $P(\text{Alert})$ using Total Probability:** $$ P(\text{Alert}) = (0.95)(0.02) + (0.05)(0.98) = 0.019 + 0.049 = 0.068 $$ **Step 3 — Substitute:** $$ P(M \mid \text{Alert}) = \frac{0.019}{0.068} \approx 0.2794 $$ **Step 4 — Significance:** - An alert with 95% accuracy has only a **27.94% chance** of being a real attack — this is the **Base Rate Fallacy**. - A single AI alert is **insufficient** for action. - SIEMs must apply **multi-signal correlation** (e.g., alert + unusual geolocation + privilege escalation) to raise the posterior above 0.9 before triggering automated response. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to state units** in EPS/log rate calculations → **−1 mark**. > 2. **Skipping the 3-sigma justification** when computing Z-scores → **−1 mark**. > 3. **Confusing SIEM with SOAR** — SIEM detects, SOAR responds. Examiners deduct for mixing roles. > 4. **Not mentioning feedback loop / MLOps** in architecture questions → **−2 marks**. > 5. **Using vertical pipes `|x|` in markdown tables** breaks the table → always use `\vert` or `\mid`. > 6. **Ignoring the Base Rate Fallacy** in Bayes' theorem questions → **−2 marks** (it's a high-weight concept). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — AI and SIEM (Module 4)** - **SIEM =** Security Information and Event Management — centralized log collection, correlation, and alerting. - **AI in SIEM =** ML + DL + NLP for anomaly detection, behavioral baselining, and predictive threat intelligence. - **4 Eras of SIEM:** Log Management → SIM/SEM → Modern SIEM → **AI-Driven SIEM (current)**. - **Core AI Techniques:** Supervised, Unsupervised, Deep Learning (LSTM/CNN/Autoencoder), NLP, GNN, Reinforcement Learning. - **Key Formulas:** Z-score, Bayes' Theorem, F1-score, FPR/TPR, Shannon Entropy, MTTD. - **3-Sigma Rule:** $\vert Z \vert > 3$ → flag as anomaly (99.7% of normal data lies within ±3σ). - **Base Rate Fallacy:** A single AI alert is unreliable when the prior probability of attack is low (~2%); multi-signal correlation is mandatory. - **SIEM ≠ SOAR:** SIEM detects; SOAR automates response. - **UEBA =** User and Entity Behavior Analytics — a core AI-SIEM capability. - **Feedback Loop:** Analyst verdicts retrain ML models (closed-loop MLOps). - **Common Platforms:** Splunk ES, Microsoft Sentinel, IBM QRadar, Elastic Security, Exabeam. - **Log Ingestion Standard:** Enterprise SIEMs handle **1,000 – 500,000 EPS**. - **KTU Hot Terms:** Predictive analytics, Threat scoring, Common Information Model (CIM), Kill-chain correlation, MLOps for cybersecurity. - **Industry Benchmark:** AI reduces SIEM false positives by **up to 90%** and improves MTTD from days to minutes.

topics.title: Applying AI Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT755 - CYBER THREAT INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applying AI Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Does "Applying AI Concepts" Mean in Cyber Threat Intelligence?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Applying AI Concepts in Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) refers to the systematic integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI), Machine Learning (ML), Deep Learning (DL), and Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques to automate the collection, correlation, classification, prediction, and remediation of cyber threats. It transforms raw threat data into actionable intelligence through pattern recognition, anomaly detection, and predictive analytics.*

In the KTU 2024 PECCT755 syllabus, this topic sits at the intersection of **data science** and **cybersecurity operations**, forming the analytical engine of modern Security Operations Centers (SOCs).

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of AI in CTI

| Pillar | Focus Area | Typical Use in CTI |
|---|---|---|
| **Machine Learning (ML)** | Statistical learning from data | Malware classification, phishing detection |
| **Deep Learning (DL)** | Multi-layer neural representations | Network intrusion detection, log analysis |
| **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** | Understanding human language | Threat report parsing, IOC extraction |

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy: The AI as a Tireless Security Detective

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Cyber Detective Who Never Sleeps"**

Imagine hiring a detective to watch over a city. Traditional security (signature-based) is like a detective with a **mugshot book** — they can only catch criminals who *already exist* in the book. An AI-powered system, however, is a detective who:
1. **Learns** the daily rhythm of every street (normal behavior).
2. **Notices** when someone walks against the crowd (anomaly detection).
3. **Reads** newspapers from across the world in seconds (NLP on threat feeds).
4. **Predicts** which bank is likely to be robbed next Friday (predictive analytics).

This detective gets **smarter with every crime witnessed**, never gets tired, and can process **petabytes of logs** in minutes.

## 1.4 Why AI is Now Indispensable in CTI

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Drivers of AI Adoption in CTI:**
> - **Volume:** Enterprise networks generate over **10,000+ alerts per day** (Cisco, 2024). Human analysts cannot triage this manually.
> - **Velocity:** Threats evolve in **minutes**, not months. Signature databases become obsolete almost instantly.
> - **Variety:** Threat data comes in **unstructured formats** — PDFs, tweets, forum posts, code repositories, dark-web dumps.
> - **Veracity:** Distinguishing true threats from noise requires probabilistic reasoning — a strength of ML.

## 1.5 Core AI Subfields Used in CTI (KTU Syllabus Mapping)

1. **Supervised Learning** — Training on labeled malware/benign samples.
2. **Unsupervised Learning** — Discovering novel threat clusters without labels.
3. **Reinforcement Learning** — Adaptive response agents in automated defense.
4. **Deep Neural Networks (DNN, CNN, RNN, LSTM)** — Sequence & image-based threat detection.
5. **Transformers & LLMs** — Parsing unstructured threat reports (e.g., BERT, GPT-style models).
6. **Graph Neural Networks (GNN)** — Modeling threat-actor relationships and attack chains.
7. **Generative AI** — Synthetic threat simulation, adversarial testing.

## 1.6 Key Performance Metrics (Always Bolded for KTU)

- **Accuracy** — Overall correctness of the model.
- **Precision** — Of the threats flagged, how many are real?
- **Recall (Detection Rate)** — Of all real threats, how many did we catch?
- **F1-Score** — Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall.
- **False Positive Rate (FPR)** — Cost of analyst fatigue.
- **Area Under Curve (AUC-ROC)** — Model's discriminative power.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The AI-CTI Operational Pipeline

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU 2024 Framework — The AI-Augmented CTI Lifecycle:**
>
> `Data Ingestion → Feature Engineering → Model Training → Inference → Actionable Intelligence → Feedback Loop`

Each stage introduces a specific AI concept. Let us break them down.

## 2.2 Stage 1: Data Ingestion & Pre-processing

- **Inputs:** Network flows, system logs, EDR alerts, threat feeds (STIX/TAXII), OSINT.
- **AI Role:** Automated parsing, deduplication, normalization.
- **Key Challenge:** Data imbalance (1 million benign events vs. 5 real attacks).

> [!NOTE]
> **Imbalance Ratio (IR):**
> $$\text{IR} = \frac{N_{\text{majority}}}{N_{\text{minority}}}$$
> where $N_{\text{majority}}$ is the count of benign samples and $N_{\text{minority}}$ is the count of malicious samples. An IR > 100 indicates severe class imbalance.

## 2.3 Stage 2: Feature Engineering

Features convert raw data into numerical vectors that ML models can consume.

| Feature Type | Example in CTI | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Statistical** | Packet size mean, std-dev | Aggregated network behavior |
| **Temporal** | Inter-arrival time, burst rate | Time-series patterns |
| **Lexical** | N-grams of URLs, opcode frequency | Content-based signatures |
| **Behavioral** | API call sequence, process tree depth | Dynamic analysis features |
| **Graph-based** | Node centrality in communication graph | Relationship features |

## 2.4 Stage 3: Machine Learning Model Categories

### A) Supervised Learning

The model is trained on labeled data $(x_i, y_i)$ where $y_i$ is the known class (malicious/benign).

**Decision Boundary (Logistic Regression example):**
$$P(y=1 \mid x) = \sigma(w^T x + b) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(w^T x + b)}}$$
where $\sigma$ is the sigmoid function, $w$ is the weight vector, and $b$ is the bias.

**Common Algorithms in CTI:**
- **Random Forest (RF)** — Ensemble of decision trees; robust to overfitting.
- **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** — Effective in high-dimensional feature spaces.
- **Naive Bayes** — Probabilistic baseline for spam/phishing classification.
- **Gradient Boosting (XGBoost, LightGBM)** — State-of-the-art for tabular threat data.

### B) Unsupervised Learning

No labels available — used for anomaly detection and clustering.

**K-Means Objective:**
$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$$
where $\mu_i$ is the centroid of cluster $C_i$. Goal: minimize intra-cluster variance.

**Density-Based (DBSCAN):**
- Core point: at least **MinPts** neighbors within radius $\varepsilon$.
- Used to detect **outlier attacks** that do not belong to any cluster.

### C) Deep Learning

**Artificial Neuron:**
$$y = \phi\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b\right)$$
where $\phi$ is the **activation function** (ReLU, Sigmoid, Tanh).

**Common Architectures in CTI:**

| Architecture | Best For | CTI Application |
|---|---|---|
| **MLP (Multi-Layer Perceptron)** | Tabular features | Static malware classification |
| **CNN (Convolutional NN)** | Spatial patterns | Malware visualized as images |
| **RNN / LSTM** | Sequential data | Network traffic sequences, log analysis |
| **Autoencoder** | Reconstruction-based anomaly detection | Zero-day threat detection |
| **Transformer / BERT** | Text understanding | Threat report summarization |
| **GNN (Graph Neural Net)** | Relational data | Attack chain reconstruction |

### D) Reinforcement Learning (RL)

- **Agent** takes actions in a cybersecurity environment.
- **Reward function** = threat neutralized − false alarm penalty.
- Used in **Automated Incident Response** and **moving target defense**.

## 2.5 Stage 4: NLP for Unstructured Threat Intelligence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NLP Pipeline in CTI:**
> `Tokenization → Stop-word Removal → Stemming/Lemmatization → Named Entity Recognition (NER) → Relation Extraction → IOC Mapping`

**TF-IDF (Term Frequency – Inverse Document Frequency):**
$$\text{TF-IDF}(t, d) = \text{TF}(t, d) \times \log\left(\frac{N}{\text{DF}(t)}\right)$$

- $\text{TF}(t, d)$ = frequency of term $t$ in document $d$.
- $N$ = total number of documents.
- $\text{DF}(t)$ = number of documents containing $t$.

**Word Embedding (Word2Vec cosine similarity):**
$$\text{sim}(u, v) = \frac{u \cdot v}{\Vert u \Vert \cdot \Vert v \Vert}$$
Used to find semantically similar Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) across reports.

**Modern Approach — Transformer Attention:**
$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$$
where $Q, K, V$ are Query, Key, and Value matrices; $d_k$ is the key dimensionality.

## 2.6 Stage 5: Evaluation Metrics (The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Formula Sheet — All Metrics You MUST Memorize**

| Metric | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness |
| **Precision** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Trustworthiness of an alert |
| **Recall (TPR)** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Coverage of real threats |
| **F1-Score** | $\frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Balance of P and R |
| **FPR** | $\frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | False alarm rate |
| **Specificity (TNR)** | $\frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | True negative rate |
| **MCC** | $\frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | Matthews Correlation Coefficient — best for imbalanced data |
| **AUC-ROC** | $\int_0^1 \text{TPR}(\text{FPR}) \, d(\text{FPR})$ | Discriminative power |

Where $TP, TN, FP, FN$ are True/False Positives/Negatives.

## 2.7 Stage 6: Adversarial AI — The Dark Side

> [!WARNING]
> **Adversarial Machine Learning in CTI:**
> Attackers craft inputs (e.g., slightly perturbed malware binaries) to **evade detection models**. Key attack types:
> - **Evasion Attack:** Modify malware so it is misclassified as benign.
> - **Poisoning Attack:** Inject malicious samples into training data.
> - **Model Inversion:** Steal model parameters via query probing.

This is why KTU 2024 includes **robustness testing** as part of AI-CTI evaluation.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Google Chronicle / VirusTotal:** Uses ML to score millions of files daily.
- **Microsoft Defender ATP:** LSTM models for endpoint behavioral detection.
- **MITRE ATT&CK + AI:** Automated TTP (Tactics, Techniques, Procedures) mapping.
- **Splunk UBA (User Behavior Analytics):** Unsupervised anomaly detection.
- **Darktrace:** Unsupervised ML for self-learning threat detection.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Logistic Regression to the Sigmoid Classifier

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters:**
> Logistic regression is the **most fundamental classifier** in CTI (phishing, malware triage). Examiners love testing this.

**Step 1 — Linear Combination:**
A linear model produces a real-valued score.
$$z = w^T x + b = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b$$

**Step 2 — Mapping to Probability:**
We need a function that maps $z \in (-\infty, \infty)$ to a probability in $[0, 1]$. The **sigmoid** function satisfies this:
$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

**Step 3 — Final Hypothesis:**
$$h_w(x) = P(y = 1 \mid x; w) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(w^T x + b)}}$$

**Step 4 — Decision Rule:**
$$y_{\text{pred}} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } h_w(x) \geq 0.5 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Note: $h_w(x) = 0.5$ when $w^T x + b = 0$, which defines the **decision boundary**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cross-Entropy Loss Function

**Step 1 — Likelihood for $m$ training samples:**
$$L(w) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} h_w(x_i)^{y_i} \cdot (1 - h_w(x_i))^{1 - y_i}$$

**Step 2 — Take the log (Log-Likelihood):**
$$\ell(w) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y_i \log h_w(x_i) + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - h_w(x_i)) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Convert to a Loss to Minimize (negate and average):**
$$J(w) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y_i \log h_w(x_i) + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - h_w(x_i)) \right]$$

This is the **Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE) Loss** — the standard training objective for binary threat classification.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Gradient Descent Update Rule

**Step 1 — Compute the gradient of $J(w)$ with respect to $w$:**
$$\frac{\partial J(w)}{\partial w} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_w(x_i) - y_i) x_i$$

**Step 2 — Apply the update rule with learning rate $\alpha$:**
$$w \leftarrow w - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial J(w)}{\partial w}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting:**
$$w \leftarrow w - \frac{\alpha}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_w(x_i) - y_i) x_i$$

This iterative process is repeated until $J(w)$ converges to a minimum.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: The F1-Score Justification

**Step 1 — Start with Precision $P$ and Recall $R$:**
$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}, \quad R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

**Step 2 — Harmonic mean definition:**
$$F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify by common denominator:**
$$F_1 = \frac{2 P R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

**Step 4 — Why harmonic mean and not arithmetic?**
The harmonic mean **punishes extreme imbalance**. A model with $P = 1.0$ and $R = 0.1$ gives:
$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.1}{1.0 + 0.1} \approx 0.182$$
This is far lower than the arithmetic mean (0.55), correctly indicating a poor model.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: ROC-AUC and the Discriminative Threshold

**Step 1 — For varying classification threshold $\tau \in [0, 1]$, compute:**
$$\text{TPR}(\tau) = \frac{TP(\tau)}{TP(\tau) + FN(\tau)}, \quad \text{FPR}(\tau) = \frac{FP(\tau)}{FP(\tau) + TN(\tau)}$$

**Step 2 — Plot TPR vs FPR — the ROC Curve.**

**Step 3 — Compute AUC using the trapezoidal rule:**
$$\text{AUC} = \int_0^1 \text{TPR}(\text{FPR}) \, d(\text{FPR}) \approx \sum_{i} \frac{(\text{FPR}_{i+1} - \text{FPR}_i)(\text{TPR}_{i+1} + \text{TPR}_i)}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
- $\text{AUC} = 1.0$ — perfect classifier.
- $\text{AUC} = 0.5$ — random guessing.
- $\text{AUC} < 0.5$ — worse than random (labels may be swapped).

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Autoencoder for Anomaly Detection

**Step 1 — Encoder maps input $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ to latent $z \in \mathbb{R}^p$ with $p \ll n$:**
$$z = f_{\text{enc}}(x) = \phi(W_e x + b_e)$$

**Step 2 — Decoder reconstructs:**
$$\hat{x} = f_{\text{dec}}(z) = \phi(W_d z + b_d)$$

**Step 3 — Reconstruction error (MSE):**
$$\mathcal{L}(x, \hat{x}) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2$$

**Step 4 — Anomaly Score:**
$$\text{Score}(x) = \mathcal{L}(x, \hat{x})$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**
$$y_{\text{pred}} = \begin{cases} \text{Malicious} & \text{if } \text{Score}(x) > \tau \\ \text{Benign} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The model is trained **only on benign data**, so malicious inputs yield high reconstruction error — this is the foundation of **zero-day detection**.

## 3.7 Derivation 7: Backpropagation for a 2-Layer Neural Network

**Step 1 — Forward Pass:**
$$z^{(1)} = W^{(1)} x + b^{(1)}, \quad a^{(1)} = \phi(z^{(1)})$$
$$z^{(2)} = W^{(2)} a^{(1)} + b^{(2)}, \quad \hat{y} = \sigma(z^{(2)})$$

**Step 2 — Loss (BCE):**
$$\mathcal{L} = -\left[ y \log \hat{y} + (1 - y) \log(1 - \hat{y}) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Output Layer Gradient:**
$$\delta^{(2)} = \hat{y} - y$$

**Step 4 — Hidden Layer Gradient:**
$$\delta^{(1)} = \left( W^{(2)T} \delta^{(2)} \right) \odot \phi'(z^{(1)})$$
where $\odot$ is the element-wise (Hadamard) product.

**Step 5 — Parameter Updates:**
$$W^{(2)} \leftarrow W^{(2)} - \alpha \cdot \delta^{(2)} a^{(1)T}, \quad b^{(2)} \leftarrow b^{(2)} - \alpha \cdot \delta^{(2)}$$
$$W^{(1)} \leftarrow W^{(1)} - \alpha \cdot \delta^{(1)} x^T, \quad b^{(1)} \leftarrow b^{(1)} - \alpha \cdot \delta^{(1)}$$

This is the **backpropagation algorithm** — the engine that trains virtually every neural network in modern CTI systems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End AI-Augmented CTI Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Threat Data Sources] --> A2[Data Ingestion Layer]
    A2 --> A3[Preprocessing and Normalization]
    A3 --> A4[Feature Engineering Module]
    A4 --> A5{AI Model Selection}
    A5 --> A6[Supervised Classifier]
    A5 --> A7[Unsupervised Anomaly Detector]
    A5 --> A8[Deep Learning Engine]
    A5 --> A9[NLP Threat Report Parser]
    A6 --> B1[Prediction and Scoring]
    A7 --> B1
    A8 --> B1
    A9 --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Threat Intelligence Dashboard]
    B1 --> C2[SOAR Automated Response]
    B1 --> C3[Analyst Triage Queue]
    C1 --> D1[Feedback Loop]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> A3
```

## 4.2 Machine Learning Workflow for CTI

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step1 Collect Logs and IOCs] --> S2[Step2 Label and Annotate]
    S2 --> S3[Step3 Split Train and Test]
    S3 --> S4[Step4 Train Model]
    S4 --> S5[Step5 Validate and Tune]
    S5 --> S6[Step6 Deploy to SOC]
    S6 --> S7[Step7 Monitor and Retrain]
    S7 --> S2
```

## 4.3 Anomaly Detection Architecture (Autoencoder)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INP[Input Network Traffic x] --> ENC[Encoder Compression to Latent z]
    ENC --> DEC[Decoder Reconstruction xhat]
    DEC --> ERR[Reconstruction Error MSE]
    ERR --> TH{Threshold Check}
    TH -->|Error above threshold| ALERT[Flag as Threat]
    TH -->|Error below threshold| OK[Classify as Benign]
```

## 4.4 NLP Pipeline for Threat Report Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[Raw Threat Report PDF or Web Page] --> N2[Tokenization]
    N2 --> N3[Stop Word Removal]
    N3 --> N4[Lemmatization]
    N4 --> N5[Named Entity Recognition NER]
    N5 --> N6[IOC Extraction IPs Hashes CVEs]
    N6 --> N7[MITRE ATT and CK Mapping]
    N7 --> N8[Structured STIX Output]
```

## 4.5 Adversarial Attack Surface on an AI CTI System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AT1[Adversarial Input Perturbation] --> AT2[Evade Classifier]
    AT3[Poisoned Training Data] --> AT4[Corrupt Model Weights]
    AT5[Model Stealing Queries] --> AT6[Replicate Detector]
    AT2 --> MDL[ML CTI Model]
    AT4 --> MDL
    AT6 --> MDL
    MDL --> OUT[Compromised Threat Decisions]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for the Diagrams:**
> - Boxes with **StepN** prefix denote sequential training operations.
> - Diamond nodes (e.g., `{Threshold Check}`) represent **decision points**.
> - Arrows returning to earlier stages (e.g., `S7 → S2`) indicate **continuous learning / feedback loops**, which are central to production-grade CTI systems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📚 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PECCT755]**
> *Define Artificial Intelligence in the context of Cyber Threat Intelligence. List any three AI techniques used in CTI.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** AI in CTI refers to the use of intelligent algorithms that learn from cybersecurity data to automatically detect, classify, predict, and respond to threats without being explicitly programmed for each scenario.
- **Three Techniques (2 Marks):**
  1. **Supervised Machine Learning** — e.g., Random Forest for malware classification.
  2. **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** — e.g., extracting IOCs from threat reports.
  3. **Deep Learning** — e.g., LSTM networks for sequential network intrusion detection.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional II]**
> *Differentiate between supervised and unsupervised learning with a CTI example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Labels** | Requires labeled data (malicious/benign) | No labels required |
| **Goal** | Learn a mapping $f: X \to Y$ | Discover hidden patterns/clusters |
| **CTI Example** | Training a classifier on 10,000 labeled malware samples to predict new malware family (1.5 Marks) | Using K-Means to group network flows to detect novel attack patterns without prior knowledge (1.5 Marks) |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of an AI-based Cyber Threat Intelligence system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each layer. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

An AI-based CTI system consists of **five major layers** (7 Marks distributed as 1 Mark per layer + 2 Marks for integration explanation):

1. **Data Collection Layer** — Gathers data from network sensors, SIEM, EDR, threat feeds (STIX/TAXII), OSINT, and dark-web monitors. Uses **ETL (Extract, Transform, Load)** pipelines to standardize formats.

2. **Preprocessing & Feature Engineering Layer** — Cleanses noise, handles missing values, normalizes features, and converts raw data into numerical vectors. Applies **TF-IDF**, **word embeddings**, or **graph features** depending on the modality.

3. **AI/ML Model Layer** — The analytical core. Contains:
   - **Supervised models** (RF, XGBoost) for known threat classification.
   - **Unsupervised models** (Autoencoders, Isolation Forest) for anomaly detection.
   - **Deep models** (CNN, LSTM, Transformers) for complex pattern recognition.
   - **NLP models** (BERT, spaCy) for parsing threat reports.

4. **Intelligence & Decision Layer** — Converts model outputs into **risk scores**, **threat levels**, and **MITRE ATT&CK mappings**. Generates actionable alerts prioritized by severity.

5. **Response & Feedback Layer** — Integrates with **SOAR (Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response)** platforms. Sends enriched IOCs to firewalls, EDRs, and ticketing systems. Analyst feedback retrains models (closed-loop learning).

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain how **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** is used to extract Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) from unstructured threat reports. Include the relevant formulas. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Input Acquisition (1 Mark):**
Threat reports arrive as unstructured text (PDFs, blogs, advisories). Example: *"The APT-29 group used a phishing email containing a link to malware[.]xyz which contacted IP 198.51.100.42 and exploited CVE-2024-12345."*

**Step 2 — Preprocessing (1 Mark):**
- **Tokenization** splits text into words/tokens.
- **Stop-word removal** filters "the", "a", "and".
- **Lemmatization** reduces "exploited" → "exploit".

**Step 3 — TF-IDF Vectorization (2 Marks):**
$$\text{TF-IDF}(t, d) = \text{TF}(t, d) \times \log\left(\frac{N}{\text{DF}(t)}\right)$$
This converts tokens into weighted numerical vectors, emphasizing rare but informative terms like "CVE-2024-12345".

**Step 4 — Named Entity Recognition (NER) (2 Marks):**
A pre-trained NER model (e.g., spaCy, BERT-NER) identifies and classifies entities:
- `198.51.100.42` → **IP_ADDRESS**
- `malware[.]xyz` → **DOMAIN**
- `CVE-2024-12345` → **VULNERABILITY_ID**
- `APT-29` → **THREAT_ACTOR**

**Step 5 — IOC Structuring (1 Mark):**
Extracted IOCs are output in **STIX 2.1 format** and pushed to the SIEM for automated blocking.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice for Internal Selection]**

**(a)** Define **Precision**, **Recall**, and **F1-Score**. A CTI model produced the following on a test set of 1000 samples: TP = 90, FP = 10, FN = 60, TN = 840. Compute all three metrics. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions (3 Marks):**
- **Precision** = $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ — Of all alerts raised, how many were real threats?
- **Recall (TPR)** = $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ — Of all real threats present, how many did we catch?
- **F1-Score** = $\frac{2 P R}{P + R}$ — Single balanced metric for imbalanced data.

**Calculations (4 Marks):**

Given: $TP = 90, FP = 10, FN = 60, TN = 840$.

**Precision:**
$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{90}{90 + 10} = \frac{90}{100} = 0.90 \quad \text{(or 90\%)}$$

**Recall:**
$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{90}{90 + 60} = \frac{90}{150} = 0.60 \quad \text{(or 60\%)}$$

**F1-Score:**
$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.90 \cdot 0.60}{0.90 + 0.60} = \frac{1.08}{1.50} = 0.72 \quad \text{(or 72\%)}$$

**Interpretation:** The model is **precise** (low false alarms) but **misses 40% of real threats**. The F1 of 0.72 indicates moderate overall performance — the SOC should aim to improve recall by lowering the decision threshold or using cost-sensitive learning.

---

**(b)** Describe the **Anomaly Detection** approach in CTI using an **Autoencoder**. Derive the anomaly score mathematically. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (2 Marks):**
An autoencoder is a neural network trained **only on benign data**. It learns to reconstruct normal patterns; malicious inputs produce **high reconstruction error**, flagging them as anomalies.

**Architecture (2 Marks):**
- **Encoder:** $z = f_{\text{enc}}(x) = \phi(W_e x + b_e)$ compresses input to latent space.
- **Decoder:** $\hat{x} = f_{\text{dec}}(z) = \phi(W_d z + b_d)$ reconstructs the input.
- Trained by minimizing reconstruction loss.

**Mathematical Derivation (3 Marks):**

**Reconstruction Error (MSE):**
$$\mathcal{L}(x, \hat{x}) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2$$

**Anomaly Score:**
$$S(x) = \mathcal{L}(x, \hat{x})$$

**Decision Rule:**
$$y_{\text{pred}} = \begin{cases} \text{Malicious} & \text{if } S(x) > \tau \\ \text{Benign} & \text{if } S(x) \leq \tau \end{cases}$$
where $\tau$ is a threshold chosen via the 95th percentile of reconstruction errors on validation data.

**Use Case:** Detecting **zero-day attacks** where no labeled samples exist.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Precision with Accuracy:** Accuracy is misleading on imbalanced data (a model predicting "all benign" gets 99.9% accuracy on a 1:1000 imbalance). Always quote **Precision, Recall, F1, or MCC** in CTI contexts.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units:** Fractions must be quoted as both decimal (0.90) and percentage (90%). Examiners deduct marks for ambiguous formats.
> 3. **Skipping the threshold interpretation in anomaly detection:** Students often compute the score but forget to state **how $\tau$ is chosen** — a common 1-mark loss.
> 4. **No diagram in architecture questions:** A block diagram is **mandatory** for 7-mark questions. Skipping it costs at least 2 marks.
> 5. **Misclassifying NLP under "Deep Learning":** NLP is a **subfield of AI** that may or may not use DL. State the specific technique (TF-IDF, BERT, spaCy) — vague answers get partial credit only.
> 6. **Ignoring adversarial robustness:** Modern KTU 2024 questions expect awareness of **evasion/poisoning attacks** when discussing AI-CTI.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — "Applying AI Concepts"**

- **Definition (1-liner):** AI in CTI = automated, learning-based threat detection and response.
- **Three Pillars:** Machine Learning, Deep Learning, NLP.
- **Learning Types:** Supervised (labels), Unsupervised (no labels), Reinforcement (reward-based).
- **Must-know algorithms:** Logistic Regression, Random Forest, SVM, K-Means, DBSCAN, Autoencoder, LSTM, BERT.
- **Activation function:** $y = \phi(w^T x + b)$ — memorize ReLU and Sigmoid.
- **Loss function:** Binary Cross-Entropy $J = -\frac{1}{m} \sum [y \log h + (1-y) \log(1-h)]$.
- **Evaluation quartet:** Precision, Recall, F1, AUC-ROC.
- **Critical formula — F1:** $F_1 = \frac{2 P R}{P + R}$.
- **Anomaly detection formula:** $S(x) = \frac{1}{n} \sum (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2$.
- **NLP formula — TF-IDF:** $\text{TF}(t,d) \cdot \log(N / \text{DF}(t))$.
- **Transformer attention:** $\text{softmax}(QK^T / \sqrt{d_k}) V$.
- **Adversarial AI:** Evasion, Poisoning, Model Inversion attacks are real CTI threats.
- **Real-world tools:** VirusTotal, Chronicle, Splunk UBA, Darktrace, Microsoft Defender ATP.
- **Imbalanced data metric:** Use **MCC** or **F1**, never plain accuracy.
- **KTU buzzwords:** "Actionable intelligence," "Closed-loop learning," "Zero-day detection," "MITRE ATT&CK mapping."
- **Pipeline to draw:** Data → Features → Model → Prediction → Action → Feedback.

> **🎯 Pro Tip for KTU 2024:** When asked to "explain AI in CTI," always structure the answer in **three layers** — *What it is*, *How it works (with one algorithm and its formula)*, *Where it is deployed (real-world tool)*. Examiners award full marks only to answers that demonstrate this triad.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applying User Behaviour Analytics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT755 - CYBER THREAT INTELLIGENCE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Applying User Behaviour Analytics ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **User Behaviour Analytics (UBA)**, also extended as **User and Entity Behaviour Analytics (UEBA)**, is a class of cybersecurity solutions that employs **machine learning, statistical analysis, and baseline profiling** to detect anomalous behaviours exhibited by users (and entities such as hosts, applications, and service accounts) within an enterprise information system. In the KTU 2024 Cyber Threat Intelligence syllabus, UBA is positioned as a **detection-layer augmentation technique** layered on top of traditional SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) platforms to identify insider threats, compromised credentials, and lateral movement that evade signature-based controls. In a KTU/CTI context, the term *entity* explicitly extends the analytical scope beyond human users to include: - **Non-human identities** (service accounts, API tokens, IoT devices). - **Network assets** (workstations, servers, domain controllers). - **Application processes** (daemons, scheduled jobs, automated agents). The defining characteristic of UBA is its shift from **rule-based detection** (e.g., "3 failed logins in 5 minutes") to **behavioural baselining**, where "normal" is learned per entity and deviations are scored probabilistically. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Trusted Librarian":** > Imagine a librarian who has worked in the same library for 20 years. She knows *exactly* that Mr. Raj, a quiet economics student, always borrows between **1 and 3 books per week**, only from the **300s (Social Sciences) section**, and **never visits after 8 PM**. One day, Raj's library card is used by someone who, in a single hour, borrows **17 books across 8 different sections**, including chemistry and rare archives, at **2:47 AM**. > > The librarian does not need a rule like *"if more than 10 books → alert."* She simply *knows this is not Raj*. That intuitive, learned sense of *"this is unusual for this person"* is precisely what **User Behaviour Analytics** accomplishes mathematically over billions of log events using algorithms. The *standard normal-period* used for baselining in industry deployments is typically **30 to 90 days** of historical telemetry, and the standard risk-score threshold above which an alert is raised is commonly the **95th percentile** of the entity's own historical behaviour distribution. ### 1.3 Why UBA Exists — The Detection Gap Traditional security tooling fails against three classes of threat: 1. **Insider threats** — authorized users acting maliciously using their own valid credentials. 2. **Compromised credentials** — adversaries who have obtained legitimate usernames/passwords (no signature, no malware). 3. **Low-and-slow attacks** — attacks spread over weeks, below the threshold of static correlation rules. UBA fills this gap by **learning normalcy and detecting deviation**, rather than matching against a known-bad list. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The UBA Operational Pipeline (Six Logical Phases) The deployment of a UBA system is decomposed into the following sequential logic stages: - **Phase 1 — Data Ingestion (Telemetry Collection)** - *Why:* Behaviour cannot be modelled without telemetry. - *How:* Aggregate logs from authentication servers (Active Directory, LDAP, RADIUS), EDR agents, VPN concentrators, DLP systems, DNS resolvers, proxy gateways, and cloud SaaS audit trails. The minimum viable data set is the **(user, action, target, timestamp)** tuple. - **Phase 2 — Feature Engineering (Behavioural Vectorisation)** - *Why:* Raw logs are too granular and high-dimensional; algorithms require numerical feature vectors. - *How:* Convert each entity's activity into per-window feature vectors. Typical features include login frequency, distinct source IPs, files accessed, data volume egressed, time-of-day entropy, and peer-group activity similarity. - **Phase 3 — Baseline Profiling (Per-Entity Normality Model)** - *Why:* A network engineer and a sales executive have radically different "normal" behaviours; one global model fails. - *How:* Train a per-entity statistical or machine-learning model over a **training window** (industry standard: 30–90 days). Output is a *behavioural baseline profile* $B_u$ for each user $u$. - **Phase 4 — Anomaly Scoring (Real-Time Deviation Measurement)** - *Why:* Detection requires a quantitative measure of "how unusual is this event." - *How:* At runtime, compute a deviation score $S_t$ between the live event vector $X_t$ and the baseline $B_u$. A higher score indicates greater behavioural drift. - **Phase 5 — Risk Aggregation & Prioritisation** - *Why:* A single low score is noise; multiple correlated low scores indicate a campaign. - *How:* Aggregate anomaly scores across time windows, peer-group comparisons, and asset criticality into a single **Risk Score** $R \in [0, 100]$. - **Phase 6 — Alerting & Response Integration** - *Why:* Unactioned alerts provide no security value. - *How:* Forward high-risk events to SOAR (Security Orchestration, Automation & Response) platforms, ticketing systems, or trigger automated containment (e.g., session revocation, MFA challenge). ### 2.2 Peer-Group Analysis — The Critical Enhancement A naïve UBA model flags any deviation from a user's own history. However, this generates excessive false positives (e.g., a user travelling on business legitimately works at 3 AM). The industry correction is **peer-group analysis**: > [!IMPORTANT] > A user is only considered anomalous if they deviate from **both** their personal baseline **and** the behaviour of their *peer group* (users in the same role, department, or with similar access privileges). This dual-baseline approach is what distinguishes modern UEBA from first-generation UBA products. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following table consolidates every quantitative construct required for a KTU 2024 scheme question on this topic. | # | Concept | Formula / Construct | Units / Domain | Engineering Use | |---|---------|---------------------|----------------|-----------------| | 1 | Behavioural feature vector for user $u$ at time $t$ | $X_t^{(u)} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]$ | Dimensionless (count, bytes, entropy) | Numerical input to ML model | | 2 | Per-user baseline (mean) | $\mu_u = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i^{(u)}$ | Same units as $X$ | Centre of normal behaviour | | 3 | Per-user baseline (standard deviation) | $\sigma_u = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (X_i^{(u)} - \mu_u)^2}$ | Same units as $X$ | Spread of normal behaviour | | 4 | Z-Score (statistical anomaly) | $Z_t = \dfrac{\vert X_t - \mu_u \vert}{\sigma_u}$ | Dimensionless ($\ge 3$ is conventional alert threshold) | Quick anomaly quantification | | 5 | Cosine similarity (peer comparison) | $\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{A \cdot B}{\vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert}$ | Range $[-1, 1]$ | Detect role deviation | | 6 | Shannon entropy (behavioural randomness) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Bits | Detects unusual diversity in activity | | 7 | Aggregated risk score (weighted) | $R = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i \cdot S_i$, where $\sum w_i = 1$ | Range $[0, 100]$ | Final prioritised alert score | | 8 | Precision (alert quality) | $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Range $[0, 1]$ | False-positive control metric | | 9 | Recall (detection coverage) | $\text{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Range $[0, 1]$ | Missed-threat control metric | | 10 | F1-Score (balanced metric) | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Range $[0, 1]$ | Single model-quality score | | 11 | Standard baselining window | $T_{\text{baseline}} \in [30, 90]$ days | Days | Industry default for normalcy learning | | 12 | Standard alert threshold percentile | $P_{95}$ of own historical scores | Percentile | Conventional high-risk cutoff | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examination Tip:** When asked to "compute the anomaly score," the most common KTU expectation is a **Z-score calculation** using formula row 4 above. When asked to "compare two users' behavioural similarity," the expected construct is **cosine similarity** (row 5). Memorise these two cold — they are the highest-yield numerical items. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility UBA is the operational backbone of modern SOCs (Security Operations Centres) and is mandated under several regulatory frameworks: - **NIST SP 800-53** control *AC-2 (Account Management)* and *AU-6 (Audit Review)* implicitly require behavioural monitoring. - **PCI-DSS v4.0** requires detection of anomalous authentication activity. - **Insider threat programs** in BFSI, defence, and critical infrastructure rely on UBA as the primary detection control because insider activity is, by definition, signature-less. In Managed Detection and Response (MDR) services, UBA-derived risk scores are now the **primary triage signal** that human analysts investigate first, replacing raw SIEM correlation rule firings. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — The Z-Score Anomaly Detection Metric The Z-score is the foundational statistical primitive of UBA. The KTU 2024 scheme may ask students to compute it from a worked log table; the full derivation is given below. **Step 1 — Define the user's historical login-frequency dataset.** Suppose we have collected the *number of logins per day* for user $u$ over the last 7 days as: $$D_u = \{4, 5, 4, 6, 5, 4, 5\}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the arithmetic mean (the baseline centre).** Sum the values and divide by the count $N = 7$: $$\mu_u = \frac{4 + 5 + 4 + 6 + 5 + 4 + 5}{7} = \frac{33}{7} \approx 4.714 \text{ logins/day}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the squared deviations from the mean.** For each observation, calculate $(X_i - \mu_u)^2$: | $X_i$ | $X_i - \mu_u$ | $(X_i - \mu_u)^2$ | |:-----:|:-------------:|:------------------:| | 4 | $-0.714$ | $0.510$ | | 5 | $+0.286$ | $0.082$ | | 4 | $-0.714$ | $0.510$ | | 6 | $+1.286$ | $1.653$ | | 5 | $+0.286$ | $0.082$ | | 4 | $-0.714$ | $0.510$ | | 5 | $+0.286$ | $0.082$ | **Step 4 — Sum the squared deviations.** $$\sum_{i=1}^{7}(X_i - \mu_u)^2 = 0.510 + 0.082 + 0.510 + 1.653 + 0.082 + 0.510 + 0.082 = 3.429$$ **Step 5 — Compute the variance.** $$\sigma_u^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{7}(X_i - \mu_u)^2 = \frac{3.429}{7} \approx 0.490$$ **Step 6 — Compute the standard deviation.** $$\sigma_u = \sqrt{0.490} \approx 0.700 \text{ logins/day}$$ **Step 7 — Apply the Z-score formula to a new live event.** Suppose on day 8, user $u$ performs $X_8 = 17$ logins. The deviation is: $$Z_8 = \frac{\vert X_8 - \mu_u \vert}{\sigma_u} = \frac{\vert 17 - 4.714 \vert}{0.700} = \frac{12.286}{0.700} \approx 17.55$$ **Step 8 — Interpret using the conventional threshold.** A Z-score of $\ge 3$ is statistically extreme (occurring in roughly **0.3%** of a normal distribution). A Z-score of $17.55$ is far beyond any reasonable threshold and is a **high-confidence anomaly indicator**, warranting immediate investigation. This is the numerical equivalent of the *librarian* sensing something is wrong. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Threats to UBA Detection Signals The following matrix maps the *threat* to the *behavioural signal* UBA detects, the *feature* monitored, and the *typical baseline period*. This is the highest-density KTU question pattern in Module 4. | Threat Category | UBA Behavioural Signal | Primary Feature Monitored | Typical Baseline Window | |-----------------|----------------------|--------------------------|-------------------------| | Compromised credentials | Login from new geo / impossible travel | Source IP geolocation, login timestamp delta | **30 days** | | Insider data exfiltration | Sudden spike in data egress volume | Bytes downloaded, files accessed | **60 days** | | Privilege escalation abuse | Use of admin tools outside change windows | Time-of-day for privileged commands | **45 days** | | Lateral movement | SMB/RDP connections to many hosts | Distinct internal hosts contacted | **30 days** | | Account takeover via MFA fatigue | Repeated MFA push requests in burst | MFA challenge rate per hour | **14 days** | | Service account abuse | Out-of-schedule automated activity | Inter-request timing entropy | **30 days** | | Reconnaissance | Spike in port scans / API enumeration | Distinct endpoints probed | **30 days** | | Data staging (pre-exfil) | Bulk file reads in unusual directory | File read count, directory entropy | **45 days** | ### 3.3 Worked Cosine Similarity Example (Peer-Group Comparison) **Step 1 — Vectorise two users' daily activity.** User A (network admin) and User B (sales executive) over a 5-day period, feature = emails sent: $$A = [40, 42, 38, 41, 39], \qquad B = [10, 12, 11, 9, 13]$$ **Step 2 — Compute the dot product $A \cdot B$.** $$A \cdot B = (40)(10) + (42)(12) + (38)(11) + (41)(9) + (39)(13) = 400 + 504 + 418 + 369 + 507 = 2198$$ **Step 3 — Compute magnitudes.** $$\vert A \vert = \sqrt{40^2 + 42^2 + 38^2 + 41^2 + 39^2} = \sqrt{1600 + 1764 + 1444 + 1681 + 1521} = \sqrt{8010} \approx 89.50$$ $$\vert B \vert = \sqrt{10^2 + 12^2 + 11^2 + 9^2 + 13^2} = \sqrt{100 + 144 + 121 + 81 + 169} = \sqrt{615} \approx 24.80$$ **Step 4 — Compute cosine similarity.** $$\cos(\theta) = \frac{2198}{(89.50)(24.80)} = \frac{2198}{2219.6} \approx 0.990$$ **Step 5 — Interpret.** A cosine similarity of $\approx 0.99$ indicates the two vectors are nearly **parallel in pattern** (similar daily rhythm) despite vastly different magnitudes. UBA uses this to detect users whose *behavioural shape* changes even if the *volume* remains constant — a subtle insider-threat indicator. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 End-to-End UBA Functional Architecture Flow The following Mermaid block renders the complete block-level functional architecture of a production-grade UBA/UEBA pipeline, from raw telemetry ingestion to analyst response. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Identity Sources
AD LDAP VPN] --> G[Telemetry Aggregator] B[Endpoint Telemetry
EDR Agents] --> G C[Network Telemetry
Proxy DNS Firewall] --> G D[Cloud SaaS Logs
Microsoft 365 AWS] --> G E[Application Audit Trails
DB ERP CRM] --> G F[Data Loss Prevention
DLP CASB] --> G G --> H[Feature Engineering Layer
Vectorisation Window 24h] H --> I[Per Entity Baseline Store
30 to 90 day history] H --> J[Peer Group Classifier
K means role clustering] I --> K[Anomaly Scoring Engine
Z score Isolation Forest LSTM] J --> K J --> L[Peer Deviation Engine
Cosine similarity KL divergence] K --> M[Risk Aggregator
Weighted score 0 to 100] L --> M M --> N{Score above
threshold P95} N -->|Yes| O[SOAR Orchestrator
Auto Containment] N -->|No| P[Silent Log
Forensics Retention] O --> Q[SOC Analyst Console
Case Management] P --> Q Q --> R{Feedback
True or False Positive} R -->|True Positive| S[Model Retraining
Update Baseline] R -->|False Positive| T[Tune Weights
Suppress Pattern] S --> I T --> I ``` ### 4.2 UBA Detection Logic — Decision Flow The following Mermaid block captures the *runtime decision logic* used to classify an observed event as benign, suspicious, or malicious based on UBA scoring. ```mermaid flowchart TD S0[Live Event Observed
User U Action A Target T Time t] --> S1[Retrieve Baseline B of User U] S1 --> S2[Compute Personal Z score Zp] S2 --> S3{Zp greater than 3} S3 -->|No| S4[Retrieve Peer Group PG of User U] S3 -->|Yes| S7[Flag Suspicious Personal] S4 --> S5[Compute Peer Cosine Similarity Cp] S5 --> S6{Cp less than 0.6} S6 -->|No| S8[Classify Benign] S6 -->|Yes| S9[Flag Peer Deviation] S7 --> S10[Aggregate Risk R] S9 --> S10 S10 --> S11{R greater than 75} S11 -->|No| S12[Log to Watchlist] S11 -->|Yes| S13[Trigger High Severity Alert and SOAR] ``` ### 4.3 UBA Layering in the Cyber Defence Stack ```mermaid flowchart LR L1[Perimeter
Firewall IDS WAF] --> L2[Network
NDR Segmentation] L2 --> L3[Endpoint
EDR Antivirus] L3 --> L4[Identity
UBA UEBA] L4 --> L5[Application
RASP API Security] L5 --> L6[Data
DLP Encryption] L4 -.fills gap.-> GAP1[Insider Threat Detection] L4 -.fills gap.-> GAP2[Compromised Credential Detection] L4 -.fills gap.-> GAP3[Low and Slow Attack Detection] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** UBA sits at the *Identity* layer in the modern defence-in-depth stack, but its analytical outputs feed upward and downward — correlating with EDR signals, enriching SIEM events, and informing data-layer DLP policies. This cross-layer integration is the architectural reason UEBA is now considered a *platform* capability, not a point product. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]** *Define User and Entity Behaviour Analytics (UEBA). How does it differ from traditional signature-based intrusion detection?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** UEBA is a cybersecurity detection methodology that uses *machine learning and statistical baselining* to model the *normal behavioural pattern* of each user and entity (hosts, accounts, applications) within an enterprise, and then identifies deviations from that learned norm as potential security threats. **Difference from signature-based IDS (1.5 marks):** | Aspect | Signature-Based IDS | UEBA | |--------|--------------------|------| | Detection basis | Known-bad patterns (signatures, rules) | Deviation from learned normal | | Zero-day capability | None | Strong (no signature needed) | | Insider threat detection | Poor | Strong | | Maintenance | Manual rule updates | Continuous ML retraining | | Data dependency | Packet / payload content | Identity and behavioural telemetry | --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]** *List any three behavioural features commonly used in UBA to model user activity and briefly state why each is useful.* **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per feature):** 1. **Login frequency (count per day)** — Useful to detect credential-stuffing success or compromised accounts generating unusual authentication volume. **(1 mark)** 2. **Time-of-day distribution of activity** — Useful to flag activity outside a user's established working-hours envelope, e.g., a 9-to-5 accountant suddenly active at 3 AM. **(1 mark)** 3. **Data egress volume (bytes downloaded per session)** — Useful to identify data exfiltration, where an insider or compromised account downloads abnormally large data volumes. **(1 mark)** *(Acceptable alternatives: distinct source IP count, peer-group cosine similarity, MFA challenge burst rate, file-access entropy.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 14 Marks]** **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **end-to-end functional architecture of a UEBA system**, clearly listing the six operational phases from telemetry ingestion to analyst response. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A user has the following *number of files accessed per day* over the last 8 days: $\{12, 14, 13, 15, 14, 12, 13, 14\}$. On day 9, the user accesses **47 files**. Compute the Z-score for day 9 and interpret whether this should trigger a UBA alert (use a Z-score threshold of $\ge 3$). **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A** **(a) — 7 marks** The end-to-end UEBA architecture consists of the following six operational phases (each phase 1 mark + 1 mark for the diagram): 1. **Data Ingestion:** Aggregate logs from identity sources (AD, LDAP, VPN), endpoints (EDR), network (proxy, DNS, firewall), cloud SaaS, and DLP. **(1 mark)** 2. **Feature Engineering:** Convert raw logs into per-entity numerical feature vectors (login count, byte volume, time-of-day, distinct IPs, etc.) over rolling time windows. **(1 mark)** 3. **Baseline Profiling:** Train a per-entity statistical or ML model (e.g., Gaussian, Isolation Forest, LSTM) over a 30–90 day historical window to produce the baseline profile $B_u$. **(1 mark)** 4. **Anomaly Scoring:** At runtime, compute a deviation score $S_t$ between the live feature vector and the baseline. **(1 mark)** 5. **Risk Aggregation:** Combine multiple anomaly scores using a weighted formula $R = \sum w_i S_i$ into a single risk score in $[0, 100]$. **(1 mark)** 6. **Alerting & Response:** Forward high-risk events to SOAR for automated containment (session revoke, MFA challenge) and to the SOC analyst console for triage; analyst feedback loops back into model retraining. **(1 mark)** **Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the architecture diagram in **Section 4.1** above; the student should reproduce an equivalent six-stage left-to-right flow ending at the SOC analyst. --- **(b) — 7 marks** **Given data:** $D = \{12, 14, 13, 15, 14, 12, 13, 14\}$, $N = 8$, live event $X_9 = 47$. **Step 1 — Compute the mean (1 mark).** $$\mu = \frac{12 + 14 + 13 + 15 + 14 + 12 + 13 + 14}{8} = \frac{107}{8} = 13.375$$ **Step 2 — Compute squared deviations (2 marks).** | $X_i$ | $X_i - \mu$ | $(X_i - \mu)^2$ | |:-----:|:-----------:|:----------------:| | 12 | $-1.375$ | $1.8906$ | | 14 | $+0.625$ | $0.3906$ | | 13 | $-0.375$ | $0.1406$ | | 15 | $+1.625$ | $2.6406$ | | 14 | $+0.625$ | $0.3906$ | | 12 | $-1.375$ | $1.8906$ | | 13 | $-0.375$ | $0.1406$ | | 14 | $+0.625$ | $0.3906$ | **Sum of squared deviations:** $$\sum (X_i - \mu)^2 = 1.8906 + 0.3906 + 0.1406 + 2.6406 + 0.3906 + 1.8906 + 0.1406 + 0.3906 = 7.875$$ **Step 3 — Compute variance and standard deviation (1.5 marks).** $$\sigma^2 = \frac{7.875}{8} = 0.9844$$ $$\sigma = \sqrt{0.9844} \approx 0.9922$$ **Step 4 — Compute Z-score for day 9 (1 mark).** $$Z_9 = \frac{\vert 47 - 13.375 \vert}{0.9922} = \frac{33.625}{0.9922} \approx 33.89$$ **Step 5 — Interpretation (1.5 marks):** Since $Z_9 \approx 33.89 \gg 3$ (the conventional threshold), this event is **statistically extreme** and falls many standard deviations outside the user's normal behaviour envelope. The UBA system **must trigger a high-severity alert** and route it to the SOC for immediate investigation. The magnitude of the deviation (33.89) suggests possible **data exfiltration, automated scraping, or ransomware staging**, all of which manifest as anomalous file-access volume. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 14 Marks]** **(a)** Describe the concept of **peer-group analysis** in UBA. Why is it essential for reducing false positives compared to per-user baselining alone? Illustrate with a suitable example. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Two users in the marketing department exhibit the following weekly email-send activity vectors: $A = [20, 22, 19, 21, 20]$ and $B = [21, 23, 20, 22, 19]$. Compute the **cosine similarity** $\cos(\theta)$ between their activity vectors and state whether their behaviour can be considered similar by UBA standards. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B** **(a) — 7 marks** **Definition (2 marks):** Peer-group analysis is a UBA technique in which the "normal" behaviour of a user is defined not only by their own historical baseline but *also* by the behaviour of a *cohort* of users with similar roles, departments, privileges, or access patterns. An event is flagged only when the user deviates from **both** their personal baseline *and* the peer-group baseline. **Why it reduces false positives (3 marks):** Per-user baselining alone flags legitimate but unusual activity (e.g., a sales rep working late during quarter-end, an admin performing emergency maintenance at 2 AM, an HR executive accessing records during open-enrolment week). Such events are *unusual for the individual* but *normal for the role*. By comparing to a peer group with the same workflow, UBA can suppress these legitimate-but-atypical events while still detecting true anomalies (e.g., the HR executive accessing finance records — a peer-group mismatch). **Illustrative Example (2 marks):** Consider *User P*, a financial analyst. Over a quarter-end close, P works until midnight for 5 consecutive days — this deviates from P's personal baseline (usually logs off by 7 PM) but is **identical to the peer group** of all other financial analysts doing quarter-close. UBA's peer comparison correctly classifies this as **benign**. The same user, however, accessing the *marketing campaign database* — something no financial analyst peer does — produces a peer deviation, and UBA correctly classifies it as **anomalous**. --- **(b) — 7 marks** **Given:** $A = [20, 22, 19, 21, 20]$, $B = [21, 23, 20, 22, 19]$. **Step 1 — Compute the dot product $A \cdot B$ (1.5 marks).** $$A \cdot B = (20)(21) + (22)(23) + (19)(20) + (21)(22) + (20)(19)$$ $$= 420 + 506 + 380 + 462 + 380 = 2148$$ **Step 2 — Compute magnitude of $A$ (1.5 marks).** $$\vert A \vert = \sqrt{20^2 + 22^2 + 19^2 + 21^2 + 20^2}$$ $$= \sqrt{400 + 484 + 361 + 441 + 400} = \sqrt{2086} \approx 45.67$$ **Step 3 — Compute magnitude of $B$ (1.5 marks).** $$\vert B \vert = \sqrt{21^2 + 23^2 + 20^2 + 22^2 + 19^2}$$ $$= \sqrt{441 + 529 + 400 + 484 + 361} = \sqrt{2215} \approx 47.06$$ **Step 4 — Compute cosine similarity (1.5 marks).** $$\cos(\theta) = \frac{A \cdot B}{\vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert} = \frac{2148}{(45.67)(47.06)} = \frac{2148}{2149.23} \approx 0.9994$$ **Step 5 — Interpretation (1 mark):** A cosine similarity of $\approx 0.9994$ is *extremely close to 1*, indicating that the two vectors are nearly perfectly parallel in shape. By UBA standards (typical peer-similarity threshold $\ge 0.6$ for *not anomalous*, and $< 0.6$ for *anomalous*), users A and B exhibit **highly similar behavioural patterns** and would be classified as belonging to the same peer group. A sudden drop in one user's similarity to the other (e.g., to $\cos(\theta) < 0.5$) would be a strong UBA peer-deviation signal warranting investigation. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Topic:** > > 1. **Confusing UBA with SIEM** — UBA is a *detection analytics layer* that runs *on top of* SIEM; it is not a replacement. Examiners specifically deduct marks for equating the two. > 2. **Forgetting the entity dimension** — KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests the *U* vs *UEB*A distinction. Writing only "user" when the question requires "user and entity" costs 1 mark. > 3. **Z-score arithmetic errors** — The most common slip is using $N$ vs $N-1$ in the denominator; the *population* formula with $N$ is the KTU-expected form for baselining. > 4. **Confusing cosine similarity with Euclidean distance** — They measure different things; cosine measures *shape*, Euclidean measures *absolute distance*. Examiners check whether the student picked the right tool. > 5. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark sub-parts** — A 7-mark "explain with diagram" question that omits the diagram forfeits at least 1.5 marks. > 6. **Forgetting the threshold justification** — A Z-score is meaningless without stating the *threshold* (conventionally $\ge 3$) and the *baseline window* (30–90 days) used in the comparison. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Applying User Behaviour Analytics"** - **UBA / UEBA** is a *behavioural baselining* detection technique, **not** a signature-matching technique. Its core value is detecting **insider threats, compromised credentials, and low-and-slow attacks** that evade rule-based controls. - The **six operational phases** are: *Ingestion → Feature Engineering → Baseline Profiling → Anomaly Scoring → Risk Aggregation → Alerting & Response*. Memorise the order and the input/output of each phase. - The standard **baselining window** is **30 to 90 days** of historical telemetry; the conventional **Z-score alert threshold** is **$\ge 3$**. - The two most important numerical tools are the **Z-score** ($Z = \vert X - \mu \vert / \sigma$) for personal anomaly and the **cosine similarity** ($\cos(\theta) = A \cdot B / \vert A \vert \vert B \vert$) for peer-group comparison. - **Peer-group analysis** is the critical enhancement that distinguishes modern UEBA from first-generation UBA; it dramatically reduces false positives by accounting for legitimate role-based variations. - **Behavioural features** typically include: login count, source IP diversity, time-of-day distribution, data egress volume, distinct hosts contacted, file-access entropy, and MFA challenge rate. - The **risk score** is a weighted aggregation: $R = \sum w_i S_i$ with $\sum w_i = 1$, scaled to a 0–100 range; alerts are raised above the **95th percentile** of the entity's own historical scores. - **Shannon entropy** $H(X) = -\sum p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ is used to detect *unusual diversity* in activity (e.g., a user suddenly accessing many distinct file types). - The **standard model-quality triad** is **Precision, Recall, F1-Score** — examiners expect you to know all three formulas and their interpretations. - UBA sits at the **Identity layer** of the modern defence-in-depth stack, but its signals correlate across all other layers (endpoint, network, application, data). - **Real-world deployments** require analyst feedback loops to handle false positives and continuously retrain baselines — UBA is not a "deploy and forget" technology. - **KTU's expected answer structure** for any 7-mark "explain" question is: *Definition → Six phases → Diagram → Example of behavioural feature → Threshold and window statement → One sentence on operational deployment.* ---

topics.title: AI and Cybersecurity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT755 - CYBER THREAT INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** AI and Cybersecurity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Artificial Intelligence (AI) in Cybersecurity** refers to the application of machine learning (ML), deep learning (DL), natural language processing (NLP), and other AI sub-disciplines to automate, augment, and accelerate the detection, analysis, prevention, and response to cyber threats. Within the **Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI)** lifecycle, AI enables the correlation of massive telemetry streams, the prediction of adversarial behaviour, and the generation of actionable indicators of compromise (IoCs) at machine speed.

The three foundational pillars that AI brings to cybersecurity are:

- **Perception** — ingesting and understanding raw, heterogeneous data (logs, packets, malware binaries, dark-web chatter).
- **Reasoning** — deriving probabilistic inferences about whether an event is benign, suspicious, or malicious.
- **Action** — triggering automated containment, response playbooks, or human-analyst alerts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key AI Sub-fields in Security (KTU Syllabus Highlights):**
> - **Machine Learning (ML):** Statistical learning from labelled/unlabelled data (e.g., Random Forest for malware classification).
> - **Deep Learning (DL):** Multi-layer neural networks (e.g., CNN/RNN for network intrusion detection).
> - **Natural Language Processing (NLP):** Extracting IoCs from threat reports, tweets, and dark-web forums.
> - **Reinforcement Learning (RL):** Autonomous agents that learn optimal defence policies through trial and error.
> - **Generative AI (GenAI):** Large Language Models (LLMs) used by both attackers (phishing, deepfakes) and defenders (alert summarisation, code review).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Digital Immune System"

Imagine your body fighting a viral infection. You do not consciously identify every foreign antigen; instead, your **immune system** has been "trained" by past infections, recognises patterns of unfamiliar proteins, and dispatches specialised cells to neutralise the threat — all without you thinking about it.

**AI in cybersecurity works in the same way.** It is the **digital immune system** of an organisation:

- **Antigen (Input)** → A new packet, login attempt, or file.
- **Immune Cells (AI Models)** → Classifiers trained on millions of past examples that flag anomalies.
- **Antibodies (Indicators of Compromise)** → Signatures, behavioural fingerprints, and YARA rules produced by the AI.
- **Memory (Retrained Models)** → The system continuously learns from new attacks, just as vaccines upgrade your immunity.

> [!TIP]
> Just as a fever tells you *something* is wrong without telling you *what*, an AI-driven anomaly detector raises an alert when behaviour deviates from the learned baseline — the analyst then performs the "doctor's diagnosis."

---

### 1.3 Why AI is Indispensable in Modern CTI

The scale of modern threats makes purely manual analysis impossible. Consider these **industry-standard metrics**:

- A typical enterprise generates **>10,000 security alerts per day** (Source-class statistic, generally cited in industry).
- Average **dwell time** (compromise-to-detection) globally: measured in **days to months**.
- The **MITRE ATT&CK** matrix now documents **>200 techniques** across 14 tactics.

Human SOC analysts can only triage a tiny fraction of this volume. AI scales the analyst's cognition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI):** Evidence-based knowledge about existing or emerging threats to assets, derived from internal telemetry, open-source intelligence (OSINT), and human intelligence (HUMINT), used to inform defensive decisions.

---

### 1.4 Dual-Use Nature of AI in Cyber Conflict

A critical KTU-level concept is that AI is a **double-edged sword**.

| Actor | Offensive AI Use | Defensive AI Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Nation-state APTs** | Automated target reconnaissance, polymorphic malware | AI-augmented national CERTs |
| **Organised Cybercrime** | Deepfake voice phishing, AI-written spear-phishing | Fraud-detection models in banks |
| **Insider Threats** | LLM-generated social-engineering scripts | User & Entity Behaviour Analytics (UEBA) |
| **Red Teams / Pentesters** | AI-assisted vulnerability discovery | Continuous automated red-teaming (CART) |
| **Blue Teams / SOC** | Honeypot deception powered by RL | SIEM/SOAR augmentation, alert triage |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often frame AI as purely defensive. Examiners specifically test the **offensive AI** dimension (deepfakes, polymorphic malware, adversarial evasion). Always present **both sides** for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The AI–Cybersecurity Operational Stack

AI in cybersecurity operates in **four logical layers**:

1. **Data Acquisition Layer** — Collects logs, NetFlow, EDR telemetry, OSINT feeds.
2. **Feature Engineering Layer** — Tokenises payloads, extracts n-grams, builds behavioural embeddings.
3. **Model Layer** — Trains classifiers, anomaly detectors, and sequence models.
4. **Decision & Response Layer** — Generates IoCs, ranks alerts, triggers SOAR playbooks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Garbage-In / Garbage-Out" matters more in security than in any other AI domain:** An attacker can deliberately craft inputs to poison the training set, making the AI itself a vulnerability.

---

### 2.2 Categories of ML Used in Threat Detection

#### A. Supervised Learning
The model is trained on **labelled** samples (malicious vs. benign).

- **Algorithms:** Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, Random Forest, SVM, k-NN.
- **Security use cases:** Malware classification, spam detection, phishing URL detection.

#### B. Unsupervised Learning
The model finds structure in **unlabelled** data — crucial because most real attacks are novel (zero-day).

- **Algorithms:** k-Means, DBSCAN, Isolation Forest, Autoencoders, Self-Organising Maps.
- **Security use cases:** Network anomaly detection, insider-threat behavioural baselining, novel malware clustering.

#### C. Semi-Supervised Learning
Combines a small set of labelled data with a large pool of unlabelled data. Used when labelling malware samples is expensive.

#### D. Reinforcement Learning (RL)
An **agent** learns an optimal **policy** $\pi(a \vert s)$ by interacting with an environment and receiving **rewards**.

- **Security use cases:** Autonomous cyber defence agents, adaptive honeypots, automated penetration testing.

The fundamental RL objective is to learn a policy $\pi$ that maximises the expected cumulative discounted reward:

$$G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$$

where $0 \leq \gamma \leq 1$ is the discount factor that prioritises **immediate** rewards over future ones.
