## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]
> **Q1.** Define **Cyber Threat Hunting**. How does it differ from traditional reactive security monitoring?

**Model Answer (Board-Standard):**
Cyber Threat Hunting is the proactive, human-driven, intelligence-led process of searching for advanced threats and adversaries that have bypassed automated security controls. It operates under the **"breach assumption"** that the adversary is already present.

**Differences from Reactive Security:**

| Aspect | Reactive Security (SOC/SIEM) | Threat Hunting |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Alert-driven | Hypothesis-driven |
| Posture | Awaits adversary action | Assumes breach |
| Focus | Known IoCs / signatures | TTPs and behaviors |
| Automation | Fully automated | Hybrid (human + machine) |
| Time Horizon | Real-time | Retrospective + Real-time |

**(3 marks awarded for definition + 2 key differences with comparison table)**

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]
> **Q2.** What is the **MITRE ATT&CK Framework**? How is it used to mitigate future attacks in threat hunting?

**Model Answer:**
MITRE ATT&CK (Adversarial Tactics, Techniques, and Common Knowledge) is a globally accessible knowledge base of adversary tactics and techniques derived from real-world observations, organized into a matrix of 14 tactic categories and hundreds of techniques.

**Use in Mitigating Future Attacks:**
1. **Gap Analysis** — Map current detection coverage; identify techniques with zero coverage (hunting priorities).
2. **Hypothesis Generation** — Each ATT&CK technique ID (e.g., T1059.001) becomes a huntable hypothesis.
3. **Threat-Informed Defense** — Prioritize hunts based on adversary groups targeting the organization's sector.
4. **Detection Engineering** — Use ATT&CK as a checklist for building YARA/SIGMA rules.
5. **Adversary Emulation** — Replay known TTP chains (e.g., APT29's full kill chain) to validate defenses.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 marks for 4 use-cases)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly lose **3–4 marks** by *omitting the quantitative derivation* (MTTD, MTTR, dwell time) — every Part B answer **must include numerical metrics** where applicable.
> - Avoid generic answers like *"Hunting improves security."* You **must cite specific TTPs** (e.g., T1059.001) and **tools** (YARA, SIGMA, MITRE Caldera).
> - **Diagram is mandatory** for full marks — even a hand-drawn block diagram of the hunting loop will secure at least **2 bonus marks** in the examiner's perception.
> - For 14-mark questions, **structure with headings** (Definition → Methodology → Architecture → Metrics → Mitigation Effect). Unstructured essays typically score 8–9 marks maximum.

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

> **Q.A(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **SANS Threat Hunting Loop** in detail. How does each stage contribute to mitigating future attacks?

**Model Answer:**

The SANS Institute defines a four-stage iterative threat hunting loop. Each stage contributes multiplicatively to future-attack mitigation.

**Stage 1 — Hypothesis Creation (2 marks):**
A hunting hypothesis is a *falsifiable, intelligence-grounded assumption* about adversary presence. Sources include: STIX/TAXII feeds, MITRE ATT&CK gaps, red team reports, and UEBA anomalies. The hypothesis must specify the **actor OR technique**, the **data source**, and the **expected observation**.
*Example:* "FIN7 is using `mshta.exe` (T1218.005) to execute remote payloads within the finance subnet."

**Stage 2 — Investigation (2 marks):**
The hunter executes queries against a centralized telemetry data lake using **KQL, SPL, or EQL**. Tools used: **Splunk, Elastic, Sentinel, Velociraptor, OSQuery**. Investigation pivots from broad to narrow (funnel approach).

**Stage 3 — Pattern Analysis (1.5 marks):**
Matches observed behaviors to ATT&CK Technique IDs. Uses statistical methods (z-score, entropy) and ML models (Isolation Forest, GNNs). Confirmed findings are escalated.

**Stage 4 — Response & Mitigation (1.5 marks):**
- Immediate: Host isolation, credential reset, IoC blocking at perimeter.
- Strategic: Convert finding to **YARA/SIGMA rule** → push to **SIEM** → automatic future detection.
- **This is the direct mitigation-of-future-attacks step**: every hunt refines automated controls, ensuring the same TTP is blocked *automatically* the next time.

---

> **Q.A(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **mitigation factor equation** for a Cyber Threat Hunting program. Assume a baseline detection coverage of 60%, automated FNR of 20%, hunting coverage of 65%, YARA conversion rate of 80%, and a time-to-hypothesis of 2 hours ($\lambda = 0.01$ per hour). Calculate the resulting mitigation percentage.

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (3.5 marks):**

Step 1: Baseline evasion probability

$$
E_0 = 1 - C_{\text{detect}}^{(0)} \cdot (1 - \text{FNR}^{(0)}) = 1 - 0.60 \cdot (1 - 0.20) = 1 - 0.48 = 0.52
$$

Step 2: Incremental hunting detection

$$
\Delta_{\text{hunt}} = H_{\text{coverage}} \cdot H_{\text{YARA}} \cdot e^{-\lambda H_{\text{latency}}}
$$

Step 3: New evasion probability

$$
E_{\text{hunt}} = E_0 \cdot (1 - \Delta_{\text{hunt}})
$$

Step 4: Mitigation factor (closed form)

$$
\boxed{M = H_{\text{coverage}} \cdot H_{\text{YARA}} \cdot e^{-\lambda H_{\text{latency}}} \times 100\%}
$$

**Numerical Calculation (3.5 marks):**

$$
\Delta_{\text{hunt}} = 0.65 \times 0.80 \times e^{-0.01 \times 2} = 0.52 \times e^{-0.02}
$$

$$
\Delta_{\text{hunt}} = 0.52 \times 0.9802 = 0.5097
$$

$$
M = 0.5097 \times 100\% \approx 50.97\%
$$

**Engineering Conclusion:** A moderately mature hunting program mitigates **~51% of future adversary success probability** by *the mere existence of the hunting process*, even before any individual alert is generated. This is the quantitative justification for the engineering investment in threat hunting.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — Supplementary 2024]

> **Q.B(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **MITRE ATT&CK-driven hunting methodology**. Explain with examples how it is used to (i) identify coverage gaps, (ii) generate hypotheses, and (iii) measure hunting effectiveness.

**Model Answer:**

**MITRE ATT&CK-Driven Hunting** is a systematic methodology that aligns every hunt to specific Technique IDs in the ATT&CK matrix, ensuring comprehensive, measurable, and repeatable coverage.

**(i) Identify Coverage Gaps (2.5 marks):**
Using the **ATT&CK Navigator** (a web-based visualization tool), defenders map which techniques are *currently detected* vs. *unobserved*. Techniques with green coverage are automated; yellow is partial; red is *uncovered* and forms the hunt backlog.
*Example:* A bank maps T1059.001 (PowerShell) as "Detected" but T1059.009 (AppLocker Bypass) as "Not Detected" → triggers an immediate hunt.

**(ii) Generate Hypotheses (2.5 marks):**
Each uncovered technique becomes a testable hypothesis. The hunter writes queries that, *if they return results*, confirm the technique is being used; *if empty*, confirm the technique is genuinely absent (or that telemetry is missing).
*Example hypothesis:* "We expect to observe PowerShell processes with `-WindowStyle Hidden` and Base64-encoded `FromBase64String` calls originating from Word/Excel macros (T1566.001 → T1059.001 chain)."

**(iii) Measure Effectiveness (2 marks):**
- **Hunt Coverage %** = Hunted Techniques / Total ATT&CK Techniques
- **MTTC (Mean Time to Cover)** = Time from ATT&CK release to internal detection rule deployed
- **Empirical Validation** — Use **Atomic Red Team** or **MITRE Caldera** to simulate the technique; verify the hunt caught it.

**Future-Attack Mitigation:** The ATT&CK-driven approach ensures that the *attack surface shrinks cumulatively* with each hunt, providing measurable, auditable security improvement.

---

> **Q.B(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **role of Deception Technology and Adversary Emulation** in mitigating future attacks through threat hunting. Cite specific tools and provide a deployment architecture.

**Model Answer:**

**Adversary Emulation (3.5 marks):**
Adversary emulation is the controlled, authorized simulation of real-world adversary TTPs in the production environment to validate detection and response capabilities.

| Tool | Purpose | Mitigation Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **MITRE Caldera** | Autonomous adversary emulation engine | Replays full kill chains; reveals true detection gaps |
| **Atomic Red Team** | Library of small, single-TTP tests | Granular per-technique validation |
| **Vectr** | Purple team campaign tracker | Quantifies adversary-emulation-driven improvements over time |
| **Prelude Operator** | Cross-platform TTP execution framework | Continuous purple-team exercises |

**Future-attack mitigation:** Each emulation cycle produces a **detection gap list** → drives the next hunt → drives new YARA/SIGMA rules → permanently hardens the environment.

**Deception Technology (3.5 marks):**
Deception deploys **decoys** (fake credentials, fake files, fake services, fake network shares) that legitimate users will *never* touch but adversaries will almost inevitably interact with during reconnaissance or lateral movement.

| Deception Tool | Decoy Type | Future Mitigation Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Thinkst Canary / Canarytokens** | URL, DNS, file canaries | Instant high-fidelity alerts (zero FPs) |
| **Acalvio ShadowPlex** | Identity deception (fake AD accounts) | Catches Kerberoasting, NTLM relay |
| **Cymulate** | Breach & attack simulation | Validates end-to-end detection pipeline |
| **TrapX (SentinelOne)** | Full decoy VMs/hosts | Engages adversary, slows them, generates IoCs |

**Deployment Architecture (in text form for board):**
1. **Layer 1** — DNS canaries embedded in phishing simulations.
2. **Layer 2** — Decoy credentials sprinkled in Active Directory (marked *canary*).
3. **Layer 3** — Fake SMB shares and FTP servers in the demilitarized zone.
4. **Layer 4** — Full decoy hosts (low-interaction honeypots) on the internal network.

Any interaction with a decoy fires a **high-confidence alert** (FPR ≈ 0), provides rich **threat intelligence** (adversary tools, malware samples, lateral movement paths), and **derails the adversary's timeline** (dwell time reduction).

**Combined Effect on Future-Attack Mitigation:** Adversary emulation *finds* the gaps; threat hunting *closes* them; deception *catches the rest* automatically. Together, they form a **virtuous cycle** of continuous defensive refinement, making the organization exponentially harder to compromise with each iteration.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Bank — Read This Twice Before the Exam:**

- ✅ **Cyber Threat Hunting = Proactive, human-driven, hypothesis-based, intelligence-led** (must use *all four* adjectives in definition).
- ✅ **Breach Assumption Model** — Always assume the adversary is *already inside*; this is the philosophical foundation.
- ✅ **SANS Hunting Loop** = Hypothesis → Investigation → Analysis → Response (closed loop with feedback to CTI).
- ✅ **MITRE ATT&CK** is the universal *common language* between CTI and hunting; every hunt must cite a **Technique ID (T-code)**.
- ✅ **Hunting vs. SOC** — SOC is *reactive* (alert-driven); Hunting is *proactive* (hypothesis-driven). They are *complementary, not substitutes*.
- ✅ **Three Hunting Triggers** — (1) CTI, (2) ATT&CK gap, (3) Anomaly.
- ✅ **Three Hunting Data Sources** — Endpoint, Network, Identity (plus Cloud for modern orgs).
- ✅ **MTTD target** $\le$ 24 hours; **MTTR target** $\le$ 4 hours; **Dwell time** industry median = 10 days.
- ✅ **Hunt Coverage (ATT&CK)** is the most important maturity metric — best-in-class is $> 70\%$.
- ✅ **Detection-as-Code** paradigm — YARA, SIGMA rules in **Git**, deployed via CI/CD; ensures repeatability and version control.
- ✅ **Adversary Emulation Tools** — MITRE Caldera, Atomic Red Team, Vectr (must be cited by name in answers).
- ✅ **Deception Tools** — Thinkst Canarytokens, Acalvio ShadowPlex, Cymulate.
- ✅ **Mitigation Factor Equation** (most important formula):
$$
M = H_{\text{coverage}} \cdot H_{\text{YARA}} \cdot e^{-\lambda H_{\text{latency}}} \times 100\%
$$
- ✅ **STIX/TAXII** = standard for CTI sharing; **MISP** = open-source CTI platform; **YARA** = file/memory signature language; **SIGMA** = log signature language.
- ✅ **Future-Attack Mitigation Mechanism** = Each hunt converts a *human insight* into a *permanent automated control* — this is the *compounding* effect.
- ✅ **Predictive Hunting** uses ML/UEBA + ATT&CK to *forecast* the adversary's next move and pre-deploy controls.
- ✅ **Purple Teaming** (red + blue combined) is the most effective hunting methodology at maturity Level 4.
- ✅ **MITRE D3FEND** complements ATT&CK by cataloging *defensive techniques* — high-yield bonus term for full marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Board Pitfalls — Avoid These:**
> - ❌ Confusing *threat hunting* with *threat intelligence* (CTI feeds the hunt; hunting is the *action* on that intel).
> - ❌ Writing generic answers without citing specific **T-codes** or **tool names**.
> - ❌ Omitting the **mitigation factor derivation** in 14-mark questions.
> - ❌ Treating hunting as a *replacement* for SOC/SIEM — they are *layered*, not *substitutes*.
